title: Dachdecker
rationale: AI cannot cut and fit roofing materials, work at heights, or physically install tiles, flashing, and gutters on a real building. Almost every task requires hands-on physical work in unstructured outdoor environments, keeping AI exposure minimal.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A small number of peripheral tasks have some AI relevance. Interpreting 2D and 3D plans could be assisted by AI tools that parse construction drawings. Calculating needs for construction supplies and answering requests for quotation involve structured data that AI can handle. Keeping records of work progress and personal administration are digitally automatable. However, these are auxiliary or optional tasks — none constitute the core work product of a roofer.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of essential skills — apply roll roofing, construct wood roofs, lay interlocking roof tiles, install roof flashing, install gutters, remove roofs, prepare roofing materials, install insulation material — require physical manipulation of materials on real structures in unstructured, variable outdoor environments. 2026 robotics cannot reliably perform delicate or heavy roofing work on pitched, irregular surfaces with chimneys, edges, and protuberances. Safety-critical tasks like follow safety procedures when working at heights and secure working area require real-time human judgment in dynamic physical contexts. Recognising signs of wood rot and inspecting roofs demand tactile and visual assessment that computer vision systems are not deployed for in this trade. Regulatory and liability frameworks in Swiss construction further slow any automation adoption.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the 0–1 tier. The primary work product is a physically installed, weatherproof roof — a real-world transformation that 2026 AI and robotics cannot deliver. Digital tools may marginally assist with quoting or record-keeping, but these represent a negligible share of working time. The occupation closely matches the tier anchor examples (roofer is literally listed as a 0–1 anchor), and no evidence suggests near-term disruption.

title: Maurer und verwandte Berufe
rationale: AI cannot lay bricks, mix mortar, or build walls — the entire job requires physical manipulation of heavy materials in unstructured outdoor environments. Robots capable of masonry work exist in early research but are far from replacing skilled tradespeople on Swiss construction sites.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A small subset of tasks involves digital or cognitive work that AI can assist with. Interpreting 2D and 3D plans is increasingly supported by AI-assisted CAD tools. Calculating needs for construction supplies, estimating restoration costs, and keeping records of work progress are information-processing tasks where AI tools can reduce time. Documenting survey operations and processing incoming construction supplies involve structured data that AI agents handle well. These auxiliary tasks represent perhaps 10–15% of working time for a typical bricklayer.

**Limiting factors:** The core work product is entirely physical: laying bricks, mixing grouts, splitting bricks, snapping chalk lines, installing construction profiles, and finishing mortar joints. Every essential skill except plan interpretation involves direct physical manipulation of materials in variable outdoor or semi-structured construction environments. Current robotic masonry systems (e.g., SAM100, Hadrian X) operate only in highly controlled conditions, achieve roughly 60–70% human efficiency on simple straight walls, and cannot handle the variety of tasks listed — arches, fireplaces, kerbs, refractory linings, scaffolding, or restoration work. Physical dexterity, real-time problem-solving in unstructured environments, and the ability to assess material quality by touch and sight remain firmly human advantages. Regulatory and safety requirements in Swiss construction further slow any autonomous deployment.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits squarely in the Low (2) tier. The primary work product is the physical transformation of raw materials into built structures, which 2026 AI and robotics cannot meaningfully substitute. AI provides marginal productivity gains on planning and administrative subtasks, but these are peripheral to the core trade. The score of 2 reflects that some digital assistance is available for plan reading and supply calculations, but no commercially deployed technology threatens the bulk of bricklaying work.

title: Reinigungspersonal und Hilfskräfte in Büros, Hotels und anderen Einrichtungen
rationale: AI cannot vacuum floors, scrub toilets, make beds, or empty bins — this job is almost entirely physical manipulation of real-world spaces and objects. A small share of tasks like reporting and inventory tracking could be assisted by AI, but the core work remains firmly human.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A narrow subset of tasks in this occupation involves information handling that AI could assist with. Completing report sheets of activity, maintaining inventory of cleaning supplies, complying with checklists, and handling customer complaints are administrative or communicative tasks where AI tools (voice-to-text logging, inventory management apps, chatbot-assisted complaint routing) could reduce manual effort. These represent perhaps 5–10% of actual working time. Agentic scheduling tools could also optimize shift assignments and task sequencing. None of these, however, constitute the core work product.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of tasks — sweeping, vacuuming, mopping, scrubbing, polishing, making beds, restocking supplies, emptying bins, cleaning glass and surfaces — require physical manipulation in unstructured, variable real-world environments. Current robotics (as of 2026) can handle narrow structured tasks like warehouse pick-and-pack at ~70% human efficiency, but hotel rooms, aircraft cabins, train interiors, and office spaces present highly variable layouts, delicate surfaces, and context-dependent judgment (e.g., distinguishing a guest's belongings from rubbish, handling stains on different fabric types). Commercially deployed cleaning robots exist for flat-floor vacuuming in open spaces, but they do not replace the full scope of this role. Regulatory and hygiene compliance requirements (food safety, biohazard waste, chemical handling) also demand human accountability and physical presence.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 2). The primary work product is a physically clean environment, not a digital artifact. AI and robotics can assist marginally with scheduling, reporting, and flat-surface floor cleaning in structured settings, but cannot replicate the dexterous, context-sensitive physical labor that defines the role. Adoption barriers — cost of specialized robotics, variability of environments, and the low wage base making automation ROI poor — further suppress near-term displacement. The score of 2 reflects the small but real administrative and scheduling assistance AI can provide.

title: Köche
rationale: Cooking is fundamentally a physical craft — chopping, grilling, plating, and tasting require hands, heat, and real-time sensory judgment that robots cannot yet reliably replicate in unstructured kitchen environments. AI can help with menu planning or recipe lookup, but the core work stays human.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A small subset of a cook's responsibilities touches AI-performable territory. Menu planning, ordering supplies, and advising customers on dietary or nutritional choices are information tasks where AI tools (recipe databases, inventory management software, dietary planning apps) already provide meaningful assistance. Creating diet plans and identifying nutritional properties of food are essentially knowledge-retrieval tasks that AI handles well. These peripheral tasks represent perhaps 10–15% of a cook's working time.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of the listed essential skills — use cooking techniques, use food cutting tools, use food preparation techniques, use culinary finishing techniques, maintain kitchen equipment at correct temperature, ensure cleanliness of food preparation area — require continuous physical manipulation in a dynamic, unstructured environment. Current robotics (as of 2026) achieve roughly 70% human efficiency only in highly structured warehouse pick-and-pack settings; a professional kitchen with variable ingredients, live fire, time pressure, and sensory feedback (taste, smell, texture) is far beyond reliable autonomous deployment. The 'work in a hospitality team' and real-time quality-checking tasks also involve interpersonal coordination and tacit sensory judgment that AI cannot substitute. Regulatory food-safety requirements further demand accountable human presence.

**Net assessment:** Cooks sit firmly in the Low (2) tier. AI contributes marginally to planning and knowledge tasks but cannot perform or meaningfully substitute for the physical core of the job. Productivity amplification is modest — a cook assisted by AI menu tools is not dramatically more productive in the way a financial analyst with AI is. Adoption barriers (unstructured physical environment, sensory demands, food-safety accountability) are high and unlikely to dissolve within the 2026 horizon. The score could edge toward 3 only if AI-assisted kitchen management software becomes deeply embedded in ordering and scheduling workflows, but this does not reduce headcount.

title: Pflegedienstleiter und Pflegefachkräfte
rationale: AI can assist with documentation, care planning, and screening decisions, but the core of nursing—physically assessing patients, administering treatments, and providing hands-on care—cannot be automated. The job is anchored in physical presence and interpersonal trust, keeping exposure moderate.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A meaningful subset of nursing tasks are AI-performable at 2026 capability levels. Documentation-heavy tasks such as 'use electronic health records in nursing,' 'manage information in health care,' and 'use e-health and mobile health technologies' are already substantially AI-assisted. Clinical decision-support tools can aid 'screen patients for disease risk factors,' 'make clinical decisions,' and 'diagnose nursing care' by surfacing evidence-based recommendations from patient data. Care plan drafting ('plan nursing care,' 'develop plans related to the transfer of care') can be templated and AI-generated from structured inputs. Administrative coordination tasks—scheduling, discharge planning documentation, referral letters—are increasingly handled by AI agents. Research-adjacent tasks like 'conduct research in advanced nursing care' and 'implement scientific decision making in healthcare' benefit from AI literature synthesis and data analysis tools.

**Limiting factors:** The dominant share of nursing work is irreducibly physical and interpersonal. Tasks such as 'clean wounds and apply surgical dressings,' 'initiate life preserving measures,' 'administer medications,' 'perform health assessment,' and 'deal with emergency care situations' require physical dexterity, real-time sensory judgment, and immediate adaptive response in unstructured environments—well beyond 2026 robotics capabilities. The therapeutic relationship ('develop a collaborative therapeutic relationship,' 'empathise with the healthcare user,' 'empower individuals, families and groups') is a core care output, not a peripheral soft skill; patients and families place high trust value on human presence. Regulatory frameworks in Switzerland and internationally require licensed human professionals to hold accountability for clinical decisions, prescriptions, and patient safety. The breadth of specialist sub-roles (anaesthetics, operating theatre, palliative, paediatrics) means physical and contextual complexity is high across the board.

**Net assessment:** Nursing sits firmly in the moderate tier (score 4). AI provides genuine productivity amplification in documentation, decision support, and care coordination—potentially reducing administrative burden by 20–30%—but cannot substitute for the physical, sensory, and relational core of the occupation. Unlike information-work occupations where AI can produce the primary deliverable, nursing's primary deliverable is embodied care. Adoption of AI tools is also moderated by healthcare regulation, liability frameworks, and the conservative pace of clinical technology adoption in Switzerland. The score reflects meaningful AI assistance without displacement risk in the near term.

title: Polizisten (ohne Polizeikommissare)
rationale: AI can assist with report writing, evidence analysis, and traffic pattern monitoring, but core police work — physically patrolling, arresting suspects, managing crowds, and responding to emergencies — requires human presence and judgment. The job is anchored in physical, unpredictable real-world environments that AI cannot operate in.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A subset of tasks in this occupation are meaningfully AI-assisted. Report writing and documentation (implied by 'handle evidence,' 'hear witness accounts,' 'analyse legal evidence') can be partially automated through AI transcription and drafting tools. Traffic pattern analysis ('analyse road traffic patterns,' 'anticipate foreseeable problems on the road') is already being augmented by AI-driven monitoring systems. Facial recognition and surveillance AI can assist with 'identify security threats' and suspect identification. Drug testing workflows and document verification ('check official documents,' 'investigate forgery cases') increasingly involve automated screening tools. These represent real productivity gains for administrative and analytical subtasks.

**Limiting factors:** The core work product of this occupation is physical presence and real-world intervention — patrolling areas, detaining offenders, controlling crowds, providing emergency first response, and managing accident scenes. These tasks require embodied action in unstructured, dynamic, and often dangerous environments that 2026 robotics and AI agents cannot replicate at operational scale. Legal and institutional constraints are severe: use-of-force decisions, arrests, and law enforcement actions carry legal accountability that cannot be delegated to AI systems. Public trust and legitimacy depend on human officers. Skills like 'comply with principles of self-defence,' 'conduct frisk,' 'manage major incidents,' and 'handle conflicts' are deeply interpersonal and situationally complex. Regulatory frameworks in Switzerland and internationally explicitly require human officers for enforcement actions.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). AI provides genuine but peripheral assistance — mainly in documentation, surveillance support, and analytical tasks — but cannot substitute for the physical, legal, and interpersonal core of frontline policing. The primary work product is public safety delivered through human presence and action, not a digital artifact. Adoption barriers (legal accountability, physical environment, public trust) are among the highest of any occupation. The score reflects real but limited AI complementarity with no meaningful substitution risk in the near term.

title: Wirtschaftsprüfer, Steuerberater und verwandte Berufe
rationale: AI can already prepare tax returns, analyse financial statements, check accounting records, and draft financial reports at near-professional quality. The remaining human value lies in judgment on complex disputes, fraud investigations, and client advisory work — keeping this just below maximum exposure.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The core work product of this occupation is almost entirely informational: preparing tax returns, interpreting financial statements, performing balance sheet operations, calculating tax, checking accounting records, and preparing trial balances. These are precisely the tasks where 2026 AI excels. Large language models with long-context windows can process thousands of pages of financial records, identify discrepancies, and generate compliant financial statements. Tax preparation software augmented by AI (e.g., tools already deployed by the Big Four) automates 80%+ of standard return preparation. Optional skills such as evaluating budgets, managing payroll reports, performing cost accounting, and preparing financial auditing reports are similarly high-automation candidates. The 'information-work detection rule' applies strongly here: every essential skill describes a cognitive operation on data or documents, with zero physical transformation involved.

**Limiting factors:** Several tasks resist full automation. Conducting financial investigations into suspected fraud or insolvency requires contextual judgment, adversarial reasoning, and often courtroom or regulatory testimony — domains where AI accuracy and legal accountability remain insufficient. Advising clients on complex tax disputes and contesting claims before Swiss tax authorities involves interpersonal negotiation and jurisdiction-specific nuance that AI handles poorly in edge cases. Statutory obligations under Swiss law (OR, MWSTG, DBG) and professional certification requirements (RAB licensing for auditors) create regulatory barriers to full AI substitution. Client trust and fiduciary responsibility also anchor a human role, particularly for high-net-worth individuals and complex corporate structures. The 'liaise with auditors' and 'advise on risk management' optional skills reflect relationship-intensive work that AI augments rather than replaces.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the 8 tier. The primary work product is digital and analytical, AI tools are already deployed at scale in this sector, and productivity amplification is so strong that fewer accountants are needed per unit of work. The human role is increasingly concentrated in oversight, exception handling, complex advisory judgment, and legal accountability — exactly the 8–9 boundary description. It does not reach 9–10 because the advisory, investigative, and regulatory-contestation subtasks are substantive and not yet reliably automated, and Swiss professional licensing requirements slow full displacement. Confidence is high given the well-documented AI penetration in accounting and tax preparation globally.

title: Lehrkräfte im Primarbereich
rationale: AI can help prepare lesson plans, grade homework, and generate teaching materials, but the core job is physically present with children — managing behaviour, supervising safety, and building relationships. The irreducibly human, in-person nature of primary teaching keeps AI exposure moderate.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** Several tasks in this occupation are meaningfully AI-assisted in 2026. Lesson plan preparation and curriculum alignment ('prepare lesson content', 'develop course outline', 'advise on lesson plans') are well within current AI capability — tools like ChatGPT and specialised edtech platforms already generate differentiated lesson plans at acceptable quality. Grading and assessment tasks ('assign homework', 'assess students', 'prepare, administering and grading tests') are partially automatable for structured formats (multiple choice, short answer, reading comprehension). Progress tracking and attendance records ('keep records of attendance', 'observe student's progress') are increasingly handled by AI-integrated school management systems. Providing personalised feedback on written work is a demonstrated 2026 AI capability. These tasks collectively represent perhaps 25–35% of working time, making AI a genuine productivity amplifier for administrative and preparation work.

**Limiting factors:** The primary work product of a primary school teacher is not a document or digital artifact — it is the supervised development and socialisation of young children in a physical environment. Tasks such as 'maintain students' discipline', 'guarantee students' safety', 'perform playground surveillance', 'manage children's problems', 'implement care programmes for children', and 'attend to children's basic physical needs' require continuous physical presence and real-time human judgment that 2026 AI cannot replicate. The relational core of the job — 'maintain relations with children's parents', 'manage student relationships', 'support the positiveness of youths', 'prepare youths for adulthood' — depends on trust, emotional attunement, and embodied interaction. Swiss regulatory and safeguarding frameworks also mandate qualified human supervision of minors at all times. Even if AI handles all preparation and grading, the classroom still requires a teacher present for the full school day.

**Net assessment:** Primary teaching sits solidly in the moderate (4–5) tier. AI provides real productivity gains in lesson preparation, differentiation, and administrative tasks, but cannot substitute for the physical supervision, emotional regulation, and relational work that constitute the majority of a teacher's day. Unlike secondary or tertiary teaching where subject-matter delivery is more separable from pastoral care, primary teachers are simultaneously instructors, caregivers, and behaviour managers — roles that are deeply resistant to AI substitution. The score of 4 reflects genuine AI complementarity without displacement risk at the 2026 frontier.

title: Führungskräfte im Personalwesen
rationale: AI can automate payroll processing, draft job postings, screen CVs, and generate compliance reports, but core HR leadership involves negotiating with unions, resolving conflicts, and advising senior management. The irreducible human judgment and trust requirements keep this in the high rather than very-high tier.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A significant share of the listed tasks are information-processing work that 2026 AI handles well. 'Manage payroll' and 'track key performance indicators' are largely automatable via existing HR platforms with AI layers (Workday, SAP SuccessFactors). 'Identify necessary human resources,' 'evaluate training,' and 'develop training programmes' involve structured data analysis and document generation where AI tools already assist substantially. 'Organise staff assessment' and 'monitor company policy' involve document review and compliance checking — tasks where large-context AI models excel. Optional skills like 'analyse financial risk,' 'calculate benefits,' and 'deliver online training' are increasingly AI-assisted or AI-replaceable. The information-work detection rule applies: most essential skills describe cognitive operations on data, documents, and policies rather than physical transformation.

**Limiting factors:** The core value of an HR manager is organizational authority, trust, and judgment in ambiguous interpersonal situations. 'Handle conflicts,' 'negotiate employment agreements,' 'negotiate with employment agencies,' and 'consult with senior management' require human credibility, political acumen, and accountability that AI cannot substitute in 2026. 'Promote gender equality in business contexts' and 'ensure compliance with labour legislation' involve legal liability and stakeholder trust that organizations are unlikely to delegate to AI systems. 'Plan medium to long-term objectives' and 'apply strategic thinking' require cross-domain synthesis under genuine organizational ambiguity. Swiss labour law complexity and co-determination norms (Mitbestimmung) add regulatory friction. The managerial title itself implies formal authority that cannot be delegated to an AI agent.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits solidly in the high-exposure tier (6). AI substantially amplifies productivity in the administrative, analytical, and compliance-monitoring subtasks — meaning fewer HR staff are needed per employee headcount — but the strategic, negotiation, and conflict-resolution core resists substitution. The role is unlikely to be eliminated but will be restructured: fewer junior HR generalists, with remaining managers handling higher-complexity cases. Confidence is medium because the occupation spans a wide range from operational HR managers (more exposed) to senior industrial relations directors (less exposed).

title: Softwareentwickler
rationale: AI can now write, debug, and refactor code autonomously for well-defined tasks, handling much of what junior-to-mid-level developers do daily. The remaining human edge lies in system architecture decisions, ambiguous requirements, and client trust — keeping this just below maximum exposure.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The core work product of software developers is digital code and documentation — precisely where 2026 AI tools are most capable. Agentic coding assistants (GitHub Copilot, Cursor, Claude Code) can autonomously handle multi-file code generation, debugging, refactoring, and test writing for well-specified tasks. ESCO essential skills such as 'debug software,' 'use software libraries,' 'use software design patterns,' 'develop software prototype,' and 'provide technical documentation' are all substantially AI-performable today. Tasks like 'create flowchart diagram,' 'create data models,' and 'translate requirement concepts into visual design' are increasingly handled by AI with minimal human editing. The optional skills list — 'design database scheme,' 'integrate system components,' 'perform ICT troubleshooting,' 'migrate existing data' — further confirms that a large share of day-to-day implementation work is within current AI capability. Productivity amplification is already measurable: studies show 30–55% throughput gains for developers using AI tools, meaning fewer developers are needed for equivalent output.

**Limiting factors:** Several tasks resist near-term AI substitution. 'Define software architecture' and 'design information system' require cross-domain judgment under genuine ambiguity — weighing organizational constraints, long-term maintainability, and non-functional requirements that are rarely fully specified. 'Build business relationships,' 'interact with users to gather requirements,' and 'consult with customers concerning maintenance' involve stakeholder trust and political navigation that AI cannot replicate. 'Execute feasibility study' and 'provide cost benefit analysis reports' require contextual business judgment beyond pattern-matching. 'Manage engineering project' and 'oversee development of software' involve accountability structures and team coordination that remain human-anchored. Regulatory requirements around 'legal requirements of ICT products' and 'develop documentation in accordance with legal requirements' create compliance accountability that organizations are reluctant to delegate to AI without human sign-off.

**Net assessment:** Software development sits firmly in the 8–9 range. The primary work product is entirely digital, the majority of implementation tasks are demonstrably AI-performable at acceptable quality today, and productivity amplification is already reducing headcount needs. The score lands at 8 rather than 9 because architecture design, requirements elicitation, and client-facing judgment remain genuinely human-advantaged — not merely as a matter of trust or regulation, but because these tasks involve irreducible ambiguity that current AI handles poorly in production. The occupation is being restructured rather than eliminated: senior developers focusing on architecture and requirements while AI handles implementation is the emerging pattern, compressing the total workforce needed.

title: Grafik- und Multimediadesigner
rationale: AI can now generate images, animations, and design layouts at near-professional quality, automating much of the routine asset creation that fills a graphic designer's workday. The remaining human value lies in creative direction, client negotiation, and brand strategy — keeping the score just below maximum.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The core work product of this occupation is entirely digital — images, animations, layouts, and multimedia assets — placing it squarely in the highest-exposure category. 2026 AI tools (Midjourney, Stable Diffusion, Adobe Firefly, Runway, Sora) can now perform or substantially assist with: creating digital images and illustrations, rendering 3D images, developing animations, performing image editing, designing graphics, translating requirement concepts into visual design, and producing motion graphics. Tasks like 'convert scribbles into virtual sketches,' 'create 3D texture maps,' 'render 3D images,' and 'develop design ideas' are directly addressed by commercially available generative AI. Desktop publishing and layout tasks are increasingly automated within tools like Adobe Express and Canva AI. The ESCO data confirms that 85%+ of designers already report AI skills as essential, and the optional knowledge list is dominated by software tools — confirming this is pure digital-environment work.

**Limiting factors:** Several task clusters resist near-term AI substitution. Client consultation and negotiation ('consult with business clients,' 'negotiate design solutions,' 'build business relationships') require interpersonal trust and contextual judgment that AI cannot independently execute. Creative direction and concept formulation ('define artistic approach,' 'develop creative ideas,' 'contextualise artistic work') still benefit from human cultural fluency and strategic brand understanding. Complex multi-stakeholder productions — art direction, assembling artistic teams, supervising production — require human coordination. Regulatory and legal tasks ('apply publicity code,' 'copyright legislation,' 'ensure legal gaming') require accountable human judgment. The breadth of sub-occupations (stop-motion animator, gambling game designer, video designer for live performance) includes some roles with physical or live-event components that are harder to automate. However, these are minority sub-roles within the broader occupation.

**Net assessment:** This occupation scores 8 — 'very high' exposure. The primary work product is digital asset creation, which AI can now perform at production-usable quality across most routine instances. The human role has shifted toward oversight, creative direction, client relationship management, and exception handling — precisely the boundary between scores 8 and 9. It does not reach 9-10 because creative strategy, brand judgment, and client-facing negotiation remain genuinely human-advantaged, and because the occupation spans heterogeneous sub-roles with varying automation timelines. The Swiss market's high wages (CHF 8,468/month) may slow displacement through premium positioning on creative strategy, but volume-based routine design work faces severe substitution pressure.

title: Datenerfasser
rationale: AI and automation tools can handle virtually all data entry work — receiving documents, typing in numbers, verifying accuracy, and moving data between systems — with greater speed and fewer errors than humans. This is the textbook case of full automation, matching the maximum-exposure anchor.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** Every core task in this occupation is a textbook automation target. Receiving and registering invoices and forms is handled today by OCR and intelligent document processing (IDP) platforms like ABBYY, AWS Textract, and Azure Form Recognizer. Entering numerical data, codes, and text from source material — the literal definition of this job — is the oldest and most mature RPA use case, deployed at scale since the early 2010s. Verifying accuracy and completeness of data maps directly to automated validation rules and ML-based anomaly detection. Importing and exporting data between database systems is handled by ETL pipelines and integration platforms (Zapier, MuleSoft, dbt) that require no human intervention once configured. The essential skills — process data, perform data cleansing, maintain data entry requirements, apply statistical analysis techniques — are all executable by 2026 AI tooling at production quality. Even the optional skills (normalise data, implement data warehousing techniques, manage data collection systems) describe tasks that are increasingly handled by automated data engineering pipelines.

**Limiting factors:** The residual human role is narrow: configuring and auditing automated systems, handling genuinely ambiguous or damaged source documents that defeat OCR, and maintaining accountability for data governance in regulated contexts. In Switzerland, data protection law (nDSG) may require a named human responsible for data processes, but this is a compliance role, not a data entry role. Physical handling of paper documents in legacy environments provides a small buffer, but even this is shrinking as document digitisation at the point of origin (e-invoicing mandates, digital forms) eliminates paper inputs. No meaningful physical, interpersonal, or creative dimension exists in this occupation to anchor human value.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the maximum-exposure anchor (10) by design — it is the canonical example of work that AI and automation displace entirely. The Swiss employment figure of 276 workers already reflects decades of attrition from earlier automation waves; the remaining roles likely persist due to legacy systems, niche document types, or organisational inertia rather than genuine task irreplaceability. The trajectory is toward zero net employment in this category as IDP and agentic workflow tools mature. Confidence is high because there is no ambiguity about task content and no meaningful limiting factor that would push the score below the maximum tier.

title: Offiziere in regulären Streitkräften
rationale: AI can assist with logistics planning, threat analysis, and writing situation reports, but commanding troops, making real-time combat decisions, and leading people under physical danger cannot be automated. The job's core output is physical leadership and command presence, keeping exposure low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A subset of the 58 essential skills involves information processing that 2026 AI tools handle well. Writing situation reports, analysing logistic needs, performing resource planning, managing administrative systems, and managing budgets are tasks where AI can draft, summarise, and optimise at near-professional quality. Geographic information systems work and threat analysis (identify security threats, analyse potential threats against national security) are increasingly AI-augmented, with tools like Palantir and military-grade AI analytics already deployed. Coordination tasks such as scheduling troop deployment or managing vessel fleet maintenance schedules are amenable to agentic workflow automation. These administrative and analytical subtasks may represent 20–30% of an officer's working time.

**Limiting factors:** The core work product of a commissioned officer is not a document or a dataset — it is command authority exercised over human beings in high-stakes, physically dangerous, and legally accountable contexts. Tasks like leading military troops, giving battle commands, performing military operations, operating armoured fighting vehicles, conducting military drill, and coordinating rescue missions require physical presence, real-time situational judgment in unstructured environments, and the legal authority to order lethal force. These are among the most AI-resistant task types that exist. Regulatory and institutional barriers are extreme: military command authority is vested in persons by law and cannot be delegated to software. Trust, morale, and unit cohesion — central to officer effectiveness — are irreducibly interpersonal. Robotics capable of replacing command presence in unstructured combat environments remain decades away from deployment.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). AI provides genuine productivity gains in the administrative and analytical fringe of the role — perhaps reducing paperwork burden and improving logistics optimisation — but cannot substitute for the physical, legal, and interpersonal core of commissioned command. The score is 3 rather than 2 because the analytical subtasks (threat assessment, logistics, reporting) are meaningfully AI-augmentable, but the primary work product is irreducibly human. Uncertainty is low; military command is one of the clearest cases of AI complementarity rather than substitution.

title: Unteroffiziere in regulären Streitkräften
rationale: AI cannot lead troops in combat, enforce discipline under fire, or make split-second tactical decisions in unstructured, dangerous physical environments. The core work is irreducibly physical, interpersonal, and high-stakes, keeping AI exposure very low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A subset of tasks involve information processing where AI offers some assistance. Geographic information systems usage, surveillance equipment monitoring, maintaining operational communications, and developing investigation strategies all have digital components where AI tools (e.g., automated threat detection via computer vision, signals intelligence analysis, map generation) are increasingly deployed. The 'identify security threats' and 'identify terrorism threats' skills overlap with AI-assisted pattern recognition in intelligence workflows. Applying information security policies is a largely procedural task AI can partially automate. These tasks represent perhaps 15–20% of the role's cognitive load.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of essential skills are physically embodied, interpersonally demanding, or safety-critical in ways that preclude AI substitution in 2026. Leading military troops, performing military combat techniques, military drill, giving battle commands, managing troop deployment, and performing covert investigations all require physical presence, real-time human judgment under extreme stress, and legal/command accountability that cannot be delegated to AI systems. Military operations occur in unstructured, adversarial, and often communications-degraded environments — precisely the conditions where current AI agents fail. Regulatory and chain-of-command structures in armed forces impose strict human accountability for use of force, creating hard institutional barriers. The supervisory and disciplinary role over other soldiers is fundamentally interpersonal.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low (2) tier. While AI tools augment intelligence analysis, surveillance, and communications at the margins, the core work product — commanding soldiers, executing physical operations, enforcing discipline in dangerous environments — is deeply resistant to AI substitution. The physical, legal, and institutional constraints are among the strongest of any occupation. Uncertainty is low; this is not a borderline case.

title: Angehörige der regulären Streitkräfte in sonstigen Rängen
rationale: AI cannot replace the physical combat, weapons handling, and field operations that define this role. The job is fundamentally about physical presence, force, and real-world action in unpredictable environments, keeping AI exposure very low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A subset of tasks within this occupational group involve information processing that AI can assist with. Intelligence communications interceptors analyse transmitted communications, identify electronic signals, and develop military intelligence records — tasks where AI signal analysis and pattern recognition tools are increasingly capable. Military engineers who define technology strategy, examine engineering principles, and design components have some exposure to AI-assisted design and analysis tools. Writing routine reports, reading maps, using geographic information systems, and monitoring technology trends are also partially automatable with current AI. These represent perhaps 15–20% of the task portfolio across the group.

**Limiting factors:** The dominant work products of this occupational group are irreducibly physical: infantry soldiers fight in combat, handle weapons, and perform military drill; bomb disposal technicians physically disarm land mines and operate in hazardous unstructured environments; paratroopers and airmen execute physical missions requiring real-time human judgment under life-or-death conditions. Even air force pilots, while operating increasingly automated aircraft, must perform take-off and landing, execute flight manoeuvres, and respond to novel emergencies. Robotics and autonomous systems are not yet deployable at scale in unstructured combat or EOD environments at 2026 capability levels. Regulatory, sovereignty, and rules-of-engagement constraints create enormous adoption barriers for autonomous lethal systems. The physical, interpersonal, and trust dimensions of military service — unit cohesion, command authority, accountability — are deeply resistant to AI substitution.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 2). The heterogeneity of sub-occupations (pilot vs. infantryman vs. bomb disposal technician) introduces some uncertainty, and the intelligence/signals sub-role has meaningfully higher exposure than the combat roles. However, the modal worker in this group performs physical, high-stakes, unstructured tasks in environments where AI substitution is both technically immature and institutionally constrained. AI serves as an auxiliary tool (better radar analysis, route planning, intelligence processing) rather than a substitute for the core work.

title: Führungskräfte, onA
rationale: AI can draft reports, analyze data, and prepare briefings that managers rely on, reducing the information-processing burden of leadership. However, the core of management — making judgment calls, motivating people, and taking accountability — resists automation.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** ISCO 1000 ('Führungskräfte, onA') is a residual catch-all category for managers not classified elsewhere, making task content highly heterogeneous. Typical managerial work includes planning, budgeting, performance monitoring, reporting, and coordination — all of which have significant AI-augmentation potential. AI tools in 2026 can draft strategic documents, synthesize KPI dashboards, prepare board presentations, automate scheduling and meeting summaries, and generate performance reports. These information-processing and communication tasks likely represent 30–50% of a typical manager's time, and AI productivity amplification here is real and already deployed in enterprise settings.

**Limiting factors:** The core managerial output is organizational outcomes achieved through people — hiring, motivating, resolving conflict, building trust, and making judgment calls under ambiguity. These interpersonal and accountability functions are deeply resistant to AI substitution in 2026. Regulatory and governance structures place legal accountability on human managers. Furthermore, the 'onA' (ohne nähere Angabe — not elsewhere classified) designation means this group likely includes operational managers in physical industries (construction, logistics, hospitality) where much of the work involves on-site presence and real-world problem-solving, not just digital information work.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the moderate tier (score 5). AI meaningfully amplifies managerial productivity on information tasks but does not substitute for the judgment, accountability, and interpersonal leadership that define the role. The low confidence reflects the extreme heterogeneity of this residual ISCO category — a logistics site manager and a digital marketing manager both fall here but have very different AI exposure profiles. The score represents a central estimate across that distribution.

title: Angehörige gesetzgebender Körperschaften
rationale: AI can help legislators draft bills, analyse policy documents, and summarise constituent concerns, but the core work — negotiating political deals, casting votes, and representing constituents — requires human legitimacy and trust. The job is fundamentally about political power and accountability, not information processing.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** Several peripheral tasks are meaningfully AI-assisted. Drafting and analysing legislation ('analyse legislation', 'prepare legislation proposition', 'advise on legislative acts') maps directly onto 2026 AI strengths in long-document analysis and structured text generation — AI can already produce first-draft bill language, summarise regulatory impact, and flag constitutional conflicts. Budget analysis ('budgetary principles', 'manage budgets', 'conduct financial audits') is similarly AI-amenable; financial document review is one of the most mature AI use cases. Writing meeting reports and preparing presentations are routine text tasks AI handles well. These auxiliary information-processing subtasks, taken together, represent perhaps 20–30% of a legislator's working hours.

**Limiting factors:** The primary work product of a legislator is not a document — it is a political decision backed by democratic legitimacy. 'Make legislative decisions', 'perform political negotiation', 'government representation', and 'perform debates' are irreducibly human: they require the exercise of constitutionally granted authority, public trust, and accountability to voters. No AI can cast a vote in parliament or hold a ministerial portfolio. 'Build community relations', 'attend community functions', and 'maintain relations with local representatives' depend on physical presence and interpersonal credibility. Regulatory and constitutional barriers are absolute: legislative authority is vested in elected or appointed persons by law, not in tools. Crisis management and diplomatic negotiation involve high-stakes ambiguity and relationship capital that 2026 AI cannot replicate.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). AI functions as a productivity tool for the information-processing periphery — research, drafting, financial review — but cannot substitute for the core: political agency, democratic legitimacy, and negotiated human judgment. The main uncertainty is whether AI-assisted drafting and constituent analysis will reduce the need for legislative staff rather than legislators themselves, leaving the headcount of elected officials essentially unchanged by design.

title: Leitende Verwaltungsbedienstete

analysis:

AI can draft policy documents, summarise legislation, prepare budget analyses, and generate briefing materials at near-professional quality. Optional skills such as 'advise on legislative acts,' 'analyse foreign affairs policies,' and 'advise on economic development' are also partially automatable through AI-assisted research and synthesis.

**Limiting factors:** The core work product of this occupation is not a document or analysis — it is the exercise of institutional authority, political judgment, and sovereign representation. **Net assessment:** This occupation sits at moderate exposure (score 5). The heterogeneity of sub-occupations (from central bank governor to fire commissioner) introduces some uncertainty; roles with heavier analytical portfolios (e.g., public administration manager) may trend toward 6, while diplomatic roles may sit closer to 4. title: Leitende Bedienstete von Interessenorganisationen

analysis:

analysis:

title: Führungskräfte in der betrieblichen Administration und in unternehmensbezogenen Dienstleistungen, onA
rationale: AI can draft reports, analyse financial data, and automate routine administrative workflows, freeing up significant managerial time. However, strategic decision-making, stakeholder leadership, and accountability for organisational outcomes keep human managers central.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** ISCO 1210 covers general managers in business administration and corporate services — a broad catch-all category. Core tasks include planning and directing administrative operations, overseeing budgets, coordinating departments, and reporting to boards. AI tools in 2026 can handle a substantial share of the information-processing substrate: financial reporting and variance analysis, drafting board presentations and policy documents, synthesising large volumes of operational data, scheduling and workflow coordination, and compliance monitoring. Agentic tools can automate multi-step administrative pipelines (expense approvals, procurement workflows, HR onboarding documentation). The 'optional knowledge' profile for this group typically includes ERP systems, financial software, and project management platforms — confirming that much of the day-to-day work is conducted in digital environments where AI integration is already underway. **Limiting factors:** The core value delivered by this occupational group is organisational leadership — setting strategy, managing people, negotiating with external partners, and being accountable for outcomes. These tasks involve high-stakes ambiguity, political judgment, and trust relationships that AI cannot replicate in 2026. Regulatory and governance requirements (e.g., Swiss OR/CO obligations, board accountability, data protection) require named human decision-makers. The occupation is also highly heterogeneous: a CFO-adjacent administrative director and a facilities services manager both fall under 1210, but their AI exposure profiles differ substantially. Physical presence for team leadership, crisis management, and stakeholder negotiations remains expected and valued. AI substitution risk is therefore concentrated in the analytical and documentation subtasks, not the core managerial function.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the boundary of the 'High' tier (6–7). However, the primary work product is organisational direction and accountability, which remains human-anchored. The wide heterogeneity of the ISCO 1210 catch-all category (ranging from pure administrative coordinators to senior corporate service directors) introduces genuine uncertainty, warranting a 'low' confidence rating. A score of 6 reflects demonstrated AI impact on the information-work substrate while acknowledging that leadership judgment is the dominant value driver.

title: Führungskräfte im Bereich Finanzen
rationale: AI can now draft financial plans, analyse company performance, model market trends, and generate financial reports at near-professional quality. However, the core value of this role lies in strategic judgment, stakeholder consultation, and organizational leadership, which keeps it out of the highest exposure tier.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The essential skills list is dominated by information-processing tasks that 2026 AI handles well. 'Financial statements' interpretation and 'enforce financial policies' involve document-heavy work that AI can largely automate. title: Führungskräfte in Unternehmenspolitik und -planung

**Limiting factors:** The dominant work product of this occupation is not a digital artifact — it is a functioning farm, plantation, or forest operation. Core tasks require physical presence: inspecting fields to assess crop maturity (task k), directing planting, irrigation, and harvesting crews (task j), maintaining buildings and water systems (task i), and supervising workers and contractors (task l). These require unstructured outdoor mobility, real-time judgment under weather and biological variability, and interpersonal authority over labor. Regulatory compliance in forestry (environmental legislation, forestry regulations, health and safety) involves local, context-specific interpretation that AI handles poorly without deep domain grounding. Contracting with farmers and buyers (tasks c, d) involves negotiation and relationship management. Physical robotics for field supervision and crew coordination remain far below human capability in unstructured agricultural environments.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). The information-processing subtasks are real but peripheral to the core value delivered — operational control of a living, physical production system. AI functions as a useful auxiliary tool (reporting, market data, disease identification support) rather than a substitute for the managerial presence and physical judgment that define the role. The main uncertainty is the pace of precision agriculture AI adoption, which could gradually shift more diagnostic and planning tasks toward automation, but this is unlikely to materially reshape the occupation by 2026.

title: Führungskräfte in der Produktion von Waren, im Bergbau und im Bau sowie in der Logistik, onA
rationale: AI can automate production scheduling, logistics planning, and performance reporting, which are core parts of this management role. However, on-site decision-making, supplier negotiations, and leading frontline teams in physical environments keep the score from going higher.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** This occupation's core outputs are managerial decisions and coordination artifacts — production plans, schedules, KPI reports, procurement decisions, and logistics workflows. These are information-work products that 2026 AI handles well. AI tools can already automate demand forecasting, production scheduling optimization (e.g., via ERP-integrated AI), supply chain risk monitoring, and performance dashboards. Skills such as 'analyse production processes,' 'manage supply chain,' 'monitor production performance,' and 'prepare reports' are substantially AI-performable. Agentic workflow tools can draft shift plans, flag bottlenecks, and generate variance analyses with minimal human input. The administrative and analytical burden of this role — historically a large share of a manager's week — is increasingly automatable.

**Limiting factors:** The occupation is anchored in physical production, mining, construction, and logistics environments where AI cannot substitute for on-site presence, safety oversight, and real-time physical problem-solving. Managing frontline workers, handling labor disputes, conducting site inspections, and responding to equipment failures or safety incidents require embodied judgment and interpersonal authority that AI cannot replicate in 2026. Regulatory compliance in Swiss industrial and construction contexts (SUVA safety rules, environmental permits) requires human accountability. Supplier and contractor negotiations involve trust, relationship capital, and contextual judgment that remain human-advantaged. These physical and interpersonal anchors are not peripheral — they are central to why this role exists.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits solidly in the High tier (6). AI will substantially reduce the analytical and administrative workload — perhaps 40–50% of time spent on reporting, scheduling, and data analysis — making each manager more productive and reducing headcount demand over time. But the physical environment, safety accountability, and people-leadership core resist substitution. The score reflects productivity amplification more than direct task replacement. Uncertainty is moderate because the heterogeneity across sub-sectors (manufacturing vs. mining vs. construction vs. logistics) means AI adoption rates will vary considerably.

title: Führungskräfte in der Produktion von Waren
rationale: AI can automate production scheduling, quality reporting, and supply chain analysis, but managing factory floors, leading shift teams, and responding to physical breakdowns require on-site human judgment. The mix of digital planning work and hands-on operational leadership keeps exposure moderate.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** Production managers in ISCO 1321 perform substantial information-processing work that is increasingly AI-amenable. Planning and scheduling production runs, analyzing output metrics, preparing cost and efficiency reports, managing procurement documentation, and monitoring KPIs against targets are all tasks where AI tools (ERP-integrated analytics, demand forecasting models, automated reporting dashboards) already operate at near-professional quality. Skills such as 'analyse production processes,' 'manage budgets,' 'report on production results,' and 'coordinate supply chain activities' map directly onto tasks that 2026 AI agents can handle with meaningful autonomy. Agentic workflow tools can draft shift schedules, flag bottlenecks from sensor data, and generate variance reports with minimal human input.

**Limiting factors:** The core value of a production manager is not the information artifact but the operational outcome — a functioning factory floor, a motivated workforce, and real-time problem-solving when machinery fails or a supplier drops out. Skills like 'manage staff,' 'resolve conflicts,' 'ensure health and safety compliance,' and 'respond to equipment breakdowns' require physical presence, contextual judgment, and interpersonal authority that 2026 AI cannot replicate. Regulatory accountability (Swiss labor law, safety certifications, ISO audits) legally anchors human responsibility. Furthermore, manufacturing environments are heterogeneous and unstructured — AI robotics and agents perform poorly when physical conditions deviate from norms, which is precisely when managers earn their pay.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the boundary of Tier 4–5 (moderate). Roughly 35–45% of tasks — the planning, reporting, and analytical work — are meaningfully AI-assisted or partially automatable, but the dominant value-add remains physical coordination, people leadership, and crisis response on the shop floor. AI is more complementary than substitutive here: it reduces the administrative burden on managers but does not replace the role. The score of 5 reflects genuine productivity amplification without near-term workforce displacement. Uncertainty is moderate because the balance between digital planning work and hands-on management varies significantly across firm size and industry sector.

title: Führungskräfte in der Produktion im Bergbau
rationale: AI can assist with cost monitoring, report generation, and risk forecasting, but the core job involves on-site safety oversight, emergency management, and regulatory accountability in hazardous physical environments. The irreducible physical presence, legal liability, and crisis judgment keep AI exposure low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** Several tasks in this role involve structured information processing where AI tools offer genuine assistance. Monitoring mine costs and production output, preparing reports, forecasting organisational risks, and assessing operating costs are all data-driven tasks where 2026 AI can accelerate analysis and flag anomalies. Budget management and financial optimisation involve spreadsheet-level work that AI copilots handle well. Maintaining plans of a mining site and preparing technical reports are document-centric tasks where AI drafting tools add productivity. The optional skill 'business analysis' and knowledge of economics and management principles are areas where AI-assisted decision support is commercially available today. Collectively, perhaps 20–30% of working hours involve these information-processing subtasks.

**Limiting factors:** The dominant share of this role is anchored in physical, regulatory, and interpersonal realities that strongly resist AI substitution. Inspecting mine safety conditions requires physical presence in hazardous underground or open-pit environments — no agentic AI system operates reliably in unstructured, dangerous physical settings at 2026 capability levels. Managing emergency procedures and coordinating responses to unexpected circumstances demand real-time situational judgment with life-safety consequences, where AI cannot hold statutory accountability. The ESCO description explicitly notes 'statutory responsibility for safety' — legal liability is non-delegable to AI. Interfacing with anti-mining lobbyists, managing staff, and maintaining stakeholder relationships require interpersonal trust and negotiation that AI cannot substitute. Geological assessment, mine development evaluation, and compliance with mineral extraction regulations require domain expertise validated by professional licensing frameworks. The physical environment (underground mines, quarries, oil and gas sites) is precisely the unstructured setting where robotics and AI agents remain far below human capability.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). The information-processing subtasks — cost monitoring, report writing, risk forecasting — are genuinely AI-assisted, preventing a score of 1–2. However, the core value delivered by a mine manager is physical-environment oversight, statutory safety accountability, emergency command, and regulatory compliance in a hazardous setting. These are not digitisable work products. AI functions as a productivity tool for the administrative margin of the role, not as a substitute for its substance. Uncertainty is low given the clear physical and legal anchors.

title: Führungskräfte in der Produktion im Bau
rationale: AI can help with document drafting, budget tracking, and compliance checking, but the core job involves coordinating people on physical construction sites, negotiating with contractors, and making real-time judgment calls under uncertainty. The irreducibly physical, interpersonal, and legally accountable nature of the role keeps AI exposure moderate.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** Several tasks in this role are meaningfully AI-assisted by 2026. Preparing tenders and contract bids, interpreting architectural drawings and specifications, identifying construction materials from blueprints, and preparing construction documents are information-processing tasks where AI tools (large-context document analysis, CAD-integrated AI, cost estimation software) already provide substantial productivity gains. Budget management, cost management, and ensuring compliance with legal requirements and construction product regulations are areas where AI can flag deviations, cross-reference regulatory databases, and automate reporting. Optional skills like managing construction archives, monitoring parameters' compliance, and carrying out tendering are increasingly supported by project management AI platforms. These tasks collectively represent perhaps 30–40% of the role's cognitive workload.

**Limiting factors:** The dominant share of this occupation's value lies in tasks that resist AI substitution. Coordinating labour resources on dynamic physical sites, negotiating with subcontractors and building owners, overseeing safety management in real-time, and managing unexpected site conditions require embodied presence, trust-based relationships, and contextual judgment that 2026 AI cannot replicate. Legal accountability is a strong barrier: construction managers bear personal and organizational liability for safety compliance, building inspections, and contract performance — regulators and courts require a named human responsible. The role also involves managing subcontractor performance and staff selection, which are interpersonal and organizational tasks where AI remains advisory at best. Physical site visits, inspections, and crisis response are entirely outside current AI capability.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the low end of the moderate tier (score 4). AI tools are genuinely useful for the document-heavy, compliance-tracking, and estimation subtasks, and productivity amplification is real — a construction manager with AI assistance can handle more projects simultaneously. However, the primary work product is a completed physical construction project delivered safely, on time, and within budget, which requires continuous human coordination, physical presence, and accountable judgment. Adoption barriers are also significant: Swiss construction regulation, liability frameworks, and the conservative procurement culture of the industry slow AI integration. The score could rise toward 5–6 as AI-assisted project management platforms mature, but the physical and legal anchors are durable.

title: Führungskräfte in der Beschaffung, Logistik und in verwandten Bereichen
rationale: AI can automate inventory tracking, route optimization, demand forecasting, and contract analysis — tasks that make up a real but minority share of this role. However, the core value lies in managing people, negotiating with suppliers, handling crises, and overseeing physical operations, which AI cannot replace.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A meaningful portion of this role involves information-processing tasks where 2026 AI tools are demonstrably capable. Supply chain analytics (analyse supply chain strategies, analyse supply chain trends, perform data analysis), inventory management (control reorder points, manage inventory, maintain financial records), cost accounting, market research, and report writing (write work-related reports, analyse work-related written reports) are all tasks where AI agents and analytical tools can now perform at near-professional quality. Contract analysis and negotiation preparation (manage contracts, negotiate sales contracts) benefit substantially from large-context LLMs that can review supplier agreements, flag risks, and draft terms. Route optimization and dispatch management (manage dispatch software systems, develop efficiency plans for logistics operations) are already heavily automated in enterprise logistics platforms. These tasks collectively represent perhaps 30–40% of the role's cognitive workload.

**Limiting factors:** The dominant value of this occupation is managerial and relational: leading teams (lead a team, manage staff, give instructions to staff, encourage teams for continuous improvement), building and maintaining supplier and customer relationships (build business relationships, maintain relationship with suppliers, maintain relationship with customers), and handling operational crises (handle incidents, handle stressful situations, solve operational transport problems). These require real-time judgment in ambiguous, high-stakes situations involving human stakeholders — an area where AI remains a support tool rather than a substitute. Physical oversight responsibilities — overseeing dispatch of vehicles, supervising warehouse and airport operations, conducting emergency exercises, enforcing safety regulations — require on-site presence and accountability that AI cannot provide. Regulatory compliance across aviation, rail, pipeline, and international transport frameworks (comply with legal regulations, ensure regulatory compliance in pipeline infrastructures, railway framework legislation) involves liability and institutional trust that organizations are not delegating to AI. The breadth of sub-occupations (airport director, pipeline superintendent, railway station manager) also means many roles are deeply embedded in safety-critical physical infrastructure.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the moderate tier (score 5). AI meaningfully augments the analytical, reporting, and planning subtasks — potentially reducing the time managers spend on these by 30–50% — but the core managerial, relational, and physical-oversight functions are resistant to substitution. The productivity amplification effect is real but unlikely to dramatically reduce headcount in the near term, given that the bottleneck is judgment and accountability rather than information processing. Confidence is medium because the sub-occupations are heterogeneous: a logistics and distribution manager in a data-rich e-commerce environment faces higher exposure than a railway station manager whose work is predominantly operational and safety-focused.

title: Führungskräfte in der Erbringung von Dienstleistungen im Bereich Informations- und Kommunikationstechnologie
rationale: AI can automate much of the analytical groundwork ICT managers rely on — drafting strategies, analysing systems, preparing reports, and managing project documentation. analysis:

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the High tier (6). title: Führungskräfte in der Erbringung von spezialisierten Dienstleistungen, onA
rationale: AI can automate reporting, scheduling, and data analysis that managers rely on, but core leadership tasks like directing teams, resolving conflicts, and making strategic calls remain human-dependent. The score reflects moderate-to-high exposure offset by the irreducibly interpersonal nature of management.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** ISCO 1340 covers managers in specialized services not elsewhere classified — a deliberately broad catch-all category. Typical managerial tasks include planning and coordinating service delivery, preparing budgets and reports, monitoring performance metrics, and communicating with stakeholders. These information-processing and document-production tasks are substantially AI-performable in 2026: AI tools can draft operational reports, analyze KPI dashboards, generate budget forecasts, prepare meeting agendas, and summarize regulatory requirements. Agentic workflow tools can handle scheduling, correspondence drafting, and routine compliance monitoring. If the underlying specialized service is itself information-intensive (e.g., managing a consulting or analytics unit), AI exposure compounds further.

**Limiting factors:** The core managerial output is organizational coordination and human direction — motivating staff, resolving interpersonal conflicts, exercising judgment in ambiguous personnel or strategic situations, and maintaining accountability relationships with clients and regulators. These tasks resist AI substitution in 2026. Additionally, the 'specialized services' label is deliberately vague: some sub-occupations in this group may involve managing teams in physical or regulated environments (e.g., social services, specialized care, technical inspection), where physical presence, professional licensing, and trust relationships create strong adoption barriers. The heterogeneity of the category is the primary source of uncertainty.

**Net assessment:** The occupation sits at the boundary of the High tier (6–7). Routine information work — reporting, analysis, scheduling — is meaningfully AI-assisted or automatable, reducing the need for support staff and amplifying individual manager productivity. However, the irreducibly human core of management (judgment, accountability, interpersonal authority) prevents a higher score. Confidence is low because ISCO 1340 aggregates very different sub-occupations; a manager of a data analytics firm faces far higher exposure than a manager of a specialized social care unit.

title: Führungskräfte in der Erbringung von Dienstleistungen der Kinderbetreuung
rationale: AI can help with budget tracking, report writing, and regulatory compliance checks, but the core work—supervising staff, protecting children, managing crises, and building trust with families—requires physical presence and human judgment. The job is too interpersonal and safety-critical to be substantially automated.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A meaningful subset of tasks in this role are information-processing in nature and partially AI-addressable. Preparing and maintaining records and accounts (task h), reviewing government codes and developing compliance procedures (task f), preparing reports, managing budgets, and writing organisational policies all involve structured document work that 2026 AI tools handle well. Skills like 'write work-related reports,' 'present reports,' 'manage budgets,' 'perform risk analysis,' 'apply organisational techniques,' and 'monitor regulations in social services' are all candidates for AI-assisted productivity gains. AI scheduling and workflow tools can support 'establish daily priorities,' 'coordinate events,' and 'coordinate educational programmes.' These administrative and compliance-oriented subtasks likely represent 20–30% of the role's time, and AI can meaningfully accelerate them.

**Limiting factors:** The dominant share of this occupation's core work product is not digital—it is the safe, developmentally appropriate care of children and the management of the humans who provide it. Tasks such as 'supervise children,' 'perform playground surveillance,' 'manage social crisis,' 'contribute to the safeguarding of children,' 'handle conflicts,' 'monitor children's progress and conferring with parents,' and 'direct and supervise child carers' all require physical presence, real-time situational judgment, and trusted interpersonal relationships. Child protection and safeguarding carry legal accountability that regulators and families will not delegate to AI systems. Skills like 'relate empathetically,' 'build helping relationship with social service users,' 'deliver social services in diverse cultural communities,' and 'manage ethical issues within social services' are deeply resistant to substitution. The regulatory environment in Switzerland (and broadly in OECD countries) for child care is highly compliance-driven and liability-sensitive, creating strong institutional barriers to AI substitution in decision-making roles.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the lower end of moderate exposure (score 4). AI will function as a productivity tool for the administrative layer—compliance monitoring, report drafting, budget tracking—but cannot substitute for the physical oversight, crisis management, staff leadership, and child-protection responsibilities that define the role. The primary work product is a safe, well-run care environment, not a digital artifact. Adoption barriers (regulatory accountability, parental trust, physical environment) are high. The score reflects genuine but bounded AI utility, consistent with other human-services management roles.

title: Führungskräfte in der Gesundheitsversorgung
rationale: AI can automate budget analysis, report preparation, and data monitoring in healthcare settings, but core leadership tasks like directing staff, negotiating with stakeholders, and making high-stakes organizational decisions require human judgment and accountability. analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A significant portion of this role involves information-processing tasks that 2026 AI handles well. Budget planning and expenditure control, report preparation, monitoring resource utilization (beds, staff, diagnostic services), analysing large-scale healthcare data, performing clinical coding procedures, reviewing patient medical data, and maintaining task records are all substantially automatable. The heavy optional skills list dominated by ETL and data integration tools (IBM InfoSphere, Oracle Data Integrator, SAP Data Services, SQL Server Integration Services) confirms that a meaningful slice of the work occurs in digital environments. **Limiting factors:** The core value of this occupation is organizational leadership and accountability in a highly regulated, high-stakes environment. The interpersonal demands — managing multi-professional teams, conflict management, hiring and training personnel — are deeply resistant to automation. **Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the boundary between moderate (4–5) and high (6–7) exposure. title: Führungskräfte in der Erbringung von Dienstleistungen für Menschen im Alter
rationale: AI can assist with budget planning, report preparation, policy monitoring, and staff scheduling, but the core work involves leading teams, managing crises, and building trust with vulnerable elderly residents and their families. title: Führungskräfte in der Erbringung von Dienstleistungen des Bildungswesens
rationale: AI can automate budget reporting, record-keeping, and drafting policy documents, but the core of this job is leading staff, evaluating teachers in classrooms, and representing the institution in the community. title: Führungskräfte in der Erbringung von Finanz- und Versicherungsdienstleistungen

title: Führungskräfte in der Erbringung von spezialisierten Dienstleistungen, anderweitig nicht genannt
rationale: AI can assist with budget analysis, report writing, and policy drafting, but the core work—directing staff, managing crises, and overseeing specialized operations like prisons or fire services—requires human judgment and physical presence. analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** This ISCO group spans highly heterogeneous sub-occupations, but the shared managerial core involves tasks with meaningful AI exposure. Budget planning and expenditure monitoring, report preparation, policy development, performance evaluation, and strategic planning are all information-processing tasks where 2026 AI tools (document analysis, financial modeling, drafting assistants) can provide substantial productivity amplification. Skills like 'manage budgets,' 'develop business plans,' 'perform market research,' 'produce statistical financial records,' and 'write work-related reports' are well within current AI capability. For sub-occupations like publications coordinator, translation agency manager, or library manager, AI exposure is considerably higher—AI can automate translation review, catalogue management, digital content production, and scheduling. The extensive optional software and digital marketing skills signal that much administrative coordination already occurs in digital environments.

**Limiting factors:** The primary work product of most sub-occupations here is not a digital artifact but organizational outcomes achieved through people—supervising correctional staff, coordinating emergency response, managing museum collections physically, overseeing airside safety. Tasks like 'extinguish fires,' 'manage major incidents,' 'react to emergency situations,' 'manage social crisis,' 'ensure public safety and security,' and 'supervise artefact movement' are firmly physical or require real-time human judgment under uncertainty. Regulatory and accountability barriers are substantial: police superintendents, prison governors, and chief fire officers operate under legal frameworks requiring human accountability that AI cannot assume. The 'apply decision making within social work' and 'contribute to protecting individuals from harm' skills reflect high-stakes interpersonal judgment that AI cannot reliably substitute. Many roles also require domain-specific professional credentials and trust relationships with government, courts, or communities.

**Net assessment:** The extreme heterogeneity of this catch-all group (ISCO 1349) is the primary source of scoring uncertainty—a translation agency manager sits near 7-8 while a prison governor or chief fire officer sits near 3-4. Averaging across the 23 sub-occupations, the managerial coordination layer sees moderate AI amplification (drafting, analysis, scheduling) but the core value delivered—directing specialized professional services in high-stakes, regulated, often physical environments—remains human-dependent. A score of 5 reflects this middle ground, with confidence set low due to the occupational heterogeneity.

title: Führungskräfte in Hotels
rationale: AI can automate revenue forecasting, scheduling, financial reporting, and guest complaint triage, but hotel management is fundamentally about coordinating people and physical operations in real time. The irreducible need for on-site leadership, staff supervision, and guest relationship management keeps exposure moderate.
