analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** Several core tasks are meaningfully AI-assisted by 2026. Forecasting occupancy demand and managing hospitality revenue are data-driven tasks where AI yield-management tools (e.g., IDeaS, Duetto) already outperform manual analysis. Creating and monitoring financial accounts, managing budgets, and presenting reports are information-processing tasks well within current AI capability. Scheduling shifts can be largely automated by workforce-management software. Implementing marketing strategies and ensuring price competitiveness involve structured data analysis that AI handles well. Handling customer complaints via digital channels (email, review platforms) is increasingly AI-assisted. These tasks collectively represent perhaps 35–45% of the role's cognitive load.

**Limiting factors:** The primary work product of a hotel manager is not a document or dataset — it is a functioning hospitality operation delivered through coordinated human teams in a physical environment. Tasks like assessing cleanliness of areas, managing maintenance operations, supervising housekeeping, and ensuring health and safety standards require physical presence and real-time judgment. Managing staff — hiring, training, motivating, disciplining — involves interpersonal dynamics that AI cannot replicate. Compliance with liquor, gaming, and safety regulations requires contextual legal judgment and accountability that regulators assign to named human managers. Guest-facing relationship management, especially for high-value or distressed guests, depends on trust and empathy. These physical and interpersonal tasks constitute the majority of the role.

**Net assessment:** Hotel management sits at the moderate tier (score 5). AI tools meaningfully amplify productivity in the analytical and administrative subtasks — a single manager can oversee more properties or handle more data with AI support — but the core value delivered is operational coordination of physical spaces and human teams, which resists substitution. The role is unlikely to shrink dramatically due to AI, but individual managers will be expected to use AI tools for revenue management and reporting. Key uncertainty: smaller properties may see consolidation as AI reduces the administrative burden that previously justified dedicated management roles.

title: Führungskräfte in Restaurants
rationale: AI can help with scheduling, stock tracking, financial monitoring, and drafting menus, but the core job is managing people, handling guests, and maintaining service quality in a physical environment. The hands-on, interpersonal, and operational nature of restaurant management keeps AI exposure moderate.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** Several tasks in this role are meaningfully AI-assisted. Financial monitoring ('monitor financial accounts', 'control of expenses', 'manage stock rotation', 'order supplies') maps directly onto AI-powered POS and inventory management systems already widely deployed in hospitality. Menu planning and pricing ('plan menus', 'set prices of menu items', 'devise special promotions') can be supported by AI tools that analyze sales data, food cost trends, and competitor pricing. Scheduling and HR administration ('manage staff', 'supervise the work of staff on different shifts', 'recruit employees') are increasingly handled by AI-assisted workforce management platforms. Customer complaint triage and reservation handling are partially automatable via chatbots and AI-driven booking systems. These digital-adjacent tasks represent perhaps 30–40% of the role's cognitive load.

**Limiting factors:** The primary work product of a restaurant manager is not a document or data artifact — it is a functioning, compliant, hospitable dining operation delivered through real-time coordination of people and physical space. Tasks like 'manage the customer experience', 'confer with customers to assess satisfaction', 'handle customer complaints' in person, 'ensure kitchen and dining facilities comply with health regulations', and 'supervise food quality' require physical presence, sensory judgment, and interpersonal authority that 2026 AI cannot replicate. Staff supervision, conflict resolution, and on-the-floor service management are deeply embodied and social. Regulatory compliance (HACCP, occupational health and safety) requires contextual judgment in dynamic physical environments. Optional skills like 'create decorative food displays', 'mould chocolate', and 'think creatively about food and beverages' are craft and creative tasks with minimal AI substitution in practice.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the lower end of moderate exposure (score 4). AI tools are genuinely useful for the administrative and analytical layer of the job — inventory, financials, scheduling, promotions — but these are support functions, not the core value delivered. The occupation is fundamentally about managing people and physical operations in real time, which anchors it well below the high-exposure tier. Adoption of AI tools in Swiss hospitality SMEs is also slower than in large enterprises, adding a practical friction barrier. Uncertainty is low: the task mix is clear and the physical/interpersonal core is unambiguous.

title: Führungskräfte in Gross- und Einzelhandel
rationale: AI can automate sales forecasting, inventory tracking, budget analysis, and marketing campaign planning that form a large part of this role. However, managing staff, supplier negotiations, and in-store operational decisions keep human judgment central, placing this firmly in the high-exposure tier.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A substantial share of the 78 essential skills are information-processing tasks that 2026 AI handles well. Sales analysis, statistical forecasting, financial overviews, market research, data analysis, customer feedback measurement, and producing sales reports are all tasks where AI tools (integrated into ERP and CRM platforms) already automate or heavily assist. Pricing strategy optimization, promotional planning, and procurement analysis are increasingly handled by AI-driven retail management software (e.g., tools from SAP, Oracle Retail, or specialized platforms). Maintaining customer records and using CRM software are near-fully automatable. The 'use IT systems for commercial purposes' and 'have computer literacy' skills confirm a digitally mediated work environment, and the optional skills list (accounting techniques, consumer buying trends, strategic thinking) further confirms information-work dominance in the analytical dimension.

**Limiting factors:** The core managerial functions — managing staff, recruiting employees, training employees, enforcing company values, and supervising sales activities — require interpersonal judgment, trust, and physical presence that AI cannot replicate in 2026. Regulatory compliance tasks (alcohol/tobacco sales to minors, health and safety, labelling) require on-site accountability and legal liability that organizations will not delegate to AI. Supplier negotiations involve relationship capital and real-time interpersonal dynamics. Managing theft prevention and store image require physical presence and contextual judgment. The role also spans highly heterogeneous sub-occupations: a small shop manager's work is far more physical and interpersonal than a regional trade manager's, which is more analytical.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the boundary of moderate and high exposure (score 6). AI tools are already reshaping the analytical and administrative half of the role — forecasting, reporting, pricing, and CRM — but the people-management, compliance, and physical-environment dimensions remain robustly human. The net effect is likely productivity amplification (fewer managers needed per store, or managers handling more stores) rather than full substitution. Confidence is medium because the sub-occupations range from small shop managers (lower exposure) to regional trade managers (higher exposure), creating meaningful heterogeneity within the group.

title: Führungskräfte in der Erbringung von Dienstleistungen im Bereich Sport, Erholung und Kultur
rationale: AI can help with budgeting, scheduling, and promoting events, but running a sports centre or theatre requires constant on-site judgment, staff leadership, and community relationships. The mix of physical operations, people management, and local stakeholder work keeps AI in a supporting role.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** Several core tasks in this occupation are meaningfully AI-assisted. Budget management, financial analysis, and operational planning (manage budgets, manage operational budgets, analyse financial performance) are well within 2026 AI capability for drafting, monitoring, and flagging variances. Scheduling and logistics (schedule recreation facilities, manage logistics, establish daily priorities) can be substantially automated via agentic workflow tools. Promotion and outreach (promote cultural venue events, coordinate advertising campaigns, coordinate marketing plan actions) are areas where AI-generated content and campaign planning tools are already production-ready. Evaluating programmes and visitor needs (evaluate cultural venue programmes, evaluate cultural venue visitor needs) can be supported by AI-driven survey analysis and attendance data interpretation. These tasks collectively represent a meaningful share of the administrative and planning workload.

**Limiting factors:** The core value of this occupation is physical and interpersonal: ensuring facilities are safe and operational, leading and supervising staff on-site, handling incidents and customer complaints in real time, liaising with local authorities, and representing the organisation in community relationships. Skills like handle incidents, provide first aid, supervise maintenance of sport facilities, involve volunteers, and build community relations require physical presence, situational judgment, and trusted human relationships that AI cannot replicate in 2026. The occupation spans highly heterogeneous venues — from riding schools to casinos to theatres — each with distinct regulatory, safety, and community contexts that resist generic automation. Staff recruitment, performance management, and cultural programming decisions (assemble an artistic team, develop cultural activities, keep abreast of new trends) require contextual human judgment and local knowledge.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the moderate tier (score 5). AI meaningfully assists the administrative, financial, and promotional dimensions — perhaps 30–40% of task volume — but the primary work product is the safe, well-run, community-embedded facility, which requires continuous physical presence and interpersonal leadership. AI is a productivity amplifier here, not a substitute: a manager with AI tools may handle more venues or reduce administrative overhead, but the role itself is not at risk of displacement. Confidence is medium because the sub-occupations (sport facility vs. cultural vs. recreational) vary in their digital-work share, and adoption of AI planning tools in Swiss cultural/sports institutions is still uneven.

title: Führungskräfte in der Erbringung von Dienstleistungen, anderweitig nicht genannt
rationale: AI can automate scheduling, customer feedback analysis, marketing campaigns, and financial reporting, but managing staff, handling complaints in person, and overseeing physical facilities still require human presence. The mix of operational, interpersonal, and physical responsibilities keeps this in the moderate tier.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A meaningful portion of this role's tasks are information-processing work that 2026 AI handles well. Skills like 'develop financial statistics reports,' 'perform market research,' 'conduct search engine optimisation,' 'plan digital marketing,' 'design materials for multimedia campaigns,' 'measure customer feedback,' 'analyse data about clients,' and 'handle touristic quantitative data' are substantially automatable with current AI tools. Scheduling (schedule shifts, fix meetings, coordinate operational activities) is increasingly handled by AI-assisted workforce management platforms. Customer inquiry handling — central to contact centre manager and travel agency manager sub-roles — is already being automated via AI chatbots and voice agents, with contact centres among the most AI-disrupted service environments. CRM tasks, supplier negotiation preparation, and pricing strategy analysis also benefit heavily from AI augmentation.

**Limiting factors:** The core managerial function — motivating employees, evaluating staff, recruiting, handling disciplinary matters, and building supplier relationships — remains interpersonally grounded and resistant to substitution. Physical oversight tasks such as 'maintain camping facilities,' 'manage inspections of equipment,' 'supervise camp operations,' and 'ensure equipment maintenance' require on-site human presence. Sub-occupations like camping ground manager, laundry and dry cleaning manager, and garage manager involve direct supervision of physical operations and hands-on problem-solving (e.g., 'diagnose problems with vehicles') that robotics cannot yet handle in unstructured service environments. Regulatory compliance (health and safety, food safety, GDPR-adjacent 'handle personal identifiable information') also requires accountable human judgment. The breadth of sub-occupations — from contact centres to campsites — means physical and interpersonal demands vary considerably.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the boundary between moderate (4–5) and high (6–7) exposure. The information-processing and digital marketing tasks push toward 6, but the dominant managerial function — coordinating people and physical operations across heterogeneous service environments — anchors it at 5. AI will amplify productivity (fewer staff needed to handle analytics, scheduling, and customer communications) but cannot substitute for the on-site, interpersonal, and accountability-bearing core of service management. Confidence is medium because the sub-occupations are heterogeneous: contact centre managers face much higher AI exposure than campsite or garage managers.

title: Intellektuelle und wissenschaftliche Berufe, onA
rationale: AI can handle research, writing, and data analysis tasks that make up much of intellectual and scientific work. However, this catch-all category spans roles from field researchers to policy advisors, making a single score unreliable.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** ISCO 2000 ('Intellektuelle und wissenschaftliche Berufe, onA') is a residual catch-all for intellectual and scientific professionals not elsewhere classified. By definition, the core work product is cognitive and information-based — research synthesis, report writing, analysis, and expert judgment. These map closely onto 2026 AI strengths: large-context document analysis, professional-quality writing and summarization, literature review automation, and structured data interpretation. Tasks like drafting reports, reviewing literature, preparing presentations, and synthesizing findings across sources are substantially AI-performable today. If the role involves producing written deliverables or structured analyses, AI tools can handle a large share of the routine workload.

**Limiting factors:** The 'onA' (nicht anderswo aufgeführt / not elsewhere classified) designation means this category aggregates highly heterogeneous workers — field scientists, policy researchers, applied analysts, and domain specialists whose work may involve physical data collection, stakeholder consultation, regulatory compliance, or novel cross-domain synthesis under genuine uncertainty. Many such roles require professional accountability, institutional trust, and judgment in ambiguous situations that AI cannot yet reliably replicate. Physical fieldwork, laboratory work, or direct advisory relationships with clients or governments would substantially reduce automation potential for subsets of this group.

**Net assessment:** The information-work nature of the category pushes exposure toward the moderate-to-high range, but the extreme heterogeneity of the residual ISCO bucket prevents confident placement. A pure research-writing role might score 7–8; a field-based scientific specialist might score 3–4. The midpoint of 5 reflects this uncertainty. Confidence is low precisely because the category is definitionally a residual — it tells us little about what these workers actually do day-to-day.

title: Naturwissenschaftler, Mathematiker und Ingenieure, onA
rationale: AI can automate data analysis, literature review, and routine calculations, which are common across science and engineering roles. However, this catch-all category spans wildly different work, making a single score unreliable.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** ISCO 2100 is a residual 'not elsewhere classified' grouping for scientists, mathematicians, and engineers, meaning it captures workers whose specific roles don't fit standard subcategories. Across these fields, AI tools are demonstrably capable of handling a significant share of typical tasks: literature synthesis and summarization (1M+ token context windows), routine mathematical computation and symbolic manipulation, data pipeline construction, report drafting, and simulation setup. For mathematicians and data-adjacent scientists, AI coding assistants can automate substantial portions of exploratory analysis and model-building. For engineers in well-defined domains, AI can accelerate design iteration, documentation, and compliance checking.

**Limiting factors:** The 'onA' (not elsewhere classified) designation implies heterogeneity — this group likely includes field scientists doing physical sampling, metrologists calibrating instruments, applied engineers supervising construction or lab processes, and niche specialists whose work involves novel cross-domain synthesis under genuine uncertainty. Physical fieldwork, laboratory manipulation, safety-critical sign-off, and regulatory accountability (common in Swiss engineering and scientific contexts) all resist AI substitution. The primary human value in most of these roles is judgment in ambiguous, novel, or high-stakes situations — exactly where 2026 AI remains weakest.

**Net assessment:** The occupation sits at moderate exposure (5). The information-processing and analytical subtasks are meaningfully AI-augmentable, pushing above the low tier, but the physical, regulatory, and judgment-intensive dimensions — plus the genuine heterogeneity of who falls into this residual category — prevent a high-tier placement. Confidence is low because the catch-all nature of ISCO 2100 means actual exposure could range from 3 (field scientist) to 8 (computational mathematician) depending on the specific worker.

title: Physiker und Astronomen
rationale: AI can accelerate data analysis, literature review, and simulation work that physicists do daily. However, designing experiments, interpreting novel results, and operating physical instruments still require human expertise, keeping this occupation at moderate exposure.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** Several core tasks in this occupation are meaningfully AI-assisted in 2026. 'Analyse scientific data,' 'analyse experimental laboratory data,' 'execute analytical mathematical calculations,' and 'apply statistical analysis techniques' are all information-processing tasks where AI tools (e.g., large-context LLMs, automated ML pipelines, symbolic math engines like Wolfram) already provide substantial productivity gains. 'Write work-related reports' and 'prepare scientific papers' are increasingly AI-assisted, with tools like Semantic Scholar, Elicit, and LLM-based drafting reducing time-to-draft significantly. 'Analyse telescope images' is a domain where computer vision and deep learning have been transformative — automated pipelines already classify galaxies, detect transients, and identify exoplanet candidates at scale. Literature synthesis and hypothesis generation are also areas where AI provides genuine leverage.

**Limiting factors:** The core identity of this occupation is generating new physical knowledge through experimental design and novel theoretical synthesis — tasks that remain firmly human-advantaged. 'Design scientific equipment,' 'perform laboratory tests,' 'operate scientific measuring equipment,' 'operate telescopes,' and 'carry out scientific research in observatory' all involve physical manipulation, calibration, and real-world judgment in unstructured or safety-critical environments. Medical physics tasks (radiation delivery, dosimetry, advising clinicians) carry direct patient safety implications and are subject to strict regulatory oversight, creating strong adoption barriers. 'Develop scientific theories' and interpreting genuinely novel experimental results require cross-domain synthesis under deep uncertainty — precisely where 2026 AI systems remain unreliable. The occupation also requires accountability and peer-reviewed validation that resists full automation.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the moderate tier (score 5). AI is a powerful productivity amplifier for the data-heavy and writing-heavy subtasks, potentially allowing individual physicists to process more data and publish faster. However, the primary value delivered — experimental design, physical instrumentation, novel theoretical insight, and safety-critical medical applications — is substantially AI-resistant. The heterogeneity of sub-occupations (medical physicist vs. cosmologist vs. nuclear physicist) introduces some uncertainty; medical physicists face stronger regulatory barriers while computational astrophysicists face higher exposure. A score of 5 reflects genuine amplification without substitution of the core scientific judgment function.

title: Meteorologen
rationale: AI can automate much of the data processing, pattern recognition, and routine forecast generation that meteorologists do daily. However, the job also requires physical instrument calibration, novel research, and expert judgment in high-stakes warnings, keeping it at moderate exposure.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A significant share of meteorologist tasks involve processing structured digital data — studying radar/satellite imagery, running numerical weather prediction models, and generating forecast maps. These are precisely the tasks where 2026 AI excels: deep learning models (e.g., Google DeepMind's GraphCast, Huawei Pangu-Weather) already match or outperform traditional NWP models for medium-range forecasts. Skills like 'review meteorological forecast data,' 'use specialised computer models for weather forecasting,' 'execute analytical mathematical calculations,' and 'manage meteorological database' are substantially AI-performable. Routine forecast preparation and dissemination — writing weather reports, generating maps — can be largely automated. Optional skills like 'create weather maps' and 'develop models for weather forecast' are increasingly AI-assisted.

**Limiting factors:** A substantial portion of the role resists AI substitution. Physical tasks — 'assemble measuring equipment,' 'calibrate precision instrument,' 'maintain technical equipment,' 'operate meteorological instruments,' and 'develop measuring equipment' — require hands-on work in field and lab environments that 2026 robotics cannot reliably replicate. Research tasks ('conducting experiments in fog dispersal, cloud seeding,' 'developing new equipment and procedures,' 'improving concepts and theories') demand genuine scientific creativity and cross-domain synthesis. High-stakes warning dissemination (cyclones, aviation hazards) carries regulatory and liability dimensions that require accountable human experts. The metrologist sub-occupation is heavily instrument-focused and even less AI-exposed than the forecasting side.

**Net assessment:** Meteorology sits at the boundary between moderate and high exposure. Routine forecasting and data analysis are being rapidly transformed by AI, but the occupation's physical instrumentation work, original research mandate, and safety-critical judgment anchor it at moderate (5). The heterogeneity between sub-occupations (weather forecaster vs. research climatologist vs. metrologist) introduces uncertainty — a pure weather forecaster might score 6-7, while a metrologist or instrument engineer might score 3-4. The blended score of 5 reflects this spread.

title: Chemiker
rationale: AI can accelerate literature review, data analysis, and report writing, but the core work—designing experiments, synthesizing compounds, and operating lab equipment—requires physical presence and expert judgment. The irreducibly hands-on nature of laboratory chemistry keeps exposure moderate.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A meaningful portion of chemist tasks involve information processing that 2026 AI handles well. Writing technical reports and scientific papers, statistical analysis (including statistical analysis system software), literature synthesis, and interpreting chromatography or analytical data are all substantially AI-augmentable. AI tools like large language models combined with scientific databases can accelerate hypothesis generation, protocol drafting (develop scientific research protocols), and documentation (archive scientific documentation, document analysis results). Cheminformatics AI can predict molecular properties, suggest synthesis routes, and flag toxicological risks—directly relevant to tasks like 'develop chemical products' and 'translate formulae into processes.' For sensory scientists specifically, AI can assist with analysing consumer buying trends and market research. These information-layer tasks likely represent 30–40% of working time.

**Limiting factors:** The core physical work of chemistry is strongly AI-resistant at the 2026 frontier. Preparing chemical samples, calibrating laboratory equipment, running chromatography, performing sensory evaluation (olfaction is inherently biological), and synthesizing compounds all require physical manipulation in unstructured lab environments where robotics remain limited. Regulatory and safety requirements (apply safety procedures in laboratory, use personal protection equipment, dispose of hazardous waste) demand human accountability and on-site presence. Interdisciplinary R&D collaboration, client-facing advisory roles (advise food industry, advise on fragrances), and novel experimental design under genuine scientific uncertainty resist automation. The sensory scientist sub-occupation is particularly resistant: olfaction and quantitative sensory testing depend on human perceptual apparatus that cannot be replicated by AI.

**Net assessment:** Chemists sit at the moderate tier (score 5). AI meaningfully accelerates the analytical, documentation, and data-interpretation layers of the job, but the primary work product—new chemical knowledge, synthesized compounds, validated experimental results—requires physical laboratory work and expert scientific judgment that 2026 AI cannot independently deliver. Productivity amplification is real (fewer hours on reporting, faster literature review), but full substitution is blocked by the physical and regulatory nature of the core tasks. Uncertainty arises from the heterogeneity of sub-roles: an industrial QC chemist has more routine, automatable tasks than a research chemist designing novel synthesis pathways.

title: Geologen und Geophysiker
rationale: AI can automate data processing, map generation, and report drafting, but core geological work requires field data collection, physical sample analysis, and expert interpretation of ambiguous subsurface conditions. The heavy fieldwork and physical measurement components keep exposure moderate despite significant digital analysis tasks.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A substantial portion of geologist/geophysicist work involves information processing that 2026 AI handles well. Tasks like 'interpret research data and prepare geological reports, maps, charts and diagrams' and 'write scientific papers' are directly addressable by large language models with domain fine-tuning. 'Process data,' 'execute analytical mathematical calculations,' 'apply statistical analysis techniques,' and 'scientific modelling' are highly automatable — AI tools already assist with seismic data interpretation, subsurface modelling, and geospatial analysis. Optional skills like 'apply digital mapping,' 'develop geological databases,' and 'create thematic maps' are increasingly handled by AI-assisted GIS platforms. Remote sensing data analysis (gravitational, magnetic, seismic signals) is a domain where machine learning has demonstrated strong performance, particularly in oil and gas exploration workflows.

**Limiting factors:** The occupation is anchored by irreducibly physical tasks: 'conduct field work,' 'collect geological data,' 'carry out geological explorations,' 'conduct soil sample tests,' 'examine geochemical samples,' 'test raw minerals,' and 'operate seismic equipment.' These require physical presence in unstructured outdoor environments — exactly where robotics and AI remain weakest in 2026. 'Calibrate laboratory equipment,' 'operate microscope,' and 'perform laboratory tests' require hands-on manipulation. Beyond physical tasks, expert geological judgment in novel subsurface conditions — advising on dam or tunnel construction sites, assessing contaminated site restoration, or interpreting ambiguous seismic anomalies — involves cross-domain synthesis under uncertainty that current AI cannot reliably replicate. Regulatory accountability in civil engineering and environmental contexts also demands human sign-off.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the moderate tier (score 5). Roughly 40-50% of tasks — data processing, report writing, mapping, statistical modelling — are meaningfully AI-assisted or partially automatable. However, the core work product is not a digital artifact but a physical-world assessment grounded in field observation and sample analysis. AI functions primarily as a productivity amplifier (faster data processing, better pattern recognition in seismic data) rather than a substitute for the geologist's physical presence and expert judgment. The score could rise toward 6 as AI-assisted remote sensing and automated subsurface modelling mature, but fieldwork requirements and professional accountability create durable barriers.

title: Mathematiker, Aktuare und Statistiker
rationale: AI can now automate statistical analysis, build predictive models, process large datasets, and generate actuarial projections with high accuracy. However, novel mathematical research, regulatory sign-off on risk models, and client-facing advisory judgment keep humans central.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The majority of routine tasks in this ISCO group are information-processing operations performed on digital data — precisely where 2026 AI excels. 'Perform data analysis,' 'apply statistical analysis techniques,' 'process data,' 'identify statistical patterns,' 'execute analytical mathematical calculations,' and 'produce statistical financial records' are all tasks where tools like Python/R with LLM-assisted code generation, AutoML platforms, and statistical AI agents already perform at or near professional level. 'Build predictive models' (optional) and 'carry out statistical forecasts' are increasingly automated via platforms like DataRobot or custom LLM-driven pipelines. 'Synthesise financial information,' 'analyse financial risk,' and 'analyse insurance risk' map directly onto tasks where GPT-4-class models with long-context document analysis already assist or replace junior-level work. The gambling/lottery sub-occupations (game developer, QA engineer) have additional exposure through AI-assisted game logic generation and automated testing pipelines. Roughly 60–70% of the listed essential skills describe cognitive operations on data or documents with no physical component.

**Limiting factors:** Several core tasks resist near-term AI substitution. 'Study, improve and develop mathematical theories' — the research frontier of mathematics — remains firmly human-advantaged; AI assists but does not independently generate validated novel proofs or paradigms at the level required for publication. 'Perform scientific research' and 'apply scientific methods' in genuinely novel domains require cross-domain synthesis under ambiguity that current AI handles poorly. Actuarial work carries significant regulatory accountability: actuaries must sign off on reserve calculations and pension scheme designs under legal frameworks (e.g., Swiss supervisory law), creating a trust and liability barrier. 'Advise on financial matters,' 'advise on risk management,' and 'advise legislators' involve stakeholder relationships and institutional credibility that clients and regulators are not yet willing to delegate to AI. The demographer sub-occupation involves survey design, ethical oversight of data collection, and policy interpretation that require human judgment. Physical and regulatory barriers are moderate but real.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits solidly in the High tier (6–7). The bulk of routine statistical and actuarial computation is already substantially AI-assisted, and productivity amplification is compressing the number of junior analysts needed per senior professional. However, the occupation's core value proposition — novel mathematical research, regulatory accountability, and expert advisory judgment — retains meaningful human advantage. The heterogeneity of sub-occupations (pure mathematician vs. gambling QA engineer vs. actuary) introduces uncertainty; a pure mathematician scores closer to 5–6 while a statistical analyst or actuarial assistant-level role scores closer to 8. The aggregate score of 7 reflects this spread, with confidence set to medium accordingly.

title: Biologen, Botaniker, Zoologen und verwandte Berufe
rationale: AI can accelerate literature review, data analysis, and bioinformatics tasks, but core work—designing experiments, collecting field specimens, and conducting lab tests—requires physical presence and expert judgment. The mix of hands-on research and digital analysis places this occupation squarely in the moderate tier.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A meaningful portion of tasks in this occupation are AI-performable at 2026 capability levels. Writing scientific papers and reports, analysing scientific data, performing statistical analysis, maintaining and querying databases, and preparing visual data are all information-processing tasks where large language models and AI-assisted tools (e.g., Copilot for research, automated bioinformatics pipelines) already provide substantial productivity gains. Bioinformatics scientists in particular—one of the seven ESCO sub-occupations—work almost entirely in digital environments: constructing biological databases, running sequence analyses, and interpreting computational outputs. AI tools like AlphaFold for protein structure prediction, automated literature synthesis, and ML-driven genomic analysis are already reshaping this sub-field. For pharmacologists, AI-assisted drug-target interaction modelling and toxicity prediction are commercially deployed. Tasks like 'interpret current data,' 'perform data analysis,' 'run laboratory simulations,' and 'maintain database' are substantially AI-augmentable today.

**Limiting factors:** The majority of core tasks in this ISCO group require physical presence, manual dexterity, or irreplaceable expert judgment. Gathering biological specimens in the field, conducting laboratory tests (calibrating equipment, operating microscopes, performing microbiological analysis), handling veterinary emergencies, safely interacting with animals, and designing and executing novel experiments cannot be delegated to AI in 2026. Regulatory and safety frameworks—pharmaceutical legislation, GMP, HACCP, biosecurity protocols—require human accountability and on-site verification. Animal behaviourists and zoologists depend on direct observation and physical interaction with animals that robotics cannot yet replicate in unstructured environments. The scientific method itself—formulating novel hypotheses, designing experiments to test them under real-world conditions, and interpreting ambiguous results—remains a domain where human expertise is not substitutable. Adoption barriers in laboratory and field settings (safety protocols, instrument certification, ethical oversight of research) further slow AI penetration.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the moderate tier (score 5). The digital sub-occupations (bioinformatics scientist, food biotechnologist with heavy data work) pull exposure upward, while the field- and lab-intensive majority (zoologist, marine biologist, animal behaviourist, immunologist doing bench work) anchor it in the moderate range. AI functions primarily as a productivity amplifier—accelerating literature review, data analysis, and simulation—rather than as a substitute for the physical and experimental core of the work. The heterogeneity of sub-occupations introduces some uncertainty; a pure bioinformatician would score 7-8, while a field zoologist might score 3-4.

title: Agrar-, Forst- und Fischereiwissenschaftler und -berater
rationale: AI can automate data collection, soil and crop analysis, report writing, and market research that form a significant part of this work. However, on-site fieldwork, hands-on advisory relationships with farmers, and real-world environmental judgment keep human experts central.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A substantial share of tasks in this occupation involve information processing that 2026 AI handles well. Preparing scientific reports, processing survey data, executing analytical mathematical calculations, performing market research, and interpreting scientific data to assess water quality are all tasks where large-language and analytical AI models perform at professional quality. Precision farming platforms already integrate AI-driven soil analysis, crop yield modelling, and pest/disease identification from satellite and sensor data — directly overlapping with 'create soil and plant improvement programmes,' 'research improvement of crop yields,' and 'monitor forest health.' Advisory content generation (advise on fertiliser and herbicide, advise on livestock productivity, advise on efficiency improvements) can increasingly be scaffolded by AI decision-support tools, reducing the time a human expert needs per client. Agentic workflows can automate data pipelines from IoT sensors in recirculation systems or hatcheries, partially automating 'monitor water quality' and 'monitor growth rates of cultivated fish species.'

**Limiting factors:** The core value proposition of this occupation is on-site, relationship-based advisory work with farmers, foresters, and fishing communities — tasks like 'consult with business clients,' 'provide on-site training in aquaculture facilities,' 'inspect fish stock,' 'inspect trees,' and 'investigate and implement management procedures for fires, floods, droughts.' These require physical presence, sensory judgment in unstructured natural environments, and trusted interpersonal relationships that AI cannot replicate in 2026. Regulatory and biosecurity responsibilities (apply forest legislation, comply with agricultural code of practice, fish welfare regulations, environmental legislation) require accountable human professionals. Emergency and time-critical decisions in field conditions — pest outbreaks, disease events, flood response — depend on contextual, embodied expertise. The breadth of sub-occupations (aquaculture manager to forestry scientist) also means some roles are more field-intensive and less automatable than others.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the moderate tier (score 5). AI tools meaningfully accelerate the analytical, reporting, and data-monitoring components — perhaps 35–45% of task time — but the dominant value delivered is expert advisory judgment exercised in physical environments with real stakeholders. Productivity amplification is the primary AI effect: fewer advisers may be needed per farm or forest district as AI handles routine analysis, but the human adviser role is not substituted. Uncertainty is moderate because sub-occupations vary considerably in their digital vs. field work balance.

title: Umweltwissenschaftler
rationale: AI can accelerate data analysis, report writing, environmental impact assessments, and literature synthesis, which are significant parts of this job. However, field sampling, ecological surveys, site inspections, and regulatory advisory roles require physical presence and expert judgment that AI cannot replace.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A substantial portion of environmental scientists' work involves information processing that 2026 AI handles well. Tasks like 'analyse environmental data,' 'analyse ecological data,' 'interpret scientific data to assess water quality,' 'prepare visual data,' 'write work-related reports,' and 'perform risk analysis' are all cognitive operations on structured data — well within current AI capability. Environmental impact assessments (EIA) follow structured templates and regulatory frameworks; AI can draft large portions of these documents given input data. Literature synthesis for 'conduct research before survey' and 'monitor legislation developments' is highly automatable. GIS-based spatial analysis tools are increasingly AI-augmented. Report generation, compliance checking against known regulations, and audit documentation (tasks e and d in the ISCO description) are partially automatable today, with AI tools already used in environmental consulting firms for these purposes.

**Limiting factors:** The occupation is anchored by substantial physical and field-based work that resists AI substitution. 'Collect samples for analysis,' 'conduct ecological surveys,' 'employ habitat survey techniques,' 'conduct environmental site assessments,' 'measure trees,' 'measure water quality parameters,' and 'build fences' all require physical presence in unstructured outdoor environments — exactly where robotics and AI remain weakest in 2026. The advisory and stakeholder-facing dimensions — 'advise on environmental risk management systems,' 'respond to enquiries,' 'conduct educational activities,' 'promote environmental awareness' — involve trust-based human relationships and contextual judgment that AI augments but does not replace. Regulatory accountability (signing off on compliance assessments, appearing before authorities) requires a named human professional. The breadth of sub-occupations (park ranger, soil scientist, airport environment officer, ecologist) means many roles are predominantly field-based. Additionally, Swiss environmental regulation and site-specific conditions require local expert judgment that generic AI models cannot reliably supply.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the moderate tier (score 5). AI meaningfully accelerates the analytical, documentation, and research-synthesis components — perhaps 35–45% of total task time — but the field sampling, ecological survey, physical site assessment, and stakeholder advisory work that defines the profession's core value proposition remains human-dependent. The occupation is more exposed than a nurse or police officer (where physical presence and real-time human judgment dominate throughout) but less exposed than an accountant or financial analyst (where the primary work product is entirely digital). Uncertainty is moderate because the sub-occupation range is wide: a desk-based environmental programme coordinator faces higher exposure (~6–7) while a countryside officer or park ranger faces lower exposure (~3–4).

title: Ingenieurwissenschaftler (ohne Elektrotechnik, Elektronik und Telekommunikation), onA
rationale: AI can draft technical reports, run simulations, and assist with design calculations, but core engineering work involves physical systems, site judgment, and safety accountability that AI cannot yet replace. The broad catch-all nature of this category keeps the score moderate.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** ISCO 2140 is a residual category covering mechanical, civil, chemical, industrial, and other engineers excluding electrical/electronics. Across these sub-fields, a substantial share of tasks involve information-processing work that 2026 AI handles well: drafting technical documentation, performing standard calculations and simulations (FEA, CFD, process modeling), reviewing compliance against codes and standards, and generating design variants. AI-assisted CAD and simulation tools (e.g., Autodesk AI features, ANSYS AI-driven meshing, generative design) are in active production use. Literature review, report writing, and project documentation — common across all engineering disciplines — are highly AI-augmentable. For process and industrial engineers, workflow optimization and data analysis tasks are increasingly handled by AI-driven analytics platforms.

**Limiting factors:** The core value of most engineers in this category is judgment about physical systems in the real world: approving designs that will be built, signing off on safety-critical calculations, conducting site inspections, managing contractors, and taking professional liability. Regulatory frameworks in Switzerland (and globally) require licensed engineers to personally certify designs — AI cannot hold professional responsibility. Physical site work, client negotiation, interdisciplinary coordination under ambiguity, and novel problem-solving (e.g., diagnosing an unexpected failure mode in a running plant) remain firmly human-advantaged. The heterogeneity of this ISCO group is extreme: a chemical process engineer and a mechanical product designer have very different AI exposure profiles.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the high end of the moderate-to-high boundary (score 6). AI meaningfully accelerates routine design, documentation, and analysis tasks — likely reducing the junior-engineer headcount needed per project — but the professional accountability structure, physical-world judgment, and regulatory certification requirements prevent deeper substitution. Confidence is low because the ISCO 2140 residual category aggregates engineers whose actual AI exposure ranges from ~4 (civil/structural site engineers) to ~7 (industrial process optimization engineers), making a single score inherently imprecise.

title: Prozess- und Produktionsingenieure
rationale: AI can automate data analysis, scheduling, and process optimization modelling, but core work requires physical plant inspections, hands-on troubleshooting, and coordinating people and machinery on the factory floor. The mix of digital analysis and irreplaceable physical presence keeps exposure moderate.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A significant share of tasks in this occupation involve information processing that 2026 AI handles well. Analysing production schedules and costs, detecting bottlenecks, performing supply chain analysis, maintaining logistics databases, scheduling production, and writing technical reports are all tasks where AI tools (LLMs, agentic data pipelines, simulation software) can already provide substantial assistance or near-full automation. Optional skills like CAD/CAE software, 3D modelling, and specific data analysis software indicate a digitally mediated work environment where AI copilots are increasingly embedded. Process optimization modelling — a core task — is an area where AI-driven simulation and digital twin technology is advancing rapidly, potentially reducing the analytical labour required per engineer significantly.

**Limiting factors:** The occupation's core deliverable is not a document or a digital artifact but a functioning physical production system. Tasks such as inspecting plant and machinery, resolving equipment malfunctions, conducting test runs, performing machine maintenance, and working safely with machines require physical presence in unstructured industrial environments where robotics and AI agents remain limited. Regulatory compliance (safety standards, GMP, HACCP), accountability for physical safety outcomes, and the need to coordinate and lead diverse teams of workers on the shop floor create strong adoption barriers. The breadth of sub-occupations — from leather goods engineers to food packaging technologists — means domain-specific tacit knowledge and hands-on judgment are central. Stakeholder negotiation, managing supplier relationships, and advising management on new methods also depend on interpersonal trust that AI cannot yet substitute.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the moderate tier (score 5). AI meaningfully accelerates the analytical and documentation-heavy subtasks — perhaps 35–45% of total task time — but the physical inspection, troubleshooting, safety oversight, and team coordination work that defines the role resists substitution. The net effect is productivity amplification rather than workforce substitution in the near term, with some reduction in junior analytical roles. Confidence is medium because the occupation aggregates heterogeneous sub-roles (process engineer vs. clothing technologist vs. logistics analyst) with varying AI exposure profiles.

title: Bauingenieure
rationale: AI can automate structural calculations, generate design variants, and draft technical reports, but civil engineers spend much of their time on site coordination, client negotiation, and solving unexpected physical problems. The mix of digital analysis and irreducible on-site judgment places this occupation in the high-exposure tier.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** Civil engineers (ISCO 2142) perform a substantial share of information-processing work: structural analysis, load calculations, cost estimation, specification writing, and compliance checking against building codes. These tasks map directly onto 2026 AI capabilities. Generative design tools (e.g., Autodesk Generative Design, AI-assisted FEM solvers) can already produce and evaluate structural variants faster than human engineers. BIM-integrated AI can flag code violations, generate quantity take-offs, and draft technical reports from model data. Scheduling and resource optimization — core project management tasks — are increasingly handled by AI-augmented tools like Primavera or Microsoft Project Copilot. The ESCO skill set includes 'interpret technical drawings,' 'perform structural calculations,' and 'prepare technical documentation,' all of which are substantially AI-performable at 2026 capability levels.

**Limiting factors:** A large share of civil engineering value is created outside the digital domain. Site supervision, geotechnical assessment under uncertainty, stakeholder negotiation, and regulatory approval processes require physical presence, professional accountability, and trust relationships that AI cannot substitute. Swiss construction law requires licensed engineers to sign off on structural designs, creating a hard regulatory floor for human involvement. Unexpected site conditions — soil anomalies, legacy infrastructure conflicts, weather-driven schedule changes — demand adaptive judgment that current AI agents handle poorly. The ESCO skill 'manage construction projects' and 'liaise with construction professionals' reflect coordination roles where human authority and accountability are legally and practically non-negotiable. Physical inspection and quality assurance on site remain firmly human-anchored.

**Net assessment:** Civil engineering sits at the boundary of the high-exposure tier (6–7). The core analytical and documentation work is increasingly AI-augmented, and productivity amplification is already measurable — fewer engineers are needed to produce the same volume of calculations and drawings. However, the profession is not approaching the 8–9 range because physical site work, legal liability, and complex stakeholder management constitute a large and resistant share of actual working hours. The main uncertainty is the pace of agentic AI adoption in Swiss engineering firms, which tend to be conservative; if BIM-AI integration accelerates, the score could drift toward 7 within a few years.

title: Umweltschutzingenieure
rationale: AI can accelerate environmental data analysis, report writing, and regulatory compliance checks, but core work requires physical site inspections, field sampling, and engineering judgment on novel contamination problems. The mix of digital analysis and irreplaceable fieldwork anchors this at moderate exposure.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A significant share of environmental engineering tasks involve information processing that 2026 AI handles well. Analysing environmental data, reporting on environmental issues, regulatory analysis (environmental legislation, pollution legislation, REACH compliance), and database development are largely digital tasks where AI tools can draft reports, flag compliance gaps, and synthesise monitoring data at speed. Environmental impact assessments — a core deliverable — involve structured document analysis and template-driven writing that AI can substantially accelerate. Technical drawing software use and standard operating procedure maintenance are also increasingly AI-assisted. These tasks collectively represent perhaps 35–45% of working hours.

**Limiting factors:** The occupation is anchored by tasks that resist AI substitution in 2026. Physical site inspections of industrial and municipal facilities, collecting samples for analysis, conducting environmental surveys, and performing environmental investigations require in-person presence in unstructured, often hazardous environments. Designing and overseeing remediation systems involves engineering judgment on site-specific, novel contamination scenarios where AI lacks reliable grounding. Collaboration with multidisciplinary teams (lawyers, planners, scientists, government agencies) and advising corporations on contaminated site clean-up involves stakeholder trust and accountability that clients and regulators still require from licensed human engineers. Regulatory sign-off and permit approval carry legal liability that further constrains AI substitution. Physical fieldwork and engineering accountability together represent the majority of core value delivered.

**Net assessment:** Environmental engineering sits at the moderate tier (score 5). AI meaningfully accelerates the analytical and documentation-heavy portions of the role — enough to reduce headcount at the margins or shift junior tasks — but the physical inspection, field sampling, site-specific design, and regulatory accountability functions are firmly human-dependent under 2026 capabilities. The occupation is heterogeneous: a desk-based environmental analyst role within this ISCO code would score higher (~6–7), while a field-focused remediation specialist would score lower (~3–4), which introduces some uncertainty and justifies medium confidence.

title: Maschinenbau- , Mechatronik- und Systemtechnikingenieure
rationale: AI can accelerate design iteration, simulate stress tests, and generate technical drawings, but core mechanical engineering work requires physical validation, safety certification, and novel problem-solving in complex systems. The job stays moderate because human judgment and regulatory accountability anchor the most critical outputs.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** Several essential and optional skills are meaningfully AI-assisted in 2026. CAD/CAE software and 3D modelling workflows are increasingly augmented by generative design tools (e.g., Autodesk Generative Design, Ansys AI-assisted simulation) that can autonomously propose geometry optimizations and run finite element analyses — tasks that map directly to 'adjust engineering designs,' 'analyse stress resistance of products,' and 'analyse test data.' Technical drawing generation from parametric models is largely automatable. 'Analyse production processes for improvement' and 'assess financial viability' involve structured data analysis where AI tools already perform at near-professional level. The breadth of 285 optional skills, many of which are software tools (CAD, CAE, ICT software specifications), confirms this is a heavily digitally-mediated profession.

**Limiting factors:** The primary work product is not a document or dataset — it is a certified physical system (engine, hull, aircraft component, industrial plant) that must perform reliably under real-world conditions. 'Perform scientific research,' 'approve engineering design,' and 'establish control standards' require accountability that regulatory frameworks (e.g., aviation certification under EASA, marine classification by DNV/Lloyd's) assign to licensed human engineers. Novel cross-domain synthesis — designing a propulsion system for a new vessel class, or diagnosing an unexpected failure mode — remains firmly human-advantaged. Physical validation, prototype testing, and on-site commissioning cannot be delegated to AI agents. The diversity of sub-domains (aeronautical, marine, mechatronics, HVAC) means no single AI tool covers the full scope.

**Net assessment:** Mechanical engineering sits at the moderate tier (score 5). AI substantially accelerates the design and simulation phases — perhaps compressing 30–50% of routine iteration work — but the occupation is anchored by physical validation, safety certification, and novel engineering judgment that AI cannot yet reliably substitute. Productivity amplification is real and will reduce headcount demand at the margins (especially for junior design and drafting roles), but the core licensed engineer role is not at high substitution risk by 2026. Confidence is medium because the occupation spans highly heterogeneous sub-specializations with very different AI exposure profiles.

title: Chemieingenieure
rationale: AI can accelerate literature review, process simulation, cost estimation, and safety documentation for chemical engineers. However, the core work—designing physical plant equipment, running pilot experiments, and overseeing live chemical processes—requires hands-on judgment that AI cannot yet replace.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A meaningful portion of chemical engineering work is information-intensive and AI-amenable. Tasks like 'prepare estimates of production costs,' 'forecast organisational risks,' 'analyse production processes for improvement,' and 'perform scientific research' (literature synthesis, data analysis) are well within 2026 AI capabilities. Process simulation software already integrates ML-driven optimization, and AI tools can draft safety procedures, quality standards documentation, and production progress reports. Skills such as 'analytical chemistry,' 'assess environmental impact,' and 'analyse test data' (optional) involve structured data interpretation that AI handles competently. Generative AI can also assist with 'design engineering components' at the conceptual level and accelerate regulatory documentation (e.g., 'contribute to registration of pharmaceutical products').

**Limiting factors:** The primary work product of a chemical engineer is not a document but a functioning physical process or plant. Tasks like 'performing tests throughout stages of production,' 'performing laboratory studies,' 'testing proposed processes in pilot plants,' 'handle chemicals,' and 'work safely with chemicals' require physical presence, tactile judgment, and real-time response to unexpected process behavior. Designing chemical plant equipment involves safety-critical decisions with regulatory accountability (process safety, ATEX, REACH compliance in the Swiss/EU context) where human sign-off is legally mandated. The 'approve engineering design' and 'establish control standards' tasks carry liability that regulators do not currently delegate to AI. Physical experimentation, equipment commissioning, and on-site troubleshooting remain firmly human-advantaged.

**Net assessment:** Chemical engineering sits at the moderate tier (score 5). AI meaningfully accelerates the information-processing and documentation half of the role—simulation, literature review, cost modeling, report writing—but the physical, experimental, and safety-critical core resists substitution. The occupation is more complemented than replaced by AI: productivity gains are real but do not dramatically reduce headcount because the bottleneck is physical process validation and regulatory accountability, not information throughput. Confidence is medium because the balance between lab/field work and desk work varies substantially across sub-roles (refinery process engineer vs. R&D synthetic materials engineer).

title: Bergbauingenieure, Metallurgen und verwandte Berufe
rationale: AI can assist with data analysis, report writing, and mine planning software, but core work involves physical site assessment, directing blasts, and managing underground operations that require on-site human judgment. The job's physical complexity and safety-critical decisions keep AI in a supporting role.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** Several tasks in this occupation are meaningfully AI-assisted. Report writing (prepare technical reports, write well data sheets, generate reconciliation reports) is well within 2026 AI capability. Data-intensive tasks like interpreting extraction data, compiling reservoir performance plans, and using mine planning software benefit from AI-augmented analysis. Analytical chemistry, statistical modelling, and geological data interpretation can be partially automated using AI tools integrated into existing engineering software. Scheduling mine production and monitoring well costs are also amenable to AI-driven optimization. These tasks collectively represent perhaps 25–35% of the work volume.

**Limiting factors:** The dominant share of this occupation's core work is physically situated and safety-critical. Tasks like examining prospective blast areas, supervising mine construction operations, deciding quantities of explosives, investigating mine accidents, and managing emergency procedures require on-site human presence, physical judgment, and legal accountability that AI cannot substitute in 2026. Regulatory frameworks around mine safety legislation and explosives management impose strict human-in-the-loop requirements. The heterogeneity of sub-occupations (from assayer to petroleum engineer to explosives engineer) means some roles are more data-intensive than others, but all share a physical operational core. Interpersonal demands — liaising with anti-mining lobbyists, managing drilling teams, training staff in mine safety — further resist automation.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the low-to-moderate boundary (score 4). AI tools are genuinely useful for the information-processing and reporting layer of the work, and adoption is plausible given the technical sophistication of the workforce. However, the primary work product is physical extraction and site management, not digital artifacts. The 'auxiliary tool rule' applies: if AI tools broke, nearly all of this job would still need to happen in person. Uncertainty is moderate because sub-occupations vary — a petroleum engineer doing reservoir modelling has higher exposure than an explosives engineer directing underground blasts.

title: Ingenieure, anderweitig nicht genannt
rationale: AI can accelerate design simulation, literature research, cost analysis, and report writing for many engineering tasks in this group. However, the core work spans highly physical, safety-critical, and novel domains—nuclear systems, optical prototyping, dismantling—where human judgment and on-site expertise remain essential.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** This catch-all group contains several sub-occupations with meaningful AI exposure. Patent engineers can leverage AI for prior-art searches, claim drafting, and IP landscape analysis—tasks where LLMs already perform at near-professional quality. Quantity surveyors use AI for cost estimation, blueprint parsing, and financial risk analysis, with tools like construction-specific AI platforms already automating 40–60% of takeoff work. Application engineers and test engineers benefit from AI-assisted software testing, requirements analysis, and automated test generation (referencing skills: 'execute software tests,' 'analyse software specifications,' 'levels of software testing'). Energy engineers can use AI for market analysis, feasibility studies, and energy system modeling. Literature research, report writing, and data analysis tasks ('conduct literature research,' 'analyse scientific data,' 'report analysis results,' 'write work-related reports') are broadly AI-performable at 2026 capability levels. The optional skills list—dominated by programming languages, CAD/CAE software, and IoT—confirms a heavily digital working environment for many sub-roles.

**Limiting factors:** The group's defining characteristic is its heterogeneity. Nuclear power generation engineers, explosive ordnance engineers, and dismantling engineers operate in safety-critical, heavily regulated physical environments where AI cannot substitute for on-site inspection, physical testing, or regulatory accountability. Skills like 'test performance of power plants,' 'disassemble machines,' 'perform chemical experiments,' 'test optical components,' and 'supervise maintenance activities in airports' require physical presence and hands-on manipulation that 2026 robotics cannot reliably replicate in unstructured environments. Biomedical and optical engineers designing physical prototypes ('design optical prototypes,' 'prepare production prototypes') require iterative physical validation. Regulatory barriers are substantial: nuclear, aviation, and explosives engineering involve certification regimes where human sign-off is legally mandated. The breadth of domains—from acoustics to textiles to marine salvage—means no single AI tool addresses the full scope.

**Net assessment:** The occupation sits at moderate exposure (score 5), pulled upward by genuinely automatable sub-roles (patent engineer, quantity surveyor, application engineer) and strong digital tool use, but anchored by the physical, safety-critical, and highly specialized nature of the majority of sub-occupations. Confidence is low because ISCO 2149 is explicitly a residual 'not elsewhere classified' group spanning radically different work content—patent engineering is arguably 7–8 exposure while nuclear plant engineering is closer to 3. The median score of 5 reflects this wide internal variance rather than a coherent occupational profile.

title: Ingenieure im Bereich Elektrotechnik
rationale: AI can automate design drafting, simulation, and documentation tasks that electrical engineers do daily. However, the core work—approving physical systems, supervising construction, and ensuring safety compliance—requires on-site judgment that AI cannot yet replace.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** Several core tasks are meaningfully AI-assisted in 2026. Design drawing and technical documentation are increasingly handled by AI-augmented CAD/CAE tools (listed as optional skills), with tools like Autodesk and Siemens NX integrating generative design. MATLAB-based simulation and analysis workflows are heavily automatable. The 'adjust engineering designs' and 'approve engineering design' tasks involve substantial document review and standards checking that LLMs with large context windows can assist with. The optional skill list is dominated by programming languages (C++, Java, Python-adjacent tools) and CAD/CAE/CAM software, confirming that a significant portion of the work is conducted in digital environments where AI productivity amplification is strong. Scientific research tasks such as literature synthesis and hypothesis generation are also well-served by 2026 AI tools.

**Limiting factors:** The primary work product is not a digital artifact but a physical electrical system—power stations, distribution networks, motors, industrial installations. The ISCO tasks of 'supervising, controlling and monitoring' physical generation and distribution systems, 'specifying electrical installation in buildings,' and 'determining manufacturing methods' all require physical presence, site-specific judgment, and accountability that AI cannot substitute. Regulatory compliance (abiding by regulations on banned materials, environmental legislation) in safety-critical infrastructure creates strong adoption barriers—licensed engineers bear legal liability for approvals. Novel cross-domain problem-solving in unstructured physical environments (fault diagnosis on aging infrastructure, adapting designs to unexpected site conditions) remains firmly human-advantaged. The 'perform scientific research' task, while partially AI-assisted, still requires domain expertise for experimental design and validation.

**Net assessment:** Electrical engineering sits at the moderate-to-high boundary (score 5). AI substantially accelerates the information-processing and design-iteration subtasks, but the occupation's core value—physical system design approval, safety oversight, and on-site engineering judgment—is anchored in the real world with strong regulatory and liability barriers. Productivity amplification is real and will likely reduce headcount at the junior level, but full substitution is far off. Uncertainty stems from the heterogeneity of sub-roles: a power grid design engineer working primarily in simulation software faces higher exposure than a field commissioning engineer.

title: Ingenieure im Bereich Elektronik
rationale: AI can accelerate circuit design, simulation, and technical report writing, but core electronics engineering requires physical prototyping, hardware validation, and safety-critical judgment that AI cannot yet reliably perform. The mix of hands-on lab work and complex system design keeps exposure moderate.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** Several core tasks are meaningfully AI-assisted in 2026. Technical report writing and documentation are well within AI capability. CAD and schematic design tools increasingly embed AI-assisted layout and routing (e.g., Cadence, Altium AI features). Feasibility studies and budget management involve structured data analysis where AI adds real productivity. Signal processing algorithm design benefits from AI-assisted code generation and optimization. Creating technical plans and design drawings using CAD software is partially automatable, and AI can generate initial circuit topologies or suggest component selections from datasheets. The optional skill 'create software design' and embedded firmware work overlap with the high-exposure software development domain. Regulatory compliance checking (REACh, environmental legislation) is increasingly handled by AI document-analysis tools.

**Limiting factors:** The primary work product of electronics engineers is not a digital document but a functioning physical system — a circuit board, an embedded controller, a radar subsystem. Tasks like 'develop apparatus and procedures to test electronic components,' 'direct production or installation work,' and 'organize maintenance and repair' require physical presence, hands-on debugging with oscilloscopes and spectrum analyzers, and judgment in unstructured hardware environments. Safety-critical design (aerospace guidance, propulsion control) carries regulatory and liability constraints that mandate human sign-off and cannot be delegated to AI. Novel cross-domain synthesis — e.g., designing a MEMS sensor for a new aerospace application — remains firmly human-advantaged. Customer needs identification and supplier risk assessment involve interpersonal and contextual judgment. Physical prototyping and test runs cannot be automated away.

**Net assessment:** Electronics engineering sits at the moderate tier (score 5). AI tools meaningfully accelerate the information-processing and design-iteration subtasks — perhaps 30–40% of daily work — but the occupation is anchored by physical hardware validation, safety-critical accountability, and novel system integration that resist substitution. The role is more complemented than replaced by AI: engineers become more productive per person, but the core judgment and physical work remain human. Uncertainty exists because the boundary between software-defined hardware (FPGAs, SoCs) and traditional electronics is blurring, which could raise exposure over time.

title: Ingenieure im Bereich Telekommunikationstechnik
rationale: AI can assist with network design, bandwidth analysis, and documentation, but core work involves specifying physical infrastructure, directing installation crews, and solving novel signal or hardware problems on-site. The mix of physical oversight and deep technical judgment keeps exposure moderate.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A meaningful portion of this role involves information-processing tasks where 2026 AI tools are genuinely capable. Analysing network bandwidth requirements, defining technical requirements, designing computer networks, and estimating costs of installing telecommunication devices are all tasks that AI-assisted tools (e.g., network simulation software with LLM integration, automated cost-estimation platforms) can now handle at draft or advisory quality. Signal processing algorithm design is increasingly supported by AI-assisted DSP toolchains and MATLAB-integrated ML workflows — the optional skill list's heavy emphasis on programming languages (C++, Java, MATLAB, Python-adjacent tools) confirms that a significant share of the work is conducted in digital environments where AI copilots are already productive. Requirements gathering and documentation (interact with users to gather requirements, define technical requirements, ICT system user requirements) are well within current LLM capability for structured elicitation and synthesis. Quality assurance methodologies and systems development life-cycle tasks are also partially automatable through AI-driven test generation and compliance checking.

**Limiting factors:** The core work product of this occupation is not a document or a piece of code — it is a functioning physical telecommunications infrastructure. Tasks (b), (c), and (e)–(g) in the ISCO description involve directing construction and installation work, organising maintenance and repair of physical systems, and making real-world deployment decisions about wired, fibre-optic, and wireless media. These require physical site assessment, coordination with installation crews, regulatory compliance in spectrum management, and accountability for safety standards — all domains where AI remains advisory at best. The essential skill 'use session border controller' and 'microwave principles' point to hands-on, hardware-specific expertise that cannot be delegated to a language model. Procurement of ICT network equipment involves vendor negotiation and supply-chain judgment. Regulatory and safety standards in Swiss telecommunications (BAKOM oversight, EMF limits, civil engineering coordination) create institutional adoption barriers. Client-facing training and user support also retain strong interpersonal components.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the boundary of Tier 4–5 (Moderate). AI tools meaningfully accelerate the design, analysis, and documentation subtasks — perhaps 35–45% of cognitive work — but the occupation's defining outputs require physical-world execution, hardware expertise, and regulatory accountability that AI cannot yet substitute. The role is more complemented than replaced by current AI: engineers using AI tools will be more productive, but the need for human engineers directing physical deployment and troubleshooting novel infrastructure problems remains robust. The score of 5 reflects genuine AI leverage on the information-work components while acknowledging that the primary deliverable is physical infrastructure, not a digital artifact. Uncertainty arises from the heterogeneity of sub-roles (broadcast engineer vs. network planner vs. signal processing specialist), which span different exposure levels.

title: Architekten, Innenarchitekten
rationale: AI can generate architectural sketches, draft blueprints, run feasibility analyses, and produce specification documents at near-professional quality. However, site inspections, client negotiation, regulatory compliance judgment, and construction oversight require physical presence and contextual expertise that AI cannot yet replace.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A significant share of architectural work is information-processing and design generation that 2026 AI handles well. CAD software use, blueprint drafting, and architectural sketch creation are increasingly augmented by generative AI tools (e.g., Midjourney, Stable Diffusion, Autodesk AI features) that can produce design variants rapidly. Writing architectural briefs, specifications, and contract documents — core ESCO tasks — are text-generation tasks where LLMs perform at professional quality. Feasibility studies and cost-benefit analysis reports involve structured data synthesis that AI agents handle competently. Interpreting technical requirements and integrating engineering principles into designs are partially automatable through parametric design tools already in commercial deployment. The 'identify customer needs' and 'analyse problems for opportunities' tasks are partially AI-assisted through structured intake workflows.

**Limiting factors:** Architecture is fundamentally a physical-world profession with strong regulatory, liability, and trust barriers. Conducting field work and performing field research require physical site presence — inspecting a construction site for compliance with specifications cannot be delegated to AI in 2026. Negotiating with stakeholders, liaising with local authorities, and communicating with construction crews involve interpersonal judgment and accountability that clients and regulators require from licensed humans. Meeting building codes and zoning compliance carries legal liability that jurisdictions assign to licensed architects, creating a hard regulatory barrier. Satisfying aesthetic requirements in context — integrating a building into a specific urban fabric, responding to a client's lived experience — involves tacit knowledge and iterative human judgment that AI tools assist but do not replace. Construction monitoring for quality standards requires on-site physical assessment.

**Net assessment:** Architecture sits at the high end of moderate-to-high exposure (score 6). AI substantially accelerates the design generation, documentation, and analysis subtasks — likely reducing the person-hours needed per project — but the licensed professional remains essential for site work, regulatory accountability, client relationship management, and construction oversight. The occupation is more complementarity than substitution in 2026: fewer junior drafters and specification writers are needed, but the senior architect role retains strong human-value anchors. Uncertainty stems from the heterogeneity of the role: a sole practitioner doing residential work faces different AI exposure than a large-firm architect focused on documentation and compliance.

title: Landschaftsarchitekten
