**Exposure drivers:** Several peripheral tasks are meaningfully AI-assisted. Preparing lesson content, providing written theory materials, teaching musical notation, and explaining musical genres and history are all information-delivery tasks where AI tools (e.g., LLMs, adaptive learning platforms) can generate high-quality content. AI can also analyze audio recordings to flag pitch errors or rhythmic inaccuracies, partially automating the 'observe student's progress' and 'give constructive feedback' tasks. Curriculum development and lesson planning — listed as both essential and optional skills — are well within 2026 AI capability for generating drafts and structured progressions. These tasks collectively represent perhaps 25–35% of the role's time investment.

**Limiting factors:** The core work product of a music teacher is not a document or a digital artifact — it is a physical, embodied skill transfer. Tasks like 'demonstrate a technical foundation in musical instruments,' 'play musical instruments,' 'instruct and demonstrate practical aspects of singing or playing,' and 'bring out performers' artistic potential' require physical co-presence, real-time tactile and auditory feedback, and nuanced interpersonal attunement that 2026 AI cannot replicate. Correcting a student's bow arm, adjusting breath support, or sensing when a student is frustrated versus focused demands embodied perception and relational trust. The 'manage student relationships,' 'encourage students to acknowledge their achievements,' and 'develop a coaching style' skills further anchor the role in human mentorship. Regulatory and institutional barriers are minimal, but physical-environment constraints are decisive: the lesson happens in a room with an instrument and a human body.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the low end of moderate exposure (score 4). AI will increasingly assist with the informational and administrative periphery — theory explanations, exercise generation, progress tracking, scheduling — and may reduce lesson preparation time. However, the irreducible core of instrument instruction is physical demonstration and real-time interpersonal coaching, which places a firm ceiling on substitution. The occupation is more likely to see productivity amplification (teachers handling slightly more students or spending less time on prep) than workforce reduction. Uncertainty is low; the physical and relational constraints are stable and well-understood.

title: Kunstlehrer
rationale: AI can help plan lesson content or give feedback on student artwork, but the core job is physically demonstrating dance moves, directing live performances, and coaching students in person. The hands-on, embodied, and relational nature of arts teaching keeps AI substitution low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A subset of administrative and preparatory tasks are AI-performable at 2026 capability levels. Preparing lesson content, selecting subject matter, conducting background research for plays, analysing scripts, and contextualising artistic work are largely information-processing tasks where AI tools (LLMs, image generators) can produce drafts or references quickly. AI can also assist with assessing student progress through video analysis tools, generating written feedback, and helping revise curricula or course materials. Optional skills like art history, copyright legislation, and creating digital images are areas where AI is already highly capable. These preparatory and administrative tasks may represent 20–30% of total working time.

**Limiting factors:** The dominant share of this occupation's core work is irreducibly physical, embodied, and interpersonal. Tasks such as demonstrating dance techniques, directing movement experiences, helping performers internalise choreographic material, instructing circus disciplines, organising and leading live rehearsals, and guaranteeing students' safety in physical environments cannot be delegated to AI in 2026. The pedagogical relationship — reading a student's body language, adjusting a posture, inspiring enthusiasm, managing group dynamics, and bringing out a performer's artistic potential — depends on physical co-presence and human judgment that AI cannot replicate. Regulatory and safety obligations (maintaining safe working conditions in performing arts, checking circus rigging) further anchor the role in physical reality. The 66 essential skills are overwhelmingly embodied: vocal techniques, movement techniques, express yourself physically, demonstrate technical expertise of your dance style — none of these are digital work products.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). AI provides genuine but peripheral assistance in lesson planning, research, and written feedback — enough to lift it above minimal exposure. However, the primary work product is live, embodied instruction and artistic coaching, which is deeply resistant to AI substitution in 2026. Adoption barriers are high: students pay for human mentorship, physical demonstration, and creative relationship. The score could rise modestly over time if AI-powered motion-capture coaching or personalised video feedback tools mature, but the interpersonal and physical core of arts teaching will remain human-dominant for the foreseeable future.

title: Ausbilder im Bereich Informationstechnologie
rationale: AI can generate training materials, build e-learning modules, and draft curricula faster than any human trainer. However, live instruction, adapting to learner confusion in real time, and building credibility with adult IT professionals still require a human presence.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A substantial portion of this role's core outputs are digital artifacts — training materials, SCORM packages, web-based courses, online tutorials, and curricula — all of which 2026 AI tools can produce at near-professional quality. Tools like GPT-4-class models can draft instructional content, generate quiz questions, and structure learning pathways from a subject brief in minutes. The essential skills 'create SCORM packages,' 'develop digital educational materials,' 'design web-based courses,' and 'create training materials' are all highly automatable content-production tasks. Optional skills referencing LMS platforms (Moodle, Litmos, Brightspace, etc.) confirm the work is conducted in a digital environment. AI can also assist with 'evaluate training' by analyzing completion rates, quiz scores, and learner feedback at scale — tasks currently done manually.

**Limiting factors:** The core human value in IT training lies in live delivery and adaptive instruction. The essential skill 'give live presentation' and optional skills like 'deliver online training' and 'online moderation techniques' require real-time responsiveness to learner confusion, group dynamics, and unexpected questions — areas where AI agents remain weak in 2026. 'Training subject expertise' and 'keep up to date with subject matter' require genuine mastery of fast-moving IT domains; while AI can summarize trends, adult learners in professional settings scrutinize trainer credibility closely. 'Coach clients' and 'develop a coaching style' involve interpersonal trust-building that AI cannot replicate in a live training context. Regulatory and procurement barriers in Swiss corporate training environments also slow AI adoption.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the boundary of the High tier (6–7). The content-creation and curriculum-design portions of the job are substantially AI-augmentable, meaning fewer trainers are needed to produce the same volume of materials — a productivity amplification effect that reduces headcount demand. However, live facilitation, subject credibility, and learner coaching anchor meaningful human involvement. The score of 6 reflects that AI reshapes the role significantly (especially the preparation and material-development phases) without yet displacing the live instructional core. Key uncertainty: if AI-driven interactive tutoring (e.g., personalized LLM-based learning assistants) matures further, the live delivery component could erode, pushing this score toward 7–8.

title: Lehrkräfte, anderweitig nicht genannt
rationale: AI can automate content creation, lesson planning, and basic student progress tracking, but tutoring, counselling, and mentoring depend on human relationship and adaptive judgment. The group's wide heterogeneity—from e-learning architects to prison instructors—pulls the score toward the middle of the high tier.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** This ISCO group is unusually heterogeneous, but several sub-occupations are heavily AI-exposed. E-learning developers, instructional designers, and e-learning architects produce digital artifacts (SCORM packages, web-based courses, multimedia content) that 2026 AI tools can substantially automate—content drafting, slide generation, assessment creation, and even basic authoring-tool workflows are well within current AI capability. Skills like 'compile content,' 'develop digital educational materials,' 'provide written content,' 'proofread text,' and 'structure information' are highly automatable. The optional skills list (JavaScript, CSS, PHP, Moodle, Brightspace, Adobe tools) confirms a large digital-production sub-population. For tutors and academic advisors, AI can already handle lesson planning, homework assistance, progress monitoring, and study-habit advice at acceptable quality—tools like Khan Academy's Khanmigo demonstrate this at scale.

**Limiting factors:** The counselling and mentoring sub-occupations (educational counsellor, learning mentor, school counsellor, prison instructor) are anchored by skills that resist AI substitution: 'client-centred counselling,' 'crisis intervention,' 'show empathy,' 'support children's wellbeing,' 'oversee rehabilitation process,' and 'consult student's support system.' These require sustained human relationship, legal accountability, and real-time emotional attunement that 2026 AI cannot reliably replicate in high-stakes settings. Prison instructors face additional regulatory and physical-environment barriers. Public speaking coaches and tutors working with children also depend on in-person demonstration and trust-building that limits substitution even where AI could handle content delivery.

**Net assessment:** The group straddles the boundary between moderate and high exposure. The digital-production sub-occupations (e-learning developer, instructional designer, e-learning architect) sit at 8–9; the counselling and mentoring roles sit at 4–5; tutors and academic advisors sit around 6. Averaging across the group's composition and weighting toward the more numerous tutoring/counselling roles, a score of 6 is appropriate—AI reshapes the work significantly through content automation and productivity amplification, but the human relationship core of most roles limits outright substitution. Confidence is low due to the extreme heterogeneity of sub-occupations within this catch-all ISCO unit.

title: Betriebswirte und vergleichbare Spezialisten, onA
rationale: AI can draft business reports, analyze financial data, and build forecasts that cover much of what business specialists do day-to-day. The score reflects high but not maximum exposure, because the catch-all 'not elsewhere classified' category spans roles from strategic advisors to operational analysts with very different AI vulnerability.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** ISCO 2400 ('Business and Administration Professionals, NEC') is a residual category capturing business generalists and specialists not fitting narrower codes. Core work products are typically digital: reports, analyses, plans, presentations, and recommendations derived from structured and unstructured data. 2026 AI tools handle these well — large-language models can synthesize market research, draft strategic memos, build financial models from templates, and generate variance analyses at near-professional quality. Tasks like interpreting KPIs, preparing management presentations, conducting competitor benchmarking, and summarizing regulatory changes are all substantially automatable or AI-amplifiable. Productivity amplification is strong: a single analyst with AI tools can produce output that previously required a small team, compressing headcount demand even where full automation is not achieved.

**Limiting factors:** The 'NEC' designation means this group likely includes roles with significant client-facing, stakeholder-management, or cross-functional coordination responsibilities — tasks where human judgment, organizational trust, and relationship capital remain decisive. Strategic recommendations in genuinely novel or politically sensitive business situations still require human accountability. Some sub-roles may involve regulatory compliance sign-off, where liability frameworks slow AI substitution. Physical site visits, negotiation, and change management facilitation are also resistant. Additionally, Swiss labor market norms and sector-specific compliance requirements (e.g., financial regulation, data privacy) create adoption friction that delays full deployment of AI agents in advisory roles.

**Net assessment:** The occupation sits solidly in the High tier (6–7). The primary work product is information-based and digital, AI tools are demonstrably capable across the core task set, and productivity amplification is already reducing headcount demand in comparable roles globally. The score does not reach 8–9 because the NEC classification introduces genuine heterogeneity — some incumbents hold roles anchored in stakeholder trust and novel judgment, not routine analysis. Confidence is low precisely because this residual category may aggregate roles with exposure scores ranging from 5 to 9.

title: Finanz- und Anlageberater
rationale: AI can already analyse financial data, model portfolios, forecast trends, and draft financial plans with high accuracy. However, the core value of this role—building client trust, interpreting ambiguous personal goals, and taking accountability for high-stakes financial decisions—keeps full automation at bay.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The majority of analytical tasks in this role are highly AI-performable at the 2026 frontier. Skills such as 'analyse financial performance of a company,' 'interpret financial statements,' 'analyse market financial trends,' 'forecast economic trends,' 'perform stock valuation,' 'review investment portfolios,' and 'assess financial viability' are precisely the kind of structured information-processing tasks where large language models and specialised financial AI tools (Bloomberg GPT, Morningstar AI, etc.) already operate at professional quality. 'Create a financial plan,' 'develop investment portfolio,' and 'financial forecasting' are increasingly handled by robo-advisory platforms (Betterment, Vanguard Digital Advisor) that automate the bulk of plan construction. 'Synthesise financial information,' 'collect financial data,' and 'monitor stock/bond markets' are near-fully automatable via agentic workflows. Even moderately complex tasks like 'analyse financial risk,' 'estimate profitability,' and 'calculate dividends' are well within current AI capability. The breadth of the essential skill list—107 items—is heavily weighted toward cognitive operations on financial data, not physical or interpersonal outputs.

**Limiting factors:** The occupation's core commercial proposition is trust-based advisory relationships with high-net-worth individuals and institutions. Tasks like 'build business relationships,' 'interview clients to determine risk tolerance,' 'explain financial technicalities to clients,' and 'communicate with beneficiaries' involve interpersonal judgment, emotional intelligence, and fiduciary accountability that AI cannot yet replicate in a client-facing context. Regulatory barriers are significant: Swiss FINMA licensing requirements, MiFID II-equivalent suitability obligations, and liability for investment advice create strong institutional resistance to full AI substitution. Sub-occupations like 'venture capitalist' and 'economic policy officer' require novel cross-domain judgment and political navigation that remain human-advantaged. 'Provide legal advice on investments,' 'advise on tax policy,' and 'advise legislators' involve high-stakes accountability that regulators and clients are unlikely to delegate to AI in this timeframe.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits solidly in the High tier (score 7). The analytical and data-processing backbone of the work is substantially AI-performable, and productivity amplification is already occurring—fewer advisers can manage more clients using AI tools. However, the client-relationship layer, regulatory accountability, and judgment in genuinely ambiguous personal financial situations provide meaningful resistance to full substitution. The score is not 8-9 because the primary remaining human value is not merely oversight of AI outputs: active client relationship management and fiduciary judgment are still core deliverables, not peripheral. Confidence is medium because the sub-occupations span a wide range (from robo-advisory-adjacent financial planners to venture capitalists), and adoption rates vary significantly by client segment and firm type.

title: Finanzanalysten
rationale: AI can already automate the core work of financial analysts: processing financial statements, generating forecasts, screening securities, and drafting investment reports. Human value remains in client relationships, novel cross-market judgment, and accountability for recommendations.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The ISCO task list is almost entirely information-processing work performed on structured digital data. Tasks (a), (c), (d), and (h) — analysing financial information, interpreting investment data, monitoring corporate developments, and comparing securities — are precisely what large language models combined with financial data APIs (Bloomberg GPT, FactSet AI, Refinitiv Workspace) already perform at near-analyst quality. Task (i), preparing written reports on economic trends and corporations, is highly automatable: 2026 LLMs produce professional-grade equity research summaries, earnings analyses, and sector reports. Essential skills such as 'interpret financial statements,' 'prepare financial projections,' 'analyse financial risk,' 'perform business analysis,' and 'provide support in financial calculation' map directly onto demonstrated AI capabilities. Optional skills like 'financial forecasting,' 'analyse financial performance of a company,' 'assess financial viability,' and 'monitor stock market' are similarly well within current AI tool capability. The work product is entirely digital — no physical transformation of the world is required.

**Limiting factors:** Several constraints prevent a score of 9–10. First, task (e) — recommending investments to clients — involves fiduciary accountability and trust that regulators and clients still require a licensed human to bear. Swiss FINMA and EU MiFID II frameworks impose personal liability on investment recommendations, creating a compliance barrier to full automation. Second, task (f), determining syndication prices, involves negotiation and relationship dynamics with underwriters and issuers that remain human-advantaged. Third, 'handle mergers and acquisitions' and 'explain financial technicalities to clients' involve interpersonal judgment and bespoke deal structuring that AI assists but does not replace. Finally, genuinely novel macro-regime shifts (e.g., a new geopolitical shock with no historical analogue) still challenge AI forecasting models that rely on pattern-matching over historical data.

**Net assessment:** Financial analysis sits firmly in the 8 tier. The primary work product — quantitative analysis, forecasting, report writing, and securities evaluation — is digital information work that 2026 AI tools handle at acceptable professional quality. Productivity amplification is already dramatic: a single analyst augmented by AI tools can cover the research load that previously required a team. The remaining human role is oversight, client-facing accountability, regulatory compliance, and judgment in genuinely novel situations. This matches the 8–9 boundary definition: 'human role reduced to oversight, exception handling, and accountability.' The score does not reach 9 because the client advisory and deal-structuring components retain meaningful human value beyond pure oversight.

title: Spezialisten im Bereich Management- und Organisationsanalyse
rationale: AI can already draft business analyses, interpret financial statements, model processes, and generate strategic recommendations from structured data. However, the core value of this role—building client trust, navigating organizational politics, and translating ambiguous problems into actionable change—still requires human judgment.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The majority of essential skills in this occupation are information-processing tasks performed on digital artifacts. Skills such as 'analyse business processes,' 'interpret financial statements,' 'perform data analysis,' 'statistical analysis,' 'track key performance indicators,' 'analyse business objectives,' and 'analyse external/internal factors of companies' are all tasks where 2026 AI tools demonstrate strong capability. Large language models with million-token context windows can ingest workflow charts, procedure manuals, financial reports, and org charts—exactly the inputs described in tasks (f) and (g)—and produce structured analyses and recommendations. Optional skills like 'data mining,' 'data models,' 'business process modelling,' and 'business intelligence' further confirm a heavily digital work environment. AI agents can already automate significant portions of the research, benchmarking, and documentation phases of consulting engagements, and several major consulting firms have deployed AI tools that handle first-draft deliverables for standard analyses.

**Limiting factors:** The occupation's core value proposition is not the analysis itself but the client relationship and organizational change management. Tasks (c) and (d)—'discussing current systems with staff,' 'observing systems at all levels,' and 'directing clients towards more efficient organization'—require physical presence, interpersonal trust, and the ability to read organizational dynamics that AI cannot replicate in 2026. 'Build business relationships,' 'liaise with managers,' and 'make strategic business decisions' involve stakeholder navigation and accountability that clients are unlikely to delegate to AI systems, particularly for high-stakes restructuring. Regulatory and liability considerations in Swiss corporate environments also create adoption friction. Furthermore, 'identify undetected organisational needs' requires the kind of tacit, cross-domain synthesis under genuine ambiguity that remains a documented weakness of current AI systems.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits solidly in the High tier (6–7). AI substantially accelerates and partially replaces the analytical and documentation work that constitutes perhaps 40–60% of billable hours, but the client-facing, trust-dependent, and politically sensitive dimensions of organizational change consulting anchor meaningful human demand. The score of 7 rather than 8 reflects that the primary remaining human value is not merely oversight of AI outputs—consultants must still originate problem framings, manage resistance, and bear accountability for recommendations in ways that clients actively require from humans. Uncertainty stems from the heterogeneity of sub-occupations: a business intelligence manager doing internal data work faces higher exposure than a senior management consultant leading a cultural transformation program.

title: Spezialisten in der strategischen Planung in Politik und Wirtschaft
rationale: AI can draft policy briefs, analyse legislation, research trends, and summarise stakeholder positions at near-professional quality. However, the job's core value lies in political judgment, stakeholder negotiation, and navigating institutional relationships — tasks AI cannot yet replace.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A large share of the 139 essential skills involve information-processing tasks that 2026 AI handles well. 'Carry out strategic research,' 'analyse foreign affairs policies,' 'monitor policy proposals,' 'draft legislation,' 'draft press releases,' 'review drafts,' 'comment drafts,' 'write work-related reports,' and 'write situation reports' are all text-generation or document-analysis tasks where large language models with 1M+ token context windows already perform at professional quality. 'Perform market research,' 'analyse unemployment rates,' 'market analysis,' and 'business analysis' are data-synthesis tasks where AI tools (e.g., Copilot, Perplexity, custom RAG pipelines) demonstrably accelerate throughput. 'Archive documentation,' 'keep task records,' 'maintain professional records,' and 'manage administrative systems' are near-fully automatable. The breadth of the skill list — spanning healthcare, trade, employment, sports, and humanitarian domains — suggests much of the analytical groundwork (trend identification, gap analysis, briefing preparation) is information work conducted on computers, making it AI-amenable regardless of domain.

**Limiting factors:** The occupation's primary deliverable is not a document but a policy outcome, which requires sustained stakeholder trust, political feasibility assessment, and institutional navigation. Skills like 'liaise with politicians,' 'liaise with government officials,' 'build international relations,' 'negotiate with stakeholders,' 'advise policy makers,' 'government representation,' and 'work in crises areas' involve interpersonal credibility and real-time political judgment that AI cannot substitute in 2026. 'Monitor political conflicts,' 'identify emerging issues in humanitarian area,' 'conduct research interviews,' and 'apply knowledge of human behaviour' require contextual situational awareness and trust-based access to information that AI agents lack. Regulatory and institutional barriers are also significant: policy advice in government contexts carries accountability requirements that mandate human sign-off. Sub-occupations like intelligence officer and humanitarian advisor operate in sensitive, often classified or physically dangerous environments where AI deployment faces hard constraints.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits solidly in the High tier (score 6). AI substantially accelerates the analytical and drafting components — potentially compressing what took a junior analyst a week into hours — but the core value proposition (political judgment, stakeholder relationships, institutional credibility) remains human-dependent. The productivity amplification effect is real and will likely reduce headcount demand at the junior/research end of the occupation, but senior policy professionals who own relationships and exercise political judgment are not near-term substitution targets. Uncertainty is moderate because the sub-occupations are heterogeneous: a parliamentary assistant or civil service administrative officer faces higher exposure than an intelligence officer or humanitarian advisor.

title: Berufsberater und -analytiker und vergleichbare Spezialisten der Personalwesen
rationale: AI can automate job posting, CV screening, interview scheduling, and draft job descriptions or reports, handling much of the administrative and analytical groundwork. However, the core value — counselling individuals, managing labour relations, and advising on sensitive personnel decisions — depends on human judgment and trust.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A substantial share of tasks in this occupation involve structured information processing that 2026 AI handles well. Writing job descriptions, conducting job analysis, preparing occupational classification systems, analysing labour market data, and generating report drafts are all tasks where LLMs with large context windows perform at near-professional quality. Recruitment workflows — posting roles, screening CVs against criteria, scheduling interviews, and documenting candidate assessments — are already heavily automated by ATS platforms augmented with AI. Skills like 'write job descriptions,' 'carry out job analysis,' 'report analysis results,' 'market analysis,' and 'online job platforms' map directly onto commercially available AI tools. Payroll management and policy compliance checking are also increasingly automated. For occupational analysts specifically, AI can synthesise large volumes of interview transcripts and produce structured occupation descriptions at scale.

**Limiting factors:** The core of most sub-occupations here is relational and advisory: counselling unemployed individuals, coaching clients through career transitions, managing labour disputes, advising on conflict, and building trust with both employers and candidates. Skills like 'counsel clients,' 'encourage counselled clients to examine themselves,' 'advise on conflict management,' 'handle conflicts,' 'negotiate employment agreements,' and 'labour relations officer' functions require sustained interpersonal judgment, emotional attunement, and accountability that AI cannot reliably replicate in 2026. Regulatory and legal dimensions — employment law, labour legislation, government policy implementation — require human accountability and contextual interpretation, especially in Swiss labour law contexts. Career guidance advisors working with vulnerable populations (unemployed, youth) face additional trust and safeguarding barriers. Physical presence in assessments, union negotiations, and workplace investigations also limits substitution.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits solidly in the High tier (6). AI significantly accelerates the information-processing, documentation, and analytical subtasks — potentially reducing headcount needed for high-volume recruitment and occupational analysis work. But the advisory, counselling, and relational core of most sub-occupations (career guidance, labour relations, HR officer) retains strong human advantage. The score reflects meaningful productivity amplification and partial substitution in administrative/analytical roles, without wholesale displacement of the interpersonal advisory functions. Confidence is medium because the occupation spans heterogeneous sub-roles: occupational analysts are more exposed (~7-8) while vocational guidance counsellors and labour relations officers are less so (~5).

title: Fachkräfte in Personalschulung und -entwicklung
rationale: AI can draft training materials, analyse skill gaps, and generate e-learning content, automating a significant share of content-creation and assessment tasks. However, live facilitation, coaching relationships, and organisational change management keep human trainers central.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A substantial portion of this role involves information-processing and content-creation tasks that 2026 AI handles well. Developing training programmes, preparing lesson content, providing lesson materials, and creating online tutorials are largely text-and-structure tasks where LLMs can produce first drafts at near-professional quality. Needs analysis (identifying necessary human resources, tracking KPIs, evaluating training effectiveness) maps onto data synthesis and reporting tasks that AI agents can automate significantly. Curriculum design, scheduling coordination, and liaising with external providers involve structured decision-making that AI tools increasingly support. The optional skill 'deliver online training' points to a growing asynchronous e-learning segment where AI-generated content and adaptive learning platforms are already displacing human-authored modules.

**Limiting factors:** The core value of this occupation in practice is relational and contextual: coaching employees, giving constructive feedback, facilitating workshops, and building business relationships require real-time interpersonal judgment that AI cannot yet replicate at acceptable quality in live organisational settings. Negotiating employment agreements and liaising with managers involve political and trust dimensions tied to human accountability. Applying intercultural teaching strategies and promoting gender equality in business contexts require nuanced situational reading. Regulatory compliance (employment law, labour legislation, legal regulations) demands human accountability for consequential decisions. Physical facilitation—demonstrations, conferences, group instruction—remains human-anchored. Adoption barriers are also real: Swiss organisations in this wage bracket tend to value certified human trainers for cultural and compliance reasons.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits solidly in the High tier (6). AI is already reshaping content creation, needs analysis, and e-learning delivery—reducing the headcount needed for those subtasks—but the facilitation, coaching, and organisational consulting core resists substitution. The score does not reach 7–8 because live human delivery and stakeholder trust remain primary value drivers, not merely residual exceptions. Key uncertainty: if AI-powered coaching tools (already emerging from vendors like BetterUp AI) mature rapidly, the score could drift toward 7 within 2–3 years.

title: Fachkräfte in Marketing und Werbung
rationale: AI can now draft ad copy, run market research analysis, generate campaign briefs, and automate performance reporting at near-professional quality. Human judgment in brand strategy, client relationships, and creative direction keeps this below the highest tier.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A large share of core ISCO tasks map directly onto 2026 AI capabilities. Writing advertising copy and media scripts (task c) is highly automatable — LLMs produce campaign-ready copy, slogans, and scripts at scale. Collecting and analysing consumer data (task d), interpreting trends (task e), and drawing conclusions from market research (essential skill: 'draw conclusions from market research results') are well within AI's analytical reach, especially with 1M+ token context windows processing large datasets. Skills like 'perform data analysis,' 'carry out statistical forecasts,' 'develop financial statistics reports,' 'maintain pricing database,' and 'produce statistical financial records' are substantially automatable via AI-assisted analytics pipelines. Content creation tasks — 'create advertisements,' 'design brand's online communication plan,' 'prepare presentation material,' 'content marketing strategy' — are increasingly handled by generative AI tools. Media buying optimisation ('buy advertising space,' 'media planning') is already heavily algorithmic. The optional skills list's heavy weighting toward data query languages (SPARQL, LINQ, MDX, N1QL) confirms this is deeply digital, information-processing work.

**Limiting factors:** Several core sub-occupations and skills resist straightforward automation. Creative directors managing teams, brand managers setting long-term positioning, and client relations managers maintaining trust relationships all require interpersonal judgment that AI cannot reliably replicate in 2026. Skills like 'build business relationships,' 'give live presentation,' 'present arguments persuasively,' 'manage staff,' and 'stimulate creativity in the team' involve social and organisational dynamics where AI is a tool, not a substitute. Strategic tasks — 'lead the brand strategic planning process,' 'make strategic business decisions,' 'integrate marketing strategies with the global strategy' — require cross-domain synthesis under genuine ambiguity, which remains human-advantaged. Regulatory compliance ('ensure compliance with purchasing and contracting regulations,' 'copyright legislation') and client accountability also create adoption friction. The breadth of the ISCO group (16 sub-occupations from copywriter to hospitality revenue manager) means exposure is uneven across roles.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the High tier (6–7). The majority of routine, repeatable tasks — copy drafting, data analysis, reporting, media planning, trend monitoring — are AI-performable today, and productivity amplification is already reducing headcount needs in agencies and in-house teams. However, the occupation's core value proposition increasingly rests on strategic judgment, client trust, creative direction, and cross-functional leadership — areas where AI augments rather than replaces. The score of 7 reflects that AI can independently complete most routine instances of the work product (crossing the 5→6 boundary) and that the primary remaining human value is judgment and stakeholder trust (approaching the 7→8 boundary), but full substitution is blocked by the relational and strategic core of senior roles. Confidence is medium due to the heterogeneity across the 16 sub-occupations: an advertising copywriter is closer to 9, while a creative director or client relations manager is closer to 5–6.

title: Fachkräfte in der Öffentlichkeitsarbeit
rationale: AI can draft press releases, write content, analyse audience data, and manage social media scheduling at professional quality. However, the core value of PR — building trust, navigating political relationships, and managing crises — still depends heavily on human judgment and interpersonal credibility.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A large share of the 130 essential skills are information-processing or content-creation tasks that 2026 AI handles well. Drafting press releases, writing promotional content, performing market research, analysing consumer buying trends, performing online data analysis, web analytics, and creating content titles are all tasks where LLMs and agentic tools already operate at professional quality. Social media marketing, online community management, content marketing strategy, and keyword optimisation in digital content are areas where AI tools (scheduling, sentiment analysis, A/B testing automation) have achieved high adoption. Campaign planning documents, annual marketing budgets, and financial analysis tasks are also substantially AI-assisted. The breadth of digital-information work here — essentially all written outputs and data analysis — places this occupation firmly in the high-exposure tier.

**Limiting factors:** The occupation's core value proposition is relational and reputational: advising executives on public image, establishing and maintaining relationships with media and government agencies, performing political negotiation, influencing legislators, and representing organisations at social functions. These tasks require human presence, trust, and contextual judgment that AI cannot replicate in 2026. Crisis communications — advising on conflict management, protecting client interests under pressure, navigating politically sensitive situations — demands real-time human judgment and accountability that clients and stakeholders are unlikely to delegate to AI. The sub-occupations (political campaign officer, election agent, intercultural communication consultant) involve high-stakes interpersonal and institutional dynamics where errors carry serious consequences, creating strong adoption barriers. Physical event coordination and press conference organisation also retain meaningful human components.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the boundary between High (6–7) and Very High (8–9). The written and analytical outputs are heavily AI-augmentable, and productivity amplification is already occurring — fewer PR professionals are needed to produce the same volume of content and analysis. However, the primary remaining human value is not merely oversight of AI outputs: genuine relationship capital, political judgment, and crisis navigation are core deliverables, not peripheral tasks. A score of 7 reflects that AI reshapes the majority of the workflow but does not yet substitute for the relational core. Uncertainty is moderate because sub-occupations vary widely — an online community manager faces higher exposure than a political campaign officer.

title: Technische Verkaufsfachkräfte (ohne Informations- und Kommunikationstechnologie)
rationale: AI can automate lead generation, draft proposals, analyze market data, and handle routine customer inquiries for technical sales reps. However, the core value—building trust with clients, demonstrating complex products in person, and negotiating contracts—still requires human presence and judgment.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A significant portion of this occupation's tasks are information-processing activities well within 2026 AI capabilities. Compiling prospective client lists, performing market analysis, producing sales reports, carrying out sales analysis, and drafting proposals and quotations are highly automatable. CRM software integration with AI agents can automate 'keep records of customer interaction,' 'maintain customer records,' 'implement customer follow-up,' and 'provide customers with order information.' AI tools can already generate competitive intelligence reports, draft sales contracts, and produce cost estimates. Skills like 'perform market research,' 'implement marketing strategies,' and 'apply social media marketing' (optional) are substantially AI-assisted. AI-powered product configurators can handle 'answer requests for quotation' and 'issue sales quotations for repairs or maintenance' for standard product lines. These tasks collectively represent perhaps 40–50% of the listed essential skills.

**Limiting factors:** The core value proposition of this occupation is deeply interpersonal and physically situated. 'Visiting regular and prospective client businesses,' 'demonstrate products' features,' 'build business relationships,' 'negotiate sales contracts,' and 'guarantee customer satisfaction' all require in-person presence, physical product demonstrations, and trust-building that AI cannot replicate in 2026. Sub-occupations like medical sales representative and after-sales service technician involve regulated environments (pharmacovigilance legislation, health care system compliance, medical device safety) where human accountability is legally mandated. After-sales service technicians perform physical installation, maintenance, and repair—tasks requiring dexterous manipulation in unstructured environments. The 'assess customers' needs' and 'create solutions to problems' tasks require contextual judgment in novel client situations that AI handles poorly. Regulatory barriers in medical/pharmaceutical sales further slow AI substitution.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the High tier (score 6). AI substantially reshapes the back-office and analytical components—lead generation, reporting, quoting, CRM management—reducing administrative burden and potentially allowing each rep to cover more accounts. However, the primary work product is a trusted client relationship and a closed deal, both of which depend on in-person persuasion, technical credibility, and negotiation. The heterogeneity across sub-occupations (solar consultant vs. medical device rep vs. after-sales technician) introduces some uncertainty, as the after-sales technician role has lower exposure due to physical service work, while the pure sales consultant roles sit closer to 7.

title: Technische Verkaufsfachkräfte in der Informations- und Kommunikationstechnologie
rationale: AI can automate quote generation, sales reporting, CRM updates, and market research, but closing complex B2B ICT deals still requires human relationship-building and technical negotiation. The role sits at high exposure because most administrative and analytical tasks are automatable, but the core value—trusted advisory selling—resists substitution.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A substantial portion of the 66 essential skills are directly automatable by 2026 AI tools. Tasks like 'produce sales reports,' 'keep records of customer interaction,' 'record customers' personal data,' 'interpret current data,' and 'perform data analysis' are routine information-processing work that CRM-integrated AI (e.g., Salesforce Einstein, HubSpot AI) already handles. 'Answer requests for quotation,' 'prepare technical reports,' and 'perform market research' are well within the capability of LLM-based tools that can synthesize product specs, pricing data, and competitive intelligence at near-professional quality. 'Monitor technology trends' and 'keep up to date on product knowledge' are tasks where AI assistants provide significant leverage, reducing the time a human must spend. Optional skills like 'apply statistical analysis techniques,' 'forecast account metrics,' and 'digital marketing techniques' are also heavily AI-augmented. Agentic CRM workflows can now handle prospect outreach sequencing, follow-up scheduling, and pipeline reporting with minimal human input.

**Limiting factors:** The core value proposition of this occupation—building trusted advisory relationships with enterprise ICT buyers—is highly resistant to AI substitution. Tasks like 'give live presentation,' 'apply conflict management,' 'negotiate prices and credit terms,' 'identify customer's needs' in complex multi-stakeholder environments, and 'develop account strategy' require human judgment, social intelligence, and accountability that 2026 AI cannot replicate in high-value B2B contexts. ICT sales at the wholesale/enterprise level involves navigating organizational politics, managing long sales cycles, and providing credible technical reassurance to risk-averse buyers—all of which depend on human presence and trust. The 'ICT presales engineer' sub-occupation in particular requires deep technical problem-solving in ambiguous customer environments. Regulatory compliance tasks ('comply with legal regulations,' 'manage contracts') require human accountability in Swiss commercial law contexts. The relatively high wage (CHF 9,385/month) reflects that employers are paying for relationship capital and technical credibility, not just information processing.

**Net assessment:** This occupation scores 6 (High exposure). AI substantially automates the administrative, analytical, and reporting backbone of the role—potentially reducing headcount needed for pipeline management and inside sales functions. However, the primary work product is not a digital artifact but a closed deal built on human trust, which anchors the score below the 7–8 range. The heterogeneity across sub-occupations (account manager vs. presales engineer vs. business development manager) introduces some uncertainty; presales engineering skews slightly higher in exposure due to its technical documentation and requirements-analysis tasks, while business development skews lower due to strategic relationship work. Confidence is medium because adoption of AI sales tools in Swiss enterprise ICT markets is real but uneven.

title: Spezialisten in der Informations- und Kommunikationstechnologie, onA
rationale: AI can now write code, analyze systems, and automate data pipelines for a wide range of ICT tasks. However, this catch-all category spans roles from network engineers to IT project managers, making a single score imprecise.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** ISCO 2500 is a residual 'not elsewhere classified' grouping for ICT specialists, which by definition covers information-work occupations whose primary outputs are digital artifacts — code, system configurations, data analyses, technical documentation, and IT project plans. For all of these, 2026 AI tools are highly capable: agentic coding assistants handle multi-file development and debugging, AI-driven documentation generators produce technical specs, and LLM-based tools automate requirements analysis and test-case generation. The 'optional knowledge as litmus test' strongly applies here — virtually any ICT specialist role relies on specific software platforms, programming languages, or digital infrastructure tools, confirming the work is conducted entirely in a digital environment. The productivity amplification effect is particularly strong: a single AI-augmented ICT specialist can now produce output that previously required two or three junior staff.

**Limiting factors:** The NEC (not elsewhere classified) nature of this group introduces significant heterogeneity. Some workers in this bucket likely perform roles closer to IT operations, field support, or infrastructure management — tasks involving physical hardware installation, on-site troubleshooting, or client-facing stakeholder management that resist full automation. Regulatory and security constraints in Swiss enterprise environments (banking, pharma, government) create adoption friction for autonomous AI agents handling sensitive systems. Novel architecture decisions, cross-domain integration challenges, and accountability for production systems also retain meaningful human advantage. Trust and liability concerns slow deployment of fully autonomous AI in critical ICT infrastructure.

**Net assessment:** The occupation sits solidly in the High tier (6–7), with the primary remaining human value being judgment in ambiguous technical decisions, stakeholder communication, and accountability for system reliability. It does not reach the Very High tier (8–9) because the NEC grouping almost certainly includes roles with significant physical, operational, or interpersonal components that are not fully captured in the ISCO description. Confidence is low precisely because this is a heterogeneous residual category — individual sub-occupations within it could plausibly score anywhere from 5 to 9.

title: Entwickler und Analytiker von Software und Anwendungen, onA
rationale: AI tools can now autonomously write, debug, and refactor code for well-defined tasks, handling much of what junior-to-mid-level developers do daily. The remaining human edge lies in system architecture, novel problem-solving, and accountability — keeping this just below the maximum tier.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The core work product of this occupation is digital code and software artifacts — precisely the domain where 2026 AI is most capable. Agentic coding tools (GitHub Copilot, Cursor, Claude Code) handle multi-file code generation, debugging, test writing, and refactoring for well-scoped tasks. ESCO-listed skills such as 'develop software prototypes,' 'perform software testing,' 'write technical specifications,' and 'analyse software requirements' all map onto tasks where AI tools already deliver production-usable output. Documentation, code review assistance, and routine data pipeline construction are substantially automated. The 'onA' (not elsewhere classified) designation suggests a broad catch-all category where many workers perform relatively standardised development tasks — the segment most exposed to AI substitution.

**Limiting factors:** The primary human advantage persists in novel system architecture decisions, cross-domain ambiguity resolution, and accountability for production systems. ESCO skills like 'design system architecture,' 'consult with stakeholders,' and 'manage software project' involve judgment under uncertainty and organisational trust that AI cannot yet reliably substitute. Regulatory and safety requirements in Swiss enterprise and financial-sector software contexts impose validation and audit obligations that require human sign-off. Complex legacy system integration — common in Swiss banking and insurance — involves undocumented constraints that defeat AI agents relying on well-defined task specifications. Senior developers directing AI tools remain more productive, not replaced.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the 8/10 'very high' tier. The primary remaining human value is oversight, architectural judgment, and accountability — exactly the boundary condition between tiers 8 and 9. It does not reach 9-10 because a meaningful share of work involves genuinely novel design challenges and stakeholder negotiation. The 'onA' classification introduces slight heterogeneity (some workers may skew toward analyst roles with more interpersonal exposure), but the dominant work product is digital code, anchoring the score at 8.

title: Systemanalytiker
rationale: AI can now automate large portions of requirements gathering, process modelling, documentation, data analysis, and even prototype code generation. However, the core value of this role—translating ambiguous business needs into coherent system designs and managing stakeholder alignment—still requires human judgment and trust.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The majority of tasks in this occupation are information-work performed on digital artifacts. AI tools in 2026 are highly capable across a wide swath of the essential skill list: 'analyse business processes,' 'create business process models,' 'create flowchart diagrams,' 'perform data mining,' 'analyse software specifications,' 'provide cost-benefit analysis reports,' 'write work-related reports,' and 'provide technical documentation' are all tasks where LLMs with large context windows already produce professional-quality outputs. Agentic coding tools handle 'develop software prototype,' 'debug software,' and 'use scripting programming' at junior-to-mid-level quality. Data analysis tasks—'analyse big data,' 'perform data cleansing,' 'normalise data,' 'interpret current data,' 'deliver visual presentation of data'—are heavily supported by AI-augmented tooling (Copilot in Power BI, Databricks AI, etc.). The optional skills list is dominated by programming languages and platforms (ABAP, C#, C++, AJAX, ASP.NET), confirming this is fundamentally digital information-work. AI can already generate first drafts of functional specifications, test plans, and integration strategies, compressing the time a single analyst needs per engagement.

**Limiting factors:** The occupation's core value proposition is bridging ambiguous, politically charged organisational needs with technically feasible system designs—a task that requires sustained stakeholder trust, negotiation, and contextual judgment that AI cannot yet replicate reliably. Skills like 'consult with business clients,' 'interact with users to gather requirements,' 'apply change management,' 'make strategic business decisions,' and 'design enterprise architecture' involve navigating organisational politics, managing resistance, and synthesising contradictory stakeholder priorities in ways that go beyond pattern-matching on prior documents. 'Perform risk analysis,' 'execute feasibility study,' and 'manage ICT legacy implication' require deep contextual knowledge of a specific organisation's constraints that AI lacks without extensive grounding. Regulatory and security dimensions ('ICT security legislation,' 'execute ICT audits,' 'perform security vulnerability assessments') carry accountability requirements that keep humans in the loop. The 18 sub-occupations span a wide range—from data analyst (very high exposure) to enterprise architect and embedded system designer (moderate)—introducing heterogeneity that pulls the score toward the middle of the high tier.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits solidly in the High tier (score 7). AI dramatically accelerates the documentation, modelling, coding, and analysis subtasks that consume much of an analyst's time, meaning fewer analysts are needed per project and productivity per analyst rises sharply. However, the primary remaining human value—translating organisational ambiguity into coherent technical direction and maintaining stakeholder trust through complex change processes—is not yet reliably automatable. The score would be higher (8) if the role were purely technical specification work, but the breadth of sub-occupations and the centrality of stakeholder-facing judgment anchor it at 7. Key uncertainty: the enterprise architect and ICT intelligent systems designer sub-occupations may warrant a lower score (6), while data analyst sub-occupations may warrant a higher one (9), making the aggregate confidence medium.

title: Mediamatiker
rationale: AI can now generate code, write web content, create graphics, and automate SEO tasks — covering the majority of what web and multimedia developers produce daily. Human value increasingly concentrates in creative direction, client requirements gathering, and architectural decisions for complex projects.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The core work product of this occupation is entirely digital: code, visual designs, web content, and multimedia assets. Among the 79 essential skills, a large majority are directly AI-performable at 2026 capability levels. Web programming, debug software, use markup languages, style sheet languages, implement front-end website design, and computer programming are all tasks where agentic coding tools (GitHub Copilot, Cursor, Claude Code) now handle substantial junior-to-mid-level output autonomously. Content-side tasks — compile content, provide written content, create content title, manage online content, keywords in digital content, conduct search engine optimisation, enhance website visibility — are highly automatable via LLMs and SEO automation platforms. Design tasks such as design graphics, design user interface, translate requirement concepts into visual design, create website wireframe, and render 3D images are increasingly handled by generative AI tools (Midjourney, Firefly, Figma AI). The optional skills list is dominated by specific programming languages and tools (CSS, AJAX, ASP.NET, C#, Adobe Illustrator, Adobe Photoshop), confirming this is pure digital information work with no physical component.

**Limiting factors:** Several tasks resist straightforward automation. Identify customer requirements and interact with users to gather requirements involve stakeholder negotiation and ambiguity resolution that AI handles poorly without human mediation. Define technical requirements and develop software prototype for novel or complex systems still benefit from experienced human judgment, particularly when business logic is poorly specified. ICT security legislation compliance and legal requirements of ICT products require accountability that clients and regulators currently assign to humans. The digital games developer sub-occupation involves creative direction and game-feel iteration that remains human-advantaged. Web content managers operating at a strategic level — publishing strategy, content marketing strategy, build business relationships — retain meaningful human value. Adoption friction also exists: many Swiss SME clients expect direct human contact and accountability for their web presence.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the 8–9 range. The primary work products (code, content, design assets) are all digital and increasingly AI-generatable at acceptable quality. The human role is shifting toward oversight, client-facing requirements gathering, quality assurance, and handling novel architectural challenges — precisely the pattern that defines the 8–9 tier. The breadth of sub-occupations (SEO expert, UI designer, web developer, games developer, content manager) introduces some heterogeneity, with content managers and SEO experts arguably closer to 9 and games developers with strong creative direction roles closer to 7. A score of 8 reflects this spread while acknowledging that productivity amplification is already reducing headcount demand even where full substitution has not occurred.

title: Anwendungsprogrammierer
rationale: AI can now write, debug, and refactor code autonomously across most common languages and frameworks, handling the bulk of routine programming tasks. The remaining human value lies in interpreting ambiguous requirements, architectural decisions, and accountability — placing this firmly in the very-high-exposure tier.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The core work product of applications programmers is digital code, placing this occupation at inherently high AI exposure. The 80 essential skills are almost entirely programming languages (Python, Java, C++, JavaScript, TypeScript, etc.) and software development tasks (debug software, develop software prototype, use software design patterns, integrate system components). As of 2026, agentic coding tools (GitHub Copilot, Cursor, Claude Code) can autonomously generate, debug, and refactor multi-file codebases in all major languages listed. Tasks like 'compile and write documentation,' 'create flowchart diagrams,' 'provide technical documentation,' and 'develop automated migration methods' are highly automatable. The ISCO task of 'revising, repairing or expanding existing programs' maps directly to demonstrated AI capabilities. Even niche skills like ABAP, COBOL, and SAP R3 integration have AI tooling. Studies suggest 40–60% of junior-to-mid-level coding work is now AI-completable at acceptable quality.

**Limiting factors:** Several tasks resist full automation. 'Identify customer requirements' and 'collect customer feedback on applications' require interpersonal judgment and stakeholder trust. 'Propose ICT solutions to business problems' and 'manage business knowledge' demand cross-domain synthesis under ambiguity that 2026 AI handles inconsistently. The sub-occupation of numerical tool and process control programmer involves physical machine integration (CNC controllers, embedded systems, measurement instruments) where real-world validation loops slow automation. 'Build business relationships' and multi-stakeholder coordination remain human-anchored. Regulatory and safety requirements in industrial software contexts (IoT, embedded systems) impose validation burdens that require human accountability. These factors prevent a score of 9–10.

**Net assessment:** Applications programmers sit solidly in the very-high-exposure tier (score 8). The primary work product is code — a digital artifact AI produces at near-professional quality for well-defined tasks. The human role has already shifted toward requirement clarification, architectural judgment, and exception handling, matching the 8–9 boundary definition. The heterogeneity of sub-occupations (industrial mobile, CNC programming, ICT configurator) introduces some variance, but all sub-roles share the same digital-artifact core. The score does not reach 9 because a meaningful share of tasks involve physical system integration, client-facing requirement elicitation, and novel cross-domain problem-solving that remain human-advantaged in 2026.

title: Entwickler und Analytiker von Software und Anwendungen, anderweitig nicht genannt
rationale: AI can now write test scripts, detect software anomalies, perform data quality checks, and generate audit documentation with high reliability. The remaining human value lies in judgment about novel failure modes, regulatory accountability, and stakeholder communication — pushing this firmly into the very-high tier.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The core work products of this occupational group — test plans, test scripts, audit reports, data quality assessments, and software defect documentation — are digital artifacts produced through structured analytical reasoning on code and data. AI tools in 2026 are highly capable across nearly all of these: GitHub Copilot and similar agentic coding tools can generate and execute unit tests, identify software anomalies, and replicate customer issues from bug reports. Tasks like 'execute software tests,' 'perform data cleansing,' 'normalise data,' 'process data,' and 'provide software testing documentation' are substantially automatable today. The optional skills list — dominated by specific programming languages (C#, C++, ABAP, COBOL, Groovy, Haskell) and ICT frameworks — confirms this is pure information work conducted in digital environments. AI-assisted static analysis, fuzzing, and regression testing already reduce manual testing effort by 50–70% in enterprise settings.

**Limiting factors:** Several sub-occupations retain meaningful human-resistant components. ICT disaster recovery analysts must make high-stakes judgment calls under novel failure conditions where AI lacks situational awareness of organizational context. ICT auditor managers must sign off on compliance findings with legal accountability — regulators in Switzerland and the EU require human responsibility for audit conclusions. 'Build business relationships,' 'conduct impact evaluation of ICT processes on business,' and 'implement strategic planning' involve stakeholder trust and organizational politics that AI cannot substitute. Additionally, 'identify ICT security risks' and 'define security policies' in novel threat environments require cross-domain synthesis that remains human-advantaged. Regulatory compliance (FINMA, ISO standards) creates adoption friction for full AI substitution in audit roles.

**Net assessment:** This group sits solidly at 8. The primary work is digital, the outputs are documents and test artifacts, and 2026 AI tools are demonstrably capable of performing the majority of routine instances — test generation, data quality checks, anomaly detection, and report drafting. The residual human role is oversight, accountability, novel-case judgment, and stakeholder trust, which matches the 8–9 boundary definition precisely. The heterogeneity across sub-occupations (software tester vs. ICT auditor manager) introduces some variance, but even the most judgment-intensive sub-roles have heavily AI-exposed task portfolios.

title: Datenbankentwickler und -administratoren
rationale: AI can now write SQL queries, generate data models, automate migrations, and assist with database schema design at a professional level. However, complex architecture decisions, performance tuning in production environments, and security governance keep human experts central.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The majority of this occupation's core tasks involve structured information work performed entirely in digital environments. AI tools in 2026 can generate and optimize SQL and query languages, draft database schemas and data models, produce documentation, perform data quality assessments, and assist with ETL pipeline design — all listed as essential skills. Agentic coding tools (GitHub Copilot, Cursor, Claude Code) handle multi-file database development tasks, automated migration scripting, and integration testing at a junior-to-mid-level standard. The optional skills list is dominated by specific programming languages and database platforms (ABAP, C#, DB2, Informatica PowerCenter), confirming this is pure information work. Tasks like 'write database documentation,' 'report analysis results,' 'create database diagrams,' and 'test ICT queries' are highly automatable today.

**Limiting factors:** Several core responsibilities resist straightforward AI substitution. Production database administration — balancing resources under live load, enforcing security and integrity controls, managing legacy system implications, and performing troubleshooting in complex distributed environments — requires contextual judgment about specific organizational infrastructure that AI cannot fully replicate without deep system access and accountability. Regulatory compliance (ICT security legislation, data governance policies) and the consequences of errors in production systems create strong adoption barriers: organizations are unlikely to delegate autonomous control of critical databases to AI agents without human oversight. Identifying customer requirements and translating ambiguous business needs into technical architecture also retains significant human value. The 'database architect' sub-role in particular involves novel cross-domain synthesis that remains human-advantaged.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the High tier (score 7). The work is entirely digital and information-based, and AI tools can already perform a large share of routine development, documentation, and query tasks. However, the occupation does not reach the Very High tier (8-9) because production DBA work involves real-time judgment in complex, high-stakes environments where errors are costly, regulatory accountability is real, and organizational context is deep. The primary remaining human value is architectural judgment, security governance, and accountability — consistent with the 7-8 boundary definition. Uncertainty exists around how quickly agentic database management tools mature for autonomous production use.

title: Systemadministratoren
rationale: AI can automate routine monitoring, log analysis, scripting, and basic troubleshooting that make up a large share of daily sysadmin work. However, hands-on hardware intervention, complex incident response, and organizational judgment keep human administrators essential.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A significant portion of sysadmin work is information-processing and pattern-matching on digital systems — tasks where 2026 AI tools are demonstrably capable. 'Monitor system performance' and 'solve ICT system problems' are increasingly handled by AIOps platforms (Dynatrace, Datadog with AI layers) that detect anomalies, correlate alerts, and suggest remediations autonomously. 'Perform backups' and 'implement ICT recovery system' are largely automated through orchestration tools that AI can configure and optimize. 'Provide technical documentation' and 'interpret technical texts' are squarely in AI's strength zone — LLMs can draft runbooks, parse vendor documentation, and generate configuration guides at near-professional quality. 'Manage changes in ICT system' and 'manage system testing' involve structured workflows that agentic AI tools can increasingly execute end-to-end for well-defined change types. Scripting and routine configuration tasks under 'ICT system programming' are well within current AI coding capabilities.

**Limiting factors:** Several core tasks resist automation meaningfully. Physical hardware intervention — installing signal repeaters, replacing components, diagnosing physical failures — requires on-site presence and dexterous manipulation that robotics cannot yet handle in unstructured data center environments. 'Manage system security' and 'implement ICT risk management' involve organizational judgment, threat modeling under ambiguity, and accountability that regulators and enterprises are reluctant to delegate to AI. 'Interact with users to gather requirements' and 'support ICT system users' require interpersonal trust and contextual understanding of organizational dynamics. Complex incident response during novel outages — where multiple interdependent systems fail in unexpected ways — still demands experienced human judgment. Regulatory and compliance contexts (common in Swiss financial and healthcare sectors) impose human accountability requirements that slow AI substitution.

**Net assessment:** Systems administration sits at the high end of moderate exposure (score 6). AI is already reshaping the role by automating the most repetitive monitoring, scripting, and documentation tasks, reducing the headcount needed for routine operations. However, the blend of physical infrastructure work, security judgment, user-facing support, and organizational accountability prevents this from reaching the very-high tier. The occupation is being restructured rather than eliminated — fewer administrators handle more infrastructure, but the role shifts toward oversight, architecture decisions, and exception handling rather than disappearing. Uncertainty is moderate because the pace of AIOps adoption varies significantly across organization sizes and sectors.

title: Fachkräfte für Computernetzwerke
rationale: AI can automate network monitoring, traffic analysis, fault diagnosis, and documentation drafting, but designing network architecture and hands-on hardware configuration still require human judgment and physical intervention. The mix of automatable analytical tasks and irreducible physical and architectural work places this occupation in the high-exposure tier.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A significant share of the listed tasks are information-processing operations well within 2026 AI capabilities. Network traffic monitoring and performance analysis (tasks b, g) are already heavily automated via AIOps platforms (e.g., Cisco ThousandEyes, Datadog). Fault diagnosis and troubleshooting (task d) increasingly leverage AI-driven root-cause analysis tools. Documentation generation — preparing network inventory records, fault logs, and maintenance instructions (task f) — is highly automatable with current LLMs. Capacity planning (tasks in 'plan ICT capacity,' 'carry out statistical forecasts,' 'forecast future ICT network needs') maps directly to AI-assisted predictive analytics. The optional skills list is dominated by programming languages and platforms, confirming this is fundamentally digital information work. Business analysis, cost-benefit reporting, and statistical forecasting are all tasks where AI tools already deliver production-quality outputs.

**Limiting factors:** Network architecture design (task a, 'design computer network,' 'define ICT network design policies') requires cross-domain judgment about organizational requirements, security trade-offs, and vendor ecosystems that AI cannot yet reliably synthesize without expert oversight. Physical hardware installation, placement, and maintenance (task e, 'maintain information network hardware,' 'design ICT hardware placement') require on-site presence and manual dexterity in unstructured environments — firmly human territory. Security responsibilities ('apply information security policies,' 'ICT network security risks,' 'information security strategy') carry regulatory and liability weight that creates strong adoption barriers for autonomous AI decision-making. Client-facing tasks like collecting customer feedback and assessing ICT knowledge also resist full automation. The role spans from hands-on physical work to high-level architectural design, creating heterogeneity that limits uniform AI substitution.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the boundary of the moderate-to-high tier (score 6). AI can independently handle the majority of routine monitoring, diagnostics, documentation, and forecasting tasks — pushing past the 5→6 threshold. However, the physical hardware dimension, architectural design judgment, and security accountability create a meaningful floor of human-required work that prevents a score of 7 or above. The sub-occupation heterogeneity (architect vs. engineer vs. capacity planner) introduces uncertainty: a pure ICT capacity planner would score closer to 7, while a field network engineer would score closer to 5. A score of 6 reflects the central tendency across the ISCO group.

title: Fachkräfte für Datenbanken und Netzwerke, anderweitig nicht genannt.
rationale: AI can automate threat detection, vulnerability scanning, log analysis, and routine security monitoring, but defending against novel attacks and making high-stakes incident response decisions still requires human judgment. The mix of automatable analytical tasks and irreplaceable adversarial reasoning places this occupation firmly in the high-exposure tier.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A substantial share of the listed essential skills are directly amenable to 2026 AI tooling. Threat intelligence aggregation ('monitor current reports of computer viruses,' 'identify ICT security risks'), log and anomaly analysis ('monitor system performance,' 'software anomalies'), vulnerability scanning ('perform security vulnerability assessments,' 'penetration testing tool'), and compliance documentation ('provide technical documentation,' 'manage IT security compliances') are all areas where AI-assisted platforms — SIEM tools with ML, automated pen-testing suites, LLM-driven policy drafting — already handle a large fraction of routine workload. 'Information extraction,' 'natural language processing,' and 'query languages' are tasks where AI performs at or above junior-analyst level. The optional skills list is dominated by programming languages and digital platforms, confirming this is almost entirely information-work conducted in digital environments.

**Limiting factors:** The adversarial nature of cybersecurity is the primary brake on AI substitution. Ethical hackers and digital forensics experts must reason about novel attack vectors that, by definition, have not appeared in training data; AI tools that rely on pattern-matching are systematically disadvantaged against zero-day exploits and sophisticated threat actors. 'Gather data for forensic purposes,' 'perform forensic preservations of digital devices,' and 'manage data for legal matters' carry chain-of-custody and legal admissibility requirements that demand accountable human professionals. Senior sub-occupations (Chief ICT Security Officer, ICT Resilience Manager) involve organisational strategy, board-level communication, and regulatory negotiation — high-trust, high-stakes interpersonal work that AI cannot independently execute. Regulatory frameworks (ICT security legislation, legal requirements of ICT products) also impose human accountability that slows full automation.

**Net assessment:** This group sits at the boundary of the High (6-7) tier. AI is already a powerful productivity multiplier — fewer analysts can cover more ground — but the adversarial, legally accountable, and strategically novel dimensions of the work prevent the 'primary remaining human value is judgment in novel cases' threshold from being crossed for the majority of sub-occupations. The heterogeneity of the group (data miners vs. CISOs vs. forensic experts) introduces uncertainty; data mining and routine security administration skew toward 7-8, while forensics and executive security roles anchor closer to 5-6. A score of 6 reflects this spread with medium confidence.

title: Juristen, onA
rationale: AI can draft legal documents, research case law, and summarize contracts at near-professional quality today. Human lawyers remain essential for courtroom advocacy, client trust, and judgment in novel legal disputes, keeping the score below the highest tier.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The core work of legal professionals — researching statutes and case law, drafting contracts and briefs, reviewing and summarizing documents, and performing due diligence — is almost entirely information-processing on text. 2026 AI tools with million-token context windows can ingest entire case files, identify relevant precedents, flag inconsistencies in contracts, and produce first-draft pleadings at near-associate quality. Tasks such as 'interpret legal texts,' 'advise on legal matters,' and 'draft legal documents' (all core ISCO 2610 activities) are precisely where large language models have demonstrated strong performance. Legal research platforms (Harvey, CoCounsel, Lexis+ AI) are already in production use at major firms, compressing research time by 50–80%. Document review in discovery — historically a major source of junior lawyer hours — is now largely AI-assisted or automated. The productivity amplification effect is significant: fewer lawyers are needed to produce the same volume of work product.

**Limiting factors:** Several factors prevent a score of 8–9.
