The breadth of sub-occupations (nuclear technician, leather laboratory technician, hydrographic surveying technician) means physical environments range from marine settings to nuclear facilities, all structurally resistant to robotic automation at 2026 capability levels. The 'auxiliary tool rule' applies strongly: digital skills like GIS use and computer modelling are tools supporting fieldwork, not the core output.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the low end of moderate exposure (score 4). AI meaningfully accelerates the data analysis, reporting, and pattern-recognition components—perhaps 30–35% of task time—but cannot replace the physical sample collection, instrument operation, field work, and safety-critical hands-on procedures that constitute the majority of the role. Productivity amplification is real but does not threaten headcount substantially, as the bottleneck is physical throughput, not analytical capacity. Uncertainty is moderate because the sub-occupations are heterogeneous: a meteorology technician working primarily with digital forecast models sits closer to 5–6, while a geology field technician or nuclear technician sits closer to 2–3.

title: Bauführer und Bautechniker
rationale: AI can assist with report writing, energy audits, and data analysis, but the core work—inspecting physical sites, testing materials, and enforcing safety on construction sites—requires in-person presence and physical judgment. The job stays at moderate exposure because most high-value tasks happen in the field, not at a desk.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A meaningful subset of tasks in this occupation are AI-performable at 2026 capability levels. Writing inspection reports, technical reports, and work-related reports (essential skills: 'write inspection reports,' 'write technical reports') can be substantially assisted or drafted by AI given structured inputs. Energy-related analytical tasks—'analyse energy consumption,' 'forecast energy prices,' 'conduct energy audit,' 'analyse energy market trends,' 'identify energy needs'—are well within AI's current capabilities for data synthesis and pattern recognition. Processing and interpreting collected survey data ('process collected survey data,' 'interpret geophysical data,' 'perform surveying calculations') can be partially automated using AI-enhanced GIS and geomatics platforms. Optional skills like 'CAD software,' 'apply digital mapping,' and 'analyse environmental data' further indicate a digital layer where AI tools are already embedded. These tasks collectively represent perhaps 25–35% of the role's workload.

**Limiting factors:** The dominant share of this occupation's core output is physical and site-dependent. Tasks such as 'monitor construction site,' 'inspect construction supplies,' 'test construction material samples,' 'inspect pipelines,' 'perform field research,' 'use measurement instruments,' 'calibrate precision instruments,' and 'operate surveying instruments' all require physical presence, tactile judgment, and real-world environmental navigation. Regulatory and safety compliance functions ('ensure compliance with safety legislation,' 'ensure compliance with environmental legislation,' 'enforce railway safety regulations,' 'supervise worker safety') carry legal accountability that regulators and employers are unlikely to delegate to AI systems in the near term. The breadth of sub-occupations—from corrosion technician to railway infrastructure inspector to construction safety manager—means physical inspection and on-site problem-solving are the irreducible core across virtually all variants. Robotics and computer vision for site inspection exist but remain far below human efficiency in unstructured construction environments.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the low end of the moderate tier (score 4). AI meaningfully assists with the documentation, reporting, and analytical subtasks, and energy analyst/consultant sub-roles have higher exposure. However, the primary work product is physical compliance and safety assurance delivered through in-person inspection—not a digital artifact. The 'auxiliary tool rule' applies: digital skills like GIS, CAD, and report writing are tools supporting fieldwork, not the core output. Adoption barriers (regulatory accountability, unstructured physical environments, liability) further constrain substitution. Uncertainty arises from the heterogeneity of sub-occupations: energy analysts within this group may score closer to 6, while railway infrastructure inspectors or corrosion technicians may sit at 3.

title: Elektrotechniker
rationale: AI can assist with drafting wiring diagrams, reading blueprints, and preparing cost estimates, but the core work — physically installing, testing, calibrating, and repairing electrical systems — requires hands-on presence in unstructured environments. The job is anchored by physical manipulation and on-site judgment, keeping AI exposure low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** Several tasks in this occupation involve information processing that AI tools can meaningfully assist with. Designing and preparing blueprints and electrical wiring diagrams maps directly onto CAD/CAE software increasingly augmented by AI (listed as optional skills). Preparing cost and quantity estimates is a structured data task where AI can accelerate work substantially. Reading engineering drawings, recording test data, and performing risk analysis are cognitive tasks where AI can serve as a capable assistant. Technical drawing software (listed as an essential skill) is already AI-augmented in 2026. For the hydropower sub-occupation, monitoring and inspection data analysis (e.g., anomaly detection in generator performance) is an area where AI-driven sensor analytics are in active deployment.

**Limiting factors:** The dominant share of core tasks are irreducibly physical: assembling and aligning components, applying soldering techniques, fastening components, performing test runs, calibrating and repairing installed equipment, and wearing protective gear on live sites. These require dexterous manipulation in varied, unstructured physical environments — precisely where 2026 robotics remain far below human capability. Regulatory compliance (electrical power safety regulations, banned materials) requires on-site human accountability that jurisdictions have not delegated to autonomous systems. Liaising with engineers and monitoring installations in the field involves real-time physical judgment that AI cannot substitute. The Swiss regulatory environment for electrical installations adds further adoption friction.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). AI provides genuine productivity uplift on the design, documentation, and estimation subtasks — perhaps 20–25% of total work time — but the primary work product is a safely functioning physical electrical installation, not a digital artifact. The physical assembly, testing, and repair tasks that constitute the majority of the role are resistant to AI substitution under 2026 capabilities. The score reflects meaningful but peripheral AI assistance rather than core-task displacement.

title: Techniker im Bereich Elektronik
rationale: AI can assist with circuit design, reading schematics, and analysing test data, but the core work involves physically assembling, soldering, testing, and repairing electronic hardware. The hands-on, physical nature of most tasks keeps AI exposure moderate.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** Several tasks in this occupation have meaningful AI support. Interpreting electronic design specifications, reading circuit diagrams and engineering drawings, and preparing cost/quantity estimates are information-processing tasks where AI tools (including CAD/CAE software with AI integration) can accelerate work. Recording and analysing test data is increasingly automatable via AI-assisted data pipelines. Designing blueprints and circuit layouts benefits from AI-augmented EDA (electronic design automation) tools. Optional skills like CAD software, CAE software, and firmware programming are areas where AI coding and design assistants are already productive. These tasks collectively represent perhaps 25–35% of the role's cognitive workload.

**Limiting factors:** The dominant share of this occupation's core output is physical: assembling electronic units, soldering components, aligning and fastening parts, configuring and testing hardware, and repairing or resolving equipment malfunctions. Robotic assembly exists in structured factory environments but cannot yet handle the varied, unstructured, and often prototype-level work described here — particularly preparing production prototypes, conducting hands-on performance tests, and troubleshooting novel faults. Liaising with engineers and ensuring compliance with electronic equipment standards requires contextual judgment and physical verification. The 2026 robotics frontier (roughly 70% human efficiency in structured pick-and-place) falls well short of the dexterity and adaptability needed for PCB-level assembly, soldering, and diagnostic repair in varied settings. Regulatory and safety compliance requirements further slow AI adoption.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the lower end of moderate exposure (score 4). AI tools meaningfully assist the design, documentation, and data-analysis subtasks, but the primary work product is physical — functioning, tested, assembled electronic hardware — which requires hands-on human skill that 2026 AI and robotics cannot reliably replicate. Productivity gains from AI design tools are real but insufficient to substantially reduce headcount. Confidence is medium because the mix of physical vs. digital work varies across sub-roles (R&D technician vs. production-line technician), and AI-assisted EDA tools are advancing rapidly.

title: Maschinenbautechniker, Techniker im Bereich Systemtechnik, Automation und Unternehmensprozesse
rationale: AI can assist with engineering design, drawing interpretation, data analysis, and cost estimation, but core tasks like physical assembly, on-site troubleshooting, and hands-on testing resist automation. analysis:

Reading and interpreting engineering drawings is increasingly handled by computer vision tools that can parse 2D/3D schematics and extract tolerances. 'Analyse test data' and 'record test data' are highly automatable: AI can process sensor streams, flag anomalies, and generate reports far faster than humans. Cost estimation ('prepare detailed estimates of quantities and costs') is well within the capability of AI tools trained on materials databases and historical project data. 'Liaise with engineers' involves structured technical communication that AI can partially support through documentation drafting and meeting summarization.

**Limiting factors:** The occupation's core physical dimension is highly resistant to AI substitution. On-site troubleshooting ('troubleshoot') in real machinery environments demands physical inspection, tactile feedback, and contextual judgment that AI cannot replicate remotely. Monitoring manufacturing compliance and safety standards (tasks d, e, h) requires physical presence, regulatory accountability, and real-time judgment in dynamic environments. Marine surveying and nuclear plant monitoring (optional skills) carry strict regulatory and safety certification requirements that create strong adoption barriers. The role also involves direct collaboration with engineers and tradespeople in ways that require trust, negotiation, and situational awareness.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the moderate tier (score 5). Roughly 30–40% of tasks — design support, data analysis, documentation, cost estimation — are meaningfully AI-augmented, improving individual productivity but not eliminating roles. The remaining 50–60% of core work involves physical presence, hands-on assembly, real-world troubleshooting, and regulated safety oversight that AI cannot yet substitute. The net effect is productivity amplification rather than workforce displacement, with some reduction in demand for purely documentation-focused or estimation-focused subtasks. Uncertainty arises from the heterogeneity of sub-roles (estimator vs. marine surveyor vs. automation technician), which span quite different AI exposure profiles.

title: Chemiebetriebs- und Verfahrenstechniker

title: Technische Zeichner
rationale: AI-powered CAD tools can now automate large portions of technical drawing, pattern grading, and circuit board layout tasks that form the core of this job. The breadth of sub-occupations (PCB designer, 3D printing technician, clothing CAD technician, drafter) means some niches are more exposed than others; PCB layout and pattern grading are more automatable than bespoke civil engineering drafting.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits solidly in the High tier (6–7). The score of 7 reflects that AI is a strong productivity amplifier reducing headcount needs, but full substitution is constrained by the diversity of physical domains served and the judgment required to translate real-world constraints into valid drawings. title: Poliere und Vorarbeiter

title: Forsttechniker
rationale: AI can assist with forest inventory data analysis and mapping, but the core work—planting trees, operating heavy equipment, fighting fires, and supervising physical harvesting operations—must happen outdoors in unpredictable terrain. The job is overwhelmingly physical and environmental, keeping AI exposure very low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A meaningful subset of tasks involves digital or analytical work where AI tools provide genuine assistance. Forest inventory surveys and management plan preparation using GIS/photogrammetric data (tasks b, j) can be substantially aided by AI-powered spatial analysis and remote sensing tools—LiDAR processing, satellite imagery classification, and automated stand delineation are commercially available. Monitoring logging operations and tracking forest productivity (optional skills: monitor logging operations, monitor forest productivity) can leverage drone imagery and computer vision. Reporting pollution incidents and coordinating timber sales involve document preparation where AI writing tools add efficiency. These tasks represent perhaps 15–20% of the role's time.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of core tasks are irreducibly physical and site-specific. Silvicultural operations—site preparation, planting, tending tree crops, performing tree thinning, applying herbicides—require physical presence and manual or machine operation in unstructured outdoor environments where 2026 robotics cannot operate reliably. Forest fire suppression and management demand real-time human judgment in rapidly changing, dangerous conditions. Maintaining forest roads, operating forestry equipment (chainsaws, skidders, harvesters), and supervising workers on active harvest sites are all physical supervisory roles. Regulatory compliance (apply forest legislation, environmental policy) requires contextual field judgment, not just document processing. The 'auxiliary tool rule' applies clearly: if digital tools broke, 90%+ of this job would still need to happen in the forest.

**Net assessment:** Forsttechniker sits firmly in the Low (2–3) tier. The primary work product is a physically managed forest ecosystem, not a digital artifact. AI contributes meaningfully only to the planning and monitoring subtasks, which are a small fraction of total work. No regulatory or trust barriers are even needed to explain the low score—the physical nature of the work is the binding constraint. A score of 2 reflects that some planning and data tasks benefit from AI tools, but substitution or significant productivity amplification across the core role is not plausible with 2026 technology.

title: Schiffsführer, Flugzeugpiloten und verwandte Berufe, onA
rationale: AI can assist with flight planning, weather analysis, and navigation calculations, but actually flying an aircraft or commanding a vessel still requires a licensed human in the cockpit or bridge. Strict safety regulations and the physical demands of real-time crisis management keep automation from replacing these roles.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** Flight planning, route optimization, weather interpretation, and fuel calculation are increasingly handled by AI-assisted avionics and navigation systems. Autopilot systems already manage large portions of cruise flight, and AI tools can process NOTAMs, weather data, and air traffic instructions faster than humans. For vessel commanders, electronic chart systems and collision-avoidance algorithms (ARPA/AIS) automate significant situational-awareness tasks. Administrative duties such as logbook entries, pre-flight documentation, and compliance reporting are partially automatable via digital workflows.

**Limiting factors:** Aviation and maritime law in Switzerland and internationally (ICAO, EASA, IMO) mandates a licensed human pilot or master in command for virtually all commercial operations. Real-time physical control during takeoff, landing, emergency procedures, and adverse conditions requires embodied judgment that 2026 AI cannot reliably replicate in unstructured, high-stakes environments. Passenger and cargo trust, liability frameworks, and union agreements further slow substitution. The occupation also involves crew resource management, communication with ATC/VTS, and split-second physical intervention — tasks where AI remains an assistant, not a replacement. The small Swiss employment base (967 workers) also reflects a highly specialized, licensed workforce with high barriers to entry.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). AI meaningfully augments planning, monitoring, and documentation tasks, but the core work product — safely commanding a vehicle carrying people or cargo through dynamic physical environments under strict regulatory oversight — is deeply resistant to substitution. The trajectory is toward AI as co-pilot assistant rather than replacement, consistent with current industry direction (e.g., single-pilot operations research is ongoing but not yet certified). Uncertainty exists around longer-term autonomous aviation timelines, hence medium confidence.

title: Technische Schiffsoffiziere
rationale: AI can assist with monitoring engine performance data, maintaining logs, and analyzing sensor readings, but the core work—physically inspecting, repairing, and operating ship machinery in harsh maritime environments—requires hands-on expertise AI cannot replicate. The job is fundamentally physical and safety-critical, keeping exposure low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A subset of tasks in this occupation involves structured information processing that AI tools can meaningfully assist with. Maintaining voyage logs, ordering fuel and stores, conducting financial audits, executing analytical mathematical calculations, and analyzing work-related written reports are all information-handling tasks where AI can reduce human effort. Engine performance monitoring (evaluate engine performance, detect malfunctions in engines) increasingly benefits from AI-driven predictive maintenance systems that analyze sensor data streams—this is an active deployment area in maritime IoT. Compliance documentation (ensuring vessel compliance with regulations, MARPOL, IMO conventions) can be partially automated through AI-assisted regulatory checklists. Optional skills like 'have computer literacy' and 'analyse work-related written reports' confirm a digital layer exists, but these are auxiliary to the core physical work.

**Limiting factors:** The dominant work product of a ship's engineer is the physical operation, maintenance, and emergency repair of mechanical, electrical, and electronic systems aboard a vessel—often in confined, hazardous, and unpredictable environments at sea. Tasks such as inspecting engine rooms, performing routine maintenance of ship engines, repairing vessel electrical and mechanical systems, mooring/unmooring vessels, operating sewage treatment plants, and surviving at sea in emergencies are firmly in the physical domain. Robotics capable of unstructured shipboard maintenance (tight bilge spaces, varied machinery configurations, emergency conditions) are not commercially deployed at scale as of 2026. Safety-critical regulatory requirements (STCW certification, watch-standing duties, emergency response) impose strong human-presence mandates. The role also requires real-time judgment under physical duress—extinguishing fires, preparing lifeboats—where AI has no meaningful substitution pathway.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits solidly in the Low tier (score 3). The information-processing and monitoring subtasks provide modest AI exposure above the minimal tier, but the overwhelming majority of core tasks require physical presence, hands-on mechanical skill, and safety-critical judgment aboard a vessel. Adoption barriers are high: maritime regulatory frameworks mandate certified human engineers on watch, and shipboard robotics remain immature for unstructured repair work. The score could rise modestly over a 5–10 year horizon as predictive maintenance AI and remote monitoring mature, but the 2026 frontier does not justify a higher placement.

title: Schiffsführer, nautische Schiffsoffiziere und Schiffslotsen
rationale: AI can assist with route planning, weather analysis, and navigation calculations, but physically commanding a vessel, docking in congested ports, and managing emergencies at sea require real-time human judgment and presence. Heavy regulatory accountability, physical unpredictability, and safety-critical decision-making keep AI substitution very low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A subset of tasks in this occupation are meaningfully AI-assisted. Route planning and plotting shipping navigation routes can be optimized by AI systems already integrated into modern ECDIS (Electronic Chart Display) platforms. Weather forecast analysis and use of meteorological information are well-served by AI-enhanced forecasting tools. Maintaining voyage and ship logs, composing stowage plans, and conducting analysis of ship data involve structured information processing that AI can partially automate. Cargo weight calculations and stability assessments are increasingly handled by onboard software. These information-processing subtasks represent perhaps 20–30% of the role's cognitive load and are already partially automated in modern vessels.

**Limiting factors:** The core work product of this occupation is the safe physical navigation and command of a vessel in real-world, unstructured maritime environments — not a digital artifact. Tasks such as steering vessels into ports, piloting through congested or dangerous waters, mooring and unmooring, managing crew during emergencies, and assisting in maritime rescue operations require physical presence, real-time situational awareness, and embodied judgment that 2026 robotics and AI cannot replicate at sea. Maritime pilots in particular must board moving vessels and make split-second decisions in highly variable conditions. Regulatory frameworks (STCW Convention, SOLAS, MARPOL) mandate certified human officers on the bridge; autonomous commercial shipping remains in very early trials and is nowhere near replacing licensed officers. The interpersonal dimensions — managing crew, controlling passenger behaviour in emergencies, supervising loading operations — further resist automation. Physical skills like swimming, operating life-saving appliances, and using rigging tools are entirely outside AI's reach.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). While digital navigation aids, AI-assisted route optimization, and automated logging tools provide genuine productivity support, they are auxiliary tools to a fundamentally physical, safety-critical, and heavily regulated job. The primary work product — safe vessel command in dynamic real-world environments — cannot be delegated to AI under 2026 capabilities. Autonomous shipping R&D is progressing but remains years from regulatory approval for crewed commercial routes. The score reflects meaningful AI complementarity without substitution risk.

title: Flugzeugpiloten und verwandte Berufe
rationale: AI can assist with flight planning, weather analysis, and monitoring instruments, but actually flying an aircraft requires real-time physical control, split-second judgment, and regulatory certification that AI cannot replace in 2026. Safety-critical liability, certification requirements, and passenger trust keep human pilots firmly in the cockpit.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** Several peripheral tasks are meaningfully AI-assisted. Flight plan preparation and weather briefing (ESCO: 'create a flight plan,' 'analyse weather forecast,' 'aviation meteorology') are increasingly automated through digital tools and AI-enhanced dispatch systems. Instrument monitoring and anomaly detection ('evaluate engine performance,' 'inspect aircraft for airworthiness,' 'operate cockpit control panels') benefit from AI co-pilot systems like Airbus's autopilot enhancements and predictive maintenance algorithms. Data collection tasks ('collect data using GPS,' 'operate GPS systems,' 'global navigation satellite system performance parameters') are largely automated already. Administrative tasks such as 'signing necessary certificates' and 'maintaining official records of flight' have digital equivalents. Autopilot systems already handle the majority of cruise-phase flying, and AI-assisted landing systems are in deployment at major airports.

**Limiting factors:** The core work product — safely transporting passengers through dynamic, unstructured physical environments — is deeply resistant to AI substitution in 2026. Regulatory frameworks (ESCO: 'civil aviation regulations,' 'common aviation safety regulations,' 'ensure compliance with civil aviation regulations') mandate licensed human pilots in command for all commercial operations, with no credible regulatory pathway to full autonomy in the near term. The physical and cognitive demands of takeoff, landing, emergency response, and real-time decision-making under novel conditions ('apply knowledge of principles and practices of flying to identify and solve problems') require embodied situational awareness that current AI cannot reliably replicate in edge cases. Passenger trust and liability frameworks further anchor human presence. The inclusion of aircraft maintenance engineering in this ISCO group adds hands-on physical inspection and repair tasks ('disassemble engines,' 'repair engines,' 'use power tools') that are firmly in the low-automation tier.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits solidly in the Low tier (score 3). AI meaningfully assists with planning, monitoring, and administrative subtasks — perhaps 20–30% of total task time — but the primary work product (safe physical operation of an aircraft) is protected by physical complexity, regulatory mandate, and safety-critical accountability. The trajectory toward autonomous commercial aviation exists but is measured in decades, not years. The score would be lower (2) were it not for the genuine AI augmentation already present in modern cockpits and the administrative/planning subtasks that are substantially automated.

title: Flugverkehrslotsen
rationale: AI can assist with flight plan analysis, weather data processing, and conflict detection, but directing live aircraft in real time requires split-second human judgment under life-or-death conditions. Strict safety regulation, liability, and the irreversible consequences of error keep human controllers central for the foreseeable future.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** Several tasks in this role involve structured information processing that AI handles well in 2026. Examining and approving flight plans, compiling meteorological information, carrying out navigational calculations, and coordinating flight schedules are largely data-driven tasks where AI decision-support tools are already deployed (e.g., SESAR and NextGen automation initiatives). Conflict-detection algorithms and trajectory prediction systems can flag potential collisions faster than humans. Skills like 'analyse work-related written reports,' 'prepare financial auditing reports,' and 'conduct aviation auditing' are information-work tasks where AI tools provide substantial productivity gains. The sub-occupation of flight operations officer, which focuses on compiling dispatch data and flight information, is the most AI-exposed component of this ISCO group.

**Limiting factors:** The core work product of an air traffic controller is not a document or a data artifact — it is the real-time, continuous safe separation of aircraft in dynamic, high-stakes airspace. Skills like 'make time-critical decisions,' 'handle stressful situations,' 'maintain concentration for long periods,' 'stay alert,' and 'initiate and organize emergency, search and rescue services' describe a role where errors are catastrophic and irreversible. Aviation is among the most heavily regulated industries globally; ICAO standards, civil aviation authorities, and national law require licensed human controllers in the loop. The 'apply knowledge of principles and practices of air traffic control to identify and solve problems' task involves genuinely novel, ambiguous, multi-variable situations (weather deviations, equipment failures, medical emergencies) that current AI agents cannot reliably handle without human oversight. Physical and cognitive demands — spatial awareness, reading 3D radar displays, simultaneous radio communication across multiple frequencies — further resist full automation. Trust and legal accountability are non-negotiable barriers.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). AI is already a productivity-amplifying co-pilot — conflict alerts, automated weather feeds, schedule optimization — but it is not substituting for the human controller. The aviation industry's safety culture, regulatory framework, and the catastrophic downside risk of AI failure create adoption barriers that will persist well beyond 2026. The score is slightly above the floor because the flight operations officer and aviation inspector sub-occupations within this ISCO group have meaningfully higher information-work content, and AI decision-support tools are genuinely reshaping the cognitive load of the role. Uncertainty is moderate because the pace of SESAR/NextGen automation investment could accelerate AI integration faster than regulatory frameworks adapt.

title: Flugsicherungstechniker
rationale: AI can assist with software debugging, documentation, and monitoring data analysis, but the core work—physically installing, calibrating, and repairing safety-critical navigation hardware—requires hands-on technical skill in regulated environments. Strict aviation safety certification requirements and the physical nature of the work keep AI exposure low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A subset of tasks in this occupation involve information processing that AI tools can meaningfully assist with. Developing, modifying, and debugging system software (task f) is partially addressable by 2026 agentic coding tools. Preparing cost estimates and technical specifications (task c) involves document generation where AI can accelerate drafting. Monitoring and surveillance data analysis—reviewing outputs from communication, navigation, and surveillance (CNS) equipment—could be augmented by AI anomaly detection. Optional skills like 'debug software,' 'design control systems,' 'design electrical systems,' and 'use technical drawing software' represent digitally-mediated subtasks where AI copilots add productivity. These collectively represent perhaps 20–25% of the role's task content.

**Limiting factors:** The dominant work product is physical and safety-critical: installing, calibrating, maintaining, and repairing ground-based air navigation hardware (tasks b, d, g, h). Skills like 'install electrical and electronic equipment,' 'maintain electronic equipment,' 'use testing equipment,' 'test circuitry,' and 'calibrate ground-based navigation systems' require hands-on manipulation in real-world environments—precisely where 2026 robotics remain far below human capability. Aviation is among the most heavily regulated industries globally; essential skills include 'air transport law,' 'common aviation safety regulations,' 'ensure aircraft compliance with regulation,' and 'safety engineering.' Every intervention must be certified by a qualified human technician. Regulatory frameworks (ICAO, EASA, BAZL in Switzerland) explicitly require human accountability for safety-critical systems, creating a hard institutional barrier to AI substitution. The physical environment—airport infrastructure, radar installations, antenna arrays—is unstructured and variable, further limiting robotic or autonomous AI action.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). The primary work product is physical transformation and maintenance of safety-critical hardware in a heavily regulated environment, not digital artifacts. AI tools offer genuine but modest productivity gains on the software and documentation subtasks, but cannot substitute for the hands-on calibration, installation, and certified repair work that defines the role. The combination of physical environment demands, aviation safety certification requirements, and the relatively small share of purely information-processing tasks places this occupation well below the moderate threshold.

title: Medizintechniker im Bereich bildgebende Verfahren und Therapiegeräte
rationale: AI can assist with image quality checks and pattern recognition in scans, but the core job requires physically positioning patients, operating complex equipment, and monitoring patient reactions in real time. The hands-on clinical environment and patient safety responsibilities keep AI in a supporting role.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** Several tasks in this occupation have meaningful AI support available by 2026. Image quality review (task d — evaluating X-rays and computer-generated images for diagnostic adequacy) is increasingly assisted by AI tools that flag suboptimal acquisitions. Radiation dosage recording and calculation (task f) is largely computational and amenable to automation. Medical informatics and e-health technologies (optional skills) are already AI-augmented in clinical workflows. AI-assisted image interpretation tools (optional skill: interpret medical images) are commercially deployed for CT, MRI, and mammography, though in this role the technician assesses image quality rather than making diagnoses — a narrower task. Documentation and data management (manage healthcare users' data) can be substantially automated.

**Limiting factors:** The dominant share of this role is irreducibly physical and interpersonal. Positioning patients — including those who are elderly, in pain, or uncooperative — requires tactile judgment and real-time adaptation that 2026 robotics cannot replicate in unstructured clinical environments. Explaining procedures, ensuring patient comfort and safety, and monitoring patient reactions (tasks b, e) demand human presence and communication. Operating breathing equipment, heart-lung machines, and resuscitation (essential skills) are high-stakes physical interventions with zero tolerance for AI error and strong regulatory requirements for certified human oversight. Compliance with healthcare legislation and radiation safety procedures creates accountability structures that mandate human responsibility. The multidisciplinary team context requires real-time interpersonal coordination.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the moderate tier (score 4). AI augments peripheral tasks — documentation, dosage calculation, image quality flagging — but the core work product is the safe, technically correct acquisition of diagnostic images from a live patient in a clinical setting. Physical manipulation, patient monitoring, and safety-critical interventions anchor the role firmly in human hands. Adoption barriers (regulatory certification, patient safety liability, unstructured physical environment) further slow displacement. The score could rise modestly if autonomous imaging positioning systems mature, but that remains beyond the 2026 frontier.

title: Pharmazeutische Techniker und Assistenten
rationale: AI can automate prescription verification, inventory tracking, and insurance claim processing, but dispensing physical medications, counselling patients face-to-face, and handling emergency situations require human presence. The job is a hybrid of regulated physical tasks and information work, keeping exposure moderate.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A meaningful portion of this role involves information-processing tasks that 2026 AI handles well. Prescription verification ('check information on prescriptions'), maintaining pharmaceutical records ('maintain pharmaceutical records', 'manage healthcare users' data'), processing insurance claims ('process medical insurance claims'), inventory management ('take pharmaceutical inventory', 'manage the logistics of medicinal products'), and preparing prescription labels are all digitally mediated tasks where AI-assisted or fully automated workflows already exist in pharmacy management systems. Agentic tools can cross-check drug interactions, flag expiry dates, and auto-generate reorder requests. The optional skills listing 'pharmacokinetics' and 'pharmaceutical chemistry' as knowledge domains suggests some analytical work that AI can increasingly support. E-health integration ('use e-health and mobile health technologies') further embeds this role in digital infrastructure susceptible to automation.

**Limiting factors:** The core physical work — filling and labelling containers, transferring medication, maintaining storage conditions, cleaning equipment — requires dexterous manipulation in a regulated environment that robotics cannot yet reliably perform at scale in Swiss community pharmacies. Patient-facing tasks ('empathise with the healthcare user', 'communicate effectively in healthcare', 'deal with emergency care situations', 'refer healthcare users') demand interpersonal judgment and trust that AI cannot substitute in 2026. Swiss healthcare regulation imposes strict accountability requirements ('accept own accountability', 'comply with legislation related to health care', 'follow clinical guidelines'), meaning a licensed human must remain in the loop for dispensing decisions. The role also involves counselling on self-medication and providing pharmaceutical advice — tasks where liability and patient safety create strong adoption barriers for autonomous AI.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the boundary between Tier 3 and Tier 4. Roughly 30–40% of tasks — prescription data processing, inventory, insurance claims, record-keeping — are meaningfully AI-automatable today, but the physical dispensing workflow, regulated patient interaction, and safety accountability anchor the role in moderate exposure. Productivity amplification (fewer technicians needed per pharmacy due to AI-assisted back-office work) is a real risk, but full substitution is blocked by physical, regulatory, and interpersonal constraints. Score: 4. Confidence is medium because the sub-occupation split (pharmacy technician vs. pharmacy assistant) creates heterogeneity — assistants skew more physical/interpersonal, technicians more toward compounding and analytical tasks.

title: Medizinische und zahnmedizinische Prothetiktechniker
rationale: AI can assist with design software and digital scanning for prosthetics and dental devices, but the core work—physically fabricating, fitting, and adjusting custom devices on real patients—requires hands-on skill that robots cannot yet reliably replicate. The job stays low-exposure because most value is delivered through physical craftsmanship and patient interaction.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A meaningful subset of tasks in this occupation involves information processing and design that AI tools can assist with. Interpreting prescriptions and specifications, archiving healthcare records, maintaining client prescription records, and using e-health technologies are all digitally mediated tasks where AI can reduce cognitive load or automate documentation. CAD/CAM systems already automate significant portions of dental prosthesis design (crowns, bridges, dentures), and digital scanning has largely replaced manual impression-taking in advanced labs. AI-assisted design tools for orthotic and prosthetic devices are commercially available and growing. These design and documentation tasks represent perhaps 20–30% of total work effort.

**Limiting factors:** The dominant share of this occupation's value is delivered through physical fabrication and patient-facing fitting. Tasks such as bending, forming, and shaping thermoplastic and metal materials; manipulating dental materials; polishing and repairing prostheses; and physically fitting and adjusting devices to individual patients all require dexterous manipulation in unstructured or semi-structured environments. Current robotics (as of 2026) cannot reliably perform the fine motor tasks involved in custom prosthetic fabrication or the iterative, tactile adjustment process of fitting a device to a patient's body. Patient interaction tasks—examining and measuring patients, instructing them on device use, responding to comfort complaints—require interpersonal judgment and physical presence. Regulatory frameworks in healthcare also impose accountability requirements that slow AI/robotic adoption. The sub-occupation of audiology technician has somewhat higher digital exposure (hearing aid programming is increasingly software-driven), but even there, ear mould production and fitting remain physical.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). While CAD/CAM and digital scanning have already transformed parts of dental lab work, the physical fabrication, fitting, and patient-interaction core of the role resists AI substitution at the 2026 frontier. The occupation is analogous to a skilled tradesperson who uses digital tools but whose primary output is a physical artifact requiring hands-on expertise. Confidence is medium because the dental technician sub-occupation (more lab-based, less patient-facing) is somewhat more exposed than the prosthetist-orthotist sub-occupation, creating heterogeneity within the group.

title: Fachkräfte Gesundheit und Pflege (mittlere Stufe)
rationale: AI can help with documentation, scheduling, and basic patient monitoring alerts, but the core work—hands-on patient care, wound treatment, and clinical assessments—requires physical presence and human judgment. The job stays largely human because most tasks involve direct bodily care that robots cannot yet reliably perform.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** Administrative and documentation tasks—recording patient data, updating care plans, and reporting to supervising physicians—are meaningfully AI-assisted in 2026. Clinical decision-support tools can flag abnormal vital signs, suggest medication interactions, and auto-populate structured health records, reducing the cognitive load of routine monitoring. Scheduling, care coordination, and patient triage questionnaires are increasingly handled by AI-assisted workflows in Swiss healthcare settings. These tasks likely represent 25–35% of daily working time for mid-level health and care workers.

**Limiting factors:** The dominant work product of ISCO 3221 is direct patient care: administering injections and medications, performing wound care, assisting with mobility and hygiene, and conducting physical assessments. These tasks require dexterous manipulation in unstructured environments (patient rooms, homes, clinics) where 2026 robotics remain far below human capability. Therapeutic relationships, patient communication, and real-time clinical judgment under uncertainty—especially with elderly, pediatric, or cognitively impaired patients—are strongly human-anchored. Swiss healthcare regulation also imposes strict accountability requirements that slow AI substitution in clinical roles. The physical and interpersonal core of the job is highly resistant to automation.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the lower end of the Moderate tier (score 4). AI tools are genuinely useful for documentation and monitoring support, and adoption is accelerating in Swiss hospitals and care facilities. However, because the primary work product is physical and relational care rather than information processing, AI functions as a productivity complement rather than a substitute. The occupation is unlikely to see significant workforce reduction from AI in the near term; the main effect is reduced administrative burden per worker.

title: Fachkräfte Geburtshilfe (mittlere Stufe)
rationale: AI cannot deliver babies, physically examine newborns, or manage emergency complications during childbirth. The core work is hands-on physical care and real-time clinical judgment, leaving very little for AI to replace.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A subset of advisory and informational tasks — advising on family planning, contraception, nutrition, breastfeeding, and prenatal health education — could be partially supported by AI tools that generate patient-facing content or decision-support prompts. Monitoring basic patient signs could eventually be augmented by wearable sensors and AI-driven alerts. Documentation and care-plan communication with nursing staff are also partially automatable. These tasks, however, represent the peripheral rather than the core of the role.

**Limiting factors:** The primary work product of this occupation is physical: attending births, performing hands-on examinations of newborns, assessing fetal and maternal progress through direct observation and palpation, and managing emergency care situations in real time. Skills such as 'childbirth,' 'examine the new-born infant,' 'deal with emergency care situations,' 'assist on pregnancy abnormality,' and 'provide postnatal care' all require physical presence, tactile judgment, and immediate human response. Robotics capable of unstructured, delicate physical care in clinical settings remain far below deployment readiness in 2026. Additionally, the deep interpersonal and emotional dimensions — empathising with the woman's family, active listening, supporting patients through one of the most vulnerable moments of their lives — are strongly resistant to AI substitution due to trust and safety requirements. Swiss healthcare regulation further constrains autonomous AI action in clinical settings.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). The vast majority of core tasks are physical, interpersonal, and safety-critical, with regulatory and trust barriers reinforcing human necessity. AI offers modest productivity support for documentation and patient education content, but cannot substitute for the hands-on clinical and emotional labor that defines the role. The score reflects demonstrated 2026 AI capabilities, not speculative future robotics.

title: Fachkräfte in traditioneller und komplementärer Medizin
rationale: AI can support documentation, patient intake, and health education content, but the core work—physical treatments like acupuncture, manipulation, and herbal therapy—requires hands-on contact with patients. The job is anchored in physical, interpersonal, and trust-based care that AI cannot replicate.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A subset of tasks in this occupation are meaningfully AI-assisted. Collecting and maintaining healthcare user data, providing health education, advising on nutrition and lifestyle, and follow-up communication are all information-processing or communication tasks where AI tools (chatbots, documentation assistants, patient management software) can reduce workload. The 'study topics' skill and research-oriented optional skills (employing foreign languages for health-related research) are highly AI-performable. Scheduling, stock monitoring, and applying organisational techniques are also automatable via existing software. These peripheral and administrative tasks may represent 20–30% of total work time.

**Limiting factors:** The primary work product of this occupation is a physical, embodied therapeutic intervention—acupuncture needling, bone manipulation, cupping, herbal preparation and administration, shiatsu, reiki, and similar hands-on treatments. These require physical presence, fine motor skill, and real-time sensory feedback that 2026 robotics cannot replicate in unstructured clinical settings. Beyond the physical dimension, the therapeutic relationship is central: empathising with the healthcare user, developing collaborative therapeutic relationships, and active listening are interpersonal competencies where AI substitution is minimal in practice. Trust is especially salient in complementary and traditional medicine, where patients often seek a human practitioner specifically for cultural, spiritual, or relational reasons. Regulatory and liability frameworks in Switzerland further constrain autonomous AI involvement in any patient-facing clinical role.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). The core deliverable is a physical, trust-intensive therapeutic encounter, not a digital artifact. AI can assist with documentation, patient communication, and research, but these are auxiliary to the hands-on work. The heterogeneity of sub-occupations (from acupuncture technician to herbalist to bonesetter) introduces some uncertainty, as more consultation-heavy roles may see slightly higher exposure than purely manual ones, but none approach the threshold where AI could substitute for the primary work product.

title: Veterinärmedizinische Fachkräfte und Assistenten
rationale: AI can assist with diagnostic imaging analysis, record-keeping, and monitoring data, but the core work—physically restraining animals, assisting in surgery, administering anaesthetics, and hands-on patient care—requires direct physical presence. The job is anchored in physical, hands-on animal care that AI cannot replicate.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A meaningful subset of tasks in this occupation involve information processing that AI tools can support. Diagnostic imaging support ('support veterinary diagnostic imaging procedures') is increasingly aided by AI-powered radiology tools that flag anomalies. Clinical record-keeping ('maintain veterinary clinical records,' 'maintain administrative records') is highly automatable via AI-assisted documentation. Monitoring hospitalised animals ('monitor condition of hospitalised animals') can be partially augmented by sensor-based AI systems that track vitals and flag deterioration. Advising on animal welfare and interpreting signs of illness could be partially supported by AI decision-support tools. These tasks collectively represent perhaps 20–25% of the role's time.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of core tasks are irreducibly physical and hands-on. Restraining animals, preparing them for surgery and anaesthesia, assisting as a scrub nurse, administering fluids, performing dental procedures, collecting samples, and producing radiographs all require physical dexterity in unstructured, unpredictable environments with live animals. Robotics in 2026 cannot reliably handle the fine manipulation and real-time adaptation required when working with stressed or sedated animals of varying sizes. Regulatory and safety requirements ('animal welfare legislation,' 'apply safe work practices in a veterinary setting') further constrain autonomous AI action. The interpersonal dimension—dealing with distressed animal owners ('deal with challenging people,' 'interview animal owners')—also resists automation. Physical biosecurity management and infection control are procedural and embodied tasks.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the moderate tier (score 4). AI provides genuine but peripheral support in documentation, imaging analysis, and monitoring, but the primary work product is physical animal care delivered in person. The role is structurally similar to a human nursing assistant—AI augments but cannot substitute for the hands-on, safety-critical, and physically variable core. Adoption barriers (animal unpredictability, regulatory oversight, physical environment) are substantial. The score would only rise meaningfully if veterinary robotics advanced significantly beyond current capabilities.

title: Zahnmedizinische Assistenten und Dentalhygieniker
rationale: AI cannot clean teeth, remove calculus, administer anaesthesia, or assist during physical dental procedures — the core of this job is hands-on clinical work inside a patient's mouth. The small administrative and educational components offer minor AI assistance, but physical presence dominates the role.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A subset of tasks in this occupation involve information processing that AI can meaningfully assist with. Dental charting and radiograph interpretation benefit from AI-assisted image analysis tools already in clinical deployment. Administrative tasks — appointment scheduling, billing records, post-treatment documentation, and patient education materials — are largely digitisable. Counselling on nutrition and oral hygiene could be partially supported by AI-generated content or chatbots. The optional skills cluster (appointment administration, billing records, payment handling) is almost entirely automatable. These tasks collectively represent perhaps 15–25% of working time.

**Limiting factors:** The dominant share of this occupation's work is irreducibly physical and interpersonal. Core clinical tasks — supra- and sub-gingival scaling, calculus and plaque removal, polishing restorations, applying fluoride and antibacterial substances, taking physical impressions, passing instruments, preparing and sterilising equipment, and assisting dentists during procedures — require fine manual dexterity in a confined, variable, and sensitive anatomical environment. Robotic dentistry exists in research settings but is nowhere near replacing chairside assistants or hygienists in 2026. Managing patient anxiety, developing therapeutic relationships, and responding to emergencies require real-time human judgment and physical presence. Regulatory frameworks in Switzerland and elsewhere require licensed professionals to perform clinical procedures, creating hard adoption barriers. The 'auxiliary tool rule' applies clearly: if the computer broke, 90%+ of this job would still need to happen in person.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low (2–3) tier. The primary work product is physical oral health care delivered through manual clinical skill, not a digital artifact. AI offers genuine but peripheral assistance in documentation, scheduling, and patient education — insufficient to materially reduce headcount or reshape the core role. The score of 3 (rather than 2) reflects the non-trivial administrative component and the emerging role of AI in radiograph analysis, which may modestly reduce time spent on diagnostic support tasks over the coming years.

title: Fachkräfte im Bereich medizinische Dokumentation und Information
rationale: AI can already automate clinical coding, transcription, records review, and data extraction — the core outputs of this job. Human oversight, compliance accountability, and edge-case judgment keep the score from reaching the highest tier.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The core work of this occupation is almost entirely information processing on structured and semi-structured data. Clinical coding (translating narrative diagnoses into ICD/SNOMED codes) is already being automated by NLP-based tools with demonstrated accuracy at or above junior coder level. Transcribing and compiling patient records, processing admission/discharge documents, collecting statistics on medical records, and managing digital archives are all tasks where 2026 AI — including large-context LLMs and agentic document-processing pipelines — performs at acceptable quality. The 'process data,' 'document management,' 'manage healthcare users' data,' and 'use electronic health records management system' skills confirm the work is conducted entirely in digital environments. Optional knowledge items (Cisco, ICT system user requirements, develop ICT workflow) further confirm a digital-native work context. AI-assisted coding tools (e.g., 3M, Nuance, Optum) are already in production deployment in Swiss and European hospitals.

**Limiting factors:** Several tasks resist full automation. Reviewing records for completeness, accuracy, and regulatory compliance requires contextual judgment that AI still makes errors on, particularly for ambiguous or incomplete clinical narratives. Protecting confidentiality and releasing information to authorized parties involves legal accountability that regulators currently require a human to own. Supervising clerical staff, recruiting employees, and managing healthcare staff are interpersonal management tasks with low AI substitutability. Waiting list coordination involves real-time negotiation with patients and surgical teams. Swiss healthcare data protection law (nDSG/GDPR alignment) creates compliance friction that slows full automation. The multicultural and multidisciplinary communication skills also reflect a human-facing coordination role that AI cannot fully replace.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits solidly in the High tier (6–7). The majority of routine, high-volume tasks — coding, transcription, data entry, archive management — are automatable with current tools, meaning fewer workers are needed per unit of output. However, the occupation retains meaningful human value in compliance oversight, exception handling, staff supervision, and accountability for data security decisions. The heterogeneity of sub-occupations (from medical records clerk, which approaches a 9, to medical records manager, which sits closer to a 5) introduces some uncertainty, justifying medium confidence and a score of 7 as the central estimate.

title: Fachkräfte in der öffentlichen Gesundheitsfürsorge
rationale: AI can help draft health education materials, maintain records, and support data collection, but the core work—visiting families at home, building trust in underserved communities, and navigating crises in person—requires physical presence and human relationship. The job is too interpersonal and field-based to be substantially automated.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** Several peripheral tasks are meaningfully AI-assisted. Maintaining records of work with service users, collecting and reporting household data, and drafting health education content (nutrition, hygiene, immunization guidance) are tasks where AI tools can reduce time and improve consistency. AI chatbots and decision-support tools can assist with 'apply decision making within social work' and 'review social service plan' by surfacing relevant protocols or flagging risk indicators. Optional skills like 'contribute to public health campaigns' and 'deliver group sessions on nutrition' have AI-generatable content components. These information-processing and documentation tasks likely represent 25–35% of total work effort.

**Limiting factors:** The primary work product of this occupation is not a digital artifact—it is a trusted human relationship and a physical presence in communities that are specifically defined by their reluctance or inability to access formal health systems. Tasks (b), (c), (d), and (e) in the ISCO description all require in-person home visits, outreach in unstructured community environments, and hands-on distribution of medical supplies. Essential skills like 'relate empathetically,' 'build helping relationship with social service users,' 'deliver social services in diverse cultural communities,' and 'manage social crisis' are deeply interpersonal and context-dependent. The population served—families in crisis, people avoiding medical establishments, marginalized groups—requires human trust-building that AI cannot replicate. Regulatory and safeguarding requirements in the Swiss social sector further constrain autonomous AI action.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the low-to-moderate boundary (score 4). AI provides genuine productivity gains in documentation, content generation, and data reporting, but these are auxiliary to the core mission of in-person community health outreach. The physical, relational, and culturally sensitive nature of the work anchors it well below the high-exposure tier. Uncertainty arises from the heterogeneity of roles within this group—a community health worker doing primarily administrative coordination faces higher exposure than one doing daily home visits—which justifies medium confidence.

title: Augenoptiker
rationale: AI can help interpret prescriptions and manage client records, but the core work—physically fitting frames, adjusting eyeglasses, cutting and verifying lenses—requires hands-on manipulation that robots cannot yet reliably perform. The job is anchored in physical craftsmanship and face-to-face client interaction, keeping AI exposure low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A subset of tasks are meaningfully AI-assisted. Interpreting optical prescriptions and preparing work orders for optical laboratories involves structured data (numerical prescriptions, lens parameters) that AI can parse and cross-check with high accuracy. Maintaining client prescription records and managing healthcare users' data are straightforward information-management tasks well within 2026 AI capability. Advising customers on eyewear selection and contact lens maintenance could be partially supported by AI recommendation engines or chatbots, and the use of e-health and mobile health technologies (an essential skill) signals an existing digital interface. Operating a lensometer and verifying lens compliance involve measurement data that AI vision systems could assist in reading and flagging deviations. These tasks collectively represent perhaps 20–25% of core work time.

**Limiting factors:** The dominant share of this occupation's output is physical and interpersonal. Adjusting eyeglasses to fit individual customers requires fine manual dexterity and real-time tactile feedback—tasks where 2026 robotics remain far below human proficiency in unstructured, person-specific environments. Cutting lenses, performing frame repairs, and repairing lenses are skilled craft operations on small, precise components. Taking facial and eye measurements for fitting requires spatial judgment and patient interaction that current computer vision and robotic systems cannot reliably replicate in a retail/clinical setting. The therapeutic relationship dimension—empathising with healthcare users, dealing with emergency care situations, working in multidisciplinary health teams—adds a regulated, trust-based interpersonal layer. Swiss healthcare regulation (compliance with health care legislation, quality standards of ophthalmic equipment) further slows automation adoption. The optional skills list is notably low in software tools, confirming the work is not primarily conducted in a digital environment.

**Net assessment:** Dispensing opticians sit firmly in the Low tier (score 3). The primary work product is a correctly fitted, physically adjusted optical appliance delivered through skilled manual work and client-facing consultation—not a digital artifact. AI provides genuine but peripheral assistance in record-keeping, prescription parsing, and possibly product recommendation. The physical craftsmanship core, regulatory environment, and direct patient-contact requirements create strong barriers to substitution. The score of 3 (upper end of Low) rather than 2 reflects the non-trivial administrative and data-management component that AI can meaningfully absorb, and the plausible near-term use of AI-assisted measurement tools.

title: Physiotherapeutische Techniker und Assistenten
rationale: AI cannot perform hands-on massage, manual therapy, or physical rehabilitation exercises with patients — the core work is physical touch and in-person movement guidance. The job is deeply embodied and interpersonal, keeping AI exposure low despite some administrative and documentation tasks being automatable.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A meaningful subset of tasks in this occupation involve information processing that AI can assist with. Monitoring and recording patient progress, managing healthcare user data, documenting treatment plans, and scheduling (administer appointments) are all digitally mediated and partially automatable with 2026 AI tools. AI can also support clinical decision-making by surfacing contraindications, suggesting exercise protocols, or generating discharge plans based on patient data — relevant to skills like 'develop plans related to client discharge,' 'record healthcare users' progress related to treatment,' and 'follow clinical guidelines.' E-health and mobile health technologies (explicitly listed as a skill) are increasingly AI-augmented, and AI-powered apps can guide patients through relaxation or breathing exercises independently. These administrative and informational subtasks may represent 15–25% of working time.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of core tasks are irreducibly physical and interpersonal. Administering massage therapy, performing shiatsu, applying pressure point therapy, fitting orthopaedic braces, guiding patients through physical exercises, and operating electrotherapy or ultrasound equipment all require physical presence, tactile judgment, and real-time adaptation to a patient's body. Robotics capable of replicating therapeutic massage or manual rehabilitation at clinical quality does not exist at scale in 2026. The therapeutic relationship — empathising with healthcare users, developing collaborative trust, motivating patients during painful rehabilitation — is a core competency that AI cannot substitute. Regulatory frameworks in Switzerland (and broadly in healthcare) impose strict accountability requirements on licensed practitioners, further limiting autonomous AI action. The animal therapy and veterinary sub-occupations add additional physical and unpredictable-environment constraints.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). The primary work product is physical therapeutic intervention delivered through human hands and presence — not a digital artifact. AI can automate peripheral documentation and scheduling tasks and may augment clinical decision support, but cannot replace the hands-on core. The score of 3 (rather than 1–2) reflects the genuine administrative automation potential and the growing role of AI-assisted patient monitoring tools, but the physical and interpersonal anchors prevent meaningful displacement of the human worker.

title: Medizinische Assistenten
rationale: AI can automate appointment scheduling, insurance billing, and medical record entry, but the core job involves hands-on patient care — taking blood, giving injections, and assisting during examinations — that requires physical presence. The majority of tasks are clinical and interpersonal, keeping AI exposure moderate.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A meaningful subset of this role is administrative and information-processing work that AI can handle well. Tasks (i) and (j) — recording patient history, scheduling appointments, preparing billing and insurance documentation, and managing medical records — are already heavily automated by EHR systems and AI-assisted coding tools. 'Process medical insurance claims,' 'health records management,' 'ensure proper appointment administration,' and 'maintain treatment records' are all digitally native tasks where AI agents and RPA tools can handle routine instances. 'Provide prescription and drug refill information to pharmacies' and 'provide test results to medical staff' are also largely information-routing tasks amenable to automation. These administrative functions may represent 25–35% of actual working time in a typical Swiss Arztpraxis setting.

**Limiting factors:** The dominant share of this occupation is irreducibly physical and interpersonal. Tasks (b) through (e) — assisting with examinations, measuring vital signs, administering medications, giving injections, removing sutures, sterilizing instruments, collecting blood and tissue specimens — require dexterous physical manipulation in unstructured clinical environments. 2026 robotics cannot reliably perform venepuncture, wound closure, or specimen handling at acceptable clinical quality outside highly controlled settings. Beyond the physical, tasks (a), (c), (f), and (g) involve direct patient interaction requiring empathy, trust-building, and real-time adaptation to anxious or vulnerable individuals — captured in skills like 'empathise with the healthcare user,' 'develop a collaborative therapeutic relationship,' and 'deal with emergency care situations.' Swiss healthcare regulation and patient safety requirements also impose strong adoption barriers for autonomous AI in direct clinical contact roles.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the lower end of moderate exposure (score 4). AI will increasingly handle the administrative tail — scheduling, billing, record entry — and may assist with clinical decision support and documentation via voice-to-text EHR tools. However, the primary work product is physical patient care delivered in person, which is firmly AI-resistant at the 2026 frontier. Productivity gains from AI on the administrative side are real but insufficient to substantially reduce headcount, as the clinical tasks that dominate working time cannot be delegated to AI. The score reflects genuine but bounded automation of a supporting function within a predominantly hands-on role.

title: Kontrolleure und Beauftragte in den Bereichen Umwelt- und Arbeitsmedizin sowie verwandte Berufe
rationale: AI can assist with writing inspection reports, analysing regulatory documents, and flagging compliance gaps, but the core work requires physical site visits, direct observation, and sample collection that AI cannot perform. The job's irreducible reliance on in-person inspection keeps exposure moderate.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A meaningful portion of this occupation's peripheral and administrative tasks are AI-performable at 2026 capability levels. Writing inspection reports and routine reports, preparing audit documentation, analysing regulatory texts (environmental legislation, food safety standards, HACCP frameworks), monitoring legislation developments, and preparing visual data are all information-processing tasks where LLMs and document-analysis tools already perform at professional quality. Risk analysis and performing inspection analysis — to the extent these involve synthesising written records, lab results, and regulatory checklists — are also substantially AI-assistable. The extensive list of optional skills referencing data analysis (analyse environmental data, apply control process statistical methods, analyse work-related written reports) further confirms a digital-work layer that AI tools can accelerate significantly. Agentic tools could plausibly automate much of the pre-inspection research, post-inspection report drafting, and regulatory cross-referencing that currently consumes inspector time.

**Limiting factors:** The primary work product of this occupation is not a document — it is a compliance determination reached through physical presence. Tasks such as inspecting places of work, collecting samples for analysis, conducting workplace audits, identifying hazards in the workplace, conducting environmental surveys, and working in cold environments all require the inspector to be physically on-site, using sensory judgment (smell, sight, tactile assessment of equipment) that 2026 robotics and computer vision cannot reliably replicate in unstructured, varied industrial environments. Interviewing workers and employers (conduct research interview, interview techniques) to uncover unreported practices or accidents requires interpersonal trust and adaptive questioning that AI agents handle poorly in adversarial or sensitive contexts. Issuing penalties, maintaining relationships with government agencies, and advocating for consumer matters involve legal authority and institutional accountability that regulators are unlikely to delegate to AI systems. Regulatory and liability frameworks in Switzerland and the EU further constrain autonomous AI action in enforcement roles.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the moderate tier (score 4). AI meaningfully reduces the administrative and analytical burden — perhaps 30–40% of time spent on documentation, regulatory research, and report writing — but cannot substitute for the physical inspection, sample collection, and enforcement authority that constitute the core deliverable. The occupation is more complemented than substituted: inspectors using AI tools may handle larger caseloads, but the role itself is not at risk of significant workforce reduction in the near term. Uncertainty arises from the heterogeneity of sub-occupations (food safety vs. industrial waste vs. transport), where the digital-to-physical work ratio varies somewhat.

title: Rettungsdienstpersonal
rationale: AI cannot perform the core work of paramedics: physically reaching patients, administering injections, intubating airways, defibrillating, or carrying people to ambulances. AI may assist with dispatch routing or documentation, but the job is overwhelmingly hands-on emergency care in unpredictable environments.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The most AI-amenable tasks in this occupation are peripheral and administrative. 'Write reports on emergency cases' and 'manage healthcare users' data' involve documentation that AI can assist with via voice-to-text and auto-population of medical records. 'Medical dispatch' and 'operate an emergency communication system' have AI-assisted triage tools (e.g., priority scoring algorithms) already in partial deployment. 'Monitor patient's vital signs' benefits from AI-enhanced sensor interpretation. 'Provide health education' and 'inform policy makers on health-related challenges' involve information transfer that AI could partially support. Collectively, these represent perhaps 10–15% of core task time.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of this occupation's essential skills are irreducibly physical and situational. Tasks such as 'intubation,' 'defibrillation,' 'intravenous infusion,' 'immobilise patients for emergency intervention,' 'drive ambulance under emergency conditions,' 'transfer patients to and from ambulance vehicles,' and 'employ specific paramedic techniques in out-of-hospital care' require dexterous manipulation of a human body in chaotic, unstructured real-world environments — precisely where 2026 robotics remain far below human capability. 'Cope with blood,' 'tolerate stress,' 'deal with emergency care situations,' and 'maintain order at scenes of accidents' reflect the irreducibly human judgment and physical presence demanded in life-threatening, rapidly evolving scenarios. Regulatory and patient-safety frameworks in Switzerland and internationally require licensed human practitioners for all invasive procedures. The physical environment (accident scenes, private homes, public spaces) is maximally unstructured, ruling out near-term robotic substitution.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 2). The primary work product is physical emergency medical intervention delivered in unpredictable environments — the polar opposite of the digital-artifact outputs that drive high AI exposure. AI offers genuine but narrow value in documentation, dispatch optimization, and vital-sign monitoring support, but these are auxiliary to the core mission. No plausible 2026 AI capability — including advanced robotics — approaches the dexterity, situational adaptability, and clinical judgment required for out-of-hospital emergency care. Employment demand is also structurally driven by population health needs and response-time requirements, not information-processing throughput, further insulating the occupation from AI-driven headcount reduction.

title: Assistenzberufe im Gesundheitswesen, anderweitig nicht genannt
rationale: AI can assist with documentation, dietary planning, and psychological assessment scoring, but core tasks like administering anaesthesia, performing osteopathic manipulation, and monitoring surgical patients require hands-on clinical presence. The group spans highly diverse sub-occupations, keeping overall AI substitution moderate.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** Several tasks within this heterogeneous group are meaningfully AI-assisted. Recording patient data, managing healthcare users' data, and maintaining treatment records are increasingly handled by AI-augmented EHR systems. Dietary planning tasks — assessing nutritional characteristics of food, calculating food energy, planning patient menus, and creating diet plans — are well within the capability of 2026 AI tools that can generate evidence-based meal plans from patient parameters. Psychological assessment scoring (conduct psychological assessment, evaluation of psychological performance, carry out neuropsychological testing) can be partially automated through validated digital instruments. Counselling information delivery and health education content generation are also AI-performable at a support level. For the assistant clinical psychologist sub-occupation in particular, AI tools for structured assessment, psychoeducation content, and progress tracking are commercially available.

**Limiting factors:** The majority of core tasks across the five sub-occupations are physically grounded and clinically hands-on. Anaesthetic technicians must prepare operating theatres, maintain anaesthetic machinery, operate heart-lung machines, and monitor patients during surgery — tasks requiring real-time physical presence and fine motor response to rapidly changing conditions. Respiratory therapy technicians operate breathing equipment and respond to emergencies in unstructured clinical environments. Osteopaths diagnose and treat musculoskeletal conditions through direct physical manipulation — a task robotics cannot replicate in 2026 outside highly structured settings. Emergency care situations, surgical asepsis, and resuscitation all demand embodied human judgment under time pressure. Regulatory and patient-safety frameworks in Switzerland further constrain autonomous AI action in clinical settings. The 'apply person-centred care' and 'develop therapeutic relationships' skills reflect interpersonal trust demands that AI cannot substitute.

**Net assessment:** This ISCO group is genuinely heterogeneous — spanning anaesthesia technicians (very low AI substitution, physically intensive) to assistant clinical psychologists (moderate exposure via assessment and documentation tools) and dietetic technicians (moderate exposure via nutritional planning AI).
