Averaging across sub-occupations, the group sits at the low-to-moderate boundary (score 4). AI primarily acts as a productivity amplifier for documentation and information tasks rather than a substitute for the hands-on clinical core. Confidence is low because the sub-occupations have meaningfully different exposure profiles that a single score cannot capture well.

title: Betriebswirtschaftliche und kaufmännische Fachkräfte und Verwaltungsfachkräfte, onA
rationale: AI can handle most routine business administration tasks like drafting reports, processing invoices, and analyzing financial data. This catch-all category spans too many sub-roles to score precisely, but the core work is information-based and substantially AI-exposed.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** ISCO 3300 is a residual 'not elsewhere classified' category for business, commercial, and administrative associate professionals. The typical work products — financial reports, administrative documents, procurement analyses, business correspondence, and data processing — are almost entirely digital information work. AI tools in 2026 can draft business documents, summarize reports, process invoices, perform basic financial analysis, and handle routine correspondence at professional quality. Agentic workflows can automate multi-step administrative pipelines such as data entry, scheduling, and form processing. The higher vocational education level suggests roles involving some judgment and coordination, but the core outputs remain text and data artifacts highly amenable to AI assistance or substitution.

**Limiting factors:** The 'onA' (not elsewhere classified) designation means this category aggregates heterogeneous roles — some may involve significant client-facing relationship management, regulatory compliance judgment, or sector-specific expertise that resists automation. Physical presence for stakeholder coordination, negotiation, or site-based commercial activities would lower exposure for some incumbents. Regulatory and organizational trust barriers in Swiss business culture may slow adoption. Additionally, roles requiring cross-domain judgment under genuine ambiguity — such as advising on complex procurement decisions or managing organizational change — retain meaningful human advantage.

**Net assessment:** The occupation lands at 7 (High) because the primary work product is digital information processing and business analysis, where AI tools are demonstrably capable of handling the majority of routine instances. However, confidence is low: the residual NEC category likely contains a wide dispersion of actual roles, some closer to 5 (moderate, e.g., relationship-heavy commercial roles) and others closer to 8 (very high, e.g., pure administrative processing). The median wage and education level suggest enough judgment-based work to keep the score below the 8–9 tier.

title: Fachkräfte im Bereich Finanzen und mathematische Verfahren, onA
rationale: AI can already handle most financial calculations, data analysis, and report preparation that form the core of this work. The catch-all 'not elsewhere classified' nature of this group means some roles may involve more human judgment or client interaction than others.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** ISCO 3310 is a residual 'not elsewhere classified' category for finance and mathematical technicians, capturing roles that apply quantitative and financial methods in support functions — tasks like preparing financial reports, performing actuarial or statistical calculations, processing loan or insurance data, and supporting analysts with structured data work. These are quintessentially information-processing tasks performed on computers. AI tools in 2026 can automate the bulk of routine financial calculations, spreadsheet modeling, data extraction and reconciliation, and standardized report generation at acceptable quality. Agentic workflows can handle multi-step data pipelines (e.g., pulling figures, applying formulas, flagging anomalies) with minimal human intervention. The high median wage (CHF 7,793) suggests these are not purely clerical roles, but the core work product remains digital and structured.

**Limiting factors:** The 'onA' (not elsewhere classified) designation introduces genuine heterogeneity — some incumbents may perform actuarial support requiring domain-specific validation, others may work in client-facing advisory roles where trust and judgment matter, and others may operate in regulated environments (e.g., banking compliance, insurance underwriting support) where human sign-off is legally required. Switzerland's financial sector has strict regulatory and audit requirements that slow full automation. Additionally, genuinely novel quantitative problems — designing new risk models, interpreting ambiguous regulatory guidance — remain human-advantaged. Physical presence for client meetings or internal stakeholder management also resists substitution.

**Net assessment:** The core work is high-exposure information work: structured financial and mathematical processing that AI handles well. However, the residual category nature, Swiss regulatory environment, and likely mix of judgment-intensive sub-roles prevent a score above 7. The occupation sits firmly in the 'High' tier — AI can independently complete most routine instances of the work, but human oversight, accountability, and domain judgment retain meaningful value. Confidence is low because the heterogeneity of this catch-all group makes a single score imprecise.

title: Wertpapierhändler, -makler und Finanzmakler
rationale: AI can already analyse market trends, screen financial instruments, generate investment reports, and execute routine trades faster and more accurately than humans. The remaining human value lies in client relationships, regulatory accountability, and judgment in novel market conditions — keeping this just below maximum exposure.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The core work of this occupation is almost entirely information-processing on digital financial data. Tasks such as 'analyse market financial trends,' 'analyse financial performance of a company,' 'examine credit ratings,' 'synthesise financial information,' and 'review investment portfolios' are precisely the tasks where 2026 AI excels — large language models combined with quantitative finance tools can ingest earnings reports, macro data, and market feeds at scale. Algorithmic and AI-driven trading already executes the vast majority of equity and FX volume on major exchanges; the 'record and transmit buy and sell orders' task (task e in the ISCO description) is essentially fully automated in institutional settings. 'Obtain financial information,' 'maintain financial records,' and 'maintain records of financial transactions' are routine data-handling tasks with near-total automation potential. Optional skills like 'interpret financial statements,' 'forecast economic trends,' and 'investment analysis' are well within demonstrated 2026 AI capability for standard cases.

**Limiting factors:** Several factors prevent a score of 9–10. First, client-facing advisory work — 'advise on financial matters,' 'protect client interests,' 'communicate with customers,' and 'identify clients' needs' — involves trust, legal accountability, and relationship management that clients and regulators still require from licensed humans, particularly for high-net-worth or institutional clients. Second, 'advise on risk management' and 'manage financial risk' in genuinely novel or stressed market conditions (e.g., a 2008-style crisis or a new asset class) require cross-domain judgment that AI still handles poorly. Third, Swiss and EU financial regulation (MiFID II, FINMA rules) imposes suitability and fiduciary obligations on named human professionals, creating a compliance barrier to full AI substitution. The 'financial jurisdiction' essential skill reflects this regulatory anchor. Finally, negotiating loan terms and capital placement (task d) involves counterparty negotiation that remains human-mediated.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the 8 tier. The primary work product is digital financial analysis and transaction execution — both heavily AI-penetrated already. The human role has already contracted toward oversight, exception handling, client relationship management, and regulatory sign-off, which matches the 8–9 boundary definition. It does not reach 9 because the client advisory and novel-market-judgment components are substantive, not residual, and Swiss regulatory requirements maintain meaningful human accountability. Confidence is medium because the occupation spans heterogeneous sub-roles: a pure algorithmic trader faces near-maximum exposure, while a relationship-focused private banking broker faces somewhat less.

title: Fachkräfte im Bereich Kredit und Darlehen
rationale: AI can already automate credit scoring, analyse financial statements, assess loan risk, and flag fraud with high accuracy. Human judgment remains essential for complex cases, client interviews, and accountability for final credit decisions.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The core work of this occupation — analysing credit history, interpreting financial statements, applying credit risk policy, carrying out statistical forecasts, and producing credit offers — is almost entirely information-processing performed on structured digital data. 2026 AI tools are demonstrably capable across these tasks: automated credit scoring models already outperform human analysts on standard consumer and SME loans; AI can synthesise financial information, examine credit ratings, inspect data, and generate risk reports at scale. Tasks like 'consult credit score,' 'analyse loans,' 'apply credit stress testing methodologies,' 'produce statistical financial records,' and 'maintain credit history of clients' are either already automated in leading institutions or highly automatable with current tools. Document-heavy tasks such as 'examine mortgage loan documents,' 'complete credit and loan documentation,' and 'trace financial transactions' are well within the capability of large-context LLMs and agentic document-processing pipelines. The optional skills list — heavily weighted toward analytical and software-adjacent tasks — reinforces that the work environment is thoroughly digital.

**Limiting factors:** Several tasks resist near-term AI substitution. Client-facing interviews ('interview applicants,' 'interview bank loanees') involve trust-building, reading non-verbal cues, and navigating sensitive financial distress conversations that AI cannot yet replicate in a regulated Swiss banking context. Final approval authority ('decide on loan applications,' 'approve or reject within authorized limits') carries legal and fiduciary accountability that regulators and institutions currently require a named human to hold. Complex or novel cases — restructuring distressed business loans, assessing non-standard collateral under property law, or managing currency exchange risk in cross-border transactions — require contextual judgment that current AI handles poorly under genuine ambiguity. Regulatory compliance in Swiss financial jurisdiction also creates adoption friction, as automated decisions on credit must meet explainability and audit requirements under FINMA guidelines.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the High tier (score 7). The majority of routine loan processing — consumer credit, standard mortgages, student loans — is already being automated or will be within the 2026 horizon, compressing headcount significantly. What remains is oversight of AI-generated recommendations, handling edge cases, client relationship management, and regulatory accountability. The occupation is not at the 8–9 threshold because final decision authority, client interviews, and complex structured finance cases still require meaningful human engagement, not merely exception-handling. The Swiss market's relatively high regulatory standards and the mid-to-senior seniority of many roles in this ISCO group (credit managers, mortgage underwriters) provide modest additional friction compared to pure data-entry credit processing roles.

title: Fachkräfte im Rechnungswesen
rationale: AI can already automate bookkeeping entries, reconcile accounts, prepare financial statements, and flag errors with high accuracy. The work is almost entirely digital information processing with limited physical or interpersonal demands, placing this occupation firmly in the very-high-exposure tier.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The core tasks of this occupation — maintaining financial records, verifying transaction accuracy, preparing financial statements, managing the general ledger, performing balance sheet operations, and preparing trial balances — are almost entirely structured data operations on digital systems. These map precisely to what 2026 AI tools do well: automated bookkeeping platforms (e.g., Xero, QuickBooks AI, Datev with AI modules) already handle transaction categorisation, reconciliation, and routine journal entries with minimal human input. The essential skills 'use accounting systems,' 'manage the general ledger,' 'perform account allocation,' 'check accounting records,' and 'identify accounting errors' are all tasks where AI-assisted or fully automated workflows are commercially deployed today. Preparing financial statements and trial balances from structured ledger data is highly automatable; large language models with accounting domain training can draft these reports and flag anomalies. Optional skills such as 'calculate tax,' 'interpret financial statements,' and 'handle financial transactions' are similarly well within 2026 AI capability. The occupation's reliance on 'standard computer software packages' (ISCO task e) confirms the work is conducted entirely in a digital environment.

**Limiting factors:** Several tasks resist full automation. Supervising accounts clerks, instructing grant recipients, and conducting research interviews require interpersonal judgment and accountability that AI cannot yet fully replace. Grant administration sub-tasks — helping grant recipients, issuing concessions, managing applications — involve regulatory interpretation, stakeholder communication, and discretionary decisions that require human sign-off, particularly under Swiss public-sector compliance requirements. Commercial law, bookkeeping regulations, and tax legislation create liability contexts where human professionals must remain accountable for outputs, even if AI generates them. The 'follow the statutory obligations' and 'comply with legal regulations' skills imply audit trails and legal accountability that slow full automation. However, these are limiting factors on the pace of displacement, not on the fundamental automability of the core work product.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits solidly at 8. The primary work product — accurate financial records and reports — is a digital artifact produced through structured rule-following on transactional data, which is the sweet spot of current AI capability. The human role is increasingly one of oversight, exception handling, and regulatory accountability rather than performing the underlying calculations and entries. The main uncertainty is adoption speed in Swiss SMEs, where legacy systems and conservative procurement may delay deployment, but the technical capability for near-full automation of core tasks already exists. A score of 9 is not warranted because grant administration, stakeholder instruction, and legal accountability functions retain meaningful human judgment requirements.

title: Statistische, mathematische und verwandte Fachkräfte
rationale: AI can now perform the core tasks of this role — running statistical calculations, identifying data patterns, preparing charts and reports — at professional quality. The job is almost entirely information-processing work with few physical or interpersonal anchors, leaving little that AI cannot assist or replace.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The core work of this occupation is almost entirely digital information processing. Essential skills such as 'execute analytical mathematical calculations,' 'perform data analysis,' 'carry out statistical forecasts,' 'identify statistical patterns,' 'process data,' and 'write technical reports' are precisely the tasks where 2026 AI tools — including Python/R-integrated LLMs, automated statistical pipelines, and agentic data analysis tools — perform at or above associate-professional level. Tools like GitHub Copilot, ChatGPT Code Interpreter, and enterprise analytics platforms can autonomously run regressions, generate visualizations, compile tabular summaries, and draft technical write-ups. The optional skills reinforce this: 'analyse big data,' 'data quality assessment,' 'deliver visual presentation of data,' 'develop financial statistics reports,' and 'design questionnaires' are all well within current AI capability. The occupation's explicit reliance on 'statistical analysis system software' (SAS, R, Python) means the work already lives in a digital environment where AI integration is direct and frictionless.

**Limiting factors:** The primary human value remaining is judgment in ambiguous or novel analytical situations — 'apply scientific methods,' 'think analytically,' and 'identify statistical patterns' in genuinely novel data contexts still benefit from human oversight. The actuarial sub-occupation involves regulatory accountability (insurance rate-setting, policy compliance with insurance law) where human sign-off is legally required, creating a structural floor for human involvement. Supervising statistical clerks and coordinating survey operations ('design questionnaires,' 'conduct public surveys') involve some interpersonal and organizational judgment. However, these are peripheral to the core quantitative work and do not substantially limit AI exposure. The associate-professional framing — working under senior statisticians — means the role is already defined by executing well-specified tasks rather than open-ended research, making it more, not less, automatable.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the 8–9 range. The primary work product is digital (statistical outputs, reports, forecasts), the tasks are well-defined and structured, and 2026 AI tools demonstrably handle the majority of the core workload. The remaining human role is oversight, regulatory accountability, and exception handling in novel cases — the classic 8–9 profile. It does not reach 9–10 because some tasks (survey design, actuarial judgment, stakeholder communication) retain meaningful human input, and adoption in Swiss insurance and public-sector contexts may lag slightly behind the technical frontier. A score of 8 is well-supported.

title: Schätzer und Schadensgutachter
rationale: AI can already automate much of the document analysis, claims classification, and report drafting that underpins this work. However, physical site inspections, fraud interviews, and judgment calls on ambiguous damage or value require human presence and expertise that AI cannot yet replace.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A substantial portion of the core tasks in this occupation involve information processing on structured and semi-structured data — exactly where 2026 AI excels. Skills such as 'analyse claim files,' 'classify insurance claims,' 'compile appraisal reports,' 'synthesise financial information,' 'perform property market research,' 'compare property values,' and 'calculate compensation payments' are all cognitive operations on documents and data. AI tools can already ingest large volumes of claims documentation, cross-reference comparable sales data, flag anomalies, and draft standardised valuation or loss reports. Fraud detection ('detect financial crime,' 'insurance fraud investigator' sub-role) is an area where ML models have demonstrated strong performance in pattern recognition across claims histories. Claims handling workflows — initiating files, managing claims processes, reviewing insurance processes — are prime candidates for agentic AI automation, with several insurers already deploying such systems. Report preparation ('write gemstone grading report,' 'compile appraisal reports') is largely templated and AI-generatable once underlying data is collected.

**Limiting factors:** The occupation has a significant physical inspection component that AI cannot yet replicate at scale. Tasks like 'examine the conditions of buildings,' 'organise a damage assessment,' 'examine gems,' 'immerse gemstones in chemical liquid,' 'operate microscope,' and 'use gemstone identification equipment' require physical presence and tactile/sensory judgment. Sub-roles like gemmologist and personal property appraiser (art, antiques, wine) depend heavily on expert sensory evaluation and provenance knowledge that remains human-advantaged. Fraud investigation involves interviewing claimants, assisting police, and reading interpersonal cues — tasks where AI is a support tool, not a substitute. Regulatory and legal dimensions ('insurance law,' 'property law,' 'tax legislation,' 'foreclosure') require professional accountability and liability that creates adoption friction. Negotiating settlements and protecting client interests involve stakeholder trust and discretionary judgment that courts and regulators still expect humans to exercise.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits solidly in the High tier (score 6). The information-processing and document-analysis tasks — which represent a large share of desk-based work — are increasingly AI-automatable, and productivity amplification is already occurring in insurance claims handling. However, the physical inspection tasks, specialist sensory sub-roles (gemology, art appraisal), fraud investigation interviews, and legal accountability requirements provide meaningful resistance to full substitution. The heterogeneity of sub-occupations (from claims handler to gemmologist) introduces some uncertainty; a pure claims handler would score closer to 7-8, while a gemmologist or art appraiser would score closer to 4-5. The blended score of 6 reflects the occupation as defined.

title: Versicherungsvertreter
rationale: AI can already automate much of the data gathering, risk calculation, and product comparison that insurance agents do daily. However, building client trust, negotiating complex contracts, and advising on sensitive personal situations still require a human.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A substantial share of the core tasks listed are information-processing operations well within 2026 AI capabilities. 'Calculate insurance rate,' 'analyse financial risk,' 'analyse insurance needs,' 'compare insurance products,' 'perform risk analysis,' and 'prepare survey report' are all analytical tasks on structured data that AI tools handle reliably today. Insurtech platforms already automate premium calculation and risk scoring for standard personal and SME lines. 'Inform on insurance products,' 'explain coverage details,' and 'conduct financial surveys' map closely to AI-powered chatbots and digital advisory tools already deployed by major Swiss insurers (e.g., Zurich, Helvetia). The optional skill 'apply statistical analysis techniques' and 'compile statistical data for insurance purposes' are near-fully automatable. Agentic workflows can handle 'obtain financial information,' 'collect property financial information,' and 'handle financial transactions' with minimal human input in structured digital environments.

**Limiting factors:** The occupation's core value proposition is relational and trust-based. 'Prospect new customers,' 'build business relationships,' 'protect client interests,' and 'negotiate terms for large or special risks' involve interpersonal dynamics, persuasion, and accountability that AI cannot replicate in high-stakes commercial contexts. Swiss regulatory requirements around financial advice (FINMA, VAG) impose liability on licensed human intermediaries, creating a structural barrier to full automation. Sub-occupations like insurance risk consultant and underwriter for complex or novel risks (marine, large infrastructure, special projects) require on-site surveys, physical inspection, and cross-domain judgment that remain human-advantaged. 'Negotiate and place reinsurance contracts' involves multi-party negotiation with significant financial stakes where human accountability is contractually expected.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits solidly in the High tier (score 6). AI is already reshaping the routine transactional and analytical layers — standard personal lines, rate calculation, product comparison — reducing the number of agents needed for volume work. However, the complex advisory, negotiation, and relationship-management functions that define the higher-value segments of this role resist substitution. The Swiss market's regulatory structure and the prevalence of complex commercial and reinsurance work in this ISCO group (which includes underwriters and brokers, not just retail agents) tempers the score from pushing into the 7–8 range. Key uncertainty: the pace at which Swiss insurers deploy AI-assisted sales tools for SME and personal lines could accelerate displacement of the lower-complexity advisory tasks within 2–3 years.

title: Verkaufsfachkräfte
rationale: AI can automate quoting prices, drafting proposals, analysing sales data, and managing follow-up emails, but closing deals still depends on reading people and building trust face-to-face. The job sits in the high tier because routine information tasks are heavily automatable, yet the relational core resists full substitution.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A substantial portion of the listed essential skills are information-processing tasks that 2026 AI handles well. 'Answer requests for quotation,' 'produce sales reports,' 'carry out sales analysis,' 'keep records of customer interaction,' 'keep records on sales,' and 'record customers' personal data' are all digital-administrative tasks that CRM-integrated AI agents already automate or near-automate. 'Implement customer follow-up' and 'respond to customers' inquiries' are increasingly handled by AI-powered email assistants and chatbots. 'Prospect new customers' and 'prospect new regional contracts' map directly onto AI-driven lead-scoring and outreach tools (e.g., Salesforce Einstein, HubSpot AI) that identify and prioritise prospects with minimal human input. Optional skills like 'e-commerce systems,' 'e-procurement,' 'apply social media marketing,' and 'analyse consumer buying trends' are also well within current AI capability. The administrative and analytical backbone of this role — perhaps 40–50% of daily task volume — is already being automated in enterprise sales environments.

**Limiting factors:** The core value proposition of a commercial sales representative is relationship-based persuasion: 'demonstrate products' features,' 'deliver a sales pitch,' 'maintain relationship with suppliers,' 'guarantee customer satisfaction,' and 'demonstrate motivation for sales' all require interpersonal trust, real-time social reading, and adaptive negotiation that AI cannot replicate in B2B contexts where deal sizes are large and buyers are sophisticated. Physical product demonstrations, on-site visits, and the credibility that comes from a human representative remain important in many sectors covered by this ISCO group (chemicals, hardware, clothing, electronic equipment). Regulatory compliance tasks ('ensure compliance with legal requirements,' 'ensure compliance with purchasing and contracting regulations') require contextual judgment and accountability that buyers and regulators still expect from humans. Adoption barriers are also real: many SME clients in Switzerland still prefer human account managers, and CRM AI adoption varies widely by sector.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits solidly in the high-exposure tier (6), not the very-high tier (8–9), because the primary work product is a commercial relationship and a closed deal — not a digital artifact. AI is reshaping the administrative and analytical scaffolding of the role significantly, likely reducing headcount needs over time through productivity amplification (fewer reps needed per account), but the persuasion and trust-building core retains meaningful human advantage. The heterogeneity of sub-sectors covered (from dairy products to electronic equipment) introduces some uncertainty, as digital-native sectors will see faster AI adoption than traditional trade sectors.

title: Einkäufer
rationale: AI can automate supplier research, bid comparison, price tracking, and purchase order generation, but core value lies in negotiating contracts and managing supplier relationships. The mix of digital information work and interpersonal negotiation keeps exposure at moderate levels.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A substantial share of the buyer's core tasks are information-processing work well-suited to 2026 AI. Tracking price trends, analysing supply chain strategies, comparing contractors' bids, identifying suppliers, and issuing purchase orders are all tasks where AI agents and procurement platforms already provide significant automation. Tools like Coupa, SAP Ariba, and AI-enhanced spend analytics can handle tender management, supplier risk assessment, and budget tracking with minimal human input. The ESCO skill 'have computer literacy' signals that the work is conducted in a digital environment, and optional skills like 'carry out internet research' and 'analyse consumer buying trends' are highly automatable. Writing technical reports and managing procurement cycles are also well within current LLM capabilities.

**Limiting factors:** The core value of a buyer is not information retrieval but negotiation — 'negotiate price,' 'determine contract terms,' 'interview suppliers,' and 'maintain relationship with suppliers' all require interpersonal judgment, trust-building, and real-time adaptive communication that AI cannot yet replicate reliably in high-stakes commercial contexts. Physical tasks such as inspecting timber, visiting trade shows, and handling materials are entirely AI-resistant. The role also spans highly heterogeneous sub-occupations (costume buyer, set buyer, timber trader) with distinct physical and creative demands. Regulatory compliance in international trade (embargo regulations, export control, import/export rules) requires human accountability that organisations are reluctant to delegate to AI.

**Net assessment:** The occupation sits at the boundary between moderate and high exposure. Roughly 40–50% of tasks — supplier research, bid analysis, price tracking, order processing, report writing — are meaningfully automatable today, pushing toward a 5. However, negotiation, relationship management, physical inspection, and regulatory accountability anchor the role in the moderate tier. The heterogeneity of sub-occupations (timber trader vs. costume buyer) introduces uncertainty, but the core purchaser role is unlikely to be deeply disrupted before AI demonstrates reliable negotiation and physical-world capabilities. A score of 5 reflects genuine productivity amplification without near-term workforce substitution.

title: Handelsmakler
rationale: AI can automate market analysis, document preparation, and freight rate comparisons, but the core of this job — negotiating deals and building trust with counterparties — still requires human judgment and relationships. The mix of automatable back-office tasks and irreplaceable relationship-driven deal-making lands this in the high-exposure tier.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A significant share of the 57 essential skills are information-processing tasks well within 2026 AI capabilities. Market analysis tasks — 'analyse economic trends,' 'analyse energy market trends,' 'analyse financial risk,' 'monitor international market performance,' and 'perform market research' — are highly automatable; AI tools already synthesize commodity price feeds, shipping indices, and macroeconomic data at scale. Documentation tasks including 'prepare bills of lading,' 'handle shipment paperwork,' 'control trade commercial documentation,' 'prepare freight shipment reports,' and 'manage import export licenses' are largely structured form-filling and compliance checking that AI agents handle well. Rate comparison and route optimization ('have access to shipping rates,' 'match vessels to shipping routes,' 'plan transport operations') are increasingly handled by digital freight platforms with embedded AI. 'Review investment portfolios' and 'maintain financial records' are also highly automatable. Taken together, perhaps 40–50% of listed tasks are substantially AI-performable today.

**Limiting factors:** The core value proposition of a trade broker is relationship-based deal execution: 'negotiate sale of commodities,' 'negotiate buying conditions,' 'negotiate sales contracts,' 'negotiate with stakeholders,' 'build business relationships,' 'initiate contact with buyers,' and 'initiate contact with sellers.' These tasks involve trust, reputation, and real-time interpersonal judgment in high-stakes, often illiquid markets where counterparty relationships determine access to deals. Commodity and shipping markets are also characterized by significant information asymmetry, bespoke contract terms, and physical logistics complexity (cargo stowage, customs compliance, freight payment methods) that require contextual judgment AI cannot yet reliably provide. Regulatory exposure — 'financial jurisdiction,' 'commercial law,' 'international tariffs,' 'keep up-to-date to current customs regulations' — adds accountability requirements that slow AI adoption. The optional skill list spanning dozens of commodity verticals (agricultural raw materials, chemical products, dairy, coffee, etc.) signals that deep domain expertise in specific physical markets is a differentiator.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the boundary of moderate and high exposure (score 6). AI is already reshaping the back-office and analytical layers of commodity brokerage — market intelligence, documentation, compliance checking — and productivity amplification is real. However, the primary work product is a negotiated deal executed through trusted relationships in fragmented, relationship-driven markets, which resists full AI substitution. The occupation is unlikely to see headcount collapse but will see significant workflow transformation, with fewer brokers needed per transaction volume. Confidence is medium because the heterogeneity across sub-occupations (NVOCC logistics vs. commodity futures dealer vs. wholesale merchant) means exposure varies meaningfully within the category.

title: Fachkräfte für Abrechnungs- und Speditionsdienstleistungen
rationale: AI can automate customs document classification, tariff coding, and compliance checks — the core paperwork-heavy tasks of this role. However, negotiating with carriers, resolving shipment problems, and managing regulatory exceptions still require human judgment, keeping exposure in the high-but-not-very-high range.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The core work product of clearing and forwarding agents is fundamentally information-processing: classifying goods using tariff codes, preparing bills of lading, checking import/export documentation, calculating duties and taxes, and ensuring regulatory compliance. These tasks map directly onto 2026 AI capabilities. Large language models with document-processing capabilities can already handle tariff classification (HS code assignment), draft standard shipping documentation, cross-check cargo manifests against regulatory databases, and flag compliance issues. Skills like 'calculate tax,' 'prepare documentation for international shipping,' 'control trade commercial documentation,' 'prepare bills of lading,' and 'write work-related reports' are highly automatable. Agentic workflow tools can already integrate with customs portals, auto-populate declarations, and monitor regulatory updates — directly addressing 'keep up-to-date to current customs regulations' and 'apply procedures to ensure cargo complies with customs regulations.' The optional skill 'have computer literacy' confirms this is a digitally-mediated role, and the heavy presence of documentation and data-classification tasks in the essential skills list places this firmly in information-work territory.

**Limiting factors:** Several dimensions resist straightforward automation. Carrier negotiation ('negotiate logistics services,' 'negotiate price,' 'make bids in forward auctions,' 'foster relationships with various types of carrier') involves real-time commercial judgment, relationship management, and adversarial bargaining that AI cannot yet conduct autonomously with counterparties. Problem resolution in international forwarding — handling shipment disruptions, resolving customs disputes, managing dangerous goods incidents — requires contextual judgment and accountability that regulators and clients still demand from humans. Regulatory liability is a significant barrier: customs declarations carry legal responsibility, and many jurisdictions require licensed human agents to sign off. Physical inspection tasks ('undertake inspections,' 'handle surveillance equipment') and oversight of cargo storage remain human-anchored. The role also spans highly variable international regulatory environments, where edge cases and novel situations are frequent.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits solidly in the High tier (6). The majority of routine documentation, classification, and compliance-checking tasks are automatable with current AI tools, and productivity amplification is already occurring through customs automation platforms. However, the combination of legal accountability requirements, active negotiation with external parties, exception-handling in complex international logistics, and some physical oversight duties prevents this from reaching the Very High tier. The key uncertainty is pace of regulatory change: if customs authorities accelerate digital-only processing and AI-assisted declaration systems become legally accepted, the score could rise to 7-8 within a few years.

title: Konferenz- und Veranstaltungsplaner
rationale: AI can draft event proposals, manage registrations, and handle vendor research, but the core work—negotiating contracts, coordinating on-site logistics, and managing client relationships in real time—requires human presence and judgment. The job is a mix of automatable planning tasks and irreplaceable physical coordination.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A meaningful portion of conference and event planning involves information-processing tasks that 2026 AI handles well. Drafting promotional materials and event topics ('develop event topics', 'promote event', 'solicit event publicity') can be largely AI-generated. Participant registration systems ('organise event participants' registration', 'maintain event records', 'review event bills') are already heavily automated through platforms like Eventbrite or Cvent, and AI agents can further streamline data entry, invoice processing, and budget tracking ('manage budgets', 'process commissioned instructions'). Vendor research and initial price comparison ('identify suppliers', 'quote prices', 'forecast catering services') are well within AI's 2026 capability for structured information retrieval. AI scheduling and logistics tools can assist with travel arrangements and accommodation coordination ('make logistical arrangements', 'organise travel arrangements for staff').

**Limiting factors:** The core value of this occupation is physical and relational. Inspecting venues ('inspect event facilities'), overseeing on-site installation ('manage event structure installation'), monitoring live event activities ('monitor event activities'), and supervising contractors and staff ('supervise event staff', 'confer with event staff') all require physical presence in unstructured, dynamic environments where robotics and remote AI agents remain inadequate. Negotiating contracts and costs with vendors ('negotiate contracts with event providers', 'negotiate buying conditions') involves real-time interpersonal persuasion, relationship capital, and contextual judgment that AI cannot replicate reliably. Liaising with local authorities for permits ('liaise with local authorities', 'obtain event permits') and managing client expectations in face-to-face meetings ('identify customer requirements', 'liaise with event sponsors') depend on trust and social intelligence. Crisis management during live events—a core competency—is highly resistant to automation.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the moderate tier (score 5). Roughly 30–40% of tasks—administrative, promotional, and research-oriented—are meaningfully AI-assisted or automatable, but the majority of core value delivery is physical, relational, and real-time. AI acts primarily as a productivity amplifier for the planning phase rather than a substitute for the coordination and execution phases. The main uncertainty is how quickly agentic AI tools will absorb the administrative back-office work, potentially reducing headcount for junior event assistants while leaving senior planners and event managers relatively intact.

title: Arbeits- und Personalvermittler
rationale: AI can already screen CVs, match candidates to job postings, and search talent databases faster than humans. However, the core value of this job—building trust with both employers and job seekers through personal relationships and judgment—still requires a human.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A significant share of the core tasks in this occupation are information-processing operations that 2026 AI handles well. Matching jobseekers with vacancies (task a) is already partially automated by platforms like LinkedIn Recruiter and ATS systems using embedding-based semantic matching. 'Search databases' (optional skill) and 'profile people' (essential skill) are highly automatable—AI can parse CVs, extract structured candidate profiles, and rank candidates against job requirements at scale. 'Document interviews' is straightforwardly automatable via transcription and summarization tools. 'Analyse unemployment rates' and 'job market offers' (essential skill) involve data synthesis that AI performs well. 'Fix meetings' and 'prepare for job interview' are agentic workflow tasks already handled by scheduling AI. Even 'advise on career' and 'provide information on study programmes' can be substantially addressed by AI chatbots with up-to-date labour market data.

**Limiting factors:** The occupation's core value proposition rests on interpersonal trust and judgment that AI cannot yet replicate reliably in production. 'Interview people,' 'listen actively,' 'assess character,' and 'mentor individuals' require reading subtle human signals, building rapport, and exercising contextual judgment about fit—tasks where AI remains unreliable and where clients and employers expect human accountability. 'Employment law' and 'labour legislation' compliance (ensuring contracts meet legal requirements, task e) involves liability that organisations are reluctant to delegate to AI without human sign-off. 'Develop professional network' is inherently relational and cannot be automated. 'Maintain privacy of service users' and 'observe confidentiality' create regulatory and trust barriers to full AI deployment. Physical and emotional support dimensions—'assist clients with personal development,' 'facilitate job market access' for vulnerable populations—resist substitution due to social trust requirements.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the high end of moderate-to-high exposure (score 6). The back-office and information-processing tasks (database search, candidate profiling, documentation, market analysis) are substantially AI-automatable, and productivity amplification is already real—one recruiter with AI tools can handle candidate pipelines that previously required two or three. However, the relationship-intensive core—building employer trust, conducting meaningful candidate interviews, exercising judgment on character and fit, and navigating sensitive employment situations—retains meaningful human advantage. The occupation is unlikely to be eliminated but will see significant headcount compression as AI handles the volume work, leaving humans to manage exceptions, relationships, and accountability. Key uncertainty: how quickly enterprise clients accept AI-mediated candidate matching without human intermediaries.

title: Immobilienmakler und -verwalter
rationale: AI can automate property valuation, market research, contract drafting, and financial analysis — tasks that make up a large share of this job. However, client negotiation, physical property inspections, and trust-based relationship management keep human agents essential.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A substantial portion of the 65 essential skills are information-processing tasks that 2026 AI handles well. 'Perform property market research,' 'compare property values,' 'value properties,' 'analyse financial performance,' 'analyse market financial trends,' 'provide cost benefit analysis reports,' and 'collect property financial information' are all tasks where AI tools (automated valuation models, LLM-based financial analysis, agentic research pipelines) already operate at near-professional quality. 'Compose real estate contract' and 'handle lease agreement administration' are document-generation tasks where AI can produce first drafts with high accuracy given structured inputs. 'Maintain financial records,' 'trace financial transactions,' and 'financial statements' are largely automatable through existing accounting software augmented by AI. Listing generation, advertising copy, and initial customer qualification ('identify customer's needs,' 'prospect new customers') are increasingly handled by AI-powered CRM and marketing tools. Taken together, perhaps 40–50% of core task volume is AI-performable today.

**Limiting factors:** The occupation's core value proposition is heavily interpersonal and trust-dependent. 'Facilitate negotiations with tenants and owners,' 'liaise with property owners,' 'liaise with local authorities,' 'communicate with tenants,' and 'maintain relationship with customers' all require human presence, social judgment, and accountability that AI cannot replicate in 2026. Physical tasks — 'examine the conditions of buildings,' 'organise a damage assessment,' 'inspect properties before, during and after tenancies,' and 'showing properties to prospective buyers' — require on-site human presence. Swiss regulatory context adds friction: property law, contract signing, and transfer of property rights involve notarial and legal requirements that mandate human accountability. 'Manage personnel' and 'supervise property development projects' involve organizational judgment that resists full automation. The heterogeneity of the ISCO group (from street-level estate agents to property acquisitions managers) also means AI exposure varies considerably across sub-occupations.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits solidly in the High tier (score 6). AI is already reshaping the information-intensive back-office and analytical work — valuations, market reports, contract drafts, financial tracking — and productivity amplification means fewer agents can handle more listings. However, the deal-closing, negotiation, inspection, and relationship-management core of the job remains human-dependent. The occupation is unlikely to be displaced wholesale but will see significant workflow restructuring, with AI handling research and documentation while humans focus on client-facing and physical tasks. Uncertainty is moderate because sub-occupations range from highly automatable (leasing manager administrative work) to more resistant (housing manager community relations).

title: Fachkräfte für unternehmensbezogene Dienstleistungen, anderweitig nicht genannt
rationale: AI can handle market research, drafting contracts, and preparing sales reports, but the core work—negotiating deals, building trust with artists or clients, and reading rooms at auctions—depends on human judgment and relationships. The mix of high-touch interpersonal roles and routine administrative tasks lands this occupation in the moderate tier.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A significant share of the 164 essential skills involves information-processing tasks that 2026 AI handles well. Market research, analysing client data, producing sales reports, keeping records of bid history and customer interactions, preparing travel packages, and drafting or reviewing contracts are all substantially automatable. CRM software integration (listed as an essential skill) is already AI-augmented. AI tools can generate auction catalogues, draft sales pitches, monitor legislation developments, and perform contract compliance audits at acceptable quality. For sub-occupations like advertising sales agent or publishing rights manager, AI can pre-qualify leads, generate pitch decks, and flag rights conflicts—tasks that previously consumed significant time.

**Limiting factors:** The core value proposition across nearly all sub-occupations in this group is relationship-based negotiation and trust. Talent agents, sports agents, theatrical agents, and tourism contract negotiators succeed or fail based on personal networks, reading counterparty intent, and creative deal-structuring in ambiguous situations—capabilities where 2026 AI remains weak in production settings. Auctioneers require real-time crowd management, performing the auction chant, and split-second bid adjudication in physical environments. Venue programmers and promoters must exercise aesthetic judgment and manage volatile creative personalities. Relocation officers handle emotionally sensitive employee transitions. These interpersonal and physical-presence demands are genuine barriers, not peripheral tasks.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the boundary between moderate (4–5) and high (6–7) exposure. The administrative and information-processing layer is substantially AI-exposed, but the primary work product—a negotiated deal, a curated programme, a closed auction—requires human presence, trust, and judgment in ways that resist full substitution. Productivity amplification is real (fewer agents needed per deal volume), but outright replacement is limited by the relationship-intensive core. The heterogeneity of sub-occupations (auctioneer vs. intellectual property consultant vs. tour operator) introduces uncertainty, warranting medium confidence and a score of 5.

title: Sekretariatsleiter
rationale: AI can automate data analysis, report writing, scheduling, and call quality monitoring — tasks that make up a large share of this role. However, managing staff conflicts, coaching employees, and coordinating across departments still require human judgment and presence.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A substantial portion of this role's core tasks are information-processing work that 2026 AI handles well. Call quality auditing (complete evaluation forms of calls, measure call quality, provide objective assessments of calls) is already being automated by AI speech analytics platforms. Data analysis tasks — analyse call performance trends, interpret automatic call distribution data, perform data analysis, carry out statistical forecasts — are highly automatable with current tools. Report writing (write work-related reports, prepare survey report, present reports, report on overall management of a business) is well within LLM capability. Scheduling and resource planning (manage schedule of tasks, forecast workload, perform resource planning, estimate duration of work) are increasingly handled by AI-assisted workforce management software. The heavy presence of query languages (LDAP, LINQ, MDX, N1QL, SPARQL, XQuery) and call-centre technologies in essential skills confirms this is a digitally-mediated role where AI tooling is already deeply embedded.

**Limiting factors:** The supervisory and people-management core of this role resists AI substitution significantly. Tasks like manage employee complaints, motivate employees, give constructive feedback, evaluate employees, recruit employees, and introduce new employees require interpersonal trust, contextual judgment, and organizational authority that AI cannot replicate in 2026. Resolving work-related problems and coordinating activities with other departments involve ambiguous, politically sensitive situations where human accountability is essential. Training and instructing employees — a core ISCO task — requires adaptive communication and relationship-building. Regulatory compliance (employment law, information confidentiality, apply information security policies) also demands human accountability. The breadth of sub-occupations (from field survey manager to call centre manager) introduces heterogeneity: some variants are more data-heavy, others more people-heavy.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits solidly in the High tier (6). AI tools are already reshaping the analytical and monitoring subtasks, and productivity amplification is real — one supervisor can oversee more staff with AI-assisted dashboards and automated quality scoring. However, the primary value delivered is supervisory coordination and people management, which remains human-advantaged. The score does not reach 7 because the people-management core is genuinely resistant, not merely a peripheral task. Confidence is medium due to the heterogeneity across the eight ESCO sub-occupations: a data entry supervisor skews higher (toward 7-8), while an office manager or field survey manager skews lower (toward 5-6).

title: Sekretariatsfachkräfte im juristischen Bereich
rationale: AI can draft and review legal documents, manage schedules, and handle routine correspondence with high accuracy. However, physical document handling, court liaison, civil registration duties, and confidentiality-sensitive client interactions keep meaningful human involvement necessary.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The core work product of legal secretaries is heavily document-centric and information-based. Tasks such as 'compile legal documents,' 'review and proofread documents,' 'observe documents,' and 'meet deadlines for preparing legal cases' are precisely the kind of structured text-processing tasks where 2026 AI excels. Large-language models can draft deeds, wills, affidavits, and briefs from templates with high accuracy, and AI-assisted legal document review tools (e.g., Harvey, Clio, LexisNexis AI) are already in production use in Swiss and European law firms. Scheduling and agenda management ('manage personnel agenda,' 'fix meetings,' 'screen requests for meetings') are highly automatable via agentic AI tools. Routine clerical duties, correspondence drafting, and budget monitoring assistance are also well within current AI capability. Optional skills like 'legal research,' 'revise legal documents,' and 'translate keywords into full texts' are increasingly handled by AI tools.

**Limiting factors:** Several task clusters resist AI substitution. Civil registration duties—'register birth,' 'register death,' 'officiate civil partnerships,' 'officiate weddings'—are legally mandated human acts in Switzerland, requiring physical presence and official authority; AI cannot substitute here. 'Check official documents' and 'handle evidence' often require physical inspection and chain-of-custody integrity. 'Observe confidentiality' and 'act discreetly' reflect trust-based relationships with clients and attorneys that create institutional resistance to AI adoption in sensitive legal contexts. Supervising office support workers and liaising with court officials ('brief court officials') involve interpersonal judgment. Regulatory and professional liability frameworks in Swiss legal practice also slow AI adoption for client-facing or court-facing outputs. The occupation's hybrid nature—part civil servant (civil registrar sub-role) and part administrative professional—introduces heterogeneity that moderates the score.

**Net assessment:** The occupation sits solidly in the High tier (6). The majority of document preparation, scheduling, and correspondence tasks are AI-performable today, and productivity amplification is already occurring—meaning fewer legal secretaries are needed per attorney. However, the civil registration sub-role, physical document handling, court liaison duties, and the trust/confidentiality context of legal work create a meaningful floor of human necessity. The score does not reach 7-8 because the occupation is not purely information-work: physical presence, official authority, and interpersonal discretion remain structurally embedded in the role.

title: Sekretariatsfachkräfte in Verwaltung und Geschäftsleitung
rationale: AI can already draft emails, schedule meetings, transcribe audio, manage documents, and prepare reports — the core daily tasks of this role. Enough high-value coordination, stakeholder liaison, and judgment work remains to keep humans in the loop, but the role is significantly reshaped.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The vast majority of core tasks in this occupation are information-processing work performed in a digital environment. Drafting corporate emails, writing correspondence, preparing minutes, transcribing audio, filing and organising documents, managing calendars, and arranging travel are all tasks where 2026 AI tools (GPT-class LLMs, agentic scheduling tools, real-time transcription, document management automation) already perform at acceptable or better-than-acceptable quality. Skills such as 'draft corporate emails,' 'type error-free documents,' 'type texts from audio sources,' 'use word processing software,' 'use spreadsheets,' 'use Microsoft Office,' 'structure information,' and 'provide written content' map almost perfectly onto current AI capabilities. Agentic workflow tools can handle calendar management, travel booking, and form-filling with minimal human input. Transcription and scopist work (a listed sub-occupation) has been heavily disrupted — AI transcription is now near-human accuracy at a fraction of the cost. The optional skill list (audio editing software, authoring software, digitise documents, draft press releases) further confirms a predominantly digital work environment.

**Limiting factors:** Several tasks resist straightforward automation. Liaising with board members and senior managers involves interpersonal trust, discretion, and reading organisational dynamics — qualities where AI acts as a tool rather than a substitute. Observing confidentiality and applying information security policies require human accountability that organisations are reluctant to delegate to AI agents. Coordinating complex multi-stakeholder events, managing sensitive personnel agendas, and maintaining statutory books or shareholder registers involve legal responsibility and contextual judgment. The supervisory function over clerical staff is inherently human. Regulatory and compliance contexts (legal terminology, media law, press law, court procedures) add a layer of domain-specific validation that slows full automation. Physical presence for visitor assistance and facility management also anchors some residual in-person value.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the High tier (score 7). The primary work product is digital — documents, communications, schedules, records — and the majority of routine instances of that work can already be handled or substantially accelerated by AI. However, the role's value increasingly concentrates in the coordination, trust, and judgment layer: managing relationships with executives, handling sensitive or ambiguous situations, and providing accountability for consequential decisions. AI amplifies productivity dramatically (one assistant can handle the output of several with AI tools), which implies workforce contraction more than full elimination. The score does not reach 8–9 because the interpersonal and organisational judgment components are genuine and central, not peripheral.

title: Sekretariatsfachkräfte im Gesundheitswesen
rationale: AI can already handle medical transcription, appointment scheduling, insurance form completion, and records management — the core outputs of this role. Human value remains in patient-facing interactions, complex coordination, and accountability within regulated healthcare environments.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The majority of core tasks in this occupation are information-processing operations on structured digital data. Medical transcription — converting dictated audio into documents — is now near-fully automatable with tools like Nuance DAX and similar AI scribes, which are already deployed in Swiss and European hospitals. Scheduling and appointment administration, insurance and billing form completion, maintaining digital archives, and compiling medical charts are all tasks where agentic AI workflows and EHR-integrated automation tools are commercially available and increasingly deployed. Skills such as 'edit dictated medical texts,' 'transcribe medical data,' 'manage digital archives,' 'use electronic health records management system,' and 'identify patients' medical records' map directly onto capabilities that 2026 AI handles at acceptable quality. Financial tasks like 'manage budgets,' 'create a financial plan,' and 'prepare financial statements' are also substantially AI-assisted. The optional skill list — heavy with accounting, financial forecasting, form-filling, and records management — reinforces that the work product is overwhelmingly digital and document-centric.

**Limiting factors:** Several tasks resist straightforward automation. Patient-facing interactions — interviewing patients to complete case histories, answering patient questions, advising on informed consent — require interpersonal trust, empathy, and contextual judgment that AI cannot reliably substitute in a healthcare setting. Regulatory compliance in Swiss healthcare (e.g., data protection under nDSG, cantonal health regulations) creates accountability requirements that keep humans in the loop. Sub-occupations like 'medical practice manager' involve supervising staff, hiring personnel, managing employee complaints, and making strategic business decisions — tasks requiring organizational authority and human judgment. Physical tasks such as maintaining chiropractic equipment and sending medical samples are minor but AI-resistant. The healthcare context also introduces higher-than-average adoption friction: patient data sensitivity, liability concerns, and institutional conservatism slow deployment even where technical capability exists.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the High tier (6–7). The primary work product is digital and document-centric, and the majority of routine task instances — transcription, scheduling, billing, records management — are already being automated or augmented by commercially deployed AI tools. However, the occupation is not approaching the 8–9 tier because a meaningful share of core work involves regulated patient interaction, staff management, and accountability functions that require human presence and judgment. The score of 7 reflects that AI will substantially reduce the headcount needed for routine administrative throughput, while retaining humans for coordination, oversight, and patient-facing roles. Confidence is medium because the occupation spans a wide range from dental secretary (highly automatable) to medical practice manager (substantially human-judgment-dependent).

title: Fachkräfte im Zolldienst und Grenzschutz
rationale: AI can assist with document verification, database checks, and flagging anomalies in cargo manifests, but the core job requires physical presence at borders, real-time human judgment, and authority to detain people. The irreducibly physical and legal-authority nature of the work keeps AI exposure low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A meaningful subset of tasks involves structured information processing that AI can support. Document verification (checking travel documentation, examining transport documents) can be partially automated via optical character recognition, biometric matching, and database cross-referencing — technologies already deployed at many border crossings. Administrative record-keeping (task g) and responding to standard enquiries are highly automatable. Surveillance equipment generates data streams that AI vision systems can analyze to flag anomalies, and AI can assist with risk-profiling cargo manifests or flagging known offenders against watchlists. The optional skill 'execute analytical mathematical calculations' and 'perform inspection analysis' for cargo valuation are also AI-amenable.

**Limiting factors:** The primary work product is not a digital artifact — it is a physical enforcement action: stopping, inspecting, detaining, and making real-time legal determinations about people and goods at a border. Tasks such as conducting frisk searches, performing body searches, detaining offenders, handling aggressive behaviour, and testifying in court are entirely human-dependent. Legal authority to detain is vested in a human officer, not an AI system. Regulatory and liability frameworks in Switzerland and internationally require a human decision-maker for immigration and customs determinations. Physical inspection of luggage and freight for concealed contraband (drugs, weapons) requires tactile and contextual judgment that robotic systems cannot yet reliably replicate in unstructured environments. The interpersonal dimension — conducting research interviews, advising on immigration procedures, liaising with law enforcement — also resists substitution.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). AI functions as a support tool — accelerating document checks, flagging risk signals, and automating paperwork — but cannot replace the officer's physical presence, legal authority, or judgment in ambiguous enforcement situations. Adoption of AI-assisted screening is already underway (e-gates, automated manifest analysis), which may modestly reduce headcount at high-volume checkpoints, but the core enforcement role remains human-dependent for the foreseeable future.

title: Fachkräfte in der Steuerverwaltung
rationale: AI can already inspect tax returns, flag discrepancies, calculate liabilities, and cross-reference financial records with high accuracy. Human judgment remains essential for contested cases, fraud investigations, and legally binding enforcement decisions.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The core work product of this occupation is information-processing on structured financial documents — exactly where 2026 AI excels. Essential skills such as 'inspect tax returns,' 'calculate tax,' 'inspect taxation documents,' 'monitor financial accounts,' and 'perform debt investigation' are all cognitive operations on structured digital data. Tax authorities in multiple countries (e.g., ESTV in Switzerland, HMRC in the UK, IRS in the US) already deploy AI-assisted compliance screening that flags anomalies, cross-references declared income against third-party data, and auto-assesses straightforward returns. The optional skill 'conduct financial audits' and 'prepare financial auditing reports' are increasingly supported by AI tools that can ingest large document sets and produce draft findings. 'Respond to enquiries' and 'inform on fiscal duties' map onto well-performing LLM capabilities for tax Q&A. The information-work detection rule applies strongly here: virtually all essential skills describe cognitive operations on data or documents with no physical transformation component.

**Limiting factors:** Several tasks anchor the occupation above the 8–9 tier. 'Law enforcement,' 'handle financial disputes,' 'assess seizable goods,' and 'insolvency law' involve legally binding decisions with due-process requirements, where human accountability is mandated by Swiss administrative law. Fraud detection in adversarial contexts — where taxpayers actively conceal — requires contextual judgment and investigative discretion that AI still struggles with in novel schemes. 'Assess debtor's financial situation' and 'debt collection techniques' often involve interpersonal negotiation and discretionary leniency that carry legal and reputational weight. Regulatory and institutional adoption barriers in Swiss public administration are also significant: government agencies face procurement constraints, data sovereignty rules, and union agreements that slow AI deployment relative to private-sector equivalents.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the High tier (score 7). The majority of routine, structured tasks — return inspection, tax calculation, document cross-referencing, standard enquiry responses — are demonstrably automatable with current AI, driving productivity amplification and reducing headcount needed for volume processing. However, the legally binding enforcement dimension, adversarial fraud investigation, and public-law accountability requirements preserve a meaningful human role beyond mere oversight. The score does not reach 8 because the enforcement and dispute-handling tasks are not peripheral — they are core to the ISCO definition and constitute a substantial share of the role's legal mandate.

title: Fachkräfte in Sozialverwaltung und -versicherung
rationale: AI can automate eligibility checks, document review, and routine application processing, but fraud investigation, client interviews, and discretionary benefit decisions still require human judgment. The mix of structured information work and interpersonal/legal complexity places this occupation at moderate exposure.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A significant share of core tasks are information-processing operations well-suited to 2026 AI. Examining applications and documents to determine eligibility ('investigate social security applications', 'check official documents') maps directly to AI document-analysis capabilities — large-context models can cross-reference applicant data against regulatory criteria at scale. Routine enquiry responses ('respond to enquiries', 'advise on social security benefits') are increasingly handled by AI-assisted chatbots and decision-support tools already deployed in several European social insurance agencies. Administrative record-keeping, report preparation ('write inspection reports', 'present reports'), and policy compliance checks ('inspect government policy compliance', 'identify policy breach') are largely structured, rule-based tasks where AI can draft outputs for human sign-off. Optional skills like 'manage administrative systems' and 'monitor regulations in social services' further confirm a heavily document- and data-centric work environment.

**Limiting factors:** Several core tasks resist automation. Fraud investigation ('social security inspector' sub-occupation) requires contextual judgment, adversarial reasoning, and often in-person or telephone interviews ('conduct research interview') that AI cannot reliably conduct autonomously — especially where claimants may be evasive or vulnerable. Discretionary decisions on exceptional cases, legally mandated by the ISCO description, require accountability that regulators are unlikely to delegate to AI in the near term. Swiss and EU regulatory frameworks around social security decisions impose strict legal liability, creating strong adoption barriers. Skills like 'protect client interests', 'show empathy', 'show intercultural awareness', and 'establish collaborative relations' reflect a client-facing dimension where trust and human presence remain important. Physical workplace audits ('conduct workplace audits') also require on-site presence.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the boundary between moderate (4–5) and high (6–7) exposure. The core work product is largely informational — eligibility determinations, compliance reports, benefit advice — which is inherently AI-amenable. However, the legal accountability requirements, fraud investigation complexity, and interpersonal client-protection duties provide meaningful resistance. AI will likely serve as a strong productivity amplifier (fewer officers needed per caseload) rather than a full substitute, placing the score at 5. Uncertainty stems from how quickly Swiss social insurance agencies adopt AI-assisted case management platforms, which are already in pilot in comparable European systems.

title: Fachkräfte bei staatlichen Pass-, Lizenz- und Genehmigungsstellen
rationale: AI can automate document checking, application routing, and correspondence drafting, but final licensing decisions require legal judgment, fraud detection, and accountability under public law. Human oversight remains essential for exceptions, discretionary rulings, and public trust in government authority.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A significant share of this occupation's core tasks are information-processing operations well-suited to 2026 AI. 'Process applications,' 'check official documents,' 'keep records of passports,' 'manage licensing fees,' 'correspond with licence applicants,' and 'prepare licence agreements' are largely document-handling and structured data tasks that AI can perform at high quality today. Optical character recognition, document classification, and LLM-based correspondence drafting are commercially deployed in government contexts. 'Respond to enquiries' and 'apply technical communication skills' map onto AI chatbot capabilities already used in public-sector portals. 'Administer and score tests' for licensing is also highly automatable. Collectively, these routine processing tasks likely represent 40–50% of daily work volume.

**Limiting factors:** The occupation's core value lies in legal judgment and accountability under public law. 'Assess licence applications,' 'assess breaches of licence agreements,' 'fraud detection,' 'grant concessions,' 'apply immigration law,' and 'monitor compliance with licensing agreements' all require discretionary interpretation of regulations in ambiguous cases — exactly where 2026 AI still struggles with reliability and where errors carry legal consequences. Government licensing is also subject to strict regulatory and procedural requirements: decisions must be defensible, auditable, and often appealable. The 'referring exceptional or important cases to senior officials' task implies a human judgment layer that cannot be fully delegated to AI without institutional and legal reform. Physical document verification and in-person identity checks (passport issuance) add a further barrier.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the moderate tier (score 5). AI will substantially accelerate throughput on routine application processing, document checking, and correspondence, likely reducing headcount needs over time through productivity amplification rather than direct substitution. However, the legal accountability structure, discretionary judgment in edge cases, fraud detection requiring contextual reasoning, and public-sector adoption inertia prevent a higher score. The heterogeneity across sub-occupations (passport officer vs. immigration adviser vs. business licensing) introduces some uncertainty — immigration advisers face higher exposure than building permit officers — which moderates confidence.

title: Polizeikommissare und Kriminalbeamte
rationale: AI can help analyse documents, search databases, and draft reports, but core detective work—interviewing suspects, preserving crime scenes, making arrests—requires physical presence and human judgment. The job stays at moderate exposure because most high-value tasks cannot be done remotely or algorithmically.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** Several tasks in this occupation are meaningfully AI-assisted in 2026. Document and computer file analysis (listed under task b) is well within current AI capability—large-context models can process case files, financial records, and digital communications at scale. Report writing ('write work-related reports') is substantially automatable; AI drafting tools already assist law enforcement agencies with incident reports. Database-driven investigative research ('investigation research methods,' 'forensic intelligence,' 'evaluate forensic data') benefits from AI pattern recognition—tools like Palantir and similar platforms already aggregate and surface connections across large datasets. Blood pattern analysis and fingerprint detection (optional skills) are increasingly handled by computer vision systems. These information-processing and analytical subtasks represent perhaps 25–35% of the role's total task load.

**Limiting factors:** The core work product of this occupation is not a digital artifact—it is a resolved investigation, a lawful arrest, and admissible evidence. Crime scene preservation, physical evidence handling, and restricting access to scenes require physical presence and trained judgment in unstructured environments. Witness and suspect interviews ('conduct research interview,' 'hear witness accounts') depend on interpersonal dynamics, deception detection, and rapport-building that AI cannot replicate in operational settings. Making arrests and applying legal use-of-force are legally and physically human-only tasks. Testifying in court requires personal accountability and cross-examination resilience. Regulatory and evidentiary standards (chain of custody, admissibility rules) create strong adoption barriers: AI-generated analysis must be validated and defended by a human officer. Managing staff and leading investigations involve organizational authority that cannot be delegated to AI agents.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the boundary between Tier 2 (Low) and Tier 3 (Moderate). The analytical and documentation subtasks are genuinely AI-assisted, pushing it above a pure physical-labor score. However, the majority of high-stakes, legally consequential tasks—physical scene work, arrests, interviews, court testimony—are firmly human-anchored. AI functions as a productivity amplifier for the information-processing minority of the role, not as a substitute for the investigative core. A score of 4 reflects meaningful but bounded exposure, with uncertainty stemming from how rapidly AI-assisted investigative platforms (predictive policing, automated evidence analysis) are adopted within Swiss law enforcement.

title: Fachkräfte in der öffentlichen Verwaltung, anderweitig nicht genannt
rationale: AI can help draft inspection reports, analyse compliance documents, and flag anomalies in data, but the core work involves physical site visits, on-the-spot judgment, and legal enforcement authority. The job's irreducible field component keeps AI exposure moderate.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A meaningful portion of this occupation's tasks involve information processing that AI handles well in 2026. Writing inspection reports, analysing work-related written reports, preparing freight shipment reports, investigating complaints, and advising on government policy compliance are all document-centric tasks where large-language-model tools can draft, summarise, and cross-reference regulatory text at near-professional quality.
