Fraud detection and audit techniques benefit from AI pattern-recognition in structured datasets. The optional skills list includes analysing environmental data, compiling legal documents, and assessing environmental impact — all tasks where AI tools are commercially deployed. For sub-occupations like consumer rights advisor, AI chatbots already handle a large share of first-line consumer complaint triage and rights information delivery.

**Limiting factors:** The primary work product of most sub-occupations in this group is a physical inspection act with legal standing — examining premises, collecting samples, observing loading operations, monitoring fishing vessels at sea, or walking a forestry site. These require physical presence, sensory judgment (quality of fish products, deterioration, hazardous freight conditions), and the legal authority of a credentialed inspector. Tasks such as 'work in outdoor conditions,' 'survive at sea in the event of ship abandonment,' 'handle fish products,' 'use rigging tools,' and 'extinguish fires' are entirely outside 2026 AI capability. Regulatory enforcement also carries accountability and chain-of-custody requirements that mandate a human officer. The breadth of sub-occupations — from gaming inspector to fisheries observer to weights and measures inspector — means physical and interpersonal demands vary but are present in all cases.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the low end of moderate exposure (score 4). AI meaningfully assists the documentation, reporting, and advisory subtasks that accompany inspections, and could reduce time spent on paperwork by 30–50%. However, the core deliverable — a legally valid, on-site inspection conducted by an authorised officer — cannot be substituted by AI in 2026. Productivity amplification is real but substitution is limited. Confidence is low because the ISCO group aggregates highly heterogeneous sub-occupations: a consumer rights advisor working entirely via phone and computer faces substantially higher exposure than a fisheries observer deployed on a vessel at sea.

title: Fachkräfte für Rechts- und verwandte Angelegenheiten
rationale: AI can automate legal research, draft standard documents like contracts and pleadings, and review property records with high accuracy. However, physical duties like serving court orders, maintaining courtroom order, escorting defendants, and conducting field investigations resist automation.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A substantial portion of this occupation's core tasks are information-processing work highly amenable to 2026 AI. Legal research (statutes, case law, precedents) is now largely automatable — tools like Harvey, Lexis+ AI, and Westlaw AI perform this at near-professional quality. Drafting standard legal documents (contracts, wills, pleadings, conveyancing documents, loan conditions) is well within current LLM capability, especially for templated or routine instruments. Document review tasks — examining mortgages, liens, easements, title chains — are already being automated by AI-powered title search platforms. Case management administration (tracking deadlines, summarizing case files, managing correspondence) maps directly onto agentic workflow tools. The essential skills 'legal research,' 'compile legal documents,' 'revise legal documents,' 'manage contracts,' 'conveyancing,' and 'record court procedures' are all substantially AI-performable today.

**Limiting factors:** A significant cluster of tasks in this occupation is physically grounded and resists AI substitution. Court bailiffs and enforcement officers physically maintain order, escort defendants, serve summonses, repossess goods, and conduct public auctions — none of which can be delegated to AI in 2026. Private detectives conduct field surveillance, interviews, and physical investigations. Skills like 'restrain individuals,' 'conduct frisk,' 'conduct security screenings,' 'escort defendants,' and 'ensure public safety and security' are firmly human. Even for the document-heavy sub-roles, Swiss legal practice involves regulatory compliance, notarial authentication requirements, and client-facing advisory interactions that carry trust and liability constraints slowing AI adoption. The occupation spans highly heterogeneous sub-roles (court bailiff vs. legal assistant vs. private detective), meaning AI exposure varies dramatically by sub-occupation.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the boundary of the High tier (6-7). The information-work sub-roles (legal assistant, conveyance clerk, case administrator, court administrative officer) are exposed at the 7-8 level, while the physically-grounded sub-roles (court bailiff, court enforcement officer, private detective) sit closer to 3-4. The blended score of 6 reflects this heterogeneity. The primary uncertainty is adoption pace in Swiss courts, which are institutionally conservative, and whether AI productivity gains translate to workforce reduction or role augmentation.

title: Soziale Fachkräfte
rationale: AI can assist with documentation, referrals, and information lookup, but the core work—crisis intervention, hands-on support for vulnerable people, and trust-based counselling relationships—requires human presence and judgment. The job is fundamentally interpersonal and physical, keeping AI exposure low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A meaningful subset of tasks in this occupation involve information processing that AI can support. Maintaining records of work with service users, reporting on social development, reviewing social service plans, and referring service users to community resources are administrative and documentation tasks where AI tools (case management software with AI assistance, automated report drafting) can reduce workload. Telephone-based services such as crisis helpline operations could see AI-assisted triage or scripted support. Scheduling, appointment administration, and compliance with organisational guidelines are also partially automatable. These tasks represent perhaps 20–30% of the working day for a typical social care worker or social work assistant.

**Limiting factors:** The dominant share of core tasks in this occupation are deeply resistant to AI substitution. Crisis intervention, counselling clients, managing social crises, building helping relationships, and providing hands-on support to disabled or elderly individuals require physical presence, real-time emotional attunement, and trust that clients extend to humans—not software. Tasks like assisting clients with physical disabilities in community activities, providing emergency shelter services, supervising group home residents, and conducting face-to-face interviews in social services cannot be delegated to AI agents in 2026. Regulatory and safeguarding requirements (child protection, legal guardianship, risk assessment of vulnerable clients) impose accountability structures that mandate human professionals. The population served—people in crisis, with disabilities, or in vulnerable circumstances—also creates strong ethical and trust-based barriers to AI substitution in direct service delivery.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). AI functions as an administrative aid—reducing paperwork burden and supporting information retrieval—but cannot substitute for the relational, physical, and judgment-intensive core of the work. The primary work product is human welfare delivered through interpersonal contact, not a digital artifact. Adoption of AI tools will likely improve efficiency at the margins (documentation, scheduling, resource lookup) without reducing headcount meaningfully, placing this closer to complementarity than substitution.

title: Ordensbrüder/-schwestern und Seelsorgehelfer
rationale: AI cannot perform religious ceremonies, provide genuine spiritual counselling, or embody a monastic vow — the core of this work is human presence, faith, and relational trust. The job is deeply interpersonal and physically enacted, leaving almost no room for AI substitution.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A small subset of tasks involve information processing that AI can assist with. Interpreting religious texts, developing educational resources, responding to enquiries, and managing administrative systems (budgetary principles, fundraising, scheduling) are all digitally performable at some level. AI tools can summarise scripture, draft sermons, answer doctrinal questions, and handle correspondence. These auxiliary tasks represent perhaps 15–20% of the role's time and are already partially supported by digital tools, but they are not the core work product.

**Limiting factors:** The primary work product of this occupation is relational and embodied: performing religious ceremonies, providing spiritual counselling, building community relations, showing empathy, mentoring individuals, and living a monastic lifestyle. These tasks require physical presence, personal trust, and authentic human witness — qualities that AI cannot replicate in any meaningful pastoral sense. Confidentiality obligations and the deeply personal nature of spiritual guidance create strong trust barriers to AI substitution. Crisis intervention, charity service delivery (food, clothing, shelter), and community-building are inherently physical and interpersonal. The monastic sub-occupation involves a lived vocation that is definitionally non-automatable. Regulatory and ecclesiastical structures further insulate the role from AI displacement.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low (2) tier. AI can assist with peripheral administrative and text-based tasks but cannot substitute for the human presence, relational depth, and embodied faith practice that define the role. The limiting factors are not merely practical but ontological — the value delivered is inseparable from the humanity of the person delivering it. Uncertainty is low; this is one of the clearest cases of AI-resistant work.

title: Athleten und Berufssportler
rationale: AI cannot run, swim, kick, or compete physically — the entire job is performing athletic feats in the real world against other humans. The product is a live physical performance, which AI cannot replicate or replace.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A small subset of tasks have AI relevance. 'Assess performance in sport events' and 'assess other competitors and conditions at venues' involve pattern recognition and data analysis where AI tools (video analysis, opponent scouting software, biometric monitoring) are already deployed. 'Communicate with media' and 'manage personal finances' are peripheral tasks where AI can assist with drafting statements or financial planning. 'Decide on strategies in consultation with coaches' benefits from AI-powered analytics. These are real productivity amplifiers but remain auxiliary to the core job.

**Limiting factors:** The primary work product — physically competing in sporting events — is entirely irreplaceable by AI. Every essential skill from the ESCO list ('participate in sport events,' 'implement relevant technical skills to perform at the highest level,' 'work to develop physical ability') requires a human body performing in the physical world. The commercial value of professional sport is inseparable from human athletic achievement and the spectacle of human competition; audiences pay to watch humans, not machines. Even chess, listed as an example occupation, retains human professional competition precisely because the human element is the product. There are no regulatory, safety, or trust barriers to overcome — the physical impossibility is absolute.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the 0–1 tier. AI tools provide marginal support in analytics, media management, and financial administration, but these are peripheral to the core output. The job is defined by physical human performance in competitive contexts, which is categorically outside AI's substitution range. The score of 1 (rather than 0) reflects the genuine but minor role of AI-assisted performance analytics and scouting tools that are increasingly standard in professional sport.

title: Sportlehrer, Sporttrainer und Sportfunktionäre
rationale: AI can assist with performance analytics, training plan generation, and officiating support, but the core work—physically demonstrating techniques, motivating athletes in real time, and making split-second officiating calls—requires embodied presence. The job is fundamentally interpersonal and physical, keeping AI exposure low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A meaningful subset of tasks in this occupation involves information processing that AI can support. Performance analysis (identifying strengths/weaknesses, monitoring technique, evaluating competition results) is increasingly assisted by computer vision and sports analytics platforms—tools like Hudl, Catapult, and AI-powered video analysis are already commercially deployed. Training programme planning and scheduling (tasks c, f, g) involve structured data manipulation that AI can partially automate. Compiling scores and athletic records (tasks i, k) are highly automatable. Optional skills like 'apply latest sport science findings,' 'develop competitive strategies in sport,' and 'assess sportive performance' map onto AI-assisted research and analytics workflows. These analytical subtasks represent perhaps 20–30% of the role.

**Limiting factors:** The dominant share of this occupation's core work is irreducibly physical and interpersonal. Demonstrating techniques ('demonstrate when teaching,' 'swim,' 'be in touch with your body'), physically assisting students with equipment, motivating athletes in real time, and officiating live events all require embodied presence that 2026 robotics and AI cannot replicate in unstructured, dynamic sporting environments. Officiating roles demand split-second judgment in ambiguous, high-stakes situations with social legitimacy requirements—referees are trusted partly because they are human and accountable. Safety responsibilities ('guarantee students' safety,' 'first aid,' 'ensure public safety and security') carry regulatory and liability dimensions that strongly resist AI substitution. The strong interpersonal dimension—managing relationships with athletes, parents, and coaching teams, working with broad personality types—further anchors the role in human interaction. Adoption barriers are high: sports governing bodies, liability frameworks, and participant expectations all favor human coaches and officials.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). AI tools provide genuine but peripheral support for analytics, scheduling, and record-keeping, but these are auxiliary to the core work of physical instruction, real-time motivation, and live officiating. The primary work product is not a digital artifact but a trained, motivated athlete or a fairly administered competition—both requiring sustained human physical and social presence. The score reflects demonstrated AI capability in the analytical fringe of the role, not the embodied core. Confidence is medium because the occupation spans heterogeneous sub-roles (e.g., a data-heavy elite performance analyst coach vs. a swimming instructor), and the analytics-heavy end of elite coaching could score somewhat higher.

title: Trainer und Betreuer im Bereich Fitness und Erholung
rationale: AI cannot demonstrate a squat, spot a client lifting weights, lead a group hike, or physically guide a horse rider — the core work is embodied, real-time, and safety-critical. The job is anchored in physical presence and human motivation, keeping AI exposure low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A modest subset of tasks involves information processing that AI can assist with. Collecting client fitness information, designing sports programmes, integrating exercise science into programme design, and providing tourism-related information are all tasks where AI tools (chatbots, planning software, fitness apps) can generate drafts or recommendations. Monitoring developments in the field of expertise and promoting healthy lifestyle content can be partially supported by AI-generated materials. Administrative tasks such as handling tour contract details, registering visitors, and collecting visitor fees are candidates for automation. These peripheral tasks represent perhaps 15–20% of the role's actual time and effort.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of core tasks are physically embodied and interpersonally immediate. Demonstrating body movements, correcting fitness customers in real time, instructing horse riding, guiding mountain expeditions, managing groups outdoors, and reacting to unexpected events in outdoor environments all require physical presence, situational awareness, and rapid human judgment. Safety monitoring — guaranteeing students' safety, applying risk management in sports, providing first aid — carries regulatory and liability weight that precludes AI substitution. Motivating clients, supporting children's wellbeing, and managing group dynamics depend on emotional attunement that 2026 AI cannot replicate in a physical setting. Robotics capable of operating in unstructured outdoor and gym environments at the required dexterity and responsiveness do not exist at commercial scale. The auxiliary digital tools (scheduling, client records) are genuinely auxiliary: if the software broke, 90%+ of the job would still need to happen in person.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). AI can marginally assist with programme planning, content generation, and administrative logistics, but the primary work product is a live, physical, safety-sensitive human experience. Adoption barriers are high: outdoor and gym environments are unstructured, client safety is paramount, and the interpersonal trust relationship is central to the service. The score of 3 reflects meaningful but peripheral AI assistance rather than any substitution of core work.

title: Fotografen
rationale: AI can now generate photorealistic images, automate photo editing, and handle retouching tasks that once required skilled post-processing work. However, operating cameras on location, directing subjects, and building client relationships still require a human presence, keeping the score moderate.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A significant share of photography's post-production pipeline is now AI-automatable. Tasks like 'create digital images,' 'select photos,' 'edit photographs,' and 'adapt existing photographic images to create new digitized images' are directly addressed by tools like Adobe Firefly, Lightroom AI, and Midjourney. AI-powered culling tools (e.g., Imagen AI) can select and batch-edit thousands of images in minutes, compressing work that once took hours. For commercial and advertising photography, generative AI can now produce product shots and lifestyle imagery at near-professional quality, directly threatening the commercial photography segment. The 'write to a deadline' and 'create online news content' skills for photojournalists are also partially AI-assisted. Optional skills like 'edit digital moving images,' 'audio editing software,' and 'digital marketing techniques' are all in domains with strong AI tooling.

**Limiting factors:** The core physical act of photography — being present at a news event, a wedding, or an industrial site — cannot be automated by current AI. Skills like 'operate a camera,' 'set up photographic equipment,' 'create artificial light,' 'determine image composition,' and 'conduct extensive international travel' require physical presence and real-time human judgment. Portrait and event photography depend heavily on interpersonal skills: directing subjects, building rapport, and reading a room. Photojournalism specifically requires 'follow ethical code of conduct of journalists,' 'build contacts to maintain news flow,' and 'interview people' — tasks grounded in trust, access, and accountability that AI cannot replicate. Regulatory and ethical constraints around AI-generated news imagery also slow displacement in the journalism segment. Additionally, clients in high-stakes contexts (weddings, corporate events) still strongly prefer human photographers.

**Net assessment:** Photography sits at the boundary of High (6-7) exposure. The post-production and commercial stock photography segments face severe AI disruption — generative AI has already materially contracted the stock photo market. However, the capture side of the profession (being on location, directing subjects, managing lighting in real environments) remains firmly human. The occupation is bifurcating: commodity commercial and stock work is being rapidly automated, while event, portrait, and documentary photography retains human value. A score of 6 reflects this split — substantial disruption to a large share of tasks and sub-occupations, but a durable core that resists full automation. Confidence is medium because outcomes vary sharply by sub-specialization.

title: Raumgestalter und Dekorateure
rationale: AI can generate mood boards, draft floor plans, and produce design concepts, but the core work—physically coordinating construction, selecting materials in person, and negotiating with clients and suppliers—resists automation. The mix of digital design tasks and hands-on physical execution places this occupation in the high-exposure tier, not very high.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A significant share of the listed tasks are AI-performable at 2026 capability levels. 'Create mood boards,' 'develop design concept,' 'conduct research on trends in design,' 'prepare detailed working drawings for interior design,' 'translate requirement concepts into visual design,' and 'graphic design' are all tasks where generative AI tools (Midjourney, Adobe Firefly, AutoCAD Copilot, spatial AI tools) already produce commercially usable outputs. 'Use specialised design software' and 'monitor trends in interior design' are increasingly AI-augmented. 'Carry out sales analysis,' 'perform market research,' and 'keep promotions records' are largely automatable information-processing tasks. For the visual merchandising and display decorator sub-occupations especially, AI-generated planograms and virtual store layouts are already in commercial deployment. The 'present detailed design proposals' and 'draw prop sketches' tasks are substantially accelerated by AI, meaning fewer hours per deliverable and thus fewer workers needed.

**Limiting factors:** The occupation's core output is not a digital artifact but a physically realized space or display. Tasks like 'build set constructions,' 'build miniature sets,' 'paint sets,' 'maintain theatre sets,' 'stock shelves,' 'assemble visual displays,' and 'change window displays' require physical presence and manual dexterity in unstructured environments—well beyond 2026 robotics capabilities. 'Negotiate terms with suppliers,' 'maintain relationship with customers,' 'consult with production director,' and 'coach staff for running the performance' involve interpersonal trust and real-time judgment that AI cannot substitute. 'Follow safety procedures when working at heights' and 'work safely with chemicals/machines' anchor a meaningful portion of the role in physical, safety-regulated environments. The set designer and set builder sub-occupations are particularly physical. Client-facing creative direction—understanding a client's unstated aesthetic preferences and translating them into a coherent spatial experience—remains a human-advantaged task.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the boundary of moderate and high exposure (score 6). The digital design and trend-monitoring tasks are substantially AI-augmentable, and productivity amplification is real—a single designer can produce more concepts faster with AI tools, reducing headcount demand. However, the physical coordination, on-site execution, supplier negotiation, and client relationship management that constitute the majority of billable hours resist substitution. The heterogeneity of sub-occupations (visual merchandiser vs. set builder vs. interior designer) introduces uncertainty: visual merchandisers and interior designers skew higher (~7), while set builders and miniature set designers skew lower (~4-5). A blended score of 6 reflects this spread, with medium confidence given the occupational diversity.

title: Fachkräfte in Kunstgalerien, Museen und Bibliotheken
rationale: AI can automate cataloguing, metadata management, and database work, but core tasks like physically handling artworks, mounting exhibits, and taxidermy require skilled hands-on work. The occupation spans very different sub-roles, pulling the score in opposite directions.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** This ISCO group is unusually heterogeneous, but several task clusters are meaningfully AI-exposed. Cataloguing printed and recorded material, entering data into databases, searching and verifying bibliographic data, and managing content metadata are classic information-processing tasks where AI tools (LLMs, computer vision for digitised collections, automated metadata extraction) already perform at acceptable quality. The big data archive librarian sub-occupation is heavily digital: skills like 'analyse big data,' 'data extraction transformation and loading tools,' 'manage digital archives,' 'query languages,' and the extensive optional skill list (SQL, Oracle, PostgreSQL, SPARQL, IBM InfoSphere) confirm that this sub-role is almost entirely information work. AI-assisted cataloguing, automated metadata tagging, and database query generation are commercially deployed in 2026. For library technician tasks, AI can handle circulation system management, bibliographic verification, and correspondence drafting with high reliability.

**Limiting factors:** The gallery technician and museum technician sub-roles are anchored in physical, hands-on work that AI cannot perform: mounting and preparing objects for display, designing and arranging exhibit furnishings, receiving and packing exhibits, assessing object condition in person, and supervising artefact movement all require physical presence, fine motor skill, and contextual judgment about fragile or irreplaceable objects. Taxidermists—skinning animals, cleaning body parts, creating and finishing animal structures—represent near-zero AI substitution; this is skilled physical craft. Conservation techniques and ensuring exhibition safety involve liability and professional judgment that institutions are unlikely to delegate to AI. Regulatory compliance (animal welfare legislation, legal regulations for cultural property) adds institutional friction to automation.

**Net assessment:** The occupation sits at moderate exposure (5) because it is genuinely split. The big data archive librarian sub-role would score 7–8 in isolation, while taxidermist would score 1–2 and gallery/museum technician around 3–4. Averaged across the ISCO group, the digital cataloguing and database management tasks are substantially AI-exposed, but the physical handling, conservation, and craft tasks are firmly resistant. Confidence is low precisely because of this heterogeneity—the score masks a wide internal range, and the actual exposure of any individual worker depends heavily on which sub-occupation they occupy.

title: Küchenchefs, stv. Küchenchefs und Souschefs
rationale: AI can help plan menus or suggest recipes, but the core work—cooking, tasting, and leading a kitchen team in real time—requires physical skill and sensory judgment no AI can replicate in 2026. The job is anchored in physical transformation of food and hands-on supervision, keeping exposure very low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A narrow slice of this role involves information-processing tasks where AI offers genuine assistance. Menu planning, recipe compilation, cost estimation, procurement scheduling, and shift scheduling are all cognitive-administrative tasks that AI tools can meaningfully accelerate. AI can generate recipe ideas, flag nutritional properties, draft supplier orders, and even suggest price points based on cost inputs. These map to ESCO skills like 'plan menus,' 'compile cooking recipes,' 'set prices of menu items,' 'order supplies,' and 'schedule shifts.' Collectively, these administrative and planning tasks might represent 15–20% of a head chef's working time, and AI can handle a meaningful share of them at acceptable quality today.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of this occupation's core work is irreducibly physical and sensory. Skills like 'use cooking techniques,' 'use culinary finishing techniques,' 'use food preparation techniques,' 'use food cutting tools,' 'maintain kitchen equipment at correct temperature,' and 'execute chilling processes' all require physical manipulation of ingredients in an unstructured, dynamic environment. Robotic kitchen systems exist in narrow fast-food contexts but are nowhere near the dexterity, adaptability, and sensory feedback required for complex or specialty cuisine. Critically, 'think creatively about food and beverages,' 'create decorative food displays,' and 'monitor quality of dishes at all stages' depend on taste, smell, and tactile judgment—sensory modalities AI cannot access. The supervisory and interpersonal core—'instruct kitchen personnel,' 'manage staff,' 'maintain customer service,' 'work in a hospitality team'—requires real-time human presence and authority in a high-pressure physical environment. Hygiene enforcement and equipment inspection similarly require physical presence and contextual judgment.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 2). AI provides modest productivity gains in the planning and administrative fringe of the role, but the primary work product is physically prepared food delivered in a live kitchen environment. The auxiliary tool rule applies strongly: if every computer in the restaurant broke, 90%+ of a head chef's job would continue unchanged. There are no meaningful regulatory or trust barriers to consider because the technology simply cannot perform the core tasks. The score of 2 reflects genuine but narrow AI utility in scheduling and recipe ideation, without any substitution risk for the physical and sensory heart of the occupation.

title: Sonstige Fachkräfte in Gestaltung und Kultur
rationale: Most work in this group involves physical setup, operation, and coordination on live sets and stages — rigging lights, operating pyrotechnics, managing props, performing stunts — tasks AI cannot physically execute. The few information-processing subtasks (scheduling, script supervision, programme coordination) have some AI exposure, but the dominant work is hands-on and presence-dependent.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A subset of roles in this heterogeneous group involves information-processing tasks with meaningful AI exposure. Script supervisors checking continuity, broadcasting programme directors building schedules, location managers handling logistics and paperwork, and assistant directors managing scheduling and planning all work substantially with documents, data, and coordination tasks that 2026 AI tools (agentic scheduling, document analysis, continuity-checking software) can partially automate. Skills like 'analyse a script,' 'prepare continuity reports,' 'develop programming schedule,' 'handle paperwork,' and 'draw up stage layouts digitally' represent digitally-mediated work where AI can provide genuine productivity amplification. Body artists (tattooing, piercing) have some AI-assisted design tools for pattern generation.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of essential skills in this group describe irreducibly physical, real-time, and safety-critical work. Lighting technicians rig, focus, and operate equipment in live environments; theatre technicians build and break down stages; pyrotechnicians operate explosive devices under strict safety protocols; stunt performers execute dangerous physical actions; dressers perform costume changes on moving performers; prompters cue live actors; fly bar operators control moving stage machinery. Skills like 'perform stunts,' 'build pyrotechnical devices,' 'maintain flying harnesses,' 'evacuate people from heights,' 'react to emergency situations in a live performance environment,' and 'work safely with pyrotechnical materials' are firmly in the domain of physical human presence. Regulatory barriers (pyrotechnic permits, safety certifications) and the unstructured, dynamic nature of live performance environments further insulate these roles. Robotics at 2026 capability levels cannot operate in these complex, human-dense, safety-critical physical environments.

**Net assessment:** This ISCO group is a catch-all for a wide range of physical production and performance-support roles. The median occupation here — lighting technician, stage technician, prop master, stunt performer, dresser — is firmly in the Low tier (2-3), with physical presence, manual dexterity, real-time responsiveness, and safety accountability as core requirements. The small minority of more information-intensive roles (script supervisor, programme coordinator, location manager) pull the score slightly upward. The aggregate lands at 3, reflecting that AI offers modest productivity tools for the administrative and planning dimensions of some roles, but cannot substitute for the dominant physical and live-performance work. Confidence is medium because the group's heterogeneity means individual sub-occupations span roughly 2-6 on the scale.

title: Techniker für den Betrieb von Informations- und Kommunikationstechnologie und für die Anwenderbetreuung, onA
rationale: AI can handle routine IT support tickets, diagnose common software errors, and automate repetitive system monitoring tasks. However, hands-on hardware troubleshooting, on-site infrastructure work, and complex user interactions keep this role partially human-dependent.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** ICT operations and user support technicians spend a significant share of their time on tasks that 2026 AI handles well: triaging and resolving Tier-1 helpdesk tickets, drafting knowledge-base articles, monitoring system logs for anomalies, and walking users through standard troubleshooting scripts. AI-powered helpdesk tools (e.g., ServiceNow AI, Zendesk AI) already automate 40–60% of routine ticket resolution in enterprise environments. Password resets, software installation guidance, and connectivity diagnostics are near-fully automatable. Agentic workflows can also handle patch management scheduling, alert correlation, and routine reporting — tasks central to the 'operations' half of this ISCO group.

**Limiting factors:** A meaningful portion of this role involves physical or on-site work: replacing hardware components, running cables, configuring physical network equipment, and visiting end-user desks. These tasks resist AI substitution entirely in 2026. Beyond the physical dimension, complex or novel incidents — multi-system outages, security breaches, or bespoke enterprise configurations — require contextual judgment that current AI tools handle poorly without significant human oversight. User trust and interpersonal communication (calming frustrated users, translating technical issues for non-technical stakeholders) also remain human-advantaged. Regulatory and security constraints in Swiss enterprise environments further slow autonomous AI deployment in IT operations.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the boundary of the 'High' tier (6–7). AI clearly automates the majority of routine Tier-1 support and monitoring tasks, but the physical infrastructure work and complex incident response anchor a substantial human role. The score of 6 reflects that AI is a strong productivity amplifier and partial substitutor here — reducing headcount needs for routine support — but not yet capable of replacing the full role. Uncertainty stems from the heterogeneity of the ISCO group: a pure helpdesk agent scores closer to 8, while a network operations technician with heavy physical duties scores closer to 4.

title: Techniker für den Betrieb von Informations- und Kommunikationstechnologie
rationale: AI can automate routine monitoring, alert triage, query writing, and performance reporting that make up a large share of this role. However, hands-on server maintenance, physical hardware handling, and escalation judgment keep full automation limited.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A significant portion of this role is information-processing work performed in digital environments. Tasks like monitoring system performance, analysing ICT systems, maintaining database performance, and providing technical documentation are well within 2026 AI capabilities — AIOps platforms already automate anomaly detection, alert correlation, and incident triage at production scale. The extensive optional skill list (SQL Server, MySQL, PostgreSQL, DB2, SPARQL, XQuery, LINQ, MDX, N1QL) confirms the work is conducted almost entirely in digital environments. Query writing and optimisation — a core competency implied by 'query languages' and 'resource description framework query language' as essential skills — is now largely automatable via AI coding assistants. Migrating existing data and developing automated migration methods are tasks where AI-assisted scripting tools already handle substantial workloads. Balancing database resources and managing database configurations are increasingly handled by cloud-native automation and AI-driven tuning tools.

**Limiting factors:** Several tasks resist straightforward AI substitution. Maintaining ICT servers and loading peripheral equipment involve physical presence and hands-on hardware interaction that robotic systems cannot yet reliably perform in unstructured data-centre environments. Developing contingency plans for emergencies requires contextual judgment about organisational risk, vendor relationships, and regulatory constraints that AI supports but does not replace. Escalation decisions — determining when to notify supervisors versus self-resolve — require situational awareness and accountability that organisations are reluctant to delegate fully to AI. Database security maintenance involves compliance and audit responsibilities where human sign-off remains a regulatory and liability requirement in Switzerland. The role also spans heterogeneous environments (legacy mainframe operators vs. modern cloud DBAs), creating uncertainty about how uniformly AI tools apply.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits solidly in the High tier (6). AI tools already automate the most repetitive monitoring, query, and reporting subtasks, and AIOps adoption is accelerating. However, the physical server maintenance component, the regulatory accountability around security and contingency planning, and the need for contextual escalation judgment prevent this from reaching the Very High tier. The score reflects a role where AI meaningfully reduces headcount needs and reshapes daily workflows, but where a skilled human technician remains necessary for exception handling, physical operations, and organisational trust — particularly in Swiss enterprise and regulated-sector contexts.

title: Techniker für die Anwenderbetreuung in der Informations- und Kommunikationstechnologie
rationale: AI can now handle a large share of routine help desk queries, ticket triage, and basic troubleshooting scripts, but physical hardware repair, on-site installation, and complex network diagnostics still require human presence. The role splits between automatable remote support tasks and hands-on work that resists AI substitution.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A significant portion of this occupation's core tasks are already being automated or augmented by AI in 2026. Answering user inquiries (task a), consulting technical manuals to implement solutions (task h), maintaining records of transactions and remedial actions (task f), and prioritising/triaging support requests via ticketing systems are all highly amenable to AI. LLM-powered chatbots and agentic help desk tools (e.g., integrated into ServiceNow, Zendesk) can resolve Tier-1 and many Tier-2 tickets autonomously — password resets, software configuration guidance, connectivity diagnostics — which represent the bulk of help desk volume. Skills like 'use ICT ticketing system,' 'provide ICT support,' 'perform ICT troubleshooting,' 'guarantee customer satisfaction,' and 'keep task records' map directly onto what AI agents demonstrably handle today. The optional skills list is dominated by programming languages and digital platforms, confirming this is fundamentally information-work conducted in a digital environment. AI-driven monitoring tools also increasingly cover 'oversee daily performance of communications and computer systems' (task d) and 'identify ICT system weaknesses.'

**Limiting factors:** A meaningful portion of the role is physically grounded and resists AI substitution. Tasks (c), (e), and the repair-oriented skills ('repair ICT devices,' 'use precision tools,' 'use repair manuals,' 'install and perform minor repairs to hardware') require physical presence and dexterous manipulation that 2026 robotics cannot reliably replicate in unstructured office environments. On-site cable installation, peripheral setup, and server maintenance ('maintain ICT server') are similarly physical. The ICT security technician sub-occupation adds regulatory and judgment-intensive work — defining firewall rules, managing access control, advising on security legislation — where human accountability and contextual judgment remain essential. The help desk manager sub-occupation involves staff management, workload forecasting, and organisational coordination that AI supports but does not replace. Trust and interpersonal communication with non-technical users also provide some friction against full automation.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits solidly in the High tier (score 6). The remote/digital support tasks — which constitute the majority of help desk volume — are substantially automatable, driving AI-assisted productivity gains that reduce headcount needs. However, the physical installation and repair tasks, security judgment work, and managerial sub-occupations prevent a Very High rating. The heterogeneity across sub-occupations (ICT technician vs. help desk agent vs. security technician vs. manager) introduces some uncertainty; a pure help desk agent role would score 7-8, while a field ICT technician focused on hardware would score 4-5. The blended ISCO-3512 category lands at 6, with medium confidence reflecting this internal variance.

title: Techniker für Computernetzwerke und -systeme
rationale: AI can automate network monitoring, diagnostics, and configuration tasks, but physical installation of hardware, cable work, and hands-on troubleshooting in real environments resist automation. The mix of physical and digital work keeps this in the moderate tier.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A significant share of this role involves information-processing tasks that 2026 AI handles well. Network monitoring ('monitor communication channels' performance'), bandwidth analysis ('analyse network bandwidth requirements'), risk analysis ('perform risk analysis'), and documentation ('write work-related reports', 'provide technical documentation') are all tasks where AI-assisted or AI-autonomous tools are commercially deployed. Network management system tools (listed as optional) increasingly incorporate AI-driven anomaly detection and auto-remediation. Configuration management, including 'maintain internet protocol configuration' and 'manage data', is heavily automated through infrastructure-as-code and AI-assisted platforms. Diagnostic workflows ('implement ICT network diagnostic tools', 'ICT debugging tools') are well within 2026 AI agentic capability for known fault patterns.

**Limiting factors:** The core physical tasks of this occupation resist AI substitution at the 2026 frontier. Installing hardware ('install electronic communication equipment', 'install signal repeaters'), using precision tools, and soldering electronics require dexterous manipulation in unstructured physical environments where robotics remain far below human efficiency. The aviation sub-specialisation ('manage flight data communications programme', 'work in an aviation team', 'common aviation safety regulations') introduces strict regulatory and safety certification requirements that slow AI adoption significantly. Hands-on troubleshooting of physical network infrastructure — tracing cable faults, replacing hardware in server rooms or aircraft — demands physical presence and judgment that AI cannot yet replicate. User support ('assisting users with network and data communications problems') also retains a human interpersonal dimension.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the boundary between moderate and high exposure. Roughly 40–50% of core tasks are meaningfully AI-assisted or automatable (monitoring, diagnostics, documentation, configuration), but the physical installation and maintenance work, combined with aviation-specific regulatory constraints, anchors the role in the moderate tier (score 5). The key uncertainty is pace of adoption: as AI-driven network automation matures, the cognitive/analytical tasks will erode further, but the physical and safety-critical dimensions provide a durable floor. A pure network administrator role would score higher; the physical technician component here keeps it at 5.

title: Webmaster
rationale: AI can now generate web pages, write scripts, debug code, and draft documentation at near-professional quality, automating a large share of a webmaster's routine coding and content tasks. However, server administration, security monitoring, and client liaison keep the role from full automation.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The webmaster role is heavily digital — its primary outputs are web pages, server configurations, scripts, and documentation, all of which are information artifacts. AI coding assistants (GitHub Copilot, Cursor) can already handle the bulk of tasks listed under 'use scripting programming,' 'use markup languages,' 'style sheet languages,' 'web programming,' and 'implement front-end website design.' Content development via 'apply tools for content development' and 'authoring software' is similarly AI-augmented, with tools generating copy, images, and layout suggestions. 'Troubleshoot website' and 'study website behaviour patterns' map well onto agentic AI workflows that can parse logs, identify anomalies, and propose fixes. Documentation and policy writing ('developing and maintaining documentation, policies and instructions') is near-fully automatable with 2026 LLMs. The optional skills list — dominated by specific programming languages (JavaScript, CSS, C#, Java, AJAX, ASP.NET) — confirms this is almost entirely digital work.

**Limiting factors:** Several tasks resist straightforward automation. 'Maintain ICT server' and 'perform web server backup and recovery operations' involve system-level administration where AI assists but a human must execute and verify in production environments, especially under failure conditions. 'Developing, coordinating, implementing and monitoring security measures' requires contextual judgment about threat landscapes and organizational risk tolerance that AI cannot fully own. 'Liaising with, and providing guidance to, clients and users' involves interpersonal trust and translating vague business requirements into technical decisions — a task where AI is a tool, not a replacement. Regulatory and security accountability (e.g., GDPR compliance, incident response) also creates adoption friction for full AI substitution.

**Net assessment:** The webmaster role sits solidly in the High tier (6–7). The majority of its core coding, scripting, front-end design, and documentation tasks are already substantially AI-augmented, and productivity amplification means fewer webmasters are needed to maintain the same web infrastructure. The remaining human value lies in server-level administration, security judgment, and client-facing guidance — tasks that are real but not dominant enough to pull the score below 6. The score of 7 reflects that AI handles most routine instances of the work product, but the role has not yet reached the 8+ threshold where human involvement is primarily oversight and exception-handling.

title: Techniker für Rundfunk und audiovisuelle Medien
rationale: AI can automate some editing, quality monitoring, and audio mixing tasks, but the core job involves physically setting up, operating, and repairing specialized broadcast hardware in real environments. The hands-on technical work and live troubleshooting keep AI substitution limited.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** Several tasks in this occupation have meaningful AI exposure. Audio editing software (listed as optional skill) is increasingly AI-assisted — tools like Adobe Podcast, iZotope RX, and Descript automate noise reduction, leveling, and even content-aware editing. 'Monitor quality of broadcasts' and 'assess sound quality' involve pattern recognition that AI vision/audio models handle well in structured monitoring contexts. 'Transfer uncut audiovisual material to computer' and 'assemble video tape footage' are largely automatable through agentic workflows. AI-driven camera tracking and automated switching systems are already deployed in sports and event broadcasting, partially automating 'operate video equipment' and 'operate a camera.' Post-production tasks like color grading and basic mixing are increasingly AI-assisted.

**Limiting factors:** The dominant work product here is not a digital artifact but the reliable operation of physical broadcast infrastructure. Tasks like 'install low voltage wiring,' 'set up broadcast equipment,' 'align antennae with receiving dishes,' 'calibrate electronic instruments,' and 'make emergency repairs to equipment' require physical manipulation in varied, often unstructured environments — exactly where 2026 robotics remain weak. Live broadcast contexts demand real-time human judgment under pressure: diagnosing a failing transmitter mid-broadcast, adapting to unexpected venue conditions, or troubleshooting satellite link failures cannot be delegated to current AI agents. Regulatory and safety standards (health and safety, broadcast licensing) also impose human accountability requirements. The role is fundamentally a physical-technical one, not an information-processing one.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at moderate exposure (score 4). Roughly 30–40% of tasks — particularly in post-production editing, quality monitoring, and routine signal management — are meaningfully AI-assisted or partially automatable. However, the core value delivered is physical infrastructure operation and live technical problem-solving, which AI cannot yet replace. Productivity amplification is real (fewer editors needed per production), but the hands-on installation, maintenance, and emergency repair tasks anchor the occupation well below the high-exposure tier. Uncertainty exists around the pace of AI-assisted broadcast automation in live production environments.

title: Telekommunikationstechniker
rationale: AI can help with documentation, fault diagnosis, and network configuration planning, but the core work involves physically installing, calibrating, and repairing telecommunications hardware on-site. The hands-on, field-based nature of the job keeps AI exposure moderate.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** Several essential skills are meaningfully AI-assisted in 2026. Interpreting technical texts (blueprints, specifications) is well within LLM capability — AI can parse and summarize technical documentation reliably. Managing system testing, quality assurance methodologies, and managing changes in ICT systems involve structured, rule-based workflows where AI agents can generate test plans, flag anomalies, and draft change-management documentation. Scripting and programming tasks (use scripting programming, session border controller configuration) are directly supported by AI coding tools, which handle routine network automation scripts competently. The optional skills list — dominated by programming languages (C++, Java, JavaScript, MATLAB, etc.) — confirms a meaningful digital/software dimension to the role, particularly for technicians working on system integration or firmware. AI-assisted network monitoring and fault prediction tools are already deployed in telecom infrastructure management.

**Limiting factors:** The primary work product of this occupation is not a digital artifact but a functioning physical telecommunications system. Core tasks — calibrating electronic instruments, operating electronic measuring instruments, installing process control monitors, upgrading firmware on physical hardware, and integrating system components — require physical presence and hands-on manipulation in varied field environments. Electromagnetism and analog electronics troubleshooting in real-world conditions (interference, degraded hardware, non-standard installations) involve unstructured physical problem-solving that 2026 robotics cannot replicate at scale. Regulatory compliance and safety supervision (ensuring compliance with specifications and regulations) require human accountability. Supporting ICT system users involves interpersonal judgment. The 'auxiliary tool rule' applies: if digital tools broke, the physical installation and repair work would still need to happen in person.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the low end of moderate exposure (score 4). AI meaningfully assists the information-processing and software-adjacent subtasks — documentation, scripting, test management, fault diagnosis — but these are not the dominant value-creating activities. The core of the role is physical: deploying, calibrating, and maintaining telecommunications infrastructure in the field. Adoption barriers include safety requirements, the unstructured nature of field environments, and the need for human accountability in regulated infrastructure. The score could rise toward 5 if agentic network management tools further automate the configuration and monitoring subtasks, but physical installation and repair work anchors the occupation in the moderate tier.

title: Allgemeine Bürokräfte
rationale: AI can already handle most of what general office clerks do — sorting and filing documents, drafting routine correspondence, processing data, and routing inquiries. A score of 7 reflects that the core work is almost entirely information-processing, with only modest barriers from physical mail handling and organisational context.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The ISCO task list for general office clerks is a near-perfect inventory of AI-automatable information work. Recording, sorting, classifying, and filing information (tasks a, h) maps directly to document management AI and OCR pipelines already in production. Preparing routine reports and correspondence (task d) is handled at professional quality by LLMs. Checking figures, preparing invoices, and recording financial transactions (task g) is 80%+ automated by accounting software with AI layers (e.g., Xero, QuickBooks Copilot). Responding to telephone or electronic inquiries (task f) is addressed by AI voice agents and chatbots at scale. From the essential skills list, 'process data,' 'file documents,' 'maintain correspondence records,' 'organise business documents,' and 'prepare correspondence for customers' are all core information-processing tasks with mature AI tooling. Optional skills reinforce this: 'use Microsoft Office,' 'digitise documents,' 'manage digital archives,' 'draft corporate emails,' and 'use software for data preservation' confirm the work is conducted entirely in a digital environment.

**Limiting factors:** Several tasks retain meaningful human involvement in 2026. Physical mail handling — 'deliver correspondence,' 'handle delivered packages,' 'handle mail,' 'sort, open and send mail' — requires physical presence and dexterous manipulation that robotic systems cannot yet perform cost-effectively in unstructured office environments. 'Organise facilities for office personnel' and 'serve beverages' are physical coordination tasks. Telephone communication with customers and routing inquiries still benefits from human judgment in ambiguous or sensitive situations, though AI is rapidly closing this gap. 'Information confidentiality' and 'respect data protection principles' create compliance-driven adoption friction, particularly in Swiss regulatory contexts (nDSG). The role also requires contextual organisational knowledge — knowing which department to route an unusual query to — that AI agents handle imperfectly without deep integration. These factors prevent a score of 9+.

**Net assessment:** General office clerks sit firmly in the High tier (score 7). The overwhelming majority of core tasks — data entry, document filing, routine correspondence, invoice processing, inquiry routing — are either already automated or automatable with 2026 tools. The remaining human value lies in physical mail/package handling, navigating organisational ambiguity, and compliance accountability. Productivity amplification is already substantial: one clerk with AI tools can handle the workload of several without AI, meaning headcount pressure is real even where full automation is not. The score does not reach 8-9 because physical tasks and organisational judgment still constitute a non-trivial share of daily work, and Swiss enterprise adoption of agentic workflows remains uneven. Confidence is high given the well-defined, homogeneous task profile.

title: Sekretariatskräfte (allgemein)
rationale: AI can already draft emails, transcribe documents, manage calendars, sort mail, and handle routine correspondence with minimal human input. The job's core output is digital information processing with little physical or high-stakes interpersonal content, leaving only coordination and oversight roles for humans.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The ISCO-08 task list for this occupation is almost entirely information-processing work performed on computers. Core tasks — checking and formatting correspondence, transcribing from dictation or drafts, using spreadsheets, drafting routine correspondence, organizing filing systems, screening meeting requests, and recording staff absences — map directly onto 2026 AI capabilities. Tools like Microsoft Copilot, integrated into the explicitly listed 'use Microsoft Office' skill, already automate drafting, formatting, summarizing, and scheduling at production quality. 'Draft corporate emails,' 'disseminate internal communications,' 'fill out forms,' 'keep task records,' and 'process commissioned instructions' are all tasks where LLM-based agents perform at or above junior-secretary level today. The optional skills — writing meeting reports, preparing presentation material, managing online content, organizing travel arrangements — are similarly well within current AI tool capability.

**Limiting factors:** The primary residual human value lies in interpersonal coordination and organizational trust: screening calls and visitors, managing the personnel agenda in ways that require social judgment, and serving as a human point of contact for staff and external parties. Physical tasks such as delivering correspondence and managing stationery orders have minimal AI substitution. Some optional skills — handling petty cash, handling financial transactions, labour law compliance — require accountability and authorization that organizations are slow to delegate to AI. Adoption friction in Swiss SMEs and public-sector offices may also slow deployment, and the role often serves as an organizational hub requiring contextual judgment about priorities that AI agents still handle poorly in ambiguous real-world settings.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the 8–9 range. The primary work product is digital (formatted documents, emails, schedules, records), the core tasks are well within demonstrated 2026 AI capability, and the main remaining human value is oversight, accountability, and interpersonal coordination — exactly the boundary condition between scores 8 and 9. It does not reach 9–10 because the role involves more contextual judgment and stakeholder-facing interaction than, say, data entry or telemarketing. A score of 8 reflects that AI will substantially reduce headcount demand and reshape the role toward exception-handling and relationship management, rather than eliminating it entirely in the near term.

title: Schalterbedienstete und Fachkräfte im Bereich Finanzdienstleistungen
rationale: AI and automation can already handle most routine transactions like deposits, transfers, and currency conversion through digital banking and ATMs. The remaining human value lies in face-to-face customer service, trust, and handling complex or unusual requests.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The core transactional tasks of this occupation — processing deposits and withdrawals, crediting/debiting accounts, currency conversion, bill payments, and reconciling cash balances — are already heavily automated through ATMs, online banking, and back-office systems. Essential skills like 'handle financial transactions,' 'maintain records of financial transactions,' 'produce statistical financial records,' and 'trace financial transactions' are almost entirely digital information-processing tasks that AI and automated systems perform at scale today. 'Provide financial product information' and 'offer financial services' are increasingly handled by AI chatbots and digital advisors. The optional skill cluster around document management, digital archives, and financial reporting is similarly AI-performable. The translation market analogy is instructive: routine counter transactions have already migrated to self-service channels, compressing headcount substantially.

**Limiting factors:** The occupation retains meaningful human-resistant elements. 'Assist customers' and 'communicate with customers' in a physical branch context involve trust, emotional reassurance, and handling distressed or elderly clients who specifically seek human interaction — a genuine adoption barrier in Switzerland's conservative banking culture. 'Manage bank vault' and 'ensure safe transport of money' involve physical security responsibilities that require human presence and accountability. Regulatory requirements around financial jurisdiction, identity verification, and anti-money-laundering compliance create friction for full automation. Post office counter work includes physical parcel handling and registered mail, which are not digitally automatable. The apprenticeship-level education profile also suggests these roles already occupy the lower-complexity end of financial services, meaning the most automatable tasks have already been stripped out over the past decade.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the high end of moderate exposure (score 6). The secular trend of branch closures and ATM/digital banking adoption has already substantially reduced headcount, and AI-powered chatbots and agentic banking tools will continue this pressure. However, the residual role — physical presence, customer trust, vault management, and handling exceptions — is not trivially automatable by 2026 AI. The score reflects ongoing substitution pressure rather than imminent full automation; the occupation is shrinking rather than disappearing. Confidence is medium because the Swiss context (high service expectations, older demographic clientele, regulatory conservatism) may slow adoption relative to global trends.

title: Buchmacher, Croupiers und verwandte Berufe im Bereich Glücks- und Wettspiele
rationale: AI can automate odds calculation, data entry, and payment processing, but the core croupier role requires physical card dealing, real-time crowd management, and trust-building presence on the casino floor. The job splits between automatable back-office tasks and human-dependent live gaming interactions.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** Several tasks in this occupation are clearly AI-performable at 2026 capability levels. Odds compilation and calculation ('calculate betting target odds,' 'calculate tote price,' 'apply statistical analysis techniques,' 'parimutuel betting') are already heavily automated in online betting platforms — algorithmic pricing engines have largely replaced human odds compilers in digital bookmaking. Data-heavy tasks like 'process data,' 'carry out end of day accounts,' 'tote reports,' 'keep task records,' and 'carry out inventory control accuracy' are routine information processing that AI handles well. 'Prevent money laundering in gambling' and 'identify indicators of problem gambling' increasingly use ML-based monitoring systems. Lottery operators and tote operators, whose work is largely data entry and system management, face high substitution pressure from automated terminals and software.

**Limiting factors:** The physical, interpersonal core of this occupation — particularly croupiers and gaming dealers — is strongly resistant to near-term AI substitution. Tasks like 'deal cards,' 'conduct gambling games,' 'operate games,' 'maintain game area,' and 'follow safety precautions in a gaming room' require dexterous physical manipulation and real-time human presence that 2026 robotics cannot replicate at casino quality or cost. Critically, the entertainment and trust dimension ('attract gamers,' 'show good manners with players,' 'apply gaming psychology,' 'interact with an audience,' 'vocal techniques' for bingo callers) is a deliberate product feature — casinos sell the human experience. Regulatory requirements ('ensure legal gaming,' 'legal standards in gambling,' 'horse racing regulation') also create compliance accountability that keeps humans in the loop. Casino gaming managers overseeing personnel and security add further interpersonal complexity.

**Net assessment:** This ISCO group is heterogeneous. The back-office sub-occupations (odds compiler, lottery operator, tote operator) score closer to 6-7 due to near-complete digitization of their core tasks. The live-floor roles (croupier, gaming dealer, bingo caller) score closer to 2-3 because physical presence and entertainment value are the product. Averaged across the group, with the majority of Swiss employment likely in live casino and gaming floor roles, a score of 4 reflects moderate exposure — meaningful automation of peripheral and administrative tasks, but the primary work product (live game operation) remains human-dependent for both practical and commercial reasons.

title: Inkassobeauftragte und verwandte Berufe
rationale: AI can automate debt tracing, record-keeping, payment reminders, and financial calculations that make up much of this job. However, negotiating repayment plans, handling conflicts, and making judgment calls on legal action still benefit from human presence.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A significant share of core tasks are information-processing operations well within 2026 AI capabilities. Tracing and locating debtors can be automated via data aggregation and skip-tracing algorithms. Preparing reports, maintaining client debt records, and recording financial transactions are routine data management tasks already handled by CRM and collections software. Sending payment reminders via phone scripts, email, or SMS is increasingly automated through AI-driven outreach platforms. Skills like 'calculate debt costs,' 'debt classification,' 'maintain records of financial transactions,' and 'obtain financial information' are essentially structured data operations that AI handles reliably. Agentic workflow tools can already manage multi-step outreach sequences, flag overdue accounts, and generate collection reports with minimal human input.

**Limiting factors:** The core interpersonal and judgment-intensive tasks resist straightforward automation. 'Enforce customer's debt repayment,' 'handle conflicts,' 'show diplomacy,' and 'facilitate official agreement' require real-time negotiation with distressed or uncooperative individuals — a domain where AI still underperforms humans in high-stakes, emotionally charged interactions. Recommending legal action involves contextual legal and ethical judgment. Swiss regulatory requirements around debt collection (SchKG/Schuldbetreibungsrecht), consumer protection obligations, and data privacy (nDSG) create compliance accountability that regulators and courts still expect humans to own. Physical visits to debtors, while declining, remain a tool in difficult cases. The optional skills around insurance law, civil process orders, and law of obligation further anchor some sub-roles in legally sensitive territory requiring professional accountability.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the high end of moderate-to-high exposure (score 6). AI can independently handle the majority of routine collection workflows — outreach, record-keeping, financial calculation, and reporting — but the negotiation, conflict resolution, and legal judgment components retain meaningful human value. The occupation is not primarily information-work in the way accounting or financial analysis is; it involves adversarial human interaction as a core deliverable. Adoption is also constrained by Swiss legal accountability norms. Uncertainty stems from the heterogeneity between sub-roles: an insurance collector doing mostly administrative follow-up is more exposed than a debt collector managing contested legal proceedings.

title: Reiseverkehrsfachkräfte
rationale: AI can already handle most of the information-retrieval, booking, and itinerary-building tasks that make up the bulk of this job — chatbots and booking platforms automate reservations, price quotes, and standard travel advice. The remaining human value lies in complex problem-solving, emotional support during disruptions, and face-to-face check-in roles that still require physical presence.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The core work products of this occupation are almost entirely informational and transactional: preparing itineraries, processing reservations, issuing tickets, quoting prices, responding to inquiries, and handling payments. All of these are tasks where 2026 AI tools — including LLM-powered travel assistants, global distribution system integrations, and agentic booking workflows — already perform at commercially acceptable quality. Skills like 'carry out internet research,' 'process booking,' 'process reservations,' 'sell tourist packages,' 'quote prices,' 'respond to customers' inquiries,' and 'use global distribution system' are highly automatable. AI chatbots (e.g., those deployed by major OTAs and airlines) already handle the majority of standard booking flows, itinerary customisation for common destinations, and FAQ-style tourist information provision. The 'produce content for tourism brochures' and 'develop tourist information materials' tasks are also well within current generative AI capability.

**Limiting factors:** Several task clusters resist straightforward automation. Physical check-in and luggage handling (assist at check-in, check in luggage, check in passengers, verify travel documentation) require in-person presence and real-time judgment — particularly for irregular operations, passengers with special needs, or security screening. Emotional labour during disruptions (handle customer complaints, tolerate stress, manage the customer experience) benefits from human empathy and de-escalation skills that AI cannot reliably replicate in high-stakes, face-to-face contexts. Highly customised, complex itineraries for niche or luxury travellers — where the consultant's personal destination knowledge and relationship with suppliers adds genuine value — also remain human-advantaged. Regulatory requirements around visa documentation verification and data privacy (handle personal identifiable information) introduce compliance friction that slows full automation. The occupation also spans heterogeneous sub-roles: a check-in attendant at an airport is far more physically anchored than a remote travel consultant whose entire workflow is digital.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits solidly in the High tier (6–7). The majority of routine, transactional work — standard bookings, price quotes, itinerary templates, tourist information — is already being automated or heavily AI-assisted, reducing headcount needs. However, the physical sub-roles (check-in, luggage, boarding) and the interpersonal complexity of disruption management and bespoke luxury travel planning provide meaningful resistance. The score of 7 reflects that AI substitution is already well underway for the digital-transactional core, but the physical and high-touch interpersonal components prevent a push into the 8–9 range. Confidence is medium because the occupation bundles very different sub-roles (airport check-in agent vs. remote travel consultant) with substantially different exposure profiles.

title: Kundeninformationsfachkräfte in Call Centers
rationale: AI can already handle the vast majority of call centre work — answering queries, processing transactions, and sending information — through voice bots and chat agents deployed at scale today. The remaining human value is narrow: de-escalating complex complaints and building rapport with frustrated customers.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The core work product of this occupation is entirely digital and conversational: answering incoming calls, responding to queries, entering events into computer systems, invoicing, and sending documents. All of these are tasks where 2026 AI is demonstrably capable. Voice AI and chat agents (e.g., deployed by major telecoms, banks, and insurers) already handle high volumes of routine inbound queries, order status requests, price information, and basic complaint triage — directly matching tasks (a), (b), (d), (e), and (f) in the ISCO description. Essential skills such as 'keep records of customer interaction,' 'collect customer data,' 'provide customer follow-up,' and 'perform customer management' map cleanly onto CRM-integrated AI agents that log, retrieve, and act on structured customer data without human intervention. Optional skills like 'analyse call performance trends,' 'measure customer feedback,' 'draft corporate emails,' and 'provide customers with order/price information' are all information-processing tasks that AI handles at professional quality today.

**Limiting factors:** The primary resistance to full automation lies in emotionally complex or ambiguous interactions: de-escalating genuinely angry customers, handling novel complaints that fall outside scripted flows, and situations where human accountability or empathy is legally or commercially required. 'Establish customer rapport' and 'guarantee customer satisfaction' in difficult cases still benefit from human judgment and social intelligence. Regulatory requirements in some Swiss industries (financial services, healthcare) may mandate human oversight for certain transaction types. Additionally, the occupation note specifies these workers handle 'straightforward transactions' — meaning the most complex cases are already routed elsewhere, which paradoxically makes the remaining work MORE automatable, not less, since it is by definition routine.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the 8–9 range. The work is almost entirely digital, conversational, and structured — precisely the domain where AI agents have demonstrated production-ready capability. The human role has already been reduced to exception handling, escalation management, and accountability in edge cases. The main uncertainty is pace of adoption in Switzerland specifically (language diversity across German, French, Italian may slow deployment slightly) and whether employers retain humans for trust/brand reasons rather than capability gaps. A score of 8 reflects that full automation is technically feasible for most tasks but that a residual human layer persists for complex emotional and regulatory edge cases.

title: Telefonisten
rationale: AI can already answer calls, route them automatically, take messages, and handle common inquiries without human involvement. The remaining human value is narrow — edge cases and callers who refuse automated systems — placing this job near the top of the exposure scale.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The core tasks of this occupation — connecting and routing calls, recording and forwarding messages, answering inquiries, and redirecting callers — are precisely what modern AI voice systems and intelligent call-routing platforms do today. Automated attendants, IVR systems, and AI voice agents (e.g., Google CCAI, Amazon Connect, Twilio AI) handle the full task cycle: greeting callers, identifying intent, routing to the right destination, taking messages, and even resolving common helpdesk queries. The essential skills 'answer incoming calls,' 'redirect callers,' 'communicate by telephone,' and 'use computer telephony integration' are all directly automatable with 2026 AI. Optional skills like 'respond to customers' inquiries' and 'handle helpdesk problems' are also well within the capability of current LLM-powered voice agents for routine cases. The work product is entirely informational and transactional — no physical transformation is required.

**Limiting factors:** Some residual human value exists for genuinely complex or emotionally sensitive calls where callers demand a human, for managing system failures ('investigating operating system problems'), and for edge cases that fall outside trained AI scenarios. Regulatory or organizational policies in some Swiss industries may require human operators for certain call types. Additionally, caller trust and preference for human interaction in high-stakes situations (medical, legal, emergency-adjacent) provides a small buffer. The optional skill 'greet guests' suggests some in-person receptionist overlap, which is more AI-resistant, but this is peripheral to the core role.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the 8–9 range. The primary work product — routing and handling telephone communications — is already being automated at scale across industries globally, and the Swiss market is not insulated from this trend. The human role has been reduced to oversight, exception handling, and serving callers who opt out of automation. A score of 9 reflects that near-complete automation is technically feasible and commercially deployed today, with only thin residual demand for human operators in edge cases and trust-sensitive contexts.

title: Hotelrezeptionisten
rationale: AI can handle online check-in, booking processing, and answering standard guest questions, but physical presence at the front desk—greeting guests, resolving complaints, and managing unexpected situations—still requires a human. The mix of automatable back-office tasks and irreplaceable in-person service keeps this in the moderate range.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A significant share of the listed tasks are already being automated or are highly automatable by 2026 AI tools.
