Processing bookings, maintaining room availability inventories, compiling guest accounts, and handling financial transactions are information-processing tasks well within reach of property management systems augmented by AI agents. Chatbots and automated check-in kiosks (already deployed in many Swiss hotels) can handle standard guest inquiries about hotel services and local information, covering tasks like 'provide tourism related information,' 'explain features in accommodation venue,' and 'process reservations.' Maintaining customer records and implementing basic marketing/sales strategies via CRM automation are also substantially AI-performable. The optional skill 'carry out end of day accounts' is nearly fully automatable.

**Limiting factors:** The core value proposition of hotel reception is physical, interpersonal presence. Tasks like 'greet guests,' 'deal with arrivals and departures in accommodation,' 'handle customer complaints,' and 'maintain relationship with customers' require real-time human judgment, empathy, and situational awareness in an unstructured social environment. Guests with special needs, intoxicated or distressed visitors, and complaint escalations demand human discretion. The optional skills 'detect drug abuse,' 'tend to clients' personal items,' and 'ensure the privacy of guests' are firmly human-anchored. Swiss hospitality culture places high value on personal service, creating a trust and brand barrier to full automation. Physical key issuance and on-the-spot problem resolution (contacting housekeeping, managing incidents) also require embodied presence.

**Net assessment:** Hotel reception sits at the moderate tier (score 5). Roughly 40–50% of tasks—booking processing, account management, information provision, financial transactions—are substantially automatable, and AI-assisted check-in is already reducing headcount at the margins. However, the occupation's defining output is a guest experience delivered through human presence, not a digital artifact. AI functions primarily as a productivity amplifier (reducing administrative burden per receptionist) rather than a full substitute. The main uncertainty is the pace of kiosk and agentic AI adoption in Swiss mid-market hotels; luxury and boutique segments are likely to retain human receptionists longer as a deliberate service differentiator.

title: Auskunftspersonal
rationale: AI can already handle the majority of inquiry clerk work — answering questions, recording complaints, processing orders, and routing complex cases — through chatbots and automated phone systems. The remaining human value lies in handling escalations and edge cases where empathy or judgment is needed, keeping the score at 7 rather than higher.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The core work product of an inquiry clerk is information exchange and transaction processing — both highly digital and AI-amenable. Answering inquiries about goods, services, prices, and policies is precisely what large language model-based chatbots and virtual agents do at scale today. Recording customer interactions, processing order forms, processing refunds, and keeping records of customer interactions are routine data-entry and workflow tasks that agentic AI handles reliably. The optional skills reinforce this: CRM software integration, data mining, e-commerce systems, and customer feedback measurement are all areas where AI tools are already deeply embedded. By 2026, the majority of routine inquiry volume in most industries is handled without human involvement.

**Limiting factors:** The occupation explicitly involves direct client contact, including in-person counter interactions, which introduces a physical/interpersonal dimension that pure automation cannot fully replicate. Applying conflict management, showing diplomacy, applying knowledge of human behaviour, and performing escalation procedures all require situational judgment and emotional attunement that AI handles poorly in genuinely difficult or emotionally charged cases. Regulatory contexts (consumer protection, facilitating official agreements) may require accountable human sign-off. In Switzerland specifically, multilingual service expectations and high service-quality standards may sustain some human presence longer than in other markets.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the High tier (6–7). The primary work product is information processing and transaction handling — both highly automatable — but the residual human value in escalation handling, in-person presence, and interpersonal conflict resolution prevents it from reaching the Very High tier. The small Swiss employment base (188 workers) suggests the role is already concentrated in contexts where human presence is specifically valued, which slightly moderates displacement speed. Key uncertainty: the pace at which Swiss employers in retail, public administration, and services deploy conversational AI agents to front-line inquiry roles.

title: Empfangskräfte (allgemein)
rationale: AI can handle phone inquiries, appointment scheduling, and basic information requests automatically. However, the core job requires physical presence to greet visitors, manage arrivals, and handle unexpected situations in person, keeping exposure moderate.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** Several core tasks are highly automatable with 2026 AI tools. 'Administer appointments' and 'process reservations' are already handled by AI scheduling systems (Calendly, automated booking platforms). 'Communicate by telephone' and 'deal with telephone requests' are increasingly managed by AI voice agents capable of natural conversation. 'Disseminate messages,' 'maintain customer records,' and 'process payments' are routine digital workflows well within current AI capability. 'Use Microsoft Office' and 'process data' confirm a digital work environment. Optional skills like 'process booking,' 'handle mail,' and 'maintain correspondence records' are similarly automatable. Chatbots and virtual receptionists already handle a significant share of front-desk inquiries in hotels and medical offices.

**Limiting factors:** The primary work product is not a digital artifact but a physical service experience — greeting guests, managing the reception area, directing visitors, and handling arrivals and departures in person. 'Greet guests,' 'maintain reception area,' 'deal with arrivals in accommodation,' and 'assist clients with special needs' all require embodied human presence that robotics cannot replicate at scale in 2026. 'Handle customer complaints' in person involves emotional intelligence and de-escalation that AI handles poorly in live, unstructured interactions. Regulatory and trust factors (security monitoring, incident reporting, detecting drug abuse) also require human judgment and accountability. The night auditor sub-occupation adds bookkeeping complexity that partially resists full automation.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the moderate tier (score 5). Roughly 40–50% of tasks — scheduling, phone handling, record-keeping, payment processing — are meaningfully automatable, and AI virtual receptionist products are already deployed in some sectors. However, the physical presence requirement for welcoming visitors, managing the reception environment, and handling in-person exceptions is a genuine constraint that prevents higher displacement. The likely trajectory is productivity amplification (one receptionist managing more with AI tools) rather than wholesale substitution, with adoption varying significantly by sector (hospitality vs. medical vs. corporate).

title: Interviewer im Bereich Umfragen und Marktforschung
rationale: AI can already conduct automated phone and online surveys, transcribe responses, and analyze results at scale. Human interviewers retain value for complex qualitative probing and building rapport with reluctant respondents, but the core data-collection work is increasingly automatable.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The core output of this occupation is structured data collection — asking scripted questions, recording responses, tabulating results, and preparing survey reports. These tasks map almost perfectly onto what AI and automated survey platforms already do commercially. Tools like Qualtrics, SurveyMonkey, and AI-driven conversational agents can administer questionnaires, branch on responses, flag inconsistencies (ESCO: 'identify and resolve inconsistencies in responses'), and enter data directly into databases — all without human interviewers. The essential skills 'tabulate survey results,' 'fill out forms,' 'document interviews,' and 'prepare survey reports' are highly automatable information-processing tasks. Optional skills like 'perform data analysis,' 'data quality assessment,' and 'conduct quantitative research' are areas where AI tools are already production-grade. Automated outbound calling and IVR systems handle telephone surveys at scale, and online panels have largely displaced in-person enumeration for standardized surveys.

**Limiting factors:** Some genuine human advantages remain. Qualitative probing — following up on unexpected answers, building rapport with hard-to-reach populations, conducting focus group interviews, or performing mystery shopping — requires social intelligence and adaptive conversation that AI handles poorly in unstructured contexts. The optional skill 'interview focus groups' and tasks involving 'capture people's attention' and persuading reluctant respondents to participate involve interpersonal dynamics that automated systems struggle with. Regulatory and methodological requirements in official statistics (e.g., national population census enumeration) may mandate human contact for certain populations. Additionally, response rates to automated surveys are lower for some demographics, preserving a niche for human interviewers in high-stakes or hard-to-reach sampling contexts.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the High tier (6–7). The primary work product — structured data collection and basic reporting — is already substantially automated by commercial survey platforms and AI agents. The human role is increasingly residual: handling edge cases, conducting qualitative or sensitive interviews, and managing respondent trust. The occupation has already contracted significantly due to online surveys and automated telephony; AI accelerates this trend. The score of 7 rather than 8–9 reflects that fully unstructured qualitative interviewing and in-person enumeration of resistant populations retain meaningful human value, and that adoption in official/government survey contexts faces institutional inertia.

title: Berufe im Bereich Kundeninformation, anderweitig nicht genannt
rationale: AI can handle routine information gathering, eligibility checks, and answering standard questions about benefits or services. However, the mix of in-person interviewing, handling vulnerable clients, and tourism/hospitality tasks keeps this occupation at moderate exposure.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The ISCO description centers on interviewing clients, verifying information, and initiating eligibility procedures — tasks that are substantially information-processing in nature. AI-powered intake systems and chatbots already handle eligibility pre-screening, FAQ responses about benefits and claims, and basic data verification in healthcare and public assistance contexts. Skills like 'identify customer's needs,' 'provide tourism related information,' and 'process reservations' are well within 2026 AI capability via conversational agents and booking automation. 'Handle personal identifiable information' and 'maintain incident reporting records' are routine data-handling tasks that AI can assist with or automate. The optional skill set — distributing local information, taking room service orders, delivering correspondence — suggests some workers in this group perform largely transactional, script-driven interactions that AI handles well.

**Limiting factors:** The occupation is notably heterogeneous (hence low confidence): it spans hospital admissions clerks, public assistance eligibility interviewers, and tourism information workers. The essential skill 'assist clients with special needs' implies face-to-face interaction with vulnerable populations where human judgment, empathy, and legal accountability matter. 'Detect drug abuse' and 'handle customer complaints' require real-time situational assessment that AI cannot reliably perform in unstructured in-person settings. Regulatory requirements around public assistance eligibility decisions and patient admissions create accountability barriers to full automation. Physical presence for greeting guests and handing over service areas is inherently non-automatable. In Switzerland specifically, multilingual service expectations and trust in human contact for sensitive matters (health, welfare) slow adoption.

**Net assessment:** This group sits at moderate exposure (5). The information-gathering and eligibility-processing core is meaningfully AI-assisted or automatable, but the in-person, interpersonal, and vulnerable-client dimensions anchor the floor. The wide heterogeneity of sub-occupations — from hospital admissions to hotel information desks — means some workers face higher exposure (tourism info clerks) and others lower (welfare eligibility interviewers with legal accountability). A score of 5 reflects this midpoint, with medium-to-high uncertainty.

title: Bürokräfte im Rechnungswesen und in der Buchhaltung
rationale: AI can already automate the core tasks of this job — recording transactions, generating invoices, reconciling accounts, and compiling financial reports — with high accuracy using current accounting software and AI agents. The remaining human value is mostly exception handling and client contact, placing this firmly in the very high exposure tier.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The ISCO-08 description defines this occupation almost entirely as structured data processing: checking figures, classifying and recording numerical data, preparing invoices and account statements, and compiling financial reports. Every one of these tasks is the primary target of existing accounting automation. Tools like QuickBooks, Xero, SAP, and their AI-augmented successors already automate transaction classification, invoice generation (ESCO: 'allocate bills,' 'issue sales invoices'), accounts receivable follow-up ('follow up accounts receivables'), and financial record maintenance ('maintain financial records'). OCR and AI-driven document ingestion handle 'file documents' and 'organise business documents.' Compiling reports on cash receipts, payables, and profit/loss ('create a financial report,' 'prepare financial statements') is well within 2026 AI capability. The optional skills list — 'check accounting records,' 'interpret financial statements,' 'carry out sales analysis,' 'keep records on sales' — are all information-processing tasks on structured data, fully within AI's demonstrated range.

**Limiting factors:** Some residual human value exists in client-facing tasks: 'communicate with customers,' 'customer service,' 'interview bank loanees,' and 'assess debtor's financial situation' require interpersonal judgment and trust that AI cannot fully replace in 2026. Handling physical cash ('count money,' 'handle petty cash') and managing physical mail ('handle mail') are minor physical components. Regulatory compliance and audit trails in Swiss financial practice create some friction for full automation, as human sign-off is often legally required. However, these factors are peripheral — they represent a small fraction of total task time and do not anchor the occupation at a lower tier.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits squarely in the 8–9 range. The primary work product is entirely digital and structured (financial records, invoices, reports), the core tasks are textbook automation targets already addressed by commercial software, and AI agents in 2026 can handle multi-step workflows like invoice-to-payment reconciliation with minimal oversight. The human role has already contracted to exception handling, client escalations, and accountability sign-off — precisely the definition of the 8–9 tier. The score of 8 rather than 9 reflects that small but real interpersonal and physical components, plus Swiss regulatory friction, prevent full displacement.

title: Bürokräfte in der Statistik, im Finanz- und Versicherungswesen
rationale: AI can already process insurance claims, prepare tax forms, compile statistical data, and handle financial record-keeping with high accuracy. The work is almost entirely information-processing on structured digital data, leaving humans mainly for exception handling and compliance oversight.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The core work of this occupation — calculating totals and percentages, preparing tax return forms, maintaining records of financial transactions, processing insurance enrolments and claims, compiling statistical data, and preparing financial documents — is almost entirely structured information-processing on digital data. These map directly to tasks where 2026 AI tools excel: tax preparation software already automates 80%+ of return preparation; AI-driven document processing handles insurance claims routing and data extraction; bookkeeping and audit support tools (e.g., Copilot for Finance, Vic.ai) automate ledger reconciliation and error identification. Skills like 'calculate debt costs,' 'identify accounting errors,' 'inspect taxation documents,' 'interpret financial statements,' and 'maintain records of financial transactions' are all performable by current AI at acceptable quality. The optional skills list is dominated by domain-specific financial knowledge (accounting techniques, banking activities, cadastral taxation, classify insurance claims) that AI models have absorbed from training data, further reducing the human-advantage zone.

**Limiting factors:** Some tasks resist full automation. 'Build business relationships,' 'communicate with customers,' 'identify customer's needs,' and 'liaise with managers' involve interpersonal judgment and trust that AI cannot fully replicate in client-facing contexts. Regulatory compliance and accountability — 'comply with legal regulations,' 'observe confidentiality,' 'ensure continuous preparedness for audits' — require a named human responsible party, creating an institutional barrier to full substitution. Fraud detection in novel or adversarial cases still benefits from human pattern recognition in ambiguous situations. Physical tasks like handling mail and paperwork are minor but present. The Swiss regulatory environment (financial market supervision, data protection law) may slow adoption in sensitive contexts.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the 8–9 range. The primary work product is entirely digital and structured — exactly the domain where AI substitution is most advanced and adoption barriers are lowest. The remaining human value lies in exception handling, client communication, regulatory accountability, and oversight of automated outputs. The occupation is not at 9–10 because it retains meaningful interpersonal and compliance-accountability components that prevent full automation, and because Swiss financial sector regulatory caution may slow deployment. A score of 8 reflects that the human role is already being compressed toward oversight and edge-case resolution, with productivity amplification likely reducing headcount substantially even before full task automation.

title: Bürokräfte in der Lohnbuchhaltung
rationale: AI and payroll software can already automate nearly all core tasks: calculating wages, processing payments, detecting discrepancies, and preparing pay statements. The remaining human role is exception handling and compliance oversight, placing this firmly in the very-high-exposure tier.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The ISCO task list for payroll clerks is almost entirely composed of structured, rule-based information processing on digital data. Maintaining attendance records, computing gross/net salaries, applying tax and deduction rules, preparing electronic payments, and reconciling discrepancies are all tasks that modern payroll platforms (SAP, Workday, ADP, Abacus in the Swiss context) already automate at scale. The essential skills — 'calculate wages,' 'manage payroll,' 'prepare paychecks,' 'carry out financial transactions,' 'trace financial transactions' — map directly onto capabilities that payroll automation software has handled for over a decade, and that 2026 AI augments further through anomaly detection, natural language querying of records, and automated compliance updates. Optional skills such as 'calculate tax,' 'calculate benefits,' 'calculate commissions,' and 'maintain financial records' are similarly high-automation tasks; Swiss tax and social insurance rules are codified and machine-readable. The 'type of file' and 'maintain data entry requirements' skills signal that a significant portion of the role is literally data entry, which scores at the maximum exposure tier.

**Limiting factors:** Some residual human value exists in edge cases: liaising with union officials over disputed pay, handling garnishment orders that require legal interpretation, managing exceptions for unusual employment contracts, and serving as an accountable point of contact for employee pay queries. The optional skill 'liaise with union officials' and 'trade union regulations' suggest occasional interpersonal and quasi-legal work that AI cannot fully replace in 2026. Swiss regulatory specificity (AHV, BVG, cantonal tax variations) requires up-to-date compliance knowledge, though AI tools are increasingly trained on such jurisdictional detail. Physical presence is not required, and there are no meaningful safety or dexterity barriers to automation.

**Net assessment:** Payroll clerks sit squarely in the 8–9 range. The primary work product is entirely digital — computed numbers, verified records, and electronic payment instructions — and the vast majority of routine instances are already handled by software with minimal human intervention. The occupation is not yet at 10 because some exception-handling, employee communication, and compliance judgment remains, and Swiss SME adoption of full payroll automation lags large enterprises. However, the trajectory is clear: headcount in this role is declining as payroll software absorbs routine volume, leaving only oversight and escalation functions. A score of 9 reflects that the human role has already been reduced to exception handling and accountability, matching the 8→9 boundary definition precisely.

title: Bürokräfte im Bereich Materialwirtschaft und Transport und verwandte Berufe, onA
rationale: AI can already handle most of the data entry, order tracking, inventory updates, and logistics documentation that form the core of this job. Human value remains in exception handling, supplier communication, and coordinating across systems when things go wrong.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** This occupational group (ISCO 4320) covers clerical workers in materials management, purchasing, and transport coordination. The core work product is informational: processing purchase orders, updating inventory records, tracking shipments, preparing freight documentation, and reconciling stock data. These are precisely the tasks where 2026 AI and robotic process automation (RPA) tools excel. Agentic workflows can already handle order entry, invoice matching, stock-level monitoring with automated reorder triggers, and standard logistics documentation (bills of lading, customs declarations) with minimal human input. ERP systems like SAP and Oracle increasingly embed AI co-pilots that automate routine transaction processing. The 'information-work detection rule' applies strongly here: all essential tasks involve cognitive operations on structured data (quantities, SKUs, delivery dates, supplier codes) with no physical transformation of materials.

**Limiting factors:** The occupation retains meaningful human value in several areas. Exception handling — resolving discrepancies between purchase orders and deliveries, managing supplier disputes, coordinating urgent rerouting of shipments — requires contextual judgment and interpersonal negotiation that AI handles poorly in ambiguous real-world scenarios. Swiss regulatory requirements around customs documentation, hazardous goods transport (ADR), and VAT compliance introduce domain-specific validation needs that slow full automation. Additionally, many Swiss SMEs operate on legacy ERP systems or mixed paper/digital workflows, creating adoption friction. The role also involves internal stakeholder communication (production planners, warehouse staff, finance) where relationship continuity and organizational knowledge matter.

**Net assessment:** The occupation sits solidly in the High tier (6–7). The majority of routine transaction-processing tasks are automatable with current tools, and productivity amplification is already reducing headcount needs in larger logistics operations. However, the occupation has not yet crossed into the 8–9 range because exception handling, supplier negotiation, and cross-system coordination in non-standardized environments remain genuinely human-advantaged. The heterogeneity of the 'onA' (not elsewhere classified) category — spanning everything from purchasing clerks to freight documentation specialists — introduces some uncertainty, warranting medium confidence.

title: Fachkräfte in der Lagerwirtschaft
rationale: AI can automate inventory tracking, stock level monitoring, and record-keeping with warehouse management systems already doing much of this today. Physical tasks like checking damaged goods and stacking remain human, but the core information-processing work is heavily AI-exposed.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The ISCO-08 description centers on maintaining records, verifying stock, estimating needs, and compiling inventories — all information-processing tasks performed on structured digital data. Essential skills like 'maintain stock control systems,' 'manage inventory,' 'monitor stock level,' and 'carry out inventory planning' map directly onto capabilities already embedded in modern warehouse management systems (WMS) and ERP platforms. AI-enhanced WMS tools (e.g., SAP Extended Warehouse Management, Oracle WMS) can automate reorder triggers, flag discrepancies, generate inventory reports, and predict stock needs using demand forecasting algorithms. The optional skill 'operate warehouse record systems' confirms the work is conducted in a digital environment. 'Issue purchase orders' and 'order supplies' are agentic workflow tasks that AI can execute autonomously once thresholds are defined. The 'products coding system' and 'mathematics' skills are trivially automatable. Roughly 50–60% of core tasks are already partially automated in digitally mature warehouses.

**Limiting factors:** Several tasks resist full automation. 'Check for damaged items' requires physical inspection and tactile/visual judgment in unstructured environments — computer vision can assist but is not yet reliable enough for autonomous sign-off in most Swiss SME contexts. 'Ensure stock storage safety' involves physical arrangement decisions and safety compliance that require human presence. 'Stack goods' and 'receive goods' are physical tasks where robotics exist (e.g., AMRs in large logistics centers) but penetration in mid-sized Swiss warehouses remains limited by cost and infrastructure. 'Handle returns' often involves condition assessment and exception handling that benefits from human judgment. Regulatory and liability considerations around inventory accuracy in pharmaceutical or food supply chains also slow full automation. The occupation sits at the boundary between clerical information work and physical warehouse labor, which moderates the score.

**Net assessment:** This occupation falls solidly in the High tier (6). The core work product is record-keeping and inventory management — fundamentally information work — but a meaningful share of daily tasks involve physical presence, inspection, and exception handling that AI cannot yet fully replace. The trajectory is toward further automation of the information-processing core, with human roles consolidating around physical verification, exception handling, and oversight. Key uncertainty: the score could rise to 7 in large, digitally mature logistics operations (e.g., Migros, Coop distribution centers) where WMS automation is already advanced, but remains at 6 for the broader Swiss occupational average including SMEs and craft-sector storerooms.

title: Bürokräfte in der Material- und Fertigungsplanung und verwandte Berufe
rationale: AI can automate much of the scheduling, inventory tracking, and materials calculation that forms the core of this job. However, coordinating across departments, investigating delays, and managing exceptions in physical production environments keeps humans central.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The core tasks of this occupation are heavily information-processing in nature: computing material quantities, preparing production schedules, verifying stocks, and recording workflow. These are precisely the tasks that ERP systems (SAP, Oracle), AI-enhanced planning tools, and demand-forecasting algorithms already handle at scale. By 2026, AI-driven production planning tools can ingest customer orders, capacity data, and inventory levels to auto-generate schedules and flag shortfalls — directly substituting for tasks (a), (b), and (c) in the ISCO description. Skills like 'keep records of work progress,' 'report on production results,' and 'communicate production plan' are largely document and data tasks that AI can draft or automate. The optional knowledge items referencing quality standards, logistics oversight, and inspection reports also point to structured, rule-based work amenable to AI monitoring and reporting tools.

**Limiting factors:** A meaningful portion of this role involves physical-environment coordination: verifying stocks on the shop floor, arranging deliveries, investigating delays caused by machine breakdowns or supplier issues, and liaising with managers and technicians. These require real-time judgment in unstructured situations — a supplier is late, a machine is down, a batch is defective — where human discretion and cross-functional communication remain essential. Skills like 'create solutions to problems,' 'liaise with managers,' and 'coordinate operational activities' involve interpersonal negotiation and contextual judgment that AI handles poorly in ambiguous, fast-moving production environments. Regulatory compliance (environmental legislation) and safety oversight (hazard identification, protective gear) also introduce accountability requirements that resist full automation. The Swiss manufacturing context, with its precision-industry norms, adds further quality-assurance demands.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the high end of moderate exposure — score 6. AI and advanced ERP/planning software can already automate the bulk of the computational and scheduling core, and adoption in manufacturing is accelerating. However, the role is not purely digital: it bridges information systems and the physical production floor, requiring exception-handling, stakeholder coordination, and on-site judgment that AI cannot yet reliably replace. The likely trajectory is significant workforce reduction through productivity amplification (fewer clerks needed per production line) rather than full substitution, placing this firmly in the 'high' tier rather than 'very high.' Confidence is medium because the actual AI exposure depends heavily on how digitized a given employer's production environment is — a highly automated Swiss precision manufacturer may already have reduced this role substantially, while a smaller firm may still rely heavily on human clerks.

title: Bürokräfte in der Transportwirtschaft und verwandte Berufe
rationale: AI can automate scheduling, record-keeping, route optimization, and report preparation — the core information-processing tasks of this role. However, real-time coordination under operational disruptions, safety-critical decisions, and multi-party communication across physical transport systems keep human oversight essential.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The core outputs of transport clerks — keeping operational records, scheduling vehicles and drivers, tracking shipments, preparing management reports, and coordinating logistics — are fundamentally information-processing tasks performed on digital systems. Skills such as 'manage dispatch software systems,' 'track shipments,' 'write work-related reports,' 'prepare freight shipment reports,' 'manage train working timetable,' and 'programme work according to incoming orders' are highly automatable with 2026 AI tools. Agentic AI systems already handle multi-step scheduling, route optimization, and document preparation in logistics contexts. The 'optional knowledge' list is dominated by software tools and digital platforms, confirming this is digital-environment work. Sub-occupations like freight transport dispatcher, taxi controller, and baggage flow supervisor involve structured data flows that AI can increasingly manage autonomously.

**Limiting factors:** A substantial share of tasks involve real-time, safety-critical decision-making under uncertainty: 'make time-critical decisions,' 'develop contingency plans for emergencies,' 'handle shipment risks,' 'oversee operational safety on trains,' and 'react calmly in stressful situations.' Many sub-occupations (rail traffic controller, aircraft dispatcher, dangerous goods safety adviser) operate under strict regulatory frameworks (OHSAS 18001, IMO conventions, dangerous goods regulations) where human accountability and sign-off are legally mandated. Physical coordination tasks — supervising loading/unloading, guiding ships into docks, inspecting cargo — require on-site presence. The breadth of 20 sub-occupations spans highly heterogeneous contexts (aviation, rail, maritime, road, pipeline), each with distinct regulatory and operational constraints that slow uniform AI adoption.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the moderate-to-high boundary (score 5). The information-processing core is substantially AI-exposed, and productivity amplification is already occurring through dispatch software and logistics platforms. However, the safety-critical nature of transport operations, regulatory accountability requirements, and the need for real-time human judgment during disruptions prevent full automation. The heterogeneity of sub-occupations adds uncertainty — a taxi controller faces higher exposure than a dangerous goods safety adviser. The score reflects a role where AI handles routine coordination and documentation while humans retain oversight of exceptions, safety decisions, and regulatory compliance.

title: Sonstige Bürokräfte und verwandte Berufe, onA
rationale: AI can handle most routine office tasks like drafting documents, scheduling, data entry, and processing standard correspondence with little human effort. The score is moderate-high rather than maximum because this catch-all category likely includes varied roles with some interpersonal or physical coordination duties.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** ISCO 4410 is a residual 'not elsewhere classified' clerical category, meaning it captures office and administrative support workers whose tasks broadly mirror other clerical occupations: document preparation, correspondence handling, data entry, filing, scheduling, and basic information processing. All of these are squarely within 2026 AI capabilities. Agentic tools can draft and route correspondence, populate forms, manage calendars, extract and summarize information from documents, and handle repetitive data workflows with minimal human oversight. The digital-first nature of office work means the primary work product — processed information and documents — is highly automatable. Optional knowledge items for clerical roles typically include office software suites and digital communication platforms, confirming a fully digital work environment.

**Limiting factors:** The 'onA' (not elsewhere classified) designation introduces genuine heterogeneity. Some workers in this bucket may perform coordination tasks requiring physical presence, handle sensitive stakeholder interactions, or operate in regulated environments (e.g., public administration, healthcare administration) where human accountability and verification are mandated. Interpersonal judgment — managing exceptions, navigating organizational politics, handling complaints — resists full automation. Additionally, Swiss regulatory and data-protection norms (FADP/nDSG) may slow adoption of AI agents for document handling in certain sectors. The relatively modest wage (CHF 6,233) and upper-secondary education profile suggest these are generalist support roles, not highly specialized, which cuts both ways: simpler tasks automate more easily, but the variety of contexts may require human adaptability.

**Net assessment:** The core work product is information processing in a digital environment, placing this occupation firmly in the high-exposure tier (6–7). It does not reach 8–9 because the residual NEC category almost certainly includes workers with meaningful interpersonal, coordination, or context-specific duties that AI cannot yet reliably handle end-to-end. Confidence is low due to the definitional vagueness of the NEC category — actual exposure could range from 5 (varied coordination roles) to 9 (pure data-entry or correspondence-processing roles) depending on which specific sub-occupations are captured in the Swiss count.

title: Bibliotheksassistenten
rationale: AI can handle catalogue searches, query answering, and record-keeping, but physically shelving books, maintaining equipment, and guiding users in person still require human presence. The mix of digital information tasks and irreducible physical work lands this role in the moderate tier.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A significant share of library clerk tasks are information-processing work that 2026 AI handles well. 'Analyse library users' queries,' 'assess informational needs,' 'manage library users queries,' and 'provide library information' are all conversational or search tasks where AI chatbots and recommendation systems already perform at acceptable quality in library contexts (e.g., automated reference chat, OPAC enhancements). 'Classify library materials,' 'organise information,' and 'develop classification systems' (optional) map directly onto AI-assisted cataloguing tools like automated metadata tagging and subject-heading assignment, which are in active deployment at larger libraries. 'Maintain library inventory' and 'maintaining library records' (ISCO task f) are database operations well within reach of automated workflows. Optional skills such as 'digitise documents,' 'manage digital libraries,' and 'use software for data preservation' describe work that is either already automated or heavily AI-assisted.

**Limiting factors:** The physical core of the job — shelving books (ISCO task b), displaying library material, maintaining library equipment, and issuing/receiving physical materials (task a) — requires dexterous manipulation in an unstructured environment. Robotic shelving exists in prototype form but is not commercially deployed at scale in 2026, especially in the varied physical layouts of Swiss public and academic libraries. 'Instruct library users' involves face-to-face pedagogical interaction, particularly with vulnerable populations (elderly, children, non-native speakers), where human presence carries trust and social value that AI cannot replicate in situ. Regulatory and institutional adoption barriers are also meaningful: Swiss library systems are largely public-sector, with slow procurement cycles and strong union protections, slowing AI tool rollout.

**Net assessment:** Library clerks sit at the boundary between moderate (4–5) and high (6–7) exposure. Roughly half the core tasks — query handling, cataloguing, record maintenance — are substantially AI-performable today, but the other half — physical shelving, equipment upkeep, in-person user instruction — are not. The occupation does not produce a purely digital work product; the physical library environment is central. A score of 5 reflects this even split: AI will meaningfully reshape the information-handling side of the role and reduce headcount needs over time, but will not eliminate the physical and interpersonal dimensions within the 2026 horizon. Key uncertainty is the pace of robotic shelving adoption and whether Swiss libraries invest in AI reference tools.

title: Postverteiler und -sortierer
rationale: AI can optimize delivery routes and automate sorting in large facilities, but physically carrying and delivering mail to homes and businesses still requires human presence. The core work is physical and location-dependent, keeping AI exposure low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** Route optimization and scheduling tasks — 'analyse ways to reduce travel time,' 'organise mail deliveries,' and 'establish daily priorities' — are already well-served by AI-powered logistics software and GIS tools. 'Operate mailing information systems,' 'keep records of merchandise delivery,' 'keep stock records,' and 'record customers' personal data' are routine data-entry and record-keeping tasks that AI agents handle with high accuracy. In large sorting facilities, automated optical character recognition and robotic sorting systems already handle a significant share of physical mail sorting, directly substituting for the 'handle mail' and 'differentiate types of packages' tasks. These digital and logistics-planning subtasks represent perhaps 25–35% of the role's time.

**Limiting factors:** The dominant core task — physically traversing a geographic area to deliver mail and parcels to specific addresses, collecting signatures, and interacting with recipients — cannot be performed by current AI without robotic hardware. Autonomous delivery robots and drones exist in pilots but are not deployed at scale in Swiss urban and suburban environments as of 2026, and unstructured environments (apartment buildings, locked gates, weather conditions) remain significant barriers. Skills like 'interpret traffic signals,' 'drive in urban areas,' 'drive two-wheeled vehicles,' 'liaise with colleagues,' and 'maintain privacy of service users' all anchor the role in physical, real-world execution. Regulatory requirements around signature collection and data protection ('ensure the integrity of mail,' 'maintain privacy of service users') also add human accountability requirements.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). AI meaningfully assists with route planning, record-keeping, and back-office sorting, but the primary work product — physical delivery to a specific address — requires human locomotion in unstructured environments that robotic systems cannot yet reliably handle at commercial scale. The main uncertainty is the pace of autonomous delivery vehicle and drone deployment; if last-mile robotics matures within 5–7 years, this score could rise substantially, but against the 2026 frontier it remains low.

title: Kodierer, Korrekturleser und verwandte Bürokräfte
rationale: AI can proofread text, fix grammar and spelling, convert information into codes, and rewrite articles faster and more accurately than humans. The core work is almost entirely digital text processing, leaving little that AI cannot handle at production quality.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The two defining tasks of this occupation — proofreading text and coding/classifying information — are among the most directly automatable by 2026 AI. Grammar and spelling correction is essentially solved (Grammarly, GPT-4-class models); AI outperforms most humans on rule-based language checking. Information coding and classification (converting data into standardized codes) maps directly onto AI classification pipelines. Optional skills reinforce the picture: rewriting articles, tracking changes in text editing, laying out digital written content, and applying desktop publishing techniques are all tasks where AI tools (large language models, Adobe Firefly, Canva AI) now perform at or above clerk-level quality. The work product is entirely digital — text and data — with no physical transformation required.

**Limiting factors:** Some residual human value exists in edge cases: interpreting ambiguous editorial intent when consulting with an editor, applying nuanced copyright judgments in contested situations, and handling highly specialized domain terminology where AI may hallucinate or miss context-specific conventions. Sorting physical forms and addressing envelopes by hand (listed in the ISCO task description) are physical tasks, but these are clearly vestigial and represent a negligible share of modern work in this category. Regulatory or institutional inertia in publishing houses or legal/medical coding environments may slow full adoption, but these are friction factors, not structural barriers.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the 8–9 range. The primary work products — corrected text and coded data — are digital artifacts that AI produces at acceptable or superior quality today. The human role has already contracted to oversight, exception handling, and accountability for output quality. The score of 8 rather than 9 or 10 reflects that some editorial judgment, copyright interpretation, and client-facing consultation tasks retain modest human advantage, and that adoption in smaller Swiss publishing or administrative contexts may lag the technical frontier by a year or two.

title: Schreiber und verwandte Arbeitskräfte
rationale: AI can now draft, transcribe, copy-edit, and format text at professional quality, directly replacing the core output of scribes and copy workers. The remaining human role is largely oversight and exception handling, placing this occupation near the top of the exposure scale.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The ISCO 4414 group — scribes and related workers — produces written or transcribed documents as its primary output. Core tasks include copying, transcribing, drafting correspondence, formatting documents, and data entry from handwritten or spoken sources. All of these are squarely within 2026 AI capabilities: large language models produce professional-quality text, speech-to-text systems transcribe audio in real time with high accuracy, OCR and document-processing pipelines handle handwritten and printed source material, and agentic tools can route, format, and file documents automatically. The translation market contraction (~90%) and the near-complete automation of data entry are direct analogues. There is no physical transformation of materials involved; the work product is entirely digital or paper-based information.

**Limiting factors:** Some residual human value exists in contexts requiring certified authenticity (notarial scribes, legal attestation), handling highly ambiguous or degraded source material, or managing sensitive interpersonal situations (e.g., scribing for people with disabilities in real time). Regulatory requirements around certified copies or sworn transcriptions in Switzerland may slow full automation in narrow sub-roles. The very small employment base (74 workers) suggests the occupation already reflects significant prior automation; remaining workers may be concentrated in these harder-to-automate niches. Physical presence for in-person scribing (e.g., accessibility support) also provides a partial barrier.

**Net assessment:** The overwhelming majority of tasks performed by scribes and related workers — transcription, copying, drafting, formatting — are fully automatable with tools available today. The occupation sits at the 8–9 boundary: human oversight, accountability, and a small set of certified or interpersonal sub-tasks prevent a score of 10, but the core work product is as exposed as any occupation in the classification. A score of 9 reflects this near-maximum exposure with a small discount for the certified/accessibility niches. Confidence is medium because the Swiss employment is tiny and may already be self-selected toward the harder-to-automate residual tasks.

title: Bürokräfte für Registratur und Dokumentation
rationale: AI and automation can sort, classify, file, retrieve, and digitise documents with minimal human input, and document management software already handles most of these tasks end-to-end. The job is almost entirely information processing with no physical or interpersonal core, leaving little that AI cannot do.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The ISCO-08 task list maps almost perfectly onto what document management software, OCR pipelines, and AI agents already do in 2026. Sorting and classifying materials (task a) is handled by ML-based document classification tools. Filing, locating, and removing records (tasks b–c) are automated in enterprise content management systems (SharePoint, OpenText, M-Files). Keeping records of materials filed/removed (task d) is a byproduct of any digital system. Photocopying, scanning, and faxing (task e) are increasingly replaced by automated scan-to-archive workflows. Among essential skills, 'carry out records management,' 'file documents,' 'organise business documents,' 'use office systems,' and 'fill out forms' are all digitally native tasks that AI agents and RPA bots handle routinely. Optional skills like 'digitise documents,' 'manage digital archives,' and 'use software for data preservation' confirm the work is conducted in a digital environment.

**Limiting factors:** The main residual human roles are exception handling (ambiguous documents that defy automated classification), physical handling of legacy paper archives not yet digitised, and accountability/compliance sign-off where regulations require a named human custodian. 'Information confidentiality' and 'respect data protection principles' (GDPR, Swiss DSG) create some governance friction, but these are procedural constraints rather than task complexity barriers. Some niche sub-roles—health records management, museum records—carry domain-specific sensitivity that slows full automation adoption, but the underlying tasks remain highly automatable.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the 8–9 range. The primary work product is entirely digital information processing with no physical transformation and no meaningful interpersonal component. The human role has already been reduced to oversight, exception handling, and compliance accountability—exactly the boundary condition for a score of 9. The main reason it does not reach 10 (like data entry clerk) is that locating and contextualising records in ambiguous legacy systems, and managing physical-to-digital transitions, still require occasional human judgment. Adoption is well underway in Swiss enterprises, and the small employment count (1,504) reflects a role already in structural decline.

title: Bürokräfte im Personalwesen
rationale: AI can already handle most of what personnel clerks do — updating records, processing applications, answering routine HR queries, and generating reports. The remaining human value lies in sensitive conversations, judgment calls, and accountability for decisions affecting employees.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The core ISCO tasks are almost entirely information-processing operations on structured data: updating employment histories, salaries, leave balances, and performance records (tasks a, f, g) are classic database operations that HRIS platforms already automate substantially, and AI agents can handle the remainder. Processing job applications and advising applicants (task c) maps directly to ATS (applicant tracking system) automation, which is already mainstream. Answering routine inquiries about entitlements (task d) is a textbook chatbot use case — large language models handle HR FAQ responses at production quality today. Sending job announcements (task e) and compiling personnel reports (task f) are fully automatable. Essential skills like 'manage payroll reports,' 'write work-related reports,' 'document interviews,' and 'fix meetings' are all digital information tasks with strong AI tooling. Optional skills such as 'manage payroll,' 'recruit employees,' 'organise training,' and 'disseminate messages' are similarly high-exposure.

**Limiting factors:** A meaningful subset of tasks resists easy automation. 'Assess character,' 'profile people,' 'listen actively,' and 'support managers' involve interpersonal judgment that AI cannot reliably replicate in 2026, particularly in sensitive HR contexts (complaints, terminations, onboarding conversations). 'Manage employee complaints' and 'negotiate employment agreements' carry legal and relational stakes that create strong trust and accountability barriers to full AI substitution. 'Observe confidentiality' and 'apply company policies' require contextual judgment about edge cases that LLMs handle inconsistently. Employment law compliance (a key optional knowledge area) demands accuracy that current AI tools cannot guarantee without human review. Physical presence for onboarding or health programme support also limits substitution.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the High tier (score 7). The majority of routine, high-volume tasks — record maintenance, report generation, application processing, inquiry responses — are already being automated or will be within the 2026 horizon. What remains is a thinner layer of interpersonal judgment, legal accountability, and sensitive employee interactions. The occupation will likely shrink in headcount as AI handles the bulk of transactional work, with surviving roles concentrated in the judgment-intensive residual. The upper secondary education baseline and relatively structured task set mean adoption barriers are low compared to more regulated professions.

title: Bürokräfte und verwandte Berufe, anderweitig nicht genannt
rationale: AI can now draft correspondence, translate documents, clip and summarize press articles, and write advertising copy at professional quality. The work is almost entirely digital information-processing with few physical or high-stakes interpersonal barriers, placing this occupation firmly in the very high exposure tier.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The core tasks of this occupational group are almost entirely information-processing operations performed on digital content. Writing and editing business correspondence, translating foreign-language communications, reading and filing press articles, preparing advertising copy, and compiling directories are all tasks where 2026 AI tools perform at or near professional quality. Essential skills such as 'translate foreign language,' 'translate language concepts,' 'apply grammar and spelling rules,' 'perform business research,' and 'use IT tools' map directly onto demonstrated AI capabilities — translation markets have already contracted ~90%, and LLMs produce publication-ready correspondence and advertising copy routinely. Optional skills like 'draft corporate emails,' 'create advertisements,' 'examine advertisement layout,' 'monitor media industry research figures,' and 'perform market research' are similarly automatable via agentic workflows and generative AI. The primary work product is digital text and structured data, meaning the auxiliary-tool rule does not apply — this IS the digital work.

**Limiting factors:** Some residual human value exists in relationship management tasks such as 'liaise with advertising agencies,' 'support managers,' and 'identify customer's needs,' which require interpersonal judgment and accountability. Coordinating advertising campaigns and managing budgets involve organizational authority and stakeholder trust that AI cannot fully substitute in 2026. The foreign language correspondence clerk sub-occupation retains some value where nuanced cultural sensitivity or legal precision is required, though even here AI post-editing is rapidly displacing full human translation. Regulatory or compliance requirements around document management ('ensure proper document management') may slow full automation in some Swiss corporate contexts.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits solidly in the 8–9 range. The primary work products — correspondence, press summaries, advertising copy, translations, directory compilation — are all digital text artifacts that AI handles well today. The remaining human role is largely oversight, exception handling, stakeholder liaison, and accountability for outputs, which matches the 8–9 tier definition. The score is held at 8 rather than 9 because the group is heterogeneous (advertising assistants have more creative coordination work than press clippers) and because Swiss enterprise adoption of full agentic workflows still lags capability. Confidence is medium due to this heterogeneity across sub-occupations.

title: Dienstleistungsberufe und Verkäufer, onA
rationale: This catch-all category covers service and sales workers who don't fit elsewhere, mixing physical customer service, cash handling, and basic sales tasks. AI can assist with scheduling and product information, but the physical and interpersonal core of most service roles limits automation.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The ISCO 5000 'not elsewhere classified' category for service and sales workers is a residual grouping with minimal ESCO task or skill detail provided. Generic service and sales work typically involves customer interaction, basic information provision, cash handling, and product/service delivery — tasks where AI can assist through chatbots, recommendation engines, and automated scheduling. For any digital-facing sub-roles (e.g., online customer service, basic data entry in retail systems), AI tools in 2026 can handle a meaningful share of routine queries and transactions. Voice AI and agentic tools can automate appointment booking, FAQ responses, and simple order processing.

**Limiting factors:** The defining characteristic of ISCO 5000 is that it captures workers who don't fit standard service/sales categories — implying atypical, hybrid, or niche roles that often involve physical presence, hands-on service delivery, or highly contextual interpersonal judgment. Physical service delivery (e.g., guiding customers, handling goods, operating in unstructured environments) remains resistant to 2026 AI. The 'not elsewhere classified' nature means many incumbents likely perform tasks requiring physical dexterity, real-time situational judgment, or trust-based human interaction that AI cannot replicate at scale. Regulatory and liability considerations in customer-facing roles also slow adoption.

**Net assessment:** Without specific task or skill data, this residual category is difficult to score precisely — hence low confidence. The moderate score of 4 reflects that generic service/sales work sits at the boundary where AI tools are commercially available for some tasks (information provision, scheduling, basic transactions) but physical presence and interpersonal service delivery anchor the majority of roles against full substitution. The heterogeneity of this catch-all group and the tiny Swiss employment count (272 workers) suggest a niche, atypical population whose actual exposure could range from 2 to 7 depending on the specific sub-roles represented.

title: Reisebegleiter und Stewards
rationale: AI cannot greet passengers, serve meals, demonstrate safety procedures, administer first aid, or physically evacuate an aircraft — the core of this job is in-person physical and interpersonal service. The role is anchored by safety-critical presence and human touch, keeping AI exposure very low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A narrow slice of tasks has some AI relevance. Preparing flight reports, processing customer orders, handling financial transactions (duty-free sales), and analysing written reports are information-processing tasks where AI tools could assist or partially automate back-office documentation. Answering routine passenger inquiries (timetable information, service questions) could be partially handled by AI chatbots or in-flight digital assistants. Upselling products and selling souvenirs involve scripted interactions that AI-driven recommendation systems already support in e-commerce contexts. These peripheral tasks represent perhaps 10–15% of actual working time.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of tasks are physically embodied and safety-critical in ways that 2026 AI cannot replicate. Greeting and directing passengers, demonstrating emergency procedures, physically inspecting cabin service equipment, administering first aid, and executing emergency evacuations all require a trained human body present in the aircraft or vessel. Regulatory frameworks (aviation safety law, common aviation safety regulations listed as essential skills) mandate certified human crew on commercial flights — no regulatory pathway exists for AI substitution of these roles. The interpersonal demands — handling distressed passengers, assisting children and disabled persons, managing stressful situations — require emotional attunement and physical dexterity that remain firmly human. Even the food and beverage service, while theoretically automatable in structured environments, occurs in a confined, dynamic, safety-regulated space where robotic deployment is not commercially viable at scale.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 2). The primary work product is safe, comfortable, in-person passenger transport — a physical and interpersonal service delivered in a regulated safety environment. AI can marginally assist with documentation and routine information queries, but cannot substitute for the certified human presence that aviation and maritime law requires. Employment displacement risk from AI is minimal; the more relevant pressures are airline capacity decisions and automation of check-in/boarding processes, which are already mature and separate from the cabin crew role itself.

title: Schaffner
rationale: AI can automate ticket checking and passenger information via apps and automated gates, but the core job involves physical presence on vehicles, assisting passengers, managing emergencies, and ensuring safety in real time. The irreducibly physical and interpersonal nature of the role keeps AI exposure low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A meaningful subset of tasks are AI-susceptible. Ticket checking and validation (already largely automated by gate systems and mobile apps), providing timetable and route information, answering standard passenger queries, and selling tickets via electronic terminals are all tasks where AI-powered kiosks, chatbots, and automated barriers have made significant inroads. The skills 'answer questions about the train transport service,' 'assist passengers with timetable information,' 'sell train tickets,' and 'operate electronic payment terminals' are increasingly handled by self-service systems. Writing work-related reports and communicating with customer service departments are also partially automatable via structured digital tools. These digital-adjacent tasks represent perhaps 20–30% of the role.

**Limiting factors:** The dominant share of this occupation is irreducibly physical and interpersonal. Core tasks — assisting disabled and elderly passengers with boarding, managing passenger behaviour during emergencies, performing equipment safety checks, controlling door opening/closing, signalling drivers, and responding to accidents — require physical presence, real-time situational judgment, and embodied action in an unstructured environment. Regulatory and safety frameworks (railway framework legislation, tramway regulations, safe driving practices) impose strict human accountability requirements. The 'handle stressful situations,' 'react calmly in stressful situations,' and 'help to control passenger behaviour during emergency situations' skills reflect a safety-critical human role that regulators and operators are extremely unlikely to delegate to AI in the near term. Robotics capable of navigating crowded train carriages and assisting a fallen passenger remain far from deployment-ready.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). While the ticketing and information-provision subtasks face ongoing automation pressure — and have already been partially displaced by gates and apps — the majority of the role involves physical presence, passenger safety, emergency response, and interpersonal care that 2026 AI cannot replicate. The occupation is not immune to gradual workforce reduction as automated ticketing expands, but the core conductor function retains strong human necessity. Confidence is high given the clear physical-work dominance.

title: Reiseleiter/Fremdenführer
rationale: AI can generate travel information, answer tourist questions, and plan itineraries, but guiding requires physical presence, real-time crowd management, and interpersonal performance that AI cannot replicate in 2026. The job is fundamentally about being there in person, keeping the score low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A meaningful subset of tasks involve information delivery that AI handles well: providing background on historical sites, distributing brochures, answering factual questions about exhibits, and explaining tour procedures. AI-powered audio guides and apps (e.g., Rick Steves Audio Europe, GPT-powered tour apps) already automate much of the narration and Q&A function. Skills like 'provide visitor information,' 'inform visitors at tour sites,' 'apply foreign languages in tourism,' and 'distribute local information materials' are all performable by AI tools today. Real-time translation earpieces and multilingual chatbots further erode the language-advantage component of the role. Administrative subtasks — 'register visitors,' 'perform clerical duties,' 'handle tour contract details,' 'measure customer feedback' — are straightforwardly automatable via digital workflows.

**Limiting factors:** The core work product is not a document or digital artifact — it is a live, embodied experience delivered to a group of people in a physical environment. Tasks like 'escort visitors to places of interest,' 'ensure health and safety of visitors,' 'monitor visitor tours,' 'manage tourist groups,' 'provide first aid,' and 'direct emergency evacuations' require physical presence and real-time situational judgment that 2026 robotics and AI agents cannot replicate in unstructured outdoor or crowded public environments. The interpersonal and performative dimension — building rapport, reading group energy, inspiring enthusiasm for nature, handling complaints in the moment, managing visitors with special needs — is a core value driver that AI cannot substitute. Regulatory and safety accountability (liability for group welfare) further anchors human presence. The optional skills around nature conservation, plant/animal identification, and intercultural competence reflect specialist knowledge that enhances the human guide's irreplaceable on-site role.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). AI tools are already displacing the pure information-delivery subtasks at the margin — audio guide apps and AI chatbots reduce demand for guides whose sole value is narration. However, the dominant share of core tasks require physical co-presence, safety oversight, group management, and interpersonal performance. Adoption barriers are high: tourists pay a premium precisely for human interaction and local personality. The occupation will see some productivity pressure (fewer guides needed for pure narration tours) but not structural displacement, placing it at the upper end of the Low tier.

title: Chefs de service, Servicefach- und Servicehilfskräfte in Restaurants
rationale: Waiting tables is almost entirely physical and interpersonal — carrying food, pouring drinks, reading the room, and handling complaints face-to-face. AI can handle reservations and payments digitally, but the core job requires a human body in a dining room.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A narrow slice of waiter tasks involves digital information processing that AI can handle well. Reservation management, payment processing, and order-taking via digital POS systems are already partially automated (self-order kiosks, OpenTable-style platforms). AI chatbots can handle initial booking inquiries and menu queries. Measuring customer feedback and maintaining incident records are also digitally manageable. These peripheral tasks represent perhaps 10–15% of actual working time.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of the 35 essential skills describe physical, embodied, or interpersonal actions: setting tables, carrying and serving food and beverages, seating guests, clearing tables, welcoming guests, handling complaints in real time, assisting VIP and special-needs clients, and supervising food quality through sensory inspection. Robotics capable of navigating a crowded, dynamically changing restaurant floor, pouring wine gracefully, and responding to a distressed guest remain far beyond 2026 deployment viability. The hospitality context also places a premium on human warmth, cultural reading, and real-time social judgment — skills like 'maintain relationship with customers,' 'identify customer's needs,' and 'assist VIP guests' that AI cannot replicate in a physical service encounter. Regulatory requirements around alcohol service (detecting intoxication, verifying age) also require human judgment and legal accountability.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low (2) tier. The primary work product is a physical service experience delivered in person, not a digital artifact. AI tools at the margins (reservations, payments, order routing) provide modest productivity gains but do not reduce headcount meaningfully — a table still needs a human to carry the plate. The main uncertainty is whether robotic service (already piloted in some Asian markets) accelerates, but 2026 deployment at scale in Swiss full-service restaurants is not credible.

title: Barkeeper
rationale: Bartending is almost entirely physical and interpersonal — mixing drinks, handling glassware, managing intoxicated customers, and creating atmosphere cannot be done by AI. Payment processing is the only meaningfully automatable task, keeping this score very low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The most automatable task in the ISCO description is payment processing — 'collecting payment, operating cash registers, balancing cash receipts' — which is already heavily automated via POS systems and contactless payment. Presenting a drinks menu and compiling price lists are trivially AI-assistable. Upselling products and identifying customer needs could theoretically be supported by AI recommendation systems, but these are executed in real-time face-to-face interaction, not through a digital interface. No core task produces a digital artifact; all outputs are physical (a poured drink, a clean glass, a stocked bar).

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of bartending tasks are irreducibly physical and interpersonal. Preparing and serving drinks, handling glassware, tapping kegs, cleaning beer pipes, assembling garnishes, and maintaining bar cleanliness all require dexterous manipulation in an unstructured, dynamic environment — well beyond 2026 robotic capabilities. Critically, tasks like detecting drug abuse, enforcing alcohol laws, checking ID, and persuading over-served customers to stop drinking require real-time social judgment, legal accountability, and physical presence. These are not edge cases — they are legally mandated core duties. The hospitality atmosphere itself (the 'experience' of a bar) is a product that customers pay for, and human presence is intrinsic to it.

**Net assessment:** Barkeeper sits firmly in the Low (2) tier. The primary work product is a physical service delivered in person, and the vast majority of tasks — from mixing cocktails to managing customer behavior — resist AI substitution entirely under 2026 capabilities. The only meaningful AI touchpoint is payment processing, which is already automated infrastructure rather than a new AI displacement. No plausible near-term AI development changes this picture, as the bottleneck is unstructured physical manipulation and real-time social judgment, not information processing.

title: Coiffeurs
rationale: AI cannot cut, colour, or style hair — the entire job requires skilled hands working directly on a person's head. A few peripheral tasks like booking appointments can be automated, but the core physical craft keeps this occupation firmly low-exposure.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The peripheral administrative tasks listed — arranging appointments, processing payments, issuing sales invoices, monitoring stock levels, and ordering supplies — are straightforwardly automatable by 2026 AI tools and scheduling software. Advising on hairstyles and recommending hair products could be partially assisted by AI (e.g., virtual try-on apps, chatbot recommendations), and staying up-to-date with fashion trends is a task where AI can surface information quickly. These tasks collectively represent perhaps 10–15% of a hairdresser's working time.

**Limiting factors:** The primary work product is a physical transformation of a person's hair, performed through tactile, manual skill in an unstructured interpersonal environment. Cutting, washing, dyeing, curling, styling, treating scalp conditions, fitting wigs, and adding extensions all require fine motor manipulation, real-time sensory feedback (texture, tension, moisture), and adaptive judgment about an individual client's hair in the moment. Robotics capable of this level of delicate, variable manipulation in an unstructured setting do not exist at commercial scale in 2026. Beyond the physical barrier, the service is inherently social — clients value the interpersonal relationship, trust, and comfort of a human practitioner. Regulatory or safety barriers are modest, but the physical and interpersonal barriers are decisive.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits squarely in the Low (2–3) tier. The overwhelming majority of core tasks are physical and interpersonal, with no plausible near-term AI substitution pathway. The small administrative and advisory periphery can be augmented or automated, but this affects neither employment levels nor the fundamental nature of the work. The score of 2 reflects that some digital tools (booking apps, product recommendation systems) are already in use, but the craft itself is essentially untouched by AI.

title: Kosmetiker und verwandte Berufe
rationale: AI cannot apply creams, give massages, shape nails, or perform waxing — the entire core of this job requires skilled physical touch on a client's body. The work is almost entirely hands-on and interpersonal, keeping AI exposure very low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A small subset of tasks has meaningful AI exposure. Scheduling appointments, processing payments, and advising clients on cosmetics, diet, or fashion trends are information-based tasks where AI chatbots or booking software can assist or partially substitute. Recommending products, analysing skin type from photos (via computer vision), and providing weight-loss or nutritional guidance are areas where AI tools are commercially available and increasingly capable. The 'beauty salon attendant' and 'weight loss consultant' sub-occupations have the highest digital task share. Optional skills like 'administer appointments' and 'advise on preparation of diet food' are similarly AI-amenable.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of essential skills — apply massage therapy, give massages, cosmetic manicure, cosmetic pedicure, cosmetic skin treatment, perform facial treatment, perform body wrapping, use hair removal techniques, apply nail polish, shape nails, decorate nails, make up performing artists — require precise, tactile, physical manipulation of a human body in real time. Current robotics cannot replicate the dexterity, sensory feedback, and adaptive touch required for massage, waxing, or nail care at acceptable quality. Client trust and the experiential/social dimension of beauty services (the 'feel good' component) create strong interpersonal barriers. Regulatory requirements around cosmetics and hygiene further anchor the work in supervised, in-person settings. Even the most AI-capable sub-occupation (weight loss consultant) still relies on motivational coaching and physical assessment.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 2). The primary work product is a physical service delivered through skilled touch and human presence — not a digital artifact. AI can assist with peripheral administrative and advisory tasks, but these represent a small fraction of total work time. Robotics capable of replacing massage or nail care are not commercially deployed and are not on the near-term horizon. The score reflects genuine but narrow AI assistance at the margins, not meaningful substitution of core work.

title: Reinigungs- und Hauswirtschaftleiter und -kräfte in Büros, Hotels und anderen Einrichtungen
rationale: AI cannot sweep floors, make beds, clean bathrooms, or physically inspect rooms — the core work is hands-on and requires constant movement through physical spaces. Some scheduling and reporting tasks can be assisted by AI, but these are minor compared to the physical and interpersonal demands.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A subset of supervisory and administrative tasks are AI-assisted in 2026. Scheduling shifts, monitoring stock levels, presenting reports, managing budgets, and procuring hospitality products are all information-processing tasks where AI tools (scheduling software, inventory management, budget dashboards) already provide meaningful automation. 'Think analytically' and 'develop working procedures' are partially AI-supportable. These administrative tasks likely represent 15–25% of total working time for supervisors in this group. AI-powered inspection tools using computer vision could theoretically assist with 'assess cleanliness of areas' and 'manage inspections of equipment,' though deployment in unstructured hotel environments remains limited in 2026.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of tasks are physically grounded and interpersonally intensive. Sweeping, vacuuming, washing floors, making beds, cleaning bathrooms, restocking minibars, handling guest luggage, and running errands are all physical tasks that current robotics cannot perform reliably in the varied, cluttered, and access-restricted environments of hotels and offices. The interpersonal core — greeting guests, handling complaints, maintaining customer relationships, ensuring guest privacy, and managing staff welfare — requires human presence, emotional attunement, and real-time situational judgment that AI cannot replicate in 2026. Regulatory and trust considerations around guest privacy and safety further constrain AI deployment.
