The 'hotel butler' sub-occupation is especially relationship-intensive and prestige-sensitive, making AI substitution negligible there.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). The primary work product is a physically clean, well-maintained, and hospitably managed environment — not a digital artifact. AI tools provide marginal productivity gains in scheduling and inventory, but cannot substitute for the physical labor or the human service interactions that define the role. Adoption barriers (unstructured physical environments, guest trust, staff management) are high. The score of 3 reflects genuine but limited administrative AI assistance, with no near-term threat to the core physical and interpersonal work.

title: Hauswirtschafter in Privathaushalten
rationale: AI cannot vacuum floors, make beds, cook meals, or clean bathrooms — the core of this job is physical work in someone's home. A few tasks like budgeting or ordering supplies could be AI-assisted, but these are minor compared to the hands-on physical work that defines the role.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A small subset of tasks in this occupation involve information processing that AI could assist with. Budget management, monitoring financial accounts, controlling expenses, ordering supplies, and maintaining customer records are administrative tasks where AI tools (spreadsheets with AI, inventory apps) could reduce time spent. Forecasting occupancy demand and managing hospitality revenue for B&B operators involve data analysis that AI handles well. Customer communication tasks — greeting guests, handling complaints, measuring feedback — could be partially supported by AI-drafted scripts or chatbots. These administrative and communicative tasks represent perhaps 15–20% of the total workload.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of tasks are irreducibly physical: vacuuming, mopping, making beds, cleaning bathrooms, washing and ironing laundry, cooking meals, dusting, and caring for pets and plants. These require dexterous manipulation in unstructured private home environments — exactly where 2026 robotics remain far below human capability. Consumer-grade domestic robots (Roomba-style) handle only narrow floor-cleaning tasks and cannot substitute for the full range of physical housekeeping. The role also involves interpersonal presence — receiving visitors, attending to children, assisting with minor illness — where physical presence and human judgment are non-negotiable. Regulatory and trust factors in private households further slow any technology adoption.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low (2) tier. The primary work product is a physically clean, organized, and functioning household — delivered through hands-on labor in unstructured domestic environments. AI can marginally assist with the administrative fringe (budgeting, ordering, scheduling), but these tasks are peripheral. The core value delivered is physical and interpersonal, placing this occupation close to the minimal-exposure anchor. The score of 2 rather than 1 reflects the genuine, if modest, AI-assistability of the administrative and customer-management subtasks present in the B&B operator variant of this role.

title: Hauswarte
rationale: AI cannot mop floors, fix leaky pipes, patrol hallways, or tend boilers — the core of this job is physical presence and hands-on maintenance. A small share of administrative tasks like filling forms or writing reports can be AI-assisted, but these are minor compared to the physical work.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A subset of tasks in this occupation involve information processing that AI can assist with. Filling out registration forms, writing work-related reports, and notifying management of repair needs are administrative tasks where AI tools (drafting, summarization, scheduling) provide genuine productivity gains. Handling customer complaints and liaising with managers also have AI-assisted communication tools available. These tasks collectively represent perhaps 15–20% of working time for a typical Hauswart.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of core tasks are physically grounded and require on-site human presence. Executing inspection walkways, managing cleaning activities, performing minor repairs, tending furnaces and boilers, patrolling for security, managing ground maintenance, and cleaning rooms and surfaces all require physical manipulation in unstructured real-world environments. 2026 robotics cannot reliably perform general building maintenance, janitorial work, or security patrols in the varied, cluttered environments of Swiss apartment buildings and offices. The social and regulatory dimensions — regulating tenant conduct, handling noise complaints, accepting deliveries, managing contractors — require interpersonal judgment and physical presence that AI cannot substitute. There are no meaningful regulatory barriers to AI adoption here because AI simply lacks the physical capability to do the work.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low (2) tier. The primary work product is a physically maintained, secure, and orderly building — not a digital artifact. The auxiliary tool rule applies clearly: if the computer broke, 95%+ of this job would still need to happen in person. AI can marginally assist with the small administrative tail of the role (form-filling, report writing), but cannot substitute for the physical core. Employment levels are unlikely to be significantly affected by AI through 2026 or beyond the near term.

title: Astrologen, Wahrsager und verwandte Berufe
rationale: AI can already generate horoscopes, interpret birth charts, and produce personalised fortune-telling content at scale. However, the core value clients pay for is the live interpersonal ritual, emotional connection, and perceived psychic presence — which AI cannot replicate.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A significant share of the technical content in this occupation is highly automatable. Casting horoscopes, preparing birth charts, interpreting planetary alignments, and applying numerology are rule-based or pattern-matching tasks that 2026 AI handles well — specialised astrology software is already listed as an essential skill, and LLMs can generate personalised, rhetorically polished horoscope readings indistinguishable from human output. Digital content creation (written readings, online consultations, social media promotion via 'digital marketing techniques') is also highly AI-performable. AI tools can 'read' a birth date, apply astrological rules, and produce a compelling, individualised narrative at near-zero marginal cost, directly automating tasks (a), (b), (c), and (d) from the ISCO description.

**Limiting factors:** The occupation's core value proposition is not informational accuracy — it is the interpersonal ritual, emotional attunement, and perceived authenticity of a human practitioner. Skills like 'read people,' 'listen actively,' 'counsel clients,' 'apply crisis intervention,' and 'provide spiritual counselling' depend on real-time emotional responsiveness and the client's belief that a sentient, spiritually attuned human is present. Mediums and psychics in particular sell a live performance and emotional experience ('perform live,' 'perform improvisation') that clients consciously or unconsciously value as human. Regulatory and trust barriers are low in the formal sense, but the product is inherently trust-based and identity-bound — clients choose a specific practitioner. Physical presence and theatrical elements (séance tools, palm reading) further anchor some sub-occupations in embodied interaction.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at a moderate exposure score of 5. The content layer (horoscope generation, chart interpretation, written readings) is substantially automatable and AI is already displacing low-end online astrology services. However, the live consultation, emotional support, and performance dimensions — which constitute the premium, in-person segment — are strongly resistant to substitution. The occupation is likely to bifurcate: commoditised digital content (apps, AI horoscope generators) will erode the low end, while human practitioners who emphasise ritual, presence, and personal relationship retain a defensible niche. The small Swiss employment base (162 workers) and the occupation's inherent theatricality introduce uncertainty about how quickly adoption pressure translates to workforce displacement.

title: Gesellschafter und Zofen/Kammerdiener
rationale: AI cannot iron clothes, make beds, clean rooms, cook meals, or physically accompany someone to activities — the entire job is hands-on personal service. Almost nothing here is digital, so AI offers virtually no substitution.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The digital or cognitive components of this role are minimal. Optional skills like 'administer appointments' could be partially assisted by scheduling AI, and 'give advice on personal matters' involves conversational interaction where AI chatbots have some capability. These are, however, peripheral to the core role. No essential skill involves producing a digital artifact or processing structured data.

**Limiting factors:** Every essential skill in the ESCO listing is a physical or deeply interpersonal task: ironing textiles, washing laundry, making beds, cleaning rooms and surfaces, preparing food, and — most centrally — providing companionship through physical presence, active listening, and empathetic relating. The primary work product is human presence and physical domestic service. Robotics in 2026 cannot reliably perform delicate domestic manipulation (ironing, laundry sorting, bed-making) in unstructured home environments at acceptable quality or cost. The companionship dimension — accompanying someone to places, conversing, participating in activities — requires embodied human presence and emotional attunement that AI cannot replicate in this context. The auxiliary tool rule applies strongly: if every computer in the world broke, 99% of this job would continue unchanged.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low (2) tier. The work is almost entirely physical and interpersonal, with no meaningful digital work product. The only reason it scores above 1 is that appointment scheduling and basic advice-giving have some marginal AI assistance potential. Adoption barriers are near-total: the value proposition of this role is precisely the human element, and clients employing companions or valets are paying for personal human service, not information processing.

title: Bestatter und Einbalsamierer
rationale: AI cannot embalm bodies, prepare graves, dress remains, or provide the human presence grieving families need during funeral arrangements. The work is almost entirely physical and interpersonal, leaving very little for AI to replace.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A small subset of administrative tasks are AI-performable: scheduling appointments, drafting obituary notices, maintaining burial records, managing budgets, and coordinating logistics (location, timing, documentation). AI tools in 2026 can handle calendar management, document drafting, and basic financial tracking competently. The 'advise on funeral services' skill has a partial digital component — chatbots or configurators could assist families in selecting caskets or urns online. These administrative and advisory tasks represent perhaps 15–20% of the total workload.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of core tasks are physically irreplaceable: embalming bodies (incising, injecting preservative fluids, reconstructing disfigured remains), dressing bodies, transferring and placing coffins, preparing graves, overseeing cremations, and affixing memorial plaques. These require fine manual dexterity in unstructured, sensitive physical environments far beyond 2026 robotic capability. Equally important is the interpersonal dimension: conducting grief-sensitive interviews with bereaved families, showing diplomacy, and providing human presence and comfort during one of life's most emotionally charged moments. Regulatory compliance (health, sanitation, legal embalming requirements, liaising with local authorities) also demands accountable human judgment. Cultural and religious sensitivity in ceremony preparation further resists automation.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low (2) tier. The primary work products — a prepared body, a conducted funeral, a supported grieving family — are physical and deeply interpersonal. AI can marginally assist with scheduling and record-keeping, but these are auxiliary tools, not the core output. No plausible 2026 AI capability meaningfully displaces the hands-on or emotionally present aspects of this role. The score of 2 reflects the small but real administrative efficiency gains AI can provide without threatening the occupation's core.

title: Tierpfleger und -betreuer
rationale: AI cannot bathe, groom, feed, restrain, or physically care for animals — the core of this job is hands-on work in unpredictable physical environments. Some record-keeping and scheduling tasks are automatable, but these are minor relative to the physical demands.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A small subset of tasks in this occupation involve structured information processing that AI can assist with. Creating and maintaining animal records (weight, condition, treatments, food intake), scheduling, ordering supplies, and managing documentation are all digitally performable tasks where AI tools can reduce time. Communication tasks such as advising customers on pet care or animal welfare could be partially supported by AI-generated content or chatbots. Optional skills like 'computerised feeding systems' and 'apply numeracy skills' suggest some digital tooling already exists in this space. These tasks collectively represent perhaps 10–15% of the working day.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of core tasks are irreducibly physical and require real-time sensory judgment in unstructured environments: bathing, grooming, clipping, feeding, restraining animals during treatment, cleaning enclosures, trimming hooves, assisting births, and providing first aid. Animals are unpredictable, and safe handling requires physical presence, tactile skill, and rapid behavioral assessment — capabilities well beyond 2026 robotics, which struggle with delicate manipulation and novel physical problem-solving. Regulatory and welfare requirements (animal welfare legislation, biosecurity, veterinary safety protocols) also demand accountable human presence. The interpersonal dimension — dealing with distressed animals, grieving owners, or challenging shelter situations — further resists automation. Even the most automatable sub-occupation (record-keeping for a kennel supervisor) is embedded in a role that is 85%+ physical.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 2). The primary work product is the physical care and welfare of animals, not a digital artifact. AI can marginally assist with scheduling, record-keeping, and customer communication, but these are auxiliary to the core job. No commercially available AI or robotic system in 2026 can groom a dog, trim bovine hooves, restrain an animal during a veterinary procedure, or respond to an animal welfare emergency. Adoption barriers are high: physical environments are unstructured, animals are unpredictable, and welfare/safety regulations require human accountability. The score of 2 reflects the small but real administrative component that AI tools can streamline.

title: Fahrschullehrer
rationale: AI can handle classroom theory, traffic law quizzes, and progress tracking, but the core job is sitting in a car with a nervous beginner and physically intervening to prevent accidents. That irreducible physical presence and real-time safety responsibility keeps this occupation firmly low-exposure.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A meaningful subset of driving instruction tasks are AI-performable. Teaching road traffic regulations (task b), explaining mechanical operation via diagrams and audiovisual aids (task f), and assessing students' theoretical readiness (task d) are all information-delivery tasks that AI tutoring systems already handle well — adaptive e-learning platforms can personalize theory instruction, quiz students on road signs, and track curriculum objectives. Skills like 'interpret traffic signals,' 'road traffic laws,' and 'driver's license structure' map directly to content AI can present and test. Administrative tasks such as scheduling, progress monitoring, and generating feedback reports are also highly automatable via agentic tools. These peripheral tasks may represent 20–30% of total working time.

**Limiting factors:** The dominant core of this occupation — sitting in a dual-control vehicle with a student under live traffic conditions — is physically irreducible with 2026 technology. Skills like 'guarantee students' safety,' 'control the performance of the vehicle,' 'perform defensive driving,' and 'give constructive feedback' in real time require a human physically present and capable of grabbing the wheel or hitting the instructor brake. Robotics capable of operating a dual-control car in unstructured urban traffic do not exist at commercial scale. Beyond the physical constraint, regulatory frameworks in Switzerland (and most jurisdictions) legally mandate a licensed human instructor in the vehicle during learner driving. The interpersonal dimension — 'adapt teaching to student's capabilities,' 'show consideration for student's situation,' 'encourage students to acknowledge their achievements' — also involves reading anxiety, building confidence, and managing stress responses in ways that current AI cannot replicate in a high-stakes physical environment.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits solidly in the Low tier (score 3). AI can automate the theory-instruction and administrative periphery, but the primary work product — a safe, competent driver produced through supervised on-road practice — requires physical co-presence, real-time intervention capability, and legal accountability that AI cannot substitute in 2026. The score is slightly above the floor (2) because theory delivery and progress tracking are genuinely automatable, but the physical safety-critical core is a hard ceiling on overall exposure.

title: Berufe im Bereich personenbezogener Dienstleistungen, anderweitig nicht genannt
rationale: AI cannot accompany someone to a nightclub, act as a dancing partner, or provide physical companionship — the core value is embodied human presence. The job is almost entirely physical and interpersonal, leaving very little for AI to automate.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A narrow subset of tasks has some AI relevance. Dating service consultants advise on online dating, document interviews, and match people — tasks where AI-powered matching algorithms (as used by Tinder, Hinge, etc.) already assist or partially substitute. 'Advise on personal matters' and 'use consulting techniques' involve information exchange that AI chatbots can partially replicate in low-stakes contexts. 'Keep personal administration' and 'maintain professional administration' are routine digital tasks easily handled by scheduling and CRM tools. Optional skills like 'administer appointments' and 'document interviews' are also straightforwardly automatable.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of core tasks require physical, embodied human presence: accompanying clients to events, acting as a dancing partner, greeting and entertaining guests, providing physical intimacy, monitoring guest access, and handling aggressive behaviour. These cannot be delegated to AI in any meaningful sense by 2026 or the foreseeable future. 'Assess character,' 'profile people,' 'listen actively,' and 'maintain relationship with customers' all depend on real-time social and emotional attunement in physical settings. Regulatory and trust dimensions — enforcing alcohol regulations, ensuring safety in escort services, maintaining client privacy — require accountable human judgment. The entire value proposition of this occupation is human presence and interpersonal connection, which is structurally AI-resistant.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). The primary work product is not a digital artifact but a lived social experience delivered in person. AI can assist marginally with scheduling, matching algorithms in the dating-consultant sub-role, and administrative tasks, but these are peripheral. The core — physical companionship, entertainment, and social facilitation — is irreducibly human. The score is slightly above the floor because the dating-consultant sub-occupation has a meaningfully higher AI-assisted component than the escort or club-host roles, introducing some heterogeneity.

title: Verkaufsstand- und Marktverkäufer
rationale: AI cannot set up a market stall, handle physical goods, or engage customers face-to-face in an outdoor market setting. The job is fundamentally physical and interpersonal, with only minor administrative tasks like record-keeping that AI could assist with.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A small subset of tasks in this occupation have meaningful AI exposure. Keeping accounts and maintaining stock records (task g) is straightforwardly automatable — point-of-sale systems already handle much of this. Operating a cash register and processing payments (essential skills) are increasingly handled by automated payment terminals. Determining product mix and price levels (task b) could be assisted by AI demand-forecasting tools. Organising product display could theoretically be informed by AI planogram software. These administrative and decision-support tasks represent perhaps 15–20% of the actual working time.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of this occupation's core work is physical and interpersonal in ways that 2026 AI cannot replicate. Erecting and dismantling stalls, transporting and loading goods, stacking and displaying merchandise — all require physical presence and manual dexterity in unstructured outdoor environments where robotics remain far from deployment. Demonstrating and selling goods, assisting customers, and guaranteeing customer satisfaction depend on real-time human interaction, product handling, and persuasion in a dynamic social setting. Adapting to different weather conditions and operating in open-air markets adds further physical unpredictability. The 'arrange permit for market stall' task involves navigating local bureaucracy, often in person. The optional skills — demonstrating product features, delivering a sales pitch, negotiating price — are inherently interpersonal and tactile. Even the digital-adjacent skills like operating a cash point are already largely automated by existing (non-AI) technology, not by generative AI.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). The primary work product is not a digital artifact but a physical retail transaction conducted in an outdoor, unstructured environment. AI can assist marginally with inventory tracking and pricing decisions, but cannot substitute for the physical setup, goods handling, and face-to-face selling that constitute the bulk of the role. Adoption barriers are high: no digital infrastructure is assumed, the work environment is outdoors and variable, and the customer interaction is tactile and spontaneous. The score of 3 reflects that some administrative tasks are AI-assistable, but the core job remains firmly human.

title: Leiter eines Einzelhandelsgeschäftes
rationale: AI can automate inventory tracking, financial record-keeping, and pricing analysis, but the core of this job — buying goods, advising customers face-to-face, and managing a physical shop — resists automation. The mix of physical presence, supplier relationships, and hands-on retail operations keeps exposure moderate.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** Several core tasks are meaningfully AI-assisted in 2026. Inventory management and stock-level tracking ('taking inventory,' 'study sales levels of products,' 'handle financial overviews') are already heavily automated in retail software with AI forecasting. Pricing strategy ('set up pricing strategies,' 'market pricing') can be informed by AI-driven competitive analysis tools. Marketing tasks ('plan marketing campaign,' 'implement marketing strategies,' 'create annual marketing budget') are substantially AI-augmentable — campaign planning, copy generation, and budget allocation tools are commercially mature. Financial record-keeping ('maintain financial records,' 'accounting techniques,' 'comprehend financial business terminology') is largely automatable. E-commerce integration ('e-commerce systems') is a digital channel where AI tools for product listings, customer targeting, and order management are well-developed. Collectively, roughly 30–40% of listed tasks involve information processing where AI provides strong assistance.

**Limiting factors:** The primary work product of a shopkeeper is not a digital artifact — it is a functioning physical retail operation. Core tasks like purchasing goods from markets and wholesalers ('identify suppliers,' 'build business relationships,' 'negotiate sales contracts,' 'maintain relationship with suppliers') require in-person judgment, trust-building, and contextual negotiation that AI cannot replicate autonomously in 2026. Direct customer interaction ('selling goods,' 'guarantee customer satisfaction,' 'perform customers' needs analysis,' 'provide customer follow-up services,' 'examine returned goods') demands physical presence and interpersonal judgment. Physical tasks like 'organise product display,' 'check deliveries on receipt,' 'examine merchandise,' and 'ensure point of sale material availability' require hands-on presence. Staff management and recruitment ('manage staff,' 'recruit employees') involve human judgment and legal accountability. Regulatory compliance ('commercial law,' 'law of obligation,' 'ensure compliance with purchasing and contracting regulations') requires contextual interpretation, though AI can assist with research.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the moderate tier (score 5). AI meaningfully assists the information-processing and administrative layer — inventory, pricing, marketing, and financial tracking — but the shopkeeper's core value lies in physical retail operations, supplier relationships, and direct customer service. AI is primarily a productivity amplifier here rather than a substitute: one shopkeeper with AI tools can manage more efficiently, but the role itself cannot be collapsed into software. The small-shop context (no large staff, owner-operator model) also limits the productivity-amplification effect that might reduce headcount in larger retail operations. Key uncertainty is the pace of agentic AI adoption in small Swiss retail, which lags larger chains.

title: Verkaufsaufsichtskräfte in Handelsgeschäften
rationale: AI can help with scheduling, inventory tracking, and sales reporting, but the core job involves supervising people on a shop floor, handling staff conflicts, and responding to real-time customer situations. The heavy reliance on physical presence and interpersonal judgment keeps AI exposure moderate.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** Several tasks in this role are meaningfully AI-assisted. Scheduling and work schedule preparation ("provide department schedule for staff") can be largely automated by workforce management software with AI optimization. Inventory management and stock ordering ("manage inventory," "order supplies") are already heavily automated in retail via demand-forecasting AI. Reporting tasks ("maintain transaction's reports," "write work-related reports") and budget monitoring ("manage budgets," "control of expenses") involve structured data processing that AI handles well. Pricing oversight ("oversee promotional sales prices," "set up pricing strategies") is increasingly handled by dynamic pricing algorithms. Consumer trend analysis ("analyse consumer buying trends") and market research are also strong AI use cases. These tasks collectively represent a meaningful share of the optional and administrative skill set.

**Limiting factors:** The core supervisory work is firmly grounded in physical presence and interpersonal management. Tasks like instructing staff on handling difficult cases, resolving grievances, recruiting and evaluating employees, and ensuring real-time customer service quality require on-floor presence, social judgment, and contextual authority that 2026 AI cannot replicate. Examining returned goods and deciding on appropriate action involves physical inspection and discretionary judgment. Enforcing safety procedures requires active monitoring of a dynamic physical environment. Managing theft prevention similarly demands physical vigilance and human response capability. The occupation's primary output — a well-functioning, compliant, customer-serving retail floor — is a physical and social outcome, not a digital artifact. Even where AI tools assist (e.g., flagging anomalies in transaction data), a human supervisor must act on that information in person.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the moderate tier (score 4). AI meaningfully assists the administrative and analytical periphery — scheduling, inventory, reporting, pricing — but the core supervisory and people-management functions are resistant to substitution due to their physical, interpersonal, and real-time nature. Productivity gains from AI tools may reduce administrative burden but are unlikely to reduce headcount significantly, as the floor-presence requirement is structural. The main uncertainty is how far agentic scheduling and inventory tools will erode the administrative portion of the role over time, but the supervisory core remains human-dependent.

title: Verkäufer in Handelsgeschäften
rationale: AI can handle inventory tracking, payment processing, and product information queries, but the core job involves physical presence — stocking shelves, demonstrating products, and reading customers face-to-face. The irreducible physical and interpersonal demands keep exposure moderate.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** Several tasks in this occupation are meaningfully AI-assisted or automatable. Payment processing and cash register operation (operate cash register, process payments, handle financial transactions) are already heavily automated via self-checkout and POS systems. Inventory management tasks (keep stock records, monitor stock level, maintain parts inventory, process order forms) are well within the capability of current ERP and AI-assisted inventory systems. Product information delivery — answering queries about characteristics of products, providing order information, notifying customers on special offers — can be handled by AI chatbots and in-store kiosks at acceptable quality for routine cases. Sales processors and online sales assistant sub-roles are particularly exposed, as order intake, shipping coordination, and customer follow-up are increasingly handled by agentic e-commerce workflows. The large optional skill list (370 items) dominated by domain-specific advisory tasks (advise on audiovisual equipment, building materials, pet care, etc.) suggests that for specialised sellers, AI product recommendation engines can replicate much of the advisory function.

**Limiting factors:** The primary work product of a shop sales assistant is not a digital artifact — it is a physical retail transaction embedded in a real-world environment. Core tasks like stacking and displaying goods, wrapping and packing merchandise, maintaining store cleanliness, preventing shoplifting, and demonstrating products physically cannot be performed by software AI in 2026. Robotic retail automation exists in narrow warehouse contexts but is not deployed at scale in general retail shop floors. The interpersonal dimension — reading customer mood, handling complaints, building rapport, active listening, showing social competences — remains a genuine human advantage, particularly for higher-value purchases (car leasing, personal shopping, specialised goods). Regulatory and trust barriers also apply: lease agreement administration and contract review require human accountability. The breadth of sub-occupations (from basic shop assistant to car leasing agent) means some roles are far more exposed than others, introducing heterogeneity.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the boundary between Low and Moderate exposure (score 4). The digital and information-processing subtasks are real but secondary to the physical, in-store, and interpersonal core. AI tools are already reshaping inventory, payments, and product information, but the physical presence requirement and the breadth of unstructured customer interaction anchor the role well below the High tier. The car leasing agent and sales processor sub-roles are more exposed (closer to 6), while the basic shop assistant role is closer to 3, yielding a composite score of 4. Confidence is medium due to this sub-occupational heterogeneity.

title: Kassierer und Kartenverkäufer
rationale: AI and self-checkout technology can already handle scanning, payment processing, and receipt issuance with minimal human involvement. The remaining human value lies in customer service, handling exceptions, and physical tasks like bagging — keeping the score just below the highest tier.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The core work product of cashiers is a transaction: scanning goods, calculating totals, processing payments, issuing receipts, and giving change. These tasks — 'operate cash register,' 'process payments,' 'process refunds,' 'issue sales invoices,' 'manage cash flow,' 'quote prices,' and 'keep task records' — are almost entirely automatable with technology already deployed at scale. Self-checkout kiosks, contactless payment terminals, and automated ticketing machines (for the ticket issuing clerk sub-occupation) have been commercially available for over a decade and are accelerating in adoption. The 'sell tickets' and 'process booking' tasks are increasingly handled by online platforms and kiosks. 'Monitor stock level,' 'carry out inventory control accuracy,' and 'monitor ticketing' are well within the scope of automated inventory and POS systems. Even 'prevent shoplifting' is increasingly addressed by computer vision systems. The digital work share of this role is very high: the primary output is a completed transaction record, which is inherently digital.

**Limiting factors:** Several tasks resist full automation. Physical interactions — 'wrapping and placing merchandise in bags,' 'stock shelves,' 'organise storage facilities,' 'maintain store cleanliness,' and 'maintain check-out area cleanliness' — require physical presence and dexterous manipulation that robotics cannot yet handle cost-effectively in unstructured retail environments. Customer-facing interpersonal tasks — 'assist clients with special needs,' 'communicate with customers,' 'handle customer complaints,' 'provide customer guidance on product selection,' and 'show good manners with players' — retain human value, particularly for elderly, disabled, or distressed customers. Regulatory requirements around 'prevent money laundering in gambling,' 'follow ethical code of conduct of gambling,' and 'handle personal identifiable information' create compliance accountability that currently requires a human in the loop. The lottery cashier and ticket issuing clerk sub-occupations also involve some advisory and upselling interactions ('carry out active selling,' 'sales argumentation,' 'upsell products') that benefit from human judgment.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the High tier (score 7). The core transactional tasks are already being automated at scale — self-checkout penetration in Swiss retail is substantial, and automated ticketing is near-universal for many event types. The occupation has not yet reached the Very High tier (8-9) because physical bagging, exception handling, interpersonal service for vulnerable customers, and compliance accountability keep a meaningful human role. However, the trajectory is clearly toward further displacement: the primary remaining human value is customer service in edge cases and physical tasks, not the core transaction processing that defines the occupation. Employment in this category is likely to continue declining as self-service technology matures.

title: Mannequins/Dressmen und sonstige Modelle
rationale: AI can generate photorealistic images of virtual models and clothing, reducing demand for some commercial photography work. However, live runway shows, physical fittings, art modeling, and brand authenticity requirements keep significant human demand intact.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The most AI-vulnerable component of this occupation is still photography for advertising and commercial media. Generative AI (Midjourney, DALL-E, Adobe Firefly) can now produce photorealistic virtual models wearing garments at near-professional quality, and several fashion brands have already piloted AI-generated campaigns. The optional skill 'promote products in advertisements' and 'commercial photography' represent the segment most at risk. AI can also assist with trend research ('conduct research on trends in design,' 'trends in fashion') and portfolio presentation. For art models, AI image generation can produce reference anatomy poses, partially substituting for live figure-drawing sessions in some educational contexts.

**Limiting factors:** The core physical tasks of this occupation are deeply resistant to AI substitution. Walking a runway, turning to display garment drape and movement, attending dress fittings to assess real-world fit on a human body, and posing nude for live artists all require physical presence that no 2026 AI can replicate. Essential skills like 'express yourself physically,' 'harmonise body movements,' 'interact with an audience,' and 'pose for artistic creation' are embodied, real-time performances. High-end fashion clients and luxury brands place significant value on the identity, personality, and cultural cachet of specific human models — a trust and authenticity dimension AI cannot replicate. Regulatory and contractual norms in fashion also favor human models for live events. The occupation is also tiny (92 workers in Switzerland), meaning adoption friction and niche market dynamics further slow displacement.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits in the Low tier (score 3). The still-photography and commercial-advertising segment faces genuine AI pressure, but it represents only a portion of the work. Live runway, art modeling, and physical fitting work — which require an actual human body in space — remain firmly human. The score is held down by the irreducibly physical and performative nature of the primary work product. Uncertainty exists around how quickly fashion brands adopt virtual models for digital-only campaigns, which could erode the commercial photography segment further by 2027–2028.

title: Produktvorführer
rationale: AI cannot physically set up displays, hand out samples, or demonstrate products in person to shoppers. The job's core value is live, embodied persuasion and human rapport at the point of sale, which keeps AI exposure low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A subset of peripheral tasks have meaningful AI exposure. Preparing presentation materials, maintaining promotions records, and distributing catalogues could be partially automated or templated using AI tools. Sales promotion techniques and product comprehension are knowledge domains where AI chatbots or digital kiosks could theoretically substitute — some retailers already deploy interactive screens that explain product features. The 'use different communication channels' skill suggests some digital touchpoints (email, social media) where AI-generated content could assist. These peripheral tasks, however, represent a small fraction of the actual working day.

**Limiting factors:** The core work product of a Produktvorführer is a live, in-person demonstration — physically setting up displays, handing product samples to customers, establishing rapport, and responding dynamically to real-time customer reactions. These tasks require physical presence, dexterous manipulation of goods, and spontaneous interpersonal persuasion. The optional skills cluster heavily around regulated products (alcohol, tobacco) and sensory goods (cheese, chocolate, confectionery), where legal compliance checks, age verification, and tactile/taste-based selling are inherently human. Robotics capable of performing unstructured retail demonstrations at human-level quality do not exist at commercial scale in 2026. Customer trust in live demonstrations is also a key sales mechanism that AI-mediated alternatives have not replicated.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low (2–3) tier. The primary work product is physical and interpersonal — live demonstration and in-person persuasion — with only minor administrative and informational subtasks that AI could assist with. There are no significant digital work products and strong physical-environment and trust-based barriers to substitution. A score of 3 reflects that AI tools can modestly assist with record-keeping and material preparation, but cannot perform the core job function.

title: Haustürverkäufer
rationale: Door-to-door sales requires physically visiting homes, reading social cues in real time, and persuading skeptical strangers face-to-face — tasks AI cannot perform in 2026. The job's core value is human presence and interpersonal trust-building, keeping AI exposure low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A handful of peripheral tasks are AI-assisted: preparing presentation materials, drafting sales scripts and argumentation, prospecting new customers via data analysis, recording orders and preparing invoices, and planning optimized visit routes. AI tools (CRM automation, route optimization, generative text for pitch decks) can meaningfully reduce administrative overhead. The 'use different communication channels' and 'keep records on sales' skills are straightforwardly automatable. These tasks represent perhaps 20–25% of total work effort.

**Limiting factors:** The core work product is a face-to-face persuasion event at a stranger's front door. 'Carry out active selling,' 'identify customer's needs,' 'demonstrate products' features,' 'negotiate price,' and 'guarantee customer satisfaction' all require physical presence, real-time social reading, and the ability to build trust with a skeptical, unscheduled interlocutor. No 2026 AI system can knock on a door, adapt body language, handle an aggressive refusal, or physically demonstrate a product. 'Adapt to different weather conditions' and 'travelling between sales areas' are inherently physical. Regulatory context (Swiss consumer protection, door-to-door sales cooling-off rules) also requires human accountability. The occupation is structurally resistant to remote or digital substitution because the customer has not opted in to any digital channel.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). AI can trim administrative and preparation tasks at the margins, but the primary work product — a live, unannounced, in-person sales interaction — is entirely outside 2026 AI's physical and social reach. The score is slightly above the floor (2) because AI-assisted prospecting, route planning, and invoice preparation do provide real productivity uplift, but substitution risk is minimal.

title: Telefonverkäufer
rationale: AI can already make outbound sales calls, follow scripts, handle objections, update CRM records, and schedule appointments — the entire core workflow of this job. This is one of the most directly replaceable roles by conversational AI agents available today.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The ISCO task list reads almost as a specification sheet for a 2026 AI voice agent. Tasks (a)–(e) — promoting goods via telephone following scripts, creating interest, arranging dispatch, scheduling appointments, and updating marketing databases — are all commercially deployable today. Tools like Bland AI, Synthflow, and enterprise platforms (Salesforce Einstein, HubSpot AI) already automate outbound calling, script adherence, CRM updates (the 'use customer relationship management software' and 'process data' skills), and follow-up scheduling. The 'maintain statistics of calls made' and 'submit periodic reports' tasks (g–h) are trivially automated. The essential skill 'keep task records' and optional skill 'perform data analysis' are pure information-processing tasks with no physical or interpersonal resistance. Even 'speak different languages' — historically a human advantage — is now handled by real-time multilingual voice AI.

**Limiting factors:** The most plausible resistance comes from regulatory constraints in Switzerland around automated calling (DSGVO/nDSG compliance, opt-in requirements) and consumer trust — some customers may refuse to engage with AI callers. The skill 'create solutions to problems' and 'handle helpdesk problems' suggest occasional non-scripted situations, but these are edge cases in a role defined by scripted, repetitive outreach. 'Show confidence' and 'tolerate stress' are human coping skills, not barriers to automation. No physical tasks exist in this role whatsoever — it is 100% digital/voice work conducted remotely.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the maximum exposure tier alongside data entry clerks. The entire value chain — outbound call, script delivery, objection handling, CRM logging, appointment booking, reporting — is replicable by AI agents already in commercial deployment. The Swiss employment figure of 294 workers reflects a role already in structural decline. The only uncertainty is adoption pace given Swiss regulatory caution around automated calls, but this delays rather than prevents displacement. Score: 10.

title: Tankwarte
rationale: AI can automate payment processing and stock reporting, but the core job involves physically fuelling vehicles, checking tyre pressure, washing windows, and doing minor repairs on a forecourt. The hands-on, outdoor, customer-facing nature of most tasks keeps AI exposure low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A subset of tasks are digitally automatable. Payment collection and cash register operation are already largely automated via self-service pumps and contactless payment terminals. Stock monitoring, preparing fuel sales reports, and order intake involve structured data that AI or simple software handles well. E-commerce systems and issue of sales invoices are routine digital workflows. Active selling, product guidance, and sales argumentation could be partially supported by AI recommendation tools. These administrative and transactional tasks represent perhaps 20–25% of the role's time.

**Limiting factors:** The dominant share of this occupation's work is irreducibly physical and on-site. Fuelling vehicles, checking and replenishing tyre pressure and fluid levels, washing windscreens, replacing tyres and wiper blades, operating car wash facilities, and cleaning the forecourt all require physical presence and manual dexterity in an unstructured outdoor environment. Robotic fuelling systems exist in prototype form but are not commercially deployed at scale in 2026. Customer-facing interpersonal tasks — identifying needs, handling complaints, preventing shoplifting — require situational awareness and physical presence. Hazardous substances compliance (fuel, lubricants) involves real-world safety checks that cannot be delegated to software. The optional skills around improvised vehicle repairs and car controls further anchor the role in physical problem-solving.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). The primary work product is a physical service delivered on a forecourt, not a digital artifact. AI and automation can handle the transactional tail of the job (payments, reporting, stock alerts), but these are auxiliary to the core physical tasks. Adoption barriers include the unstructured outdoor environment, safety regulations around fuel handling, and the low cost of human labour relative to robotic alternatives at this wage level. No meaningful displacement pressure is expected by 2026.

title: Imbissverkäufer
rationale: AI cannot hand food to customers, clean equipment, or physically prepare and portion meals at a counter. The job is almost entirely hands-on physical and interpersonal work, leaving very little for AI to automate.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A small subset of tasks have some AI relevance. Payment processing (task h) is already largely automated via POS systems and self-checkout kiosks. Order-taking (task b) can be partially handled by digital ordering screens common in fast-food settings. Stock monitoring (task g, 'monitor stock level') could be assisted by inventory management software. The skill 'use different communication channels' and 'process customer orders' have modest digital components. However, these represent peripheral or already-automated elements rather than the core work.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of tasks are physically grounded and require human presence: cleaning and peeling foodstuffs, preparing and reheating food, portioning and wrapping, stocking refrigerators, and greeting and serving guests face-to-face. Robotics capable of performing unstructured food-handling tasks (slicing, trimming, portioning varied items, managing a salad bar) at acceptable quality and cost are not commercially deployed at scale in 2026. Supervisory and team-management skills ('manage staff,' 'schedule shifts,' 'supervise crew,' 'train employees') require human judgment and interpersonal presence. Food safety compliance, handling customer complaints, and maintaining hygiene standards all depend on embodied, real-time human action. The auxiliary tool rule applies firmly: digital tools like POS systems are incidental; if the computer broke, 95% of this job would still need to happen in person.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits squarely in the Low tier (score 2). The primary work product is physical — prepared and served food delivered to customers in person — and the core skills are manual, hygienic, and interpersonal. AI contributes marginally through automated ordering kiosks and inventory tools, but these are already baked into the industry and do not reduce headcount substantially. No plausible 2026 AI capability meaningfully displaces the hands-on counter attendant role.

title: Verkaufskräfte, anderweitig nicht genannt
rationale: AI can automate booking reservations, process payments, generate rental contracts, and handle routine customer queries. However, physical vehicle inspection, damage assessment, and in-person customer service keep full automation limited, landing this role in the high-exposure tier.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A substantial portion of this role involves information-processing tasks that 2026 AI handles well. Processing reservations, handling financial transactions, recording customer personal data, producing sales reports, managing contracts, and processing payments are all digitally mediated workflows that rental management software already partially automates — and AI agents can extend further. Skills like 'use rental management software,' 'process data,' 'write work-related reports,' and 'produce sales reports' are squarely in AI's current capability zone. Customer-facing tasks such as providing price information, conducting customer identification, and handling routine complaints can increasingly be handled by AI-powered chatbots and voice agents. Forecasting product demand and analysing marketplace competition in the rental industry are analytical tasks where AI tools already outperform unaided human judgment in data-rich environments.

**Limiting factors:** Several core tasks resist automation meaningfully. Physically checking vehicle damage requires on-site visual inspection — while computer vision is advancing, deploying it reliably across diverse vehicle conditions in unstructured rental lot environments is not yet standard practice. Arranging pick-up and drop-off, managing vehicle inventory physically, and performing vehicle acquisition involve real-world coordination and judgment. Handling rental overdues and managing claims processes often require negotiation, empathy, and legal nuance that AI handles poorly in adversarial or emotionally charged situations. The rental manager sub-occupation adds staff supervision and budget management responsibilities where human accountability and interpersonal authority remain important. Regulatory requirements around contract law and customer identification also impose human-in-the-loop obligations in many jurisdictions.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the boundary of moderate and high exposure (score 6). The majority of routine transactional and administrative tasks — reservations, payments, contracts, reporting — are automatable with current tools, and AI agents could handle a large share of standard customer interactions. Physical vehicle inspection and interpersonal service in complex or disputed situations provide meaningful resistance. The rental manager sub-role adds managerial complexity that slows full substitution. Uncertainty arises from the heterogeneity across sub-occupations (agent vs. manager) and the pace of computer vision deployment in vehicle inspection contexts.

title: Kinderbetreuer
rationale: AI cannot physically help a child dress, eat, or play, and cannot supervise children's safety or manage their behaviour in person. The job is almost entirely hands-on physical care and human presence, leaving very little for AI to replace.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A small subset of tasks involves information processing that AI could assist with. Keeping records on individual children (daily observations, meals, medications) could be partially automated or streamlined with AI-assisted logging tools. Communicating with parents via written messages or apps could be aided by AI drafting. Assessing children's developmental progress and identifying behavioural patterns could benefit from AI-assisted observation tools. These peripheral administrative tasks represent perhaps 10–15% of the role's actual time and effort.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of core tasks are irreducibly physical and interpersonal. Assisting children to wash, dress, and feed themselves; playing games; reading stories; supervising outdoor recreation; managing behaviour in real time; attending to basic physical needs (toileting, first aid, wound care); and escorting children on buses or field trips all require physical presence, embodied judgment, and trusted human relationships. Robotics in 2026 cannot perform delicate, responsive physical caregiving with children in unstructured environments. Regulatory and safeguarding requirements in Switzerland mandate qualified human supervision ratios. Parents and institutions place extremely high trust requirements on human carers that AI cannot substitute. The emotional attunement required — relating empathetically, supporting children who have experienced trauma, managing conflict — is deeply resistant to AI substitution.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low (2) tier. The primary work product is physical care, safety supervision, and human relationship — none of which AI can deliver in 2026 or the near term. AI may marginally assist with record-keeping or parent communication, but these are auxiliary tasks. The occupation is anchored by physical, regulatory, and trust-based constraints that place it close to the minimal-exposure tier, with a score of 2 reflecting only the minor administrative components where AI tools could offer modest productivity assistance.

title: Lernbetreuer
rationale: AI cannot physically supervise children, assist a student with motor skills, or respond to a child's emotional distress in the classroom. The job is fundamentally about in-person care, physical presence, and human relationship-building with children.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A small subset of tasks has meaningful AI exposure. Compiling course material, preparing lesson materials, and copying/collating written material are straightforward information tasks that AI can handle well. Learning needs analysis and observing student progress could be partially supported by AI-assisted assessment tools. Advising on lesson plans and keeping attendance records are also digitally automatable. These peripheral administrative tasks represent perhaps 15–20% of actual working time.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of core tasks are physically and interpersonally grounded in ways that 2026 AI cannot replicate. Performing playground surveillance, attending to children's basic physical needs, facilitating motor skill activities, managing children's behavioural problems, and guaranteeing student safety all require physical presence and real-time human judgment. Assisting students with special educational needs, disability care, and supporting children's wellbeing involve tactile, emotional, and relational dimensions that AI cannot substitute. Regulatory and safeguarding requirements in Swiss schools mandate qualified human supervision of minors. The sub-occupations — early years assistant, SEN assistant — are especially anchored in hands-on physical and emotional care. Even the most automatable tasks (lesson prep, attendance) are auxiliary to the core work, which passes the 'broken computer' test easily: 90%+ of this job still happens in person regardless of digital tools.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). AI can assist with a narrow band of administrative and preparation tasks, but the primary work product is in-person developmental support, supervision, and care for children — none of which AI can deliver in a school setting. Adoption barriers (child safety regulations, trust requirements, physical environment) further suppress any displacement effect. The score of 3 reflects modest productivity gains from AI-assisted lesson prep and record-keeping, with no meaningful substitution of core duties.

title: Pflegehelfer in Institutionen
rationale: AI cannot lift, reposition, bathe, or feed patients — the core physical care tasks in this job. The work is hands-on and interpersonal in a way that 2026 AI and robotics cannot replicate at scale.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A subset of tasks involve information processing that AI can assist with: managing healthcare users' data, monitoring basic patient signs (where sensor-based AI tools are emerging), conveying routine medical information, and using e-health and mobile health technologies. Documentation, care plan adherence tracking, and basic anomaly flagging (e.g., vital sign deviations) are areas where AI tools are already deployed as aids. Computer literacy and e-health skills confirm a digital interface exists, but these are auxiliary tools, not the core work product.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of tasks are irreducibly physical and interpersonal: positioning, lifting, and turning patients; assisting with personal hygiene, feeding, and dressing; providing massage and non-pharmacological pain relief; changing bed linen; and responding to emergency care situations. These require dexterous manipulation in unstructured, human-body environments — precisely where 2026 robotics remain far below human capability. Beyond physical constraints, the therapeutic relationship, empathy, active listening, and working in multidisciplinary teams under direct supervision all demand human presence and judgment. Regulatory and safety requirements in Swiss healthcare further slow any automation adoption.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low (2–3) tier. AI contributes marginally as a documentation and monitoring aid, but the primary work product is physical personal care delivered in real time to vulnerable patients. No commercially available AI or robotic system in 2026 can substitute for the core hands-on tasks. The score of 3 (rather than 2) reflects the genuine and growing role of e-health tools, sensor-based monitoring, and AI-assisted documentation that do reshape a meaningful minority of the role's peripheral tasks.

title: Haus- und Familienpfleger und -betreuer
rationale: AI cannot bathe, dress, lift, or physically accompany elderly or disabled people in their homes. The job is almost entirely hands-on physical and emotional care, leaving very little for AI to replace.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A small subset of tasks involves information processing that AI could assist with: maintaining care records and reporting changes in client condition, scheduling appointments, and advising clients on nutrition or hygiene. AI tools could plausibly draft care notes, flag scheduling conflicts, or surface dietary guidance. These administrative and informational subtasks represent perhaps 10–15% of actual working time. Voice-based AI companions could partially substitute for the 'keep company' and 'reading aloud' functions in limited contexts, though this faces significant trust and adoption barriers in vulnerable populations.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of tasks in this occupation are irreducibly physical and interpersonal: assisting with personal hygiene, dressing, feeding, lifting and repositioning clients, washing laundry, cleaning living quarters, preparing meals, and accompanying clients to appointments. Robotics capable of performing these tasks in unstructured private home environments — with fragile, elderly, or disabled individuals — remain far beyond 2026 deployment readiness. The emotional and relational dimension (psychological support, trust-building with vulnerable clients and families) is a core deliverable, not a peripheral one. Regulatory and safeguarding requirements in Swiss care settings further constrain any autonomous AI role. The 'auxiliary tool rule' applies strongly here: if every digital device broke, 95%+ of this job would still need to happen in person.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low (2) tier. The primary work product is physical care and human presence delivered in private homes — neither of which AI can replicate at scale in 2026. Administrative assistance from AI is plausible but marginal. The occupation is structurally similar to hairdresser or cook in terms of AI exposure: some peripheral tasks are digitally assisted, but the core value is manual skill and human presence. No meaningful displacement risk is foreseeable within the scoring horizon.

Regulatory compliance (measuring catch, returning illegal sizes) requires real-time physical handling. **Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 2). The primary work product is physical — caught and processed fish delivered via vessel operations — not a digital artifact. AI tools offer marginal assistance in navigation planning and record-keeping, but cannot substitute for the core physical, safety, and environmental demands. No meaningful adoption barriers need to be invoked; the tasks themselves are simply not AI-performable at the 2026 frontier.

title: Handwerks- und verwandte Berufe, onA
rationale: This catch-all category covers miscellaneous craft and trade workers whose core work involves physical manipulation of materials — cutting, assembling, repairing, or fabricating things by hand. AI can assist with planning or ordering supplies, but the hands-on physical work itself remains largely beyond current automation.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** ISCO 7000 is a residual 'not elsewhere classified' grouping for craft and related trades workers, meaning it captures a heterogeneous mix of occupations that don't fit neatly into the more specific ISCO 71–74 sub-major groups. Without detailed ESCO task or skill descriptors for this specific code, inference must rely on the broader ISCO 7 major group characteristics. Craft workers in this tier typically perform tasks such as measuring, cutting, shaping, assembling, and finishing physical materials. Some peripheral tasks — ordering materials, reading technical drawings, logging work orders — involve structured digital information and are increasingly assisted by AI tools or ERP systems. Scheduling and quoting software with AI features is commercially available and in use in the trades sector.

**Limiting factors:** The core work product of craft and trade occupations is a physical transformation of the real world — a repaired object, a fabricated component, an installed fixture. Current robotics (as of 2026) handles structured, repetitive pick-and-place tasks in factory settings at roughly 70% human efficiency, but unstructured physical environments, varied materials, and the fine motor demands of craft work remain firmly human-advantaged. Regulatory requirements (safety certifications, quality inspections) and client-facing trust relationships further slow AI substitution. The 'not elsewhere classified' nature of this group also suggests it includes niche or highly specialized manual trades where automation investment is economically unattractive.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits in the Low (2–3) tier. The primary work is physical and craft-based, with AI playing only a supporting role in administrative or planning subtasks. The main uncertainty driving low confidence is the extreme heterogeneity of the residual ISCO 7000 grouping — some sub-occupations within it could be more digitally oriented (e.g., instrument makers, precision workers) and would score higher, while others are purely manual and would score lower. A score of 3 reflects the modest but real AI assistance available for the administrative fringe of these roles.

title: Baukonstruktions- und verwandte Berufe, onA
rationale: Building construction work involves physical tasks like cutting, shaping, and assembling structural materials on real job sites, which AI cannot perform. The score is low because the core output is a physical structure, not a digital artifact.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** ISCO 7110 ('Building construction workers and related trades, not elsewhere classified') is a residual catch-all category for construction trades not captured elsewhere. Typical tasks include measuring, cutting, shaping, and assembling building materials; reading technical drawings; and using hand and power tools. Some peripheral tasks have AI exposure: reading and interpreting technical drawings could be assisted by AI-based plan analysis tools, and scheduling or materials estimation could be partially automated. Digital tools for site documentation (photos, reports) are increasingly AI-assisted. These peripheral tasks might represent 15–25% of total work time.

**Limiting factors:** The core work product is a physical structure or structural component, requiring manual dexterity, on-site judgment in unstructured environments, and physical manipulation of materials. Robotic construction tools exist in narrow niches (e.g., bricklaying robots in controlled settings) but are far from general deployment on Swiss construction sites by 2026. Safety regulations, site variability, and the need for real-time physical problem-solving create strong barriers. The interpersonal coordination with site managers, inspectors, and other trades also resists automation.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 2). The residual 'not elsewhere classified' nature of ISCO 7110 introduces uncertainty — it could include some more digitally-oriented construction roles — but the dominant work is physical and site-based. The modest digital periphery (drawing interpretation, documentation) justifies a score of 2 rather than 1. Confidence is low due to the heterogeneous nature of this catch-all category.

title: Steinmetze, Steinspalter, -bearbeiter und Steinbildhauer
rationale: AI cannot cut, carve, shape, or polish stone — the entire job is skilled physical transformation of hard materials using hand tools and power equipment. A few peripheral tasks like quoting or record-keeping have some AI assistance, but the core craft is firmly manual.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A small subset of peripheral tasks carry some AI exposure. Interpreting 2D/3D plans could be assisted by AI-powered CAD viewers. Answering requests for quotation, estimating restoration costs, and keeping records of work progress are information-processing tasks where AI tools (drafting emails, cost estimation templates, scheduling software) can save time. The optional skill 'program a CNC controller' is notable: CNC stone-cutting machines are increasingly software-driven, and AI-assisted toolpath generation is a real capability in 2026. However, CNC operation is listed as a single optional skill and is separately classified under ISCO 8112 (machine operators), meaning it is peripheral to this occupation's core identity.

**Limiting factors:** The primary work product is the physical transformation of stone — cutting, carving, shaping, polishing, and setting blocks of granite, marble, and similar materials using chisels, grinders, and hand-held power tools. Every core essential skill (use stonemason's chisel, polish stone by hand, operate grinding hand tools, mark stone workpieces, inspect stone surface, prepare stone for smoothing) requires dexterous physical manipulation in unstructured environments with highly variable raw materials. Stone carving and monumental masonry demand tactile feedback, spatial judgment about material grain and fracture lines, and fine motor control that 2026 robotics cannot replicate outside highly structured factory settings. Restoration work on historic buildings and monuments adds further complexity: each project is unique, materials are irreplaceable, and regulatory/heritage conservation requirements demand human accountability and craft judgment. Physical safety requirements (working at heights, securing work areas, rigging loads) also resist remote or automated substitution.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low (2) tier. The work is almost entirely physical craft in unstructured environments, with no digital primary output. AI can marginally assist with quoting, record-keeping, and plan interpretation — perhaps 10–15% of total task time — but cannot perform or meaningfully accelerate the core stone-working tasks. Adoption barriers are high: the materials are irreplaceable, the environments are variable, and the craft tradition carries regulatory and heritage weight. The score of 2 reflects that some digital peripheral tasks exist, but the occupation's core is robustly resistant to AI substitution through at least the near-term horizon.

title: Betonierer, Betonoberflächenfertiger und verwandte Berufe
rationale: AI cannot pour concrete, screed surfaces, set terrazzo, or physically manipulate forms and reinforcement on a construction site. The work is almost entirely hands-on physical transformation of materials in unstructured outdoor environments, leaving very little for AI to automate.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A small subset of tasks has some AI relevance. Interpreting 2D/3D plans (optional skill) could be assisted by AI tools that parse construction drawings. Inspecting concrete structures and recognizing signs of corrosion are tasks where computer vision systems are being piloted in construction, though deployment in unstructured field conditions remains limited. Record-keeping and calculating supply needs are minor administrative tasks that AI can handle. Collectively, these represent perhaps 10–15% of actual working time.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of core tasks — pouring concrete, screeding, placing and removing forms, mixing terrazzo, grinding and grouting terrazzo surfaces, applying proofing membranes, and monitoring curing in real time — require dexterous physical manipulation in variable, unstructured outdoor or semi-outdoor environments. Current robotics cannot perform these tasks at commercial scale; warehouse-grade robots operate in highly structured settings, and construction-site robotics for finishing work remain experimental and far below human efficiency. There are no regulatory barriers needed to protect this occupation — the physical capability gap is the barrier. The work also demands real-time reactive judgment in time-critical environments (e.g., concrete setting windows), which agentic AI cannot yet handle in physical space.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low (2) tier. The primary work product is a physical structure or surface, not a digital artifact. AI tools offer marginal assistance on planning interpretation and administrative tasks, but the core value delivered — skilled physical manipulation of concrete and terrazzo materials — is essentially untouched by 2026 AI capabilities. Uncertainty is low; no plausible near-term AI development changes this picture.

title: Baukonstruktions- und verwandte Berufe, anderweitig nicht genannt

Robotics capable of operating in these conditions (variable heights, unstable surfaces, confined demolition sites, suspended access) remain far beyond 2026 deployment capability. Safety-critical regulatory requirements and the inherently unpredictable physical environment create strong adoption barriers even for partial automation. **Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the 0–1 tier. The work is almost entirely physical transformation of real-world structures in unstructured, hazardous environments. title: Ausbaufachkräfte und verwandte Berufe, onA

title: Glaser

title: Metallarbeiter, Mechaniker, Polymechaniker, Produktionsmechaniker und verwandte Berufe, onA
rationale: AI can assist with design specifications and quality documentation, but the core work—cutting, welding, assembling, and machining metal parts—requires hands-on physical skill in varied environments. Robotic automation exists in structured factory settings but cannot yet replace the adaptability of skilled tradespeople across diverse tasks.
analysis:

Documentation tasks (maintenance logs, work orders, compliance records) are partially automatable. The 'onA' (not elsewhere classified) designation means this group is heterogeneous—ranging from highly manual metalworkers to semi-automated production mechanics—making blanket automation difficult. Regulatory and safety requirements in Swiss industrial settings also slow adoption. Physical dexterity, troubleshooting unexpected mechanical failures, and working in unstructured or confined environments remain firmly human-advantaged.

**Net assessment:** The occupation sits at the low end of exposure (score 3). AI and robotics provide real but limited productivity gains—primarily in programming, documentation, and structured inspection—without displacing the core physical and adaptive skills. The heterogeneity of the residual ISCO category introduces uncertainty: some sub-roles (e.g., CNC programmers) may score higher (4–5), while purely manual metalworkers score lower (1–2). title: Blechkaltverformer, Baumetallverformer, Former (für Metallguss), Schweisser, Anlagen- und Apparatebauer und verwandte Berufe, onA
rationale: AI cannot bend sheet metal, weld joints, or cast molten metal — these are hands-on physical tasks requiring strength, dexterity, and real-time judgment in unstructured environments.
