analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The ISCO 7210 grouping covers sheet metal cold-forming, structural metal fabrication, metal casting, welding, and plant/apparatus construction. Several peripheral tasks have meaningful AI exposure: reading and interpreting technical drawings can be assisted by AI vision tools; quality inspection of welds and formed parts is increasingly supported by computer vision systems (automated weld inspection is in early industrial deployment); CNC programming for metal-forming machines is partially automatable via AI-assisted CAM software; and documentation, work-order processing, and material calculations are straightforward information tasks AI handles well. These auxiliary digital tasks may represent 15–25% of total work time.

**Limiting factors:** The dominant work product is a physically transformed metal object — a welded seam, a bent panel, a cast component, a pressure vessel. None of these can be produced by software alone. Welding in particular requires fine motor control, real-time sensory feedback (heat, arc sound, material behaviour), and adaptive judgment in varied spatial configurations that 2026 robotics handles only in highly structured, repetitive factory settings (~70% human efficiency in ideal conditions). Field welding, custom fabrication, and repair work in unstructured environments remain firmly human. Metal casting involves handling molten material with safety-critical physical precision. Regulatory frameworks for pressure vessels and structural metalwork (e.g., EN/ISO welding certifications, Swiss SUVA safety requirements) mandate certified human accountability. Physical danger and liability further slow automation adoption.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits solidly in the Low tier (score 2). The core value delivered is physical transformation of metal, which 2026 AI and robotics cannot reliably replicate outside narrow factory automation. AI tools provide modest productivity gains in drawing interpretation, CNC programming, and inspection support, but do not threaten the primary skilled manual work. The heterogeneity of the grouping (welders vs. casters vs. apparatus builders) introduces some uncertainty, as highly repetitive factory welding is closer to automation than bespoke structural fabrication — hence medium confidence.

title: Former und Kernmacher (für Metallguss)
rationale: AI cannot pack sand, assemble mould sections, cut sprue holes, or physically repair surface defects — the entire job involves hands-on shaping of physical materials in a foundry environment. Minor digital tasks like recording production data are peripheral, keeping this firmly in the low-exposure tier.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A small subset of tasks has some AI relevance. 'Handle metal work orders' and 'record production data for quality control' involve structured information that AI or simple automation could assist with. 'Select mould types' and 'troubleshoot' involve decision-making that AI advisory tools could partially support, particularly for well-documented defect patterns. 'Monitor gauge' is a task where computer vision and sensor-based automation are already deployed in industrial settings. These peripheral digital and decision-support tasks represent perhaps 10–15% of the role's actual time and cognitive load.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of tasks — constructing moulds by hand or machine, mixing and filling moulding material, inserting mould structures, clamping sections, cutting spouts and runner holes, extracting products, repairing surface defects — require dexterous physical manipulation in an unstructured, high-temperature foundry environment. Current robotics (as of 2026) can handle structured pick-and-place at roughly 70% human efficiency in warehouse settings, but foundry work involves irregular heavy objects, molten metal proximity, tactile feedback for sand compaction uniformity, and real-time adaptation to material variability. None of the essential skills describe cognitive operations on data; all describe physical transformation of materials. There are no regulatory barriers per se, but the physical environment itself is the primary barrier. The optional skill list is dominated by physical process knowledge (ferrous/non-ferrous processing, operating smelters, smoothing burred surfaces), not software tools.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits squarely in the Low (2) tier. The primary work product is a physical mould produced through skilled manual and machine-assisted labour in a demanding industrial environment. AI can marginally assist with scheduling, work order management, and defect-pattern lookup, but cannot substitute for the core physical craft. Robotic foundry automation exists in highly structured die-casting contexts but does not yet displace the flexible, judgment-intensive hand-moulding and core-making work described here. The score of 2 reflects that some structured data tasks exist but the job is fundamentally physical.

title: Schweisser und Brennschneider
rationale: AI cannot weld, cut, or braze metal — these are hands-on physical processes requiring dexterity, heat judgment, and real-time adaptation to material behaviour. Some peripheral tasks like reading plans or recording production data have AI tools, but the core work is firmly physical.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A subset of peripheral tasks carry some AI exposure. Interpreting 2D/3D plans could be assisted by AI vision tools that parse technical drawings. Recording production data for quality control and inspecting quality of products could be partially supported by computer vision systems already deployed in some industrial settings. The welding coordinator sub-role involves supervising staff, liaising with managers, and evaluating employee work — administrative and communicative tasks where AI scheduling and reporting tools add marginal productivity. Optional skills like 'consult technical resources' and 'communicate schedules' are also digitally assisted. In aggregate, these peripheral tasks represent perhaps 10–15% of the occupation's actual work content.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of tasks — applying arc, MIG, TIG, brazing, and soldering techniques; operating oxy-fuel torches; monitoring gauge readings in real time; ensuring correct metal temperature; performing demanding weldments; smoothing burred surfaces; and removing processed workpieces — require precise physical manipulation in unstructured or semi-structured environments. Robotic welding exists in high-volume automotive manufacturing, but it operates in tightly controlled, pre-programmed fixtures. The ESCO description emphasises adaptive judgment: monitoring for warping, distortion, and overheating; spotting metal imperfections and corrosion; and troubleshooting in situ. These demand tactile feedback, spatial reasoning in three dimensions, and real-time response to material variability that 2026 robotics cannot reliably replicate outside structured factory cells. Regulatory and safety requirements (PPE, hazardous materials handling, height safety) further anchor the role in physical presence.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 2). The primary work product is a physically transformed metal joint or cut, not a digital artifact. AI and robotics can assist with documentation, scheduling, and quality logging, and automated welding robots handle repetitive high-volume tasks in structured environments — but the broad, adaptive, multi-process welding described here (including repair work, lead fixtures, brazing, and demanding weldments) remains human-dependent. The score of 2 reflects that some digital tools touch the periphery of the role without threatening its core.

title: Blechkaltverformer
rationale: AI cannot bend, cut, weld, or rivet sheet metal — the core of this job is physically shaping and assembling metal parts in varied real-world environments. Some tasks like reading blueprints or recording production data have AI tools, but these are minor supports to overwhelmingly hands-on work.
analysis:

Recording production data for quality control and consulting technical resources are also partially automatable. Collectively, these represent perhaps 10–15% of working time. Computer vision systems could theoretically assist with quality inspection and recognising signs of corrosion, though deployment in unstructured workshop environments remains limited in 2026.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of this occupation's essential skills describe direct physical manipulation of materials: cutting, welding, riveting, soldering, forging, casting, smoothing, and assembling metal components. These require dexterous manipulation in varied, unstructured physical environments — exactly where 2026 robotics remain far below human capability. Tasks like operating oxy-fuel cutting torches, handling gas cylinders, working at heights, and installing containers in construction settings involve safety-critical physical judgment that no commercially deployed robot handles reliably. The 68 essential skills are dominated by hands-on metalworking techniques (arc welding, spot welding, cold forging, smithing, precision metalworking) with no primary digital work product. Even the most automatable sub-task — blueprint reading — feeds directly into physical execution that AI cannot perform.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 2). The primary work product is a physically fabricated or installed metal object, not a digital artifact. AI can marginally assist with planning, documentation, and inspection support, but cannot substitute for the core manual craft. Adoption barriers are high: unstructured environments, safety regulations, and the need for real-time physical problem-solving all slow any robotic encroachment. The score of 2 reflects that some peripheral tasks benefit from AI tools, but the occupation's core is essentially untouched by 2026 AI capabilities.

CAD and CAE software (listed as optional skills) represent digital interfaces where AI co-pilots are increasingly capable. AI can assist marginally with drawing interpretation and administrative tasks, but these are auxiliary to the core job. No commercially deployed AI or robotic system in 2026 can substitute for the physical, site-adaptive labor that defines this role. title: Verspannungsmonteure und Seilspleisser
rationale: Rigging and cable splicing is almost entirely physical work — lifting heavy loads, splicing cables, erecting scaffolding, and working at heights in unpredictable environments. AI cannot perform these tasks, and the planning aids it could offer are minor compared to the hands-on core.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A small subset of tasks involve information processing where AI could assist. Interpreting 2D and 3D plans, reading crane load charts, understanding rigging work orders, and providing rigging plans are cognitive tasks that AI tools can support — for example, AI can parse technical documentation, flag load limit violations, or help generate rig work schedules. Planning and scheduling tasks (plan rig work schedules, organise resources for artistic production) could be partially assisted by AI-powered project management tools. These represent perhaps 10–15% of the total task portfolio.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of essential skills are irreducibly physical and situational. Core tasks — rig loads, assemble tent constructions, attach roofing cover, dismantle scaffolding, set up stage decks, secure heavy construction equipment, work safely at heights — require dexterous manipulation in unstructured, dynamic environments (construction sites, theatre rigging lofts, outdoor events, ski lift towers). 2026 robotics cannot operate at the required speed, dexterity, or safety margin in these settings. The react-to-events-in-time-critical-environments skill is emblematic: real-time physical judgment under load is a domain where AI has no near-term deployment path. Regulatory and safety requirements (PPE, construction site safety, working-at-heights procedures) further embed human accountability. The sub-occupations — theatrical rigger, tent installer, tower rigger — all share this physical-environment dependency.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 2). AI can marginally assist with documentation, scheduling, and plan interpretation, but these are auxiliary to the core physical work product. There are no digital outputs being produced; the job produces safely rigged physical structures and loads. Adoption barriers are high: unstructured outdoor and performance environments, safety-critical loads, and the absence of viable robotic platforms for this work type. The score of 2 reflects the small but real planning/documentation assistance AI can provide, without overstating substitution potential.

title: Grobschmiede, Hammerschmiede und Schmiedepresser
rationale: AI cannot heat metal in a forge, swing a hammer, or physically shape steel on an anvil — the core work is manual transformation of hot metal in demanding physical environments. Some machine monitoring and blueprint reading have minor AI assistance, but the job remains firmly hands-on.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A small subset of tasks involves structured information processing that AI can assist with. Reading work orders and blueprints to determine tolerances and sequences (task f) can be partially supported by AI document parsing. Monitoring gauges and automated machines (skills: monitor gauge, monitor automated machines, set up the controller of a machine) could benefit from computer vision and sensor-based AI alerting. Quality inspection for conformity with product specifications (task g, skill: remove inadequate workpieces, inspect quality of products) is an area where machine vision systems are increasingly deployed in structured manufacturing environments. These represent perhaps 10–15% of core task time.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of essential skills describe direct physical manipulation of hot metal in unstructured or semi-structured environments: heating metal in furnaces, shaping on anvils, operating drop hammers and forging presses, using tongs, chisels, and hand tools, joining and tempering. These require dexterous manipulation under high heat, variable material behavior, and real-time tactile and visual feedback that 2026 robotics cannot replicate at production scale outside highly structured automotive-grade forging lines. Even in automated forging press operation, a human worker sets up dies, loads workpieces, monitors the process, and handles exceptions. Blacksmithing sub-occupations involve artisanal and bespoke work that is entirely resistant to current automation. Regulatory and safety requirements in Swiss manufacturing further slow adoption of autonomous physical systems.

title: Werkzeugmaschineneinrichter und -bediener
rationale: AI can optimize CNC programs, assist with quality inspection via computer vision, and automate some monitoring tasks, but the core work—physically setting up machines, changing tooling, and responding to real-world malfunctions—requires hands-on presence. The job remains anchored in physical manipulation of metal and machinery, keeping AI exposure low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The most AI-susceptible tasks in this occupation are those involving digital information processing and programmable control. CNC programming (listed as 'program a CNC controller,' 'use CAM software,' 'use automatic programming') is increasingly assisted by AI-driven CAM tools that auto-generate toolpaths from CAD geometry, reducing skilled programming time. Quality inspection tasks ('inspect quality of products,' 'carry out measurements of parts,' 'monitor manufacturing quality standards,' 'statistical process control') are addressable by computer vision systems already deployed in some Swiss precision manufacturing facilities. Monitoring tasks ('monitor automated machines,' 'monitor gauge,' 'monitor moving workpiece') are candidates for sensor-based AI oversight. The optional skills list—dominated by programming languages (C++, Java, MATLAB)—suggests some sub-roles (e.g., CNC machine programmers) operate in more digital environments, but these are peripheral to the median worker.

**Limiting factors:** The dominant share of essential skills describe irreducibly physical tasks: setting up machine controls, changing worn cutting tools, holding workpieces, loading/extracting materials from furnaces, shaping metal over anvils, operating hydraulic machinery, and resolving equipment malfunctions in unstructured real-world conditions. Robotics capable of performing these tasks in the varied, unstructured environments of a Swiss job shop (different machine types, custom fixtures, non-standard workpieces) remain well below human efficiency in 2026. The occupation also requires tactile judgment—detecting vibration anomalies, assessing surface finish by touch, responding to unexpected material behavior—that current AI systems cannot replicate. Regulatory and safety requirements ('ensure compliance with safety legislation,' 'apply health and safety standards') further slow automation adoption in Swiss manufacturing contexts.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). AI and automation do meaningfully touch the digital periphery—CNC programming assistance, vision-based inspection, process monitoring—and these effects will modestly reduce headcount demand at the margins, particularly for pure programming sub-roles. However, the primary work product is a physically machined metal part, produced through hands-on machine setup, tooling changes, and real-time physical problem-solving. The 2026 robotics frontier cannot yet replicate the dexterity and adaptive judgment required across the breadth of this occupation. The programming-language-heavy optional skills list is anomalous and likely reflects ESCO taxonomy artifacts rather than typical worker profiles, so it does not elevate the score. Confidence is medium because the CNC-heavy sub-occupations (computer numerical control machine operator) may warrant a slightly higher individual score, while scrap metal operatives and heat treatment furnace operators sit lower.

title: Metallpolierer, Rundschleifer und Werkzeugschärfer
rationale: AI cannot grind, polish, or sharpen physical metal objects — this work requires hands-on manipulation of real materials with specialized machines. The job is almost entirely physical and tactile, keeping AI exposure very low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A limited subset of tasks has some AI relevance. Quality inspection ('inspect quality of products,' 'spot metal imperfections,' 'operate precision measuring equipment') can be partially assisted by computer vision systems, which are increasingly deployed in manufacturing for surface defect detection. Machine monitoring ('monitor moving workpiece in a machine,' 'set up the controller of a machine') is also an area where sensor-based automation and AI-assisted process control are advancing. Consulting technical resources and keeping records of work progress are minor administrative tasks that AI tools can support. These peripheral tasks represent perhaps 10–15% of the role's actual work content.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of core tasks are irreducibly physical: applying polishing lubricants, executing buffing motions, manipulating metal workpieces, mounting and dressing grinding wheels, sharpening tools, and adjusting saw blades. These require dexterous manipulation in unstructured or semi-structured environments — exactly where 2026 robotics remain far below human capability for precision craft work. The tactile feedback required to 'feel' workpiece surfaces and judge finish quality, combined with the variety of workpiece geometries and materials ('types of metal,' 'assess suitability of metal types'), makes full automation impractical. There are no regulatory barriers per se, but the physical nature of the work is itself the barrier. The apprenticeship-level skill set reflects embodied craft knowledge that is not digitally transferable.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low (2) tier. The primary work product is a physically transformed object — a polished surface, a sharpened edge — not a digital artifact. AI and robotics can assist at the margins (vision-based inspection, CNC machine control), but the hands-on grinding, polishing, and sharpening tasks that define the role remain human-dependent. Adoption of robotic polishing exists in high-volume automotive manufacturing but has not displaced skilled craft polishers and tool grinders working on varied, precision, or small-batch work, which characterizes this Swiss workforce segment.

title: Maschinenmechaniker und -schlosser, onA
rationale: AI can help with diagnostics, maintenance scheduling, and looking up repair manuals, but the core work—physically dismantling, repairing, and reassembling machinery—requires hands-on skill in unstructured environments. Physical manipulation and on-site problem-solving keep AI substitution low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** Machine mechanics and fitters (ISCO 7230) perform tasks such as diagnosing mechanical faults, interpreting technical drawings, ordering spare parts, and documenting maintenance work. These information-adjacent subtasks are increasingly AI-assisted: fault diagnosis can be supported by AI-driven predictive maintenance systems, technical documentation can be generated or retrieved via AI tools, and scheduling/parts ordering can be handled by agentic workflows. Computer vision systems are also beginning to assist with defect detection in structured industrial environments. These peripheral tasks may represent 20–30% of total work time.

**Limiting factors:** The dominant work product is physical: dismantling, repairing, adjusting, and reassembling mechanical equipment—often in situ, in varied and unstructured environments. 2026 robotics cannot reliably perform delicate or varied mechanical manipulation outside highly controlled factory settings. The job requires tactile feedback, spatial reasoning in novel configurations, and improvised problem-solving when machinery deviates from spec. There are no regulatory barriers per se, but the physical nature of the work and the diversity of machinery encountered (from industrial presses to HVAC systems) create high variance that current robotic systems cannot handle. Client-facing troubleshooting and safety accountability also retain a human dimension.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). AI tools provide genuine but peripheral assistance—diagnostics, documentation, scheduling—while the core physical repair and assembly work remains beyond 2026 AI/robotics capabilities in real-world settings. The score reflects modest productivity amplification rather than meaningful substitution. Uncertainty is moderate because the boundary between AI-assisted diagnostics and full automation of simpler, repetitive maintenance tasks (e.g., on standardized equipment) is shifting.

title: Kraftfahrzeugmechaniker und -schlosser
rationale: AI can assist with fault diagnosis via diagnostic software, but the core work — physically dismantling engines, replacing parts, and reassembling vehicles — requires hands-on manipulation in unstructured environments. The job is overwhelmingly physical, keeping AI exposure low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The most AI-amenable tasks in this occupation are diagnostic and informational. Skills like 'diagnose defective engines,' 'use automotive diagnostic equipment,' 'interpret technical requirements,' 'read standard blueprints,' and 'provide technical documentation' are already partially supported by AI-enhanced OBD (on-board diagnostics) systems and manufacturer diagnostic software. AI can increasingly interpret fault codes, cross-reference repair histories, suggest likely failure modes, and generate repair estimates. Administrative tasks such as 'maintain vehicle records,' 'maintain records of financial transactions,' 'order supplies,' and 'provide customer information related to repairs' are also highly automatable with current tools. These represent perhaps 15–25% of total task time.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of tasks in this occupation require physical manipulation in unstructured, grease-covered, spatially variable environments: 'disassemble engines,' 'bolt engine parts,' 're-assemble engines,' 'replace tyres,' 'perform metal work,' 'use welding equipment,' 'carry out chassis modifications,' 'install transport vehicle interior components,' and dozens more. Current robotics cannot perform these tasks at commercial scale — automotive repair bays are not structured factory floors, and each vehicle presents unique physical configurations, corrosion states, and access constraints. Skills like 'carry out improvised vehicle repairs' and 'perform road testing' explicitly require adaptive human judgment in physical contexts. Regulatory compliance (emission standards, warranty contracts) and customer-facing trust ('guarantee customer satisfaction,' 'communicate with customers') add further friction to automation. The 112 essential skills are dominated by physical manipulation verbs: fit, bolt, disassemble, weld, clamp, replace, install.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). AI provides meaningful assistance in the diagnostic and administrative fringe of the job, and this assistance will grow — but the core value delivered is physical transformation of mechanical systems in variable real-world environments, which 2026 robotics cannot replicate at scale. The score of 3 (rather than 2) reflects that AI-assisted diagnostics are already commercially deployed and genuinely reduce the cognitive search time for fault identification, providing modest but real productivity amplification. Employment displacement risk remains low; the more likely outcome is that each technician handles slightly more vehicles per day.

title: Flugmotorenmechaniker und -schlosser
rationale: AI can assist with reading manuals, logging maintenance records, and diagnosing faults via sensor data, but the core work—physically disassembling, inspecting, repairing, and certifying aircraft engines—requires hands-on manipulation in complex, safety-critical environments. Strict aviation regulatory certification requirements and irreplaceable physical dexterity keep AI substitution very low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A subset of tasks in this occupation are meaningfully AI-assisted. Maintenance documentation (maintaining repair logs, using computerised maintenance management systems) is already heavily digitised and AI can automate scheduling, anomaly flagging, and report generation. Fault diagnosis—'diagnose defective engines,' 'evaluate engine performance,' 'troubleshoot'—can be partially supported by AI-driven predictive maintenance systems that analyse sensor telemetry and historical data. Reading and interpreting technical manuals, service bulletins, and engineering drawings is a task where large-context LLMs now perform well. The 'aircraft maintenance coordinator' sub-occupation has the highest exposure, as planning, scheduling, and communication tasks are substantially information-work. These digital-adjacent tasks represent perhaps 20–25% of total work content.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of core tasks are irreducibly physical: disassembling and re-assembling engines, bolting and adjusting engine parts, aligning components, operating soldering equipment, using power tools, recognising corrosion by touch and sight, installing hydraulic and pneumatic systems, and physically certifying airworthiness. Aviation robotics in 2026 cannot perform delicate, unstructured manipulation in confined engine bays or on airframes with the reliability required for safety-critical certification. Critically, aviation maintenance is governed by extremely strict regulatory frameworks (EASA Part-66, FAA A&P licensing, Swiss BAZL requirements): a licensed human engineer must physically sign off on every maintenance action. This regulatory accountability requirement is a structural barrier to AI substitution that will not dissolve within the scoring horizon. Physical presence, tactile judgment, and legal accountability are non-negotiable.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). AI provides genuine but peripheral productivity gains in documentation, scheduling, and diagnostic support—pushing it above the minimal tier. However, the primary work product is a physically safe, certified aircraft, not a digital artifact. The 'auxiliary tool rule' applies: if the computer broke, 90%+ of this job would still need to happen in person. Regulatory certification requirements create an additional hard floor on substitution. The score of 3 reflects real but bounded AI assistance, with no credible path to workforce reduction through AI in the near term.

title: Landmaschinen- und Industriemaschinenmechaniker und -schlosser
rationale: AI can assist with diagnostics, maintenance records, and reading technical drawings, but the core work — physically disassembling engines, replacing worn parts, and repairing heavy machinery on-site — requires hands-on manipulation in unstructured environments. The job is anchored in physical transformation of mechanical systems, keeping AI exposure low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A meaningful subset of tasks in this occupation involves information processing that AI can assist with. Consulting technical documentation, reading engineering drawings and blueprints, recording maintenance interventions, interpreting fault codes, and troubleshooting engine malfunctions all have commercially available AI support tools (e.g., AI-assisted diagnostic systems, computer vision for defect detection, NLP-based technical manual search). Condition monitoring via sensors and predictive maintenance AI is already deployed in industrial settings, partially automating the 'detect malfunctions in engines' and 'evaluate engine performance' tasks. Record-keeping tasks such as 'write records for repairs' and 'maintain records of maintenance interventions' are highly automatable. Regulatory and safety requirements (health and safety standards, vessel compliance, mining regulations) further demand accountable human presence. The breadth of sub-occupations — from marine mechanics to forge equipment technicians — means the physical context is highly variable, compounding robotic deployment difficulty. The 'auxiliary tool rule' applies: digital skills like record-keeping and reading drawings are support activities; if the computer broke, 90%+ of the job still requires a person on-site with tools.

title: Fahrradmechaniker und verwandte Berufe
rationale: AI cannot tighten brake cables, true wheels, or replace worn drivetrain components — the core work is hands-on mechanical repair of physical objects. A few peripheral tasks like scheduling or invoicing have AI tools, but these are minor compared to the physical craft at the heart of the job.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A small subset of tasks in this occupation have meaningful AI exposure. Administrative optional skills — administer appointments, issue sales invoices, keep stock records, maintain professional administration, and manage a small-to-medium business — are information-processing tasks that AI scheduling assistants, invoicing software, and inventory tools can largely automate today. Repair manuals (use repair manuals) are increasingly AI-searchable, and diagnostic guidance for common faults can be AI-assisted. Selling bicycles involves product knowledge that AI chatbots can partially replicate in an e-commerce context. These peripheral tasks represent perhaps 15–20% of total work time.

**Limiting factors:** The primary work product is the physical repair and maintenance of bicycles and related equipment — assembling bicycles, maintaining braking systems, replacing defective components, tuning bicycles, changing tyres, lubricating bearings, and spray-painting frames. Every one of these core tasks requires dexterous physical manipulation in an unstructured workshop environment. 2026 robotics cannot perform fine mechanical adjustments (cable tension, derailleur indexing, wheel truing) at acceptable quality or cost in a small-shop setting. The work involves tactile feedback — feeling bearing play, hearing brake rub, sensing chain stretch — that current AI systems cannot replicate. Customer service and diagnosing intermittent faults also require in-person interaction and experiential judgment. Regulatory or safety barriers are minimal, but the physical nature of the work is an absolute constraint.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low (2) tier. The primary work product is physical transformation of mechanical objects, not digital artifacts. AI tools can assist with the thin administrative layer (scheduling, invoicing, parts lookup) but cannot substitute for the hands-on mechanical core. The occupation is structurally similar to an electrician or cook — some digital periphery, but the job would still need to happen in person even if every computer broke. No near-term AI capability shift is likely to change this materially.

title: Präzisionshandwerker und kunsthandwerkliche Berufe, onA
rationale: AI can help with design planning and pattern generation, but the core work—shaping, carving, and assembling physical materials by hand—requires dexterous manipulation that robots cannot yet reliably perform. The mix of highly varied craft specialties makes a single score uncertain.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The ISCO 7310 'not elsewhere classified' category covers precision workers and craft artisans whose work involves some design and planning tasks that AI can assist with. Pattern generation, design visualization, and material estimation are increasingly AI-augmentable. If workers use CAD or digital design tools (plausible for some sub-occupations like instrument makers or model builders), AI coding and generative design tools could automate a meaningful share of the planning phase. Administrative tasks such as quoting, invoicing, and client communication are also AI-assistable.

**Limiting factors:** The defining characteristic of precision and craft work is skilled physical manipulation—carving, filing, assembling, finishing—in unstructured or semi-structured environments with variable materials. As of 2026, robotic systems achieve roughly 70% human efficiency only in highly structured warehouse pick-and-pack tasks; fine dexterous manipulation of irregular objects (wood, metal, leather, ceramics) remains firmly human-advantaged. Many craft occupations also carry strong authenticity and provenance value: clients pay specifically for human-made goods, creating a trust and market barrier to automation. Regulatory or guild standards in Switzerland may further slow adoption.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the moderate tier (4). AI can meaningfully assist the design, planning, and administrative periphery of craft work, but the core physical output—handmade precision artifacts—is not substitutable by 2026 AI or robotics. Confidence is low because ISCO 7310 is a residual 'not elsewhere classified' category aggregating highly heterogeneous sub-occupations (e.g., musical instrument makers, taxidermists, model makers, engravers), some of which may be more digitally exposed than others.

title: Uhrmacher, Uhrenarbeiter, Mikromechaniker
rationale: AI cannot yet replace the fine manual dexterity required to disassemble, repair, and reassemble mechanical watches or precision instruments — these are intricate physical tasks in unstructured micro-environments. The job is primarily hands-on craftsmanship with physical materials, keeping AI exposure low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A subset of tasks in this occupation involve digital or semi-automatable elements. Testing and calibrating instruments using electronic meters, operating automated optical inspection machines, reading engineering drawings, and diagnosing defects via standardized test equipment are areas where AI-assisted tools (computer vision for defect detection, automated calibration rigs, AI-guided diagnostic software) are increasingly capable. Battery changes and strap replacements are routine and low-skill. Customer-facing tasks — advising customers on watches, providing follow-up services, administering appointments — could be partially handled by AI chatbots or scheduling tools. Optional skills like 'automation technology' and 'calibrate electronic instruments' suggest some workers already operate in digitally augmented environments.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of core tasks require precise, delicate physical manipulation in micro-scale environments: attaching clock hands and dials, mounting wheelwork, demagnetising watches, grinding and joining lenses, cold and hot forging, tending lathes and boring machines, and using watchmaker's tools. These demand fine motor control, tactile feedback, and spatial reasoning in highly unstructured micro-physical settings — well beyond 2026 robotic capabilities, which remain limited to structured pick-and-place tasks at macro scale. Surgical instrument making and optical instrument repair add further complexity: safety-critical tolerances and regulatory conformity requirements create strong adoption barriers for automation. The craft knowledge embedded in mechanical watchmaking (Swiss tradition, heritage repair) also carries significant trust and provenance value that clients specifically seek from human artisans.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). Perhaps 15–25% of peripheral tasks (diagnostics, customer communication, inspection logging) have meaningful AI tool support, but the primary work product — a physically repaired, calibrated, and assembled precision instrument — requires irreplaceable manual dexterity and tactile judgment. Robotics capable of watchmaker-level manipulation are not commercially deployed at scale in 2026. The main uncertainty is whether automated optical inspection and AI-guided calibration will gradually erode the diagnostic and quality-control subtasks, but this would not fundamentally displace the assembly and repair core.

title: Musikinstrumentenbauer und -stimmer
rationale: AI cannot carve violin bodies, bend brass tubing, voice organ pipes, or tune instruments by ear and hand. The work is almost entirely physical craftsmanship and sensory judgment, leaving very little for AI to automate.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A small subset of tasks touches AI-accessible territory. Design work (3D modelling, develop design plans, translate artistic concepts to technical designs) could be partially assisted by CAD/AI tools. Documentation tasks (document own practice, use technical documentation, draw up instrument setup) are straightforward for AI. Estimating restoration costs and instrument value involves pattern-matching over historical data where AI tools could provide reference points. These peripheral tasks represent perhaps 10–15% of actual working time.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of the 46 essential skills describe irreducibly physical, tactile, and auditory work: woodturning, metalworking, assembling and creating instrument parts from wood, metal, leather, and organic materials, tuning by ear (comparing reed pitch aurally, adjusting organ pipe lips with hand tools), repairing and restoring physical components, and working safely with machines and chemicals. Tuning a piano or voicing organ pipes requires fine motor manipulation and real-time acoustic feedback in a physical environment — tasks that 2026 robotics cannot perform at acceptable quality. The sensory judgment involved (playing and inspecting instruments to evaluate sound quality, locating defects by ear) is a form of expert embodied cognition that AI cannot replicate in the field. Regulatory and safety requirements around chemical handling and electrical systems further anchor the work in supervised, in-person execution.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low (2) tier. It is artisanal physical craftsmanship with a strong sensory and tactile core. AI can assist marginally with design sketching or cost estimation, but the primary work product — a functioning, well-tuned, beautifully crafted instrument — requires hands, ears, and years of embodied skill. There is no plausible near-term pathway for AI to substitute meaningfully for the core tasks, and even productivity amplification is minimal given the bespoke, one-at-a-time nature of the work.

title: Schmuckwarenhersteller und Edelmetallbearbeiter
rationale: AI can assist with jewellery design software and gemstone valuation databases, but the core work—cutting and polishing stones, casting metal, setting gems, and repairing pieces by hand—requires precise physical manipulation that robots cannot yet reliably perform. The job is fundamentally craft-based, keeping AI exposure low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A subset of tasks in this occupation have meaningful AI touchpoints. Jewellery design (task b, skills 'develop jewellery designs', 'design objects to be crafted') can be augmented by generative CAD tools and AI-assisted 3D modelling software, which are already commercially available and used in the industry. Gemstone appraisal and valuation ('calculate value of gems', 'appraise gemstones', 'examine gems', 'characteristics of diamonds') can be partially supported by AI-powered spectroscopic analysis and pricing databases. Administrative tasks such as 'record jewel processing time', 'record jewel weight', 'estimate cost of jewellery and watches maintenance', and 'conduct jewellery market research' are straightforwardly automatable with existing tools. Customer advisory tasks ('advise customers on jewellery and watches') could be partially handled by AI chatbots for routine queries.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of core tasks are physical craft operations that 2026 robotics cannot perform at acceptable quality in unstructured, high-precision, high-value contexts. Cutting and polishing gemstones, casting metal by hand, assembling and mounting stones, engraving, adjusting and repairing existing pieces, and grinding jewel bearings for precision instruments all require fine motor dexterity, tactile feedback, and adaptive judgment about unique, irreplaceable materials. Each gemstone and piece of jewellery is physically distinct; there is no standardised environment for robotic manipulation. The stakes are high—errors destroy valuable and often sentimental items—creating strong trust and liability barriers to automation. Repair and restoration work ('repair jewellery', 'apply restoration techniques', 'evaluate restoration procedures') is especially resistant, as it demands diagnosis of and response to unpredictable physical conditions. Regulatory and insurance contexts ('handle jewellery and watches insurance claims') also require human accountability.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). Perhaps 15–20% of task time—design assistance, valuation support, record-keeping—can be meaningfully augmented or partially automated by 2026 AI tools, but this translates to productivity amplification rather than workforce substitution. The primary work product is a physical object produced through skilled hand-craft, and the physical manipulation barrier is decisive. The score of 3 (upper end of Low) rather than 2 reflects the genuine design and appraisal components that AI tools are beginning to reshape, without overstating the impact on the craft core.

title: Keramiker und verwandte Berufe
rationale: AI cannot throw clay on a wheel, operate a kiln, apply glaze by hand, or inspect ceramic surfaces through touch and sight in an unstructured workshop environment. The work is almost entirely physical craft, keeping AI exposure very low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A small subset of tasks has some AI relevance. Reading technical drawings and verifying shapes against specifications could be assisted by computer vision tools. Quality inspection ('inspect quality of products', 'examine finished ware for defects') is an area where industrial vision systems are increasingly deployed in structured factory settings. Record-keeping ('keep records of work progress', 'record production data for quality control') and consulting technical resources are minor administrative tasks that AI can assist with. Designing objects to be crafted could be partially supported by generative 3D design tools. However, these tasks represent a small fraction of the total work.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of essential skills are irreducibly physical and tactile: shaping clay on a wheel, adjusting wheel speed by feel, applying glaze coatings, managing kiln firing techniques, smoothing surfaces with hands and sponges, filling and extracting products from moulds, and polishing clay products. Robotics capable of replicating the fine motor dexterity required for hand-throwing pottery or hand brick moulding in unstructured workshop environments does not exist at commercial scale in 2026. The sensory feedback loop — adjusting pressure and speed based on how clay feels as walls thin — is a paradigmatic example of embodied skill that current AI-robotic systems cannot replicate. Kiln operation requires continuous monitoring and adjustment based on material behavior. The artisanal and craft nature of much of this work (especially production potters and hand brick moulders) further limits standardization that would enable automation.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low (2) tier. The primary work product is a physical ceramic object produced through skilled manual transformation of raw materials, not a digital artifact. AI tools offer marginal assistance in design ideation, quality documentation, and possibly vision-based defect detection in highly industrialized settings, but these are peripheral to the core craft. The physical, tactile, and sensory demands of the role constitute a strong and durable barrier to AI substitution through 2026 and well beyond.

title: Glasmacher, -schneider, -schleifer und -veredler
rationale: AI cannot blow, grind, cut, or shape physical glass — the core work is manual transformation of a material in the real world. Some peripheral tasks like reading blueprints or monitoring automated machines have AI assistance, but the hands-on craft keeps exposure low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A subset of tasks in this occupation involve digital or semi-digital work that AI can assist with. Reading work orders and blueprints, monitoring gauges and computer printouts, and setting up CNC/robotic cutting equipment controllers are all tasks where AI-assisted interfaces and computer vision can reduce cognitive load or partially automate decision-making. Quality inspection — examining glass for defects like chips, scratches, or unacceptable shapes — is an area where computer vision systems are increasingly capable in structured industrial settings. The optional skill 'inspect quality of products' and the core task of examining finished glass stock are plausible targets for automated vision inspection. Design tasks such as 'design stained glass' and 'create artwork' could be partially assisted by generative AI tools for pattern generation or layout, though execution remains manual.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of core tasks require direct physical manipulation of glass: blowing, bending, grinding, polishing, cutting, spraying, and heat-forming. These involve fine motor control, real-time tactile and visual feedback, and operation in semi-structured or artisanal environments — all areas where robotics in 2026 remain far below human capability for delicate, variable materials. Glass is fragile, thermally sensitive, and highly variable in form; robotic handling at the precision required for bevelling, blowing, or stained glass work is not commercially deployed at scale. The artistic and craft sub-occupations (glass-blower, stained glass designer) add further resistance: 'maintain an artistic portfolio,' 'study artistic techniques,' and 'monitor art scene developments' reflect a creative-craft identity that clients and markets value as human-made. Regulatory and safety considerations around handling broken or molten glass also slow automation adoption.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). The primary work product is a physically transformed glass object, not a digital artifact. AI and robotics can assist at the margins — quality inspection, blueprint reading, machine monitoring — but cannot replicate the core manual and artisanal skills. The score is slightly above the floor (2) because CNC glass cutting and automated inspection are real and growing, and design-assist tools are increasingly used even in craft contexts. Uncertainty arises from the heterogeneity of sub-occupations: industrial glass cutters operating CNC lines face modestly higher automation pressure than artisanal glass-blowers, but neither approaches the moderate tier.

title: Schildermaler, Dekormaler, Graveure und Ätzer
rationale: AI can generate design concepts, automate vinyl sign cutting, and assist with lettering layouts, but the core work — hand-engraving glass, freehand decorative painting, and physical manipulation of materials — requires skilled manual craft. The mix of digital design tasks and irreplaceable physical execution keeps exposure moderate.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A meaningful subset of tasks in this occupation are AI-addressable. Sign-making tasks (tasks d, g, h, i) involving layout, lettering design, and vinyl cutting are already heavily supported by software like Adobe Illustrator, CorelDRAW, and CNC routing equipment — AI can now generate sign layouts, suggest typography, and drive routing machines with minimal human input. The 'graphic design' and 'develop design concept/ideas' essential skills are squarely in territory where generative AI tools (Midjourney, Adobe Firefly) can produce production-ready concepts. 'Conduct research on trends in design,' 'gather reference materials,' and 'present detailed design proposals' are largely information-processing tasks AI handles well. For sign makers specifically, the digital-to-physical pipeline is increasingly automated, and AI-assisted CNC engraving (task g, m) reduces the skill threshold for producing engraved lettering on flat surfaces.

**Limiting factors:** The core physical craft skills — hand engraving glass and metal, freehand decorative painting on pottery and curved surfaces, manipulating glass, using glass engraving tools, and applying paint techniques to irregular 3D objects — remain firmly human-dependent. Robotic systems cannot yet replicate the fine motor control, tactile feedback, and adaptive judgment required for freehand ornamental engraving or decorative painting on non-uniform surfaces. Tasks like 'manipulate glass,' 'use glass engraving tools,' 'paint surfaces' with artistic intent, and 'handle etching chemicals' involve physical dexterity and real-time material response that 2026 robotics cannot match at acceptable quality or cost. Additionally, much of this work is bespoke, low-volume, and client-specific — the transaction cost of AI integration is high relative to job size. Regulatory and safety considerations around chemical etching and precision metalworking add further friction.

**Net assessment:** This occupation straddles two worlds: a digitally-assisted design and sign-making component (moderate-to-high AI exposure) and a physical craft component (low AI exposure). The ISCO group bundles sign makers — whose work is increasingly digital — with glass engravers and decorative painters whose core output is irreducibly physical. Averaging across sub-occupations and weighting by task centrality, a score of 4 (moderate) is appropriate. The primary uncertainty is whether sign makers within this group are shifting rapidly toward full digital workflows, which could push that sub-occupation to 6-7, while glass engravers and decorative painters remain at 2-3.

title: Kunsthandwerkliche Berufe für Holz, Korbwaren und verwandte Materialien
rationale: AI cannot carve wood, weave baskets, or shape wicker — the entire job is manual transformation of physical materials using hand tools and traditional craft techniques. The work is almost entirely physical and tactile, leaving almost no foothold for AI substitution.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A small number of peripheral tasks carry some AI exposure. Design work — 'design objects to be crafted' and 'follow a brief' — could be partially assisted by AI image generation or CAD tools (listed as optional skills). Estimating restoration costs involves structured information processing that AI could support. Customer-facing tasks like 'identify customer's needs' and 'provide customer follow-up services' could be partially assisted by AI communication tools. These peripheral tasks represent perhaps 10–15% of actual working time.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of essential skills describe irreducibly physical, manual operations: carving materials with knives, gouges and chisels; performing basket weaving by interlacing fibres; manipulating wicker material; joining wood elements; sanding and finishing surfaces; operating hand tools and small machinery. 2026 robotics cannot replicate the fine motor dexterity, tactile feedback, and adaptive problem-solving required for unstructured craft work on irregular natural materials like rattan, willow, reeds, and raw wood. The 'primary work product test' is decisive: the output is a physical handmade object, not a digital artifact. Even if a robot could theoretically weave, the artisanal and handicraft nature of this occupation — where human skill and provenance are core to the product's value — creates strong market-level resistance to automation.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low (2) tier. The work is almost entirely physical manipulation of natural materials in unstructured craft contexts, which is precisely where 2026 AI and robotics have the least capability. The small design and customer-service components provide marginal AI complementarity but no meaningful substitution pressure. The Swiss market for these 304 workers is likely niche and artisanal, further insulating it from automation economics.

title: Kunsthandwerkliche Berufe für Textilien, Leder und verwandte Materialien
rationale: AI cannot spin yarn, weave cloth on a hand loom, knot carpets, or tan and cut leather by hand — these are skilled physical crafts requiring tactile judgment and manual dexterity. The work is almost entirely physical transformation of materials, leaving very little for AI to automate.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A small subset of tasks involves digital or cognitive work that AI could assist with: creating textile patterns and designs (AI image generation tools can generate motifs and colorways), evaluating textile characteristics and quality (computer vision can detect defects), and placing orders for materials (routine procurement). The 'produce textile designs' and 'create patterns for textile products' skills are the most AI-exposed elements, as generative design tools can now produce professional-quality pattern proposals. However, these are peripheral to the core physical craft.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of tasks in this occupation are irreducibly physical: spinning and winding yarn by hand, drawing warp threads into looms, weaving on hand looms, knotting carpets, crocheting, making nets, preparing and dyeing hides, and cutting leather manually. 2026 robotics cannot replicate the fine motor dexterity, tactile feedback, and adaptive physical judgment required for these handicraft techniques — particularly in unstructured, artisanal settings where each piece may differ. The 'handicraft' designation is itself a market signal: buyers pay a premium precisely because human hands made the object, creating a trust and authenticity barrier to AI substitution. Regulatory and cultural preservation contexts (e.g., traditional Swiss textile crafts) further insulate the occupation.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 2). The primary work product is a physical artifact produced through manual transformation of raw materials — wool, leather, yarn, hide — using hand tools and traditional techniques. AI can marginally assist with pattern design and quality inspection, but cannot perform the core craft. The score is 2 rather than 1 because digital design assistance is genuinely available and some quality-control vision tools are commercially deployed in textile manufacturing, providing modest productivity amplification without threatening the craft's human core.

title: Kunsthandwerkliche Berufe, anderweitig nicht genannt
rationale: AI cannot melt wax, shape candles by hand, repair tennis rackets, or carve materials — the core work is physical transformation of real-world objects. A few peripheral tasks like record-keeping or customer follow-up have some AI assistance potential, but the job remains firmly hands-on.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A small subset of tasks in this occupation involves information processing that AI can assist with. Maintaining records of maintenance interventions, keeping stock records, issuing sales invoices, and managing customer interaction logs are all administrative tasks where AI-assisted tools (voice-to-text, automated invoicing, CRM systems) can reduce manual effort. Advising customers on equipment type or candle products could be partially supported by AI chatbots or recommendation engines. Following trends in sporting equipment is a research task where AI summarization tools add value. These peripheral tasks represent perhaps 10–15% of the total work content.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of tasks in this occupation are physical and craft-based. Melting wax, inserting wicks, filling and extracting moulds, dyeing and shaping candles, drip-forming, and tending bleaching machinery all require direct physical manipulation in unstructured or semi-structured environments. Sports equipment repair — restringing rackets, replacing components, performing test runs — similarly demands dexterous hands-on work with varied, worn, or damaged objects. 2026 robotics cannot reliably perform these delicate, variable, low-volume craft tasks at acceptable quality or cost. There are no regulatory barriers per se, but the artisanal and bespoke nature of the work (small batches, custom orders, physical inspection) makes automation economically unviable at this scale. The Swiss employment figure of 55 workers further signals a niche, craft-oriented segment where industrial automation investment is implausible.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low (2) tier. The primary work product is a physical object — a candle or repaired piece of sports equipment — produced through manual craft techniques. AI and robotics in 2026 offer marginal assistance on administrative peripherals but cannot substitute for the core physical work. The score of 2 (rather than 1) reflects the genuine, if modest, AI-assistable administrative layer around customer service, record-keeping, and supplier management.

title: Techniker in der Druckvorstufe
rationale: AI tools can now automate colour correction, image retouching, layout composition, and file preparation — the core digital tasks of this job. However, operating physical plate-making equipment, calibrating presses, and hands-on quality inspection keep the score from reaching the highest tier.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The majority of core tasks in pre-press are digital information-processing operations performed on computers. Skills like colour separation, colour correction, retouching, layout composition (lay out digital written content), typography, scanning, and producing prepress proofs are all tasks where 2026 AI tools — including Adobe Firefly, AI-assisted Photoshop features, and automated imposition software — now perform at commercially acceptable quality. Adobe Photoshop and Illustrator (both listed as essential) have deeply integrated generative AI and automated correction pipelines. Colour profile setting and scanner control optimisation are increasingly handled by automated calibration software. The optional skill 'edit photographs' is now largely automatable, and 'graphic design' assistance tools are mature. File preparation and output (output electronic files, create digital files) are highly automatable through agentic workflows. Roughly 60–70% of the listed essential skills describe tasks that AI can now perform or substantially accelerate.

**Limiting factors:** Several tasks anchor this occupation in the physical world and resist full AI substitution. Operating laser plate-maker machines, preparing printing forms, plate mounting, maintaining printing machinery, and troubleshooting press hardware require physical presence and hands-on manipulation in an industrial environment. Chemical proofing processes and exposing carbon tissue for cylinder transfer are physical-chemical operations with no current AI substitute. Quality evaluation of physical printed proofs — checking for registration errors, ink density, and substrate interaction — still benefits from trained human sensory judgment, though computer vision is beginning to encroach. Regulatory and production-quality accountability in commercial print runs also creates adoption friction. These physical and equipment-operation tasks represent perhaps 30–40% of the role.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the High tier (score 7). The digital half of the work — layout, colour management, retouching, file output, scanning — is substantially AI-exposed today, and productivity amplification means fewer technicians are needed per job. The physical half — press operation, plate making, hardware maintenance — provides a meaningful floor of human necessity. The score does not reach 8–9 because the occupation is not purely information work; physical equipment operation and quality control of tangible outputs remain central. Uncertainty stems from the heterogeneity of workplaces: a purely digital pre-press studio is more exposed than one integrated with physical press operations.

title: Drucker
rationale: AI can optimize print job settings and quality monitoring via computer vision, but the core work—physically setting up presses, mixing inks, loading substrates, and maintaining machinery—requires hands-on operation in an industrial environment. The job is primarily physical machine operation, keeping AI exposure low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** Several tasks within this occupation have meaningful AI touchpoints. Quality monitoring and defect detection (tasks c, g) are addressable by computer vision systems already deployed in industrial printing environments, capable of detecting color deviations, registration errors, and substrate defects faster than human inspection. Digital printing workflows (task i—producing and transferring digital print images) involve file preparation, color profile setting, and RIP software management, which AI tools can increasingly assist with or automate. Skills like 'set colour profiles,' 'use colour printing programs,' 'produce prepress proof,' and 'digitise documents' are partially automatable through AI-assisted prepress software. Agentic scheduling tools can also assist with 'follow production schedule' and job queue management.

**Limiting factors:** The dominant share of this occupation's core tasks are physical and machine-centric: loading paper, adjusting feed mechanisms, mixing inks and solvents, preparing plates and cylinders, maintaining and repairing presses, and operating flexographic, gravure, offset, and screen printing machinery. These require tactile judgment, physical dexterity, and real-time response to mechanical conditions in an unstructured industrial environment—precisely where 2026 robotics remain limited (~70% human efficiency in structured warehouse settings, far less in complex machine operation). Ink mixing, troubleshooting mechanical malfunctions, replacing dies, and cleaning rollers are not digitally substitutable. Regulatory and safety requirements (chemical handling, press safety) further slow automation adoption. The breadth of press types (flexo, gravure, offset, screen, hot foil, embossing) means no single automation solution covers the role.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). While AI-assisted quality inspection and digital prepress workflows provide genuine productivity uplift for a subset of tasks, the primary work product is a physical printed artifact produced through hands-on machine operation. The 'auxiliary tool rule' applies: digital skills like color profiling and document digitization are peripheral to the core physical craft. The occupation is analogous to a skilled machine operator in manufacturing—AI augments monitoring and scheduling but cannot yet substitute for the physical setup, adjustment, and maintenance work that defines the role. Key uncertainty is the pace of computer vision adoption for inline quality control, which could reduce headcount in high-volume digital print operations specifically.

title: Berufe in der Druckweiterverarbeitung und Buchbinder
rationale: AI cannot fold paper, bind books, operate guillotines, or restore damaged volumes — these are physical machine-operation and craft tasks. Some scheduling and quality-inspection tasks have AI support, but the core work is hands-on and machine-dependent, keeping exposure low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A modest share of peripheral tasks have AI relevance. Reading job tickets and following production schedules could be partially automated through workflow management software. Quality inspection of binding work is an area where computer vision systems are emerging, capable of detecting misaligned folds, skewed cuts, or incomplete stitching in structured production environments. The 'use ICT resources to solve work-related tasks' skill and machine controller programming (setting up electronically operated units) involve digital interfaces where AI-assisted parameter optimization is plausible. For the book restorer sub-occupation, AI tools can assist with condition assessment documentation, museum database queries, and estimating restoration costs — perhaps 20–25% of that sub-role's cognitive overhead.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of tasks in this ISCO group are physical machine operation and manual craft work: folding, collating, sewing signatures, operating paper guillotines, adjusting fold plates, lifting stacks of paper, supplying machines, and embossing designs. These require dexterous manipulation in semi-structured industrial environments where robotics in 2026 remain at roughly 70% human efficiency for pick-and-pack tasks and are far less capable for the varied material handling, machine setup, and tactile quality checks involved here. Book restoration is a particularly AI-resistant sub-occupation: it demands hands-on application of restoration techniques, aesthetic and scientific judgment about historic artifacts, and physical treatment of fragile materials — tasks that are firmly in the human domain. Regulatory and institutional trust barriers (museum standards, conservation ethics) further slow any AI encroachment on the restorer role. Physical safety requirements around guillotines and industrial machinery also demand human presence and judgment.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits solidly in the Low tier (score 3). The primary work product is a physical object — a bound book, a folded printed sheet, a restored volume — produced through machine operation and manual craft. AI can assist at the margins (scheduling, some inspection, documentation), but cannot substitute for the physical transformation tasks that constitute the core of the job. The heterogeneity between sub-occupations (industrial bindery operator vs. book restorer) introduces some uncertainty, but both sub-roles are anchored in physical, hands-on work that resists 2026 AI substitution.

title: Elektriker und Elektroniker, onA
rationale: Electricians and electronics workers spend most of their time physically installing, wiring, testing, and repairing electrical systems in buildings and equipment — hands-on work AI cannot perform. Some planning, fault diagnosis, and documentation tasks are partially AI-assisted, but the physical core keeps exposure low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The ISCO 7400 catch-all group ('not elsewhere classified') for electricians and electronics workers includes tasks such as reading technical diagrams, fault diagnosis, and documenting work — all of which have meaningful AI support. Diagnostic reasoning (e.g., interpreting sensor readings, tracing faults in circuits) is increasingly assisted by AI-powered tools that can suggest likely failure points from symptom descriptions. Documentation, compliance checklists, and materials ordering are partially automatable. Computer-aided design tools for wiring schematics are well-established, and AI copilots can now generate or check circuit layouts. These information-processing subtasks may represent 20–30% of working time.

**Limiting factors:** The dominant work product is physical: running conduit, pulling cable, terminating connections, mounting panels, and testing live systems in unstructured real-world environments (residential, commercial, industrial sites). Robotic systems capable of this kind of dexterous, spatially variable electrical work do not exist at commercial scale in 2026. Safety regulations in Switzerland (NIV/AEFV) require licensed human electricians to sign off on installations, creating a hard regulatory floor. Customer interaction, site coordination, and on-the-spot problem-solving in novel physical environments further resist automation. The 'onA' (not elsewhere classified) designation suggests a heterogeneous group, but the physical installation and repair core is consistent across sub-types.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). AI provides genuine but peripheral assistance in diagnosis, documentation, and planning — perhaps lifting individual productivity modestly — but cannot substitute for the physical execution that constitutes the majority of the job. Regulatory requirements and the unstructured physical environment are strong adoption barriers. Confidence is low because ISCO 7400 is a residual category that may include some electronics technicians with higher information-work shares (e.g., bench repair, quality control), which could push individual sub-roles slightly higher.

title: Bauelektriker und verwandte Berufe
rationale: AI cannot pull wire through conduit, connect terminals, or install switchboards — the core work is physical manipulation of electrical systems in unstructured building environments. Some planning and documentation tasks are AI-assisted, but these are a small fraction of the job.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A subset of tasks involve structured information processing that AI can assist with: reading and interpreting electrical wiring plans, examining blueprints, planning installation layouts, and keeping records of work progress. AI tools (e.g., CAD-integrated assistants, document analysis) can accelerate blueprint interpretation and help generate material lists or quotations (the optional skill 'answer requests for quotation'). Energy performance calculations and compliance checks against standards are also increasingly AI-assisted. These tasks represent perhaps 10–20% of working time for a typical building electrician.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of core tasks are irreducibly physical: binding wire, cutting and connecting cable to terminals, splicing cable, stripping wire, positioning and installing switchboards, installing sockets and switches, cutting wall chases, and mounting lightning protection systems. These require dexterous manipulation in highly unstructured environments — inside walls, above ceilings, in cramped distribution boards — where 2026 robotics cannot operate reliably. Safety-critical inspection (identifying hazards, testing circuit continuity, resolving equipment malfunctions) requires on-site judgment and physical access. Regulatory and safety frameworks in Switzerland (NIV/AEFV) mandate licensed human electricians for installation and sign-off, creating a hard adoption barrier. The 'react to events in time-critical environments' skill reflects real-time physical problem-solving that AI agents cannot perform.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low (2) tier. The primary work product is a physically installed, tested, and certified electrical system — not a digital artifact. AI provides marginal productivity gains on planning and documentation subtasks, but cannot substitute for the physical installation, inspection, and fault-resolution work that constitutes the bulk of the role. Adoption barriers (regulation, physical environment, safety liability) further suppress displacement risk. The score of 2 reflects that some structured digital tasks exist but do not materially reduce headcount demand.

title: Automatiker und verwandte Berufe
rationale: AI can assist with fault diagnosis and reading wiring diagrams, but the core work—physically installing, repairing, and testing electrical machinery in vehicles, mines, lifts, and turbines—requires hands-on manipulation in unstructured environments. The job is anchored in physical trades work that 2026 robotics and AI cannot reliably replace.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A meaningful subset of tasks involves information processing that AI can support. Fault diagnosis and troubleshooting (e.g., 'perform vehicle diagnostic procedures,' 'use automotive diagnostic equipment,' 'resolve equipment malfunctions') are increasingly AI-assisted, with modern diagnostic tools already embedding ML-based fault prediction. Reading and interpreting electrical wiring diagrams and technical documentation is within 2026 AI vision/analysis capabilities. Maintaining records of maintenance interventions and producing reports (e.g., 'produce airport lighting system reports,' 'maintain records of mining operations') are partially automatable. AI-powered predictive maintenance platforms are being deployed in wind turbine and lift contexts, reducing the frequency of reactive repair visits. These digital-adjacent tasks represent perhaps 20–25% of the role.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of tasks require physical presence and dexterous manipulation in highly varied, often hazardous environments: splicing cables in mine shafts, installing hydraulic systems in vessels, guiding lift car installation in hoistways, repairing amusement park rides, and replacing defective components in automotive or rolling stock systems. The 155 essential skills are dominated by hands-on physical operations—assembling electromechanical systems, operating welding and soldering equipment, lifting heavy weights, working at heights, and using precision tools. Current robotics (even at ~70% human efficiency in structured warehouse settings) cannot operate reliably in the unstructured, confined, and safety-critical environments these workers inhabit. Regulatory compliance (electrical safety regulations, lift safety legislation, airport standards) also mandates certified human accountability. The breadth of sub-occupations—from mining electricians to ride technicians to marine electricians—means each context introduces novel physical problem-solving that AI agents cannot yet handle.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). AI provides genuine but peripheral value in diagnostics, documentation, and predictive maintenance scheduling. However, the primary work product is physical—a functioning electrical system installed, repaired, or maintained in the real world—and the environments are too varied and unstructured for 2026 robotics to substitute meaningfully. The score is 3 rather than 2 because diagnostic AI tools are already commercially deployed in automotive and wind turbine contexts, providing real productivity uplift, but the physical execution bottleneck keeps displacement risk low.

title: Elektroleitungsinstallateure und Wartungspersonal
rationale: AI cannot climb poles, splice high-voltage cables, or physically install and repair power lines. The job is almost entirely hands-on outdoor work in unstructured environments, keeping AI exposure very low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A small subset of tasks have some AI relevance. Fault diagnosis — 'identifying defective sectionalizing devices, circuit breakers, fuses, voltage regulators' using wiring diagrams and testing instruments — could be partially assisted by AI-powered diagnostic tools or computer vision inspection systems. Route planning and 'assess areas for power line installation' could be supported by AI-assisted geospatial analysis. Reading electricity meters and calculating construction supply needs are straightforward information tasks. Collectively, these represent perhaps 10–15% of working time and are better characterized as AI-assisted rather than AI-replaced.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of core tasks are irreducibly physical: climbing transmission towers, making cable joints in overhead and underground lines, opening switches, attaching grounding devices, and physically installing or repairing power infrastructure. These occur in highly unstructured outdoor environments — roadside poles, underground conduits, elevated bucket trucks — where 2026 robotics cannot operate reliably. Safety-critical physical judgment (erecting barriers, managing live-line hazards, responding to electrical contingencies) requires real-time human presence and accountability. Regulatory frameworks governing electrical utility work impose strict certification and on-site human responsibility requirements that further block AI substitution. No commercially deployed robotic system approaches the dexterity and situational adaptability needed for cable jointing or overhead line repair.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low (2) tier. The primary work product is physical infrastructure transformation in unstructured environments, not digital artifacts. AI tools may marginally assist with diagnostics or planning, but cannot substitute for the hands-on core of the role. The main uncertainty is whether advanced inspection drones or robotic climbing systems might automate a larger share of inspection subtasks over the medium term, but this remains well beyond 2026 deployment reality.

title: Elektroniker und Elektronik-Servicetechniker
