rationale: AI can assist with fault diagnosis, reading circuit diagrams, and writing maintenance reports, but the core work — physically replacing components, soldering, and repairing equipment on-site — requires hands-on manipulation that robots cannot yet reliably perform. The job is anchored in physical, unstructured environments, keeping AI exposure low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** Several tasks in this occupation have meaningful AI support. Fault diagnosis and troubleshooting — interpreting test data, detecting malfunctions, reading circuit diagrams and engineering drawings — are information-processing tasks where AI diagnostic tools and LLM-assisted manuals already provide real value. Writing routine maintenance reports and keeping records of interventions are highly automatable. Customer-facing tasks such as providing repair information, instructing clients on equipment usage, and follow-up services can be partially handled by AI-assisted communication tools. Optional skills like CAD/CADD/CAE software use and checking system parameters against reference values are increasingly AI-augmented. These tasks collectively represent perhaps 20–30% of working time.

**Limiting factors:** The dominant share of this occupation's work is irreducibly physical: soldering electronics, replacing defective components, assembling and fastening parts, operating hand and power tools, and performing on-site repairs across highly varied environments (aircraft avionics bays, railway signal cabinets, vessel electronics, ATM interiors, customer premises). Current robotics — even in structured warehouse settings — operate at roughly 70% human efficiency and cannot handle the fine manipulation, spatial variability, and tool-switching required here. Regulatory constraints are also significant: aviation (common aviation safety regulations, avionics certification), railway safety enforcement, and electrical equipment compliance all require certified human accountability. Physical safety requirements (wearing PPE, lifting heavy weights, working in confined or mobile environments) further resist automation.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). AI provides genuine but peripheral assistance — better diagnostic software, AI-assisted manuals, automated report drafting — but cannot substitute for the physical core of the job. The breadth of sub-occupations (avionics, marine, railway, ATM, consumer electronics) all share this physical-first character. Adoption of AI tools will modestly raise individual productivity but is unlikely to reduce headcount materially before 2026. The score would only rise significantly if robotic manipulation in unstructured environments advances substantially beyond current capability.

title: Installateure und Servicetechniker im Bereich Informations- und Kommunikationstechnik
rationale: AI cannot physically install cables, mount antennae, solder components, or diagnose hardware faults on-site — the core of this job is hands-on work in the physical world. Some diagnostic and documentation tasks benefit from AI tools, but the job remains firmly physical and low-exposure.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A meaningful subset of tasks involves information processing where AI can assist: interpreting electronic design specifications, providing technical documentation, identifying ICT security risks, monitoring communication channel performance, and troubleshooting logical faults in network configurations. AI-powered diagnostic tools can accelerate fault diagnosis for known failure modes in computer hardware and telecommunications equipment. Remote monitoring and predictive maintenance platforms increasingly flag issues before on-site visits are needed, potentially reducing the number of service calls. Documentation tasks — maintaining records of maintenance interventions, providing customer follow-up services — are partially automatable with AI-assisted logging and report generation.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of core tasks are irreducibly physical: installing cable TV services, splicing cable, sealing wire sheaths, soldering electronics, operating aerial work platforms, operating digging construction equipment, mounting antennae, fitting hardware components, and repairing equipment on-site. 2026 robotics cannot perform these tasks in unstructured field environments — rooftops, cable ducts, customer premises, and outdoor infrastructure — at anywhere near human efficiency or reliability. The job also requires real-time physical judgment: checking material compatibility, inspecting cables and electrical supplies, and resolving unexpected on-site conditions. Customer-facing tasks (communicating with customers, demonstrating hardware use, providing repair information) add a trust and interpersonal layer that resists substitution. Regulatory and safety requirements (health and safety procedures in construction, low-voltage wiring standards) further anchor human accountability on-site.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). The primary work product is physical installation, maintenance, and repair of hardware infrastructure — not a digital artifact. AI tools provide genuine but peripheral assistance in diagnostics, documentation, and remote monitoring, justifying a score above the minimal tier. However, the physical, unstructured, and safety-critical nature of the core work means AI substitution remains very limited through 2026 and beyond. The optional skills list (dominated by programming languages like C++, Java, ABAP) reflects edge cases in firmware/software work for a small subset of technicians, not the typical role.

title: Metzger, Fischhändler und -verarbeiter und verwandte Berufe
rationale: AI cannot slaughter animals, bone carcasses, fillet fish, or operate cutting equipment in unstructured physical environments — these are the core tasks of this job. The work is almost entirely manual and physical, keeping AI exposure very low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A small subset of tasks has some AI relevance. Inventory monitoring, stock level tracking, and order processing (listed as 'monitor stock level,' 'process customer orders,' 'carry out end of day accounts') could be partially assisted by digital tools or AI-driven systems. Weighing, labelling, and wrapping products involve structured data that automated systems already handle in industrial settings. Quality inspection of carcasses ('inspect animal carcasses,' 'inspect raw materials') is an area where computer vision is being piloted in large abattoirs, though deployment in small retail butcheries remains limited. Documentation and traceability ('documentation concerning meat production,' 'trace meat products') can be partially automated. These tasks represent perhaps 10–15% of the total work content.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of tasks — slaughtering, stunning, flaying, boning, filleting, skinning, splitting carcasses, grinding, and preparing specialised meat products — require dexterous physical manipulation in wet, cold, unstructured environments with irregular biological material. Current robotics cannot reliably perform fine-grained cutting and boning at acceptable quality or speed outside highly controlled industrial lines, and even there human workers remain dominant for complex cuts. The physical demands ('lift heavy weights,' 'work in cold environments,' 'cope with blood,' 'tolerate strong smells') and the need to handle live or distressed animals ('control animals in distress,' 'ensure animal welfare') are firmly beyond 2026 AI/robotics capabilities in typical butchery settings. Customer-facing advisory tasks ('advise customers on seafood choices,' 'handle customer complaints') add a trust and interpersonal dimension that further resists substitution.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits squarely in the Low tier (score 2). The primary work product is a physically transformed food item — a dressed carcass, a fillet, a sausage — produced through skilled manual labour in challenging physical conditions. AI tools can assist marginally with administrative and inventory tasks, but these are peripheral to the core craft. Adoption barriers (unstructured environments, food safety regulations requiring human accountability, small-scale retail settings) further slow even the limited automation that is technically feasible. No meaningful displacement pressure is expected by 2026.

title: Bäcker, Konditoren und Konfektmacher
rationale: AI cannot knead dough, shape pastries, temper chocolate, or monitor oven conditions through touch and smell — the core work is physical and sensory. A few tasks like scheduling or recipe documentation could use AI tools, but these are peripheral to the job.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A small subset of tasks in this occupation has meaningful AI exposure. Recipe creation and development ('create new recipes,' 'develop new food products') can be assisted by generative AI tools that suggest ingredient combinations or flavor pairings. Production scheduling ('follow production schedule') and inventory/ingredient management ('store raw food materials,' 'select adequate ingredients') can be partially automated through AI-driven planning software. Marketing and sales strategy tasks ('implement marketing strategies,' 'implement sales strategies') listed as essential skills are also AI-assistable. Financial capability tasks could leverage AI bookkeeping tools. These peripheral information-processing tasks represent perhaps 10–15% of the actual working time of a baker or pastry cook.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of core tasks are irreducibly physical and sensory. Kneading dough ('operate kneading of food products'), shaping and moulding ('operate moulding of doughs,' 'mould chocolate'), tempering chocolate ('temper chocolate'), sculpting ('sculpt chocolate'), applying glazes and toppings, and monitoring oven temperatures through visual and olfactory cues all require dexterous manipulation in unstructured, variable physical environments. Robotic systems in 2026 can handle highly repetitive, structured food production (the ISCO-adjacent 'Bakery products machine operator' role), but the artisanal, variable, and tactile nature of craft baking and confectionery — including sensory evaluation ('perform sensory evaluation'), quality checking by feel and appearance, and artistic food creation — remains well beyond deployable robotics. HACCP compliance, hygiene enforcement, and equipment maintenance also require physical presence and judgment. The Swiss market context (apprenticeship-trained, CHF 5,099 median wage) reflects a craft-oriented segment, not industrial production.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 2). The primary work product is a physical food item produced through manual, sensory, and craft-intensive processes. AI tools can assist marginally with recipe ideation and scheduling, but these are auxiliary to the core job. Industrial baking automation exists but is classified separately (ISCO 8160). The artisanal baker, pastry cook, and chocolatier roles described here face minimal displacement pressure from 2026 AI capabilities, with the main uncertainty being whether robotic dough-handling improves enough to penetrate craft settings — which remains unlikely in this timeframe.

title: Molkerei- und Käsereifachkräfte
rationale: AI cannot curdle milk, press cheese into moulds, turn aging wheels, or perform hands-on sensory evaluation of texture and aroma. The work is almost entirely physical transformation of raw materials in a production environment, leaving very little for AI to automate.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A small subset of tasks has meaningful AI exposure. Recording ingredient amounts, test results, and time cycles (listed in ISCO tasks h) is straightforwardly automatable via sensors and MES/SCADA systems already common in food manufacturing. Monitoring product quality through sampling and adjusting treatment conditions (task g) can be partially supported by computer vision and inline sensors for parameters like pH, fat content, and temperature — the optional skill 'measure pH' and 'collect samples for analysis' point to data-generating activities that AI can assist with. 'Have computer literacy' and 'follow production schedule' suggest some digital workflow interaction. Compliance documentation (GMP, HACCP, food safety legislation) can be partially templated and AI-assisted. These automatable subtasks represent perhaps 10–15% of actual working time.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of core tasks are irreducibly physical: pasteurizing and boiling milk, churning cream into butter, curdling milk, heating curd, draining and pressing cheese into moulds, salting, piercing, smearing with cultured wash, and turning cheese blocks on shelves during curing. 'Perform sensory evaluation' — assessing aroma, texture, and taste — is a core quality-control skill that 2026 AI cannot replicate in a physical production context without expensive and still-immature robotic/sensor arrays. The ESCO sub-occupation explicitly describes 'artisanal' processing, and even industrial dairy work involves unstructured physical manipulation, temperature-sensitive timing, and microbial process management that requires hands-on intervention. Regulatory requirements around food safety (HACCP, legislation on animal-origin products) create accountability structures that keep humans central. Robotics for delicate food manipulation (pressing, turning, smearing cheese) remains far below human efficiency in unstructured environments.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low (2) tier. The primary work product is a physical food item produced through hands-on transformation of raw materials — not a digital artifact. AI and automation can assist with data logging, scheduling, and some inline quality monitoring, but cannot replace the physical craft, sensory judgment, or manual dexterity that defines the role. Adoption of even the automatable subtasks is constrained by the artisanal and small-batch nature of Swiss dairy production and existing food-safety oversight requirements. The score of 2 reflects that structured digital information processing exists at the margins (recording, scheduling, compliance) but does not reshape the core occupation.

title: Nahrungsmittel- und Getränkekoster und -klassierer
rationale: AI cannot taste, smell, or physically handle food and beverages — the core of this job is sensory evaluation that requires a human palate and nose. Some peripheral tasks like record-keeping or data analysis can be assisted by AI, but the irreplaceable sensory work keeps exposure low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A subset of tasks in this occupation involve information processing that AI can assist with. Recording grades and identification numbers (task d), analysing milk control test results, preparing visual data, performing food risk analysis, and keeping up-to-date with regulations are all digitally performable tasks where AI tools can provide meaningful support. AI-assisted spectroscopy, electronic noses, and machine vision systems are increasingly used in industrial food grading for objective parameters like colour, size, and chemical composition — relevant to skills like 'check processing parameters,' 'assess cereal quality for brewing,' and 'analyse characteristics of food products at reception.' Marketing-adjacent skills such as 'implement sales strategies,' 'implement marketing strategies,' and 'prepare visual data' are also substantially AI-augmentable.

**Limiting factors:** The core work product of this occupation is irreducibly sensory and embodied. Skills like 'perform sensory evaluation,' 'evaluate coffee characteristics,' 'perform coffee tastings,' 'tolerate strong smells,' 'grade coffee beans,' 'grade foods,' 'assess quality characteristics of food products,' and 'roast malt' all depend on human olfactory and gustatory perception. While electronic sensors can measure some chemical proxies, they cannot replicate the holistic, context-sensitive, culturally-informed judgment of an expert taster — particularly for premium products like wine, whisky, or specialty coffee where consumer acceptance and nuanced flavour profiling are the deliverable. Sub-occupations like master coffee roaster and malt master also involve craft knowledge, recipe creation, and mentorship that resist automation. Physical tasks — maintaining industrial ovens, operating pneumatic conveyor chutes, milking operations, handling animals — add further non-automatable content.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). The primary work product is sensory judgment delivered through the human body, which 2026 AI cannot replicate at production quality for premium or artisanal contexts. AI and sensor technology can automate some peripheral tasks (record-keeping, data analysis, regulatory compliance checks) and assist with objective quality parameters in industrial settings, but cannot substitute for the core tasting and grading function. The main uncertainty is the pace of adoption of electronic sensing in industrial food processing, which could gradually erode the lower-skill end of food grading while leaving expert tasters relatively insulated.

title: Holzbearbeiter, Möbeltischler und verwandte Berufe, onA
rationale: AI cannot cut, shape, join, or finish wood — the core work of cabinetmakers and woodworkers is physical transformation of materials using hand and machine tools. Some design planning and quoting tasks can be AI-assisted, but the hands-on craft keeps overall exposure low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A meaningful share of peripheral tasks in this occupation involve information work that AI can assist with. Design planning, reading technical drawings, calculating material quantities, and preparing cost estimates are all cognitive tasks where AI tools (CAD assistants, generative design software, quoting tools) can accelerate or partially automate the workflow. CNC machine programming — increasingly common in woodworking shops — is a digital task where AI-assisted code generation is already commercially available. If the ISCO 7520 grouping includes workers who primarily operate CNC equipment rather than hand-craft furniture, the digital share of their work rises noticeably.

**Limiting factors:** The primary work product is a physical object — a piece of furniture, a fitted cabinet, a wooden component — produced through physical manipulation of materials. Cutting, planing, joining, sanding, finishing, and fitting require dexterous manipulation in unstructured workshop environments. Current robotics (as of 2026) cannot replicate the fine motor judgment needed for bespoke joinery, surface assessment, or fitting work on-site. Client consultation and bespoke customisation also involve interpersonal trust and tactile judgment that AI cannot substitute. Regulatory and safety requirements around workshop machinery further slow any automation adoption. The 'auxiliary tool rule' applies: if the computer broke, nearly all of this job would still need to happen in person with hand and power tools.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). The core value delivered is physical craftsmanship, which 2026 AI and robotics cannot replicate at production quality in unstructured workshop settings. AI provides genuine but limited uplift on design, quoting, and CNC programming subtasks — enough to push the score above the minimal tier but not enough to threaten the occupation's core. The medium confidence reflects heterogeneity within ISCO 7520: a bespoke furniture maker scores closer to 2, while a CNC operator in a production facility might score closer to 4.

title: Holztrockner und -konservierer
rationale: AI can automate monitoring gauges, logging production data, and optimizing kiln schedules, but the core work—physically loading kilns, handling timber, mixing chemicals, and maintaining equipment—requires hands-on presence. Most tasks involve physical transformation of materials in industrial environments, keeping AI exposure low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A narrow slice of this occupation involves digital or data-driven tasks that AI can meaningfully assist with. Monitoring gauges and panel lights, recording wood treatment information, preparing kiln schedules, and setting controller parameters are all candidates for automation or AI-assisted optimization. Industrial IoT sensors combined with AI process-control systems are already deployed in some kiln operations to optimize drying curves, detect moisture deviations, and flag equipment anomalies. These capabilities map directly onto tasks like 'monitor gauge,' 'control kiln firing,' 'manage kiln ventilation,' and 'record wood treatment information.' Predictive maintenance algorithms could also assist with 'assisting in maintaining processing equipment.' These represent perhaps 15–20% of the task portfolio by time and criticality.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of tasks are physically grounded and require direct manipulation of materials in an industrial environment. Loading kilns, stacking timber, transporting materials with hoists and forklifts, cleaning and lubricating equipment, mixing chemicals, and moving treated wood all require physical dexterity and situational awareness in unstructured or semi-structured settings. Current robotics (as of 2026) can handle structured warehouse pick-and-place at ~70% human efficiency, but the irregular shapes of timber, the variability of kiln environments, and the need for chemical handling make full automation of these tasks technically and economically out of reach. Regulatory requirements around hazardous chemical handling and workplace safety further constrain autonomous operation. The occupation also involves sensory judgment—identifying drying defects, distinguishing wood quality, assessing felled timber—that requires tactile and visual inspection AI vision systems cannot yet reliably replicate in variable field conditions.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low (2–3) tier. The primary work product is physically treated and dried timber, not a digital artifact. AI-assisted process control and data logging provide incremental productivity gains but do not substitute for the physical labor that constitutes the bulk of the role. The adoption of automated kiln control systems is real but has been ongoing for decades as industrial automation, not as a new AI disruption. A score of 2 reflects that some monitoring and scheduling tasks are partially automatable, but the physical core of the job is highly resistant to AI substitution at the 2026 capability frontier.

title: Möbeltischler und verwandte Berufe
rationale: AI cannot cut, shape, join, or finish wood — the core of this job is physical transformation of materials using machines and hand tools. Some design and planning tasks can be AI-assisted, but the hands-on craft work keeps overall exposure very low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A modest subset of tasks has AI relevance. CAD software (listed as both essential and optional) is already AI-augmented, with tools like Autodesk and SketchUp integrating generative design features that can accelerate furniture design and technical drawing preparation. 'Design original furniture,' 'design objects to be crafted,' and 'design scale models' involve creative and planning work where AI can generate concepts, suggest proportions, or produce 2D/3D visualizations. 'Estimate restoration costs,' 'do historical research,' and 'document restoration' are information-processing tasks where AI tools (text analysis, image recognition for identifying period styles, cost-estimation models) can provide meaningful assistance. 'Art history' and 'contextualise artistic work' for antique reproduction and restoration could be partially supported by AI knowledge bases. These tasks collectively represent perhaps 10–15% of working time.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of tasks in this occupation require direct physical manipulation of materials in unstructured environments — cutting, shaping, joining, bending, sanding, finishing, assembling, and repairing wood. Robotics capable of replicating the fine motor dexterity needed for carving, inlay work, fitting joints, applying veneer, or restoring antique furniture do not exist at commercial scale in 2026. The work involves constant tactile feedback, adaptation to material variability (wood grain, moisture content, defects), and judgment calls that current robotic systems cannot replicate. Sub-occupations like furniture restorer, antique furniture reproducer, and cooper involve highly bespoke, one-off physical problem-solving. Customer-facing tasks (discussing frame specifications, providing conservation advice) require interpersonal trust. Regulatory and quality standards in restoration and conservation add further human accountability requirements.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 2). The primary work product is a physical object — a cabinet, barrel, frame, or restored antique — produced through skilled manual craft. AI can assist at the margins with design visualization and documentation, but cannot substitute for the core physical transformation work. Adoption of even the available AI design tools is likely slow given the apprenticeship-trained workforce and bespoke, workshop-based production context. No meaningful displacement pressure is expected through 2026.

title: Einrichter und Bediener von Holzbearbeitungsmaschinen
rationale: AI can assist with reading specifications and optimizing machine programs, but the core work—physically setting up, adjusting, and monitoring woodworking machines—requires hands-on manipulation in an unstructured workshop environment. The job is anchored in physical machine operation, keeping AI exposure low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A subset of tasks in this occupation has meaningful AI exposure. Reading and interpreting specifications (task f) and programming CNC-style machine controllers (set up the controller of a machine) are partially automatable: AI can parse technical drawings, suggest toolpath parameters, and flag specification mismatches. CAD software appears as an optional skill, and modern CAM/CNC integration increasingly automates the translation from design to machine instruction. Monitoring automated machines and conveyor belts is also a candidate for computer-vision-based automation, with systems already deployed in industrial settings to detect defects or jams. Troubleshooting guidance can be partially offloaded to AI diagnostic tools. These tasks collectively represent perhaps 20–30% of the role.

**Limiting factors:** The dominant share of work is physical and tactile: selecting and installing blades, cutter heads, boring bits, and sanding belts; adjusting machine geometry; supplying workpieces; removing processed parts; and hands-on quality inspection of wood surfaces and cuts. Wood is a heterogeneous natural material—grain direction, moisture content, and defects vary per piece—requiring real-time tactile and visual judgment that robotic systems handle poorly in unstructured workshop environments. The 2026 robotics frontier (roughly 70% human efficiency in structured pick-and-pack) does not yet extend reliably to the fine manipulation and adaptive setup required here. Regulatory and safety requirements (wear appropriate protective gear, work safely with machines) further embed human presence. The occupation also spans highly manual sub-roles like wood sander and woodturner, where craft judgment and physical dexterity dominate.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). The information-processing component—machine programming, specification reading—is real but secondary to physical machine setup and operation. AI and automation will incrementally assist with CNC programming and quality monitoring, but the hands-on, adaptive, material-handling core of the job resists near-term substitution. The main uncertainty is the pace of CNC/CAM automation adoption in Swiss furniture manufacturing SMEs, which could gradually shift the balance toward higher exposure over a 5–10 year horizon, but not by 2026.

title: Berufe der Bekleidungsherstellung und verwandte Berufe, onA
rationale: AI can help with pattern design and cutting optimization software, but the core work—sewing, fitting, and assembling garments by hand—requires physical dexterity in unstructured environments that robots still handle poorly. Most exposure comes from design-assist tools, not automation of the physical craft itself.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The ISCO 7530 residual category ('not elsewhere classified') covers tailors, dressmakers, furriers, hatters, and related craft workers whose work involves some digitally-assisted tasks. Pattern-making and grading software (CAD tools like Lectra or Gerber) can automate layout optimization and reduce fabric waste—tasks that previously required skilled manual calculation. AI-assisted design tools can generate pattern variations and suggest alterations. For any administrative or client-communication tasks (quoting, scheduling, order tracking), AI tools offer clear productivity gains. In industrial sub-segments, automated cutting machines guided by AI vision systems are already deployed in larger factories, reducing the human role in straight-cut operations.

**Limiting factors:** The primary work product is a physical garment, not a digital artifact. Core tasks—hand-sewing, fitting adjustments on a live body, working with delicate or irregular materials (fur, leather, bespoke fabrics), and executing fine motor operations like buttonholing or embroidery—remain firmly in the domain of human dexterity. Robotic manipulation of limp, deformable textiles is one of the hardest unsolved problems in industrial robotics; current systems achieve roughly 60–70% human efficiency only in highly structured, repetitive industrial settings, not the varied bespoke or repair work typical of this Swiss cohort. The 'onA' (not elsewhere classified) designation also suggests these are craft specialists, likely in bespoke tailoring, alterations, or niche production—contexts where client interaction, fitting judgment, and material handling are central and AI substitution is minimal. Regulatory and safety barriers are low, but physical-environment barriers are high.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits in the Low tier (score 3). The modest uplift above the floor reflects genuine AI assistance in pattern design, cutting optimization, and administrative tasks, but the physical craft core is highly resistant to automation given the current state of textile robotics and the bespoke nature of Swiss employment in this category. The low confidence reflects the heterogeneity of the residual ISCO group—an industrial cutter in a factory faces meaningfully higher exposure than a bespoke tailor doing alterations—and the small Swiss sample size (573 workers) which may skew toward craft/artisan roles.

title: Herren- und Damenschneider, Kürschner und Hutmacher
rationale: AI can assist with pattern design software and sketching, but the core work—cutting fabric, hand-sewing, fitting garments to individual bodies, and crafting wigs or hats—requires skilled physical manipulation that robots cannot yet replicate. The job is fundamentally hands-on craft work, keeping AI exposure low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A subset of tasks in this occupation has meaningful AI touchpoints. Pattern design and grading (create patterns for garments, grade patterns for wearing apparel, marker making) can be substantially assisted by CAD software and AI-enhanced tools like Optitex or Gerber AccuMark, which are already in commercial use. The skill 'draw sketches to develop textile articles using softwares' and optional skills like 'CAD for garment manufacturing' and '3D body scanning technologies' indicate that some workers already operate in digitally augmented workflows. AI can accelerate design ideation, generate mood boards, and assist with technical drawings. E-tailoring and digital pattern fitting tools are advancing. These digital-adjacent tasks represent perhaps 15–25% of the overall task portfolio.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of tasks in this occupation are irreducibly physical and tactile. Cutting fabrics, sewing pieces together using manual and machine techniques, fitting garments to individual human bodies, dyeing and finishing, assembling costume parts, and crafting wigs all require fine motor dexterity, material judgment, and adaptive physical problem-solving in unstructured environments. Robotics in 2026 cannot perform bespoke tailoring, drape silk around a hat brim, or adjust a fur coat's lining to a specific client's posture. The bespoke and made-to-measure nature of the work—fitting to individual body measurements, adapting to artists' creative demands, maintaining theatrical costumes—adds interpersonal and contextual complexity that resists automation. Regulatory and quality standards for theatrical and luxury garments further demand human accountability and craft judgment.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). The primary work product is a physical garment or accessory produced through skilled manual craft, not a digital artifact. AI tools provide genuine but peripheral assistance in the design and pattern-making phase, but cannot substitute for the physical fabrication, fitting, and finishing that constitute the bulk of working hours. Adoption of even the available digital tools (CAD, 3D scanning) remains partial in this trade, particularly in Switzerland's artisan-oriented tailoring sector. The score of 3 reflects real but limited AI complementarity, with no near-term substitution threat to the core craft.

title: Schnittmustermacher und Zuschneider
rationale: AI and CAD software can already automate pattern grading, nesting, and digital prototyping, and these tools are listed as core skills in this occupation. However, physical cutting, material handling, fitting sample garments, and judging fabric stretch or leather flaws still require hands-on expertise that robots cannot reliably replicate in 2026.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A substantial portion of this occupation's core tasks are already conducted in digital environments. Essential skills such as 'CAD for garment manufacturing,' 'use pattern-cutting softwares,' 'marker making,' 'grade patterns for wearing apparel,' 'draw sketches to develop textile articles using softwares,' and 'operate computerised control systems' are all information-processing tasks where AI tools are commercially mature. Pattern grading — scaling patterns across sizes — is highly algorithmic and largely automated by existing CAD/PLM systems. Nesting and marker-making (optimising fabric yield) are solved problems in industrial software, with AI-enhanced nesting algorithms outperforming manual layout. The 'digital prototyper' sub-occupation is particularly exposed: transforming paper patterns into digital form and operating monitoring machines is close to fully automatable. 3D body scanning and analysis of scanned body data are also areas where AI-driven fit algorithms are advancing rapidly, reducing the need for manual pattern adjustment.

**Limiting factors:** The physical half of this occupation resists automation significantly. Tasks such as 'cut fabrics,' 'bundle fabrics,' 'position leather on the cutting bed maximising usage according to skin grain, skin flaws and skin stretch,' 'test patterns by making and fitting sample garments,' and 'trim excess material' require dexterous manipulation in unstructured physical environments. Leather and fur work in particular demands tactile judgment about material irregularities that computer vision and robotics cannot reliably replicate at scale in 2026. CNC cutting machines automate straight-line fabric cutting in high-volume industrial settings, but bespoke, small-batch, or luxury contexts (common in Switzerland's fashion and leather goods sector) still depend on skilled human judgment. Regulatory and quality standards in apparel manufacturing also create friction for full automation. The 'wearing apparel patternmaker' and 'leather goods patternmaker' sub-occupations, which rely heavily on interpreting design sketches and hand-tool work, are more resistant than the CAD-focused sub-occupations.

**Net assessment:** This occupation straddles the moderate-to-high boundary. The digital sub-occupations (CAD patternmaker, digital prototyper) sit closer to a 6–7, while the physical cutting and fitting roles sit closer to a 3–4. Averaged across the full ISCO group, a score of 5 reflects that AI tools are already deeply embedded in the digital workflow side, providing meaningful productivity amplification and some substitution, but that physical manipulation, material judgment, and sample fitting keep full automation at bay. The small Swiss employment base (110 workers) and likely concentration in quality-oriented ateliers further moderates displacement risk in the near term. Key uncertainty: the pace at which robotic cutting systems improve for irregular materials like leather and fur.

title: Näher, Sticker und verwandte Berufe
rationale: AI cannot sew, embroider by hand, cut fabric, or assemble physical textile articles — the core work is manual manipulation of materials. A small share of peripheral tasks like sketching designs or quality documentation can be AI-assisted, keeping exposure low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A narrow subset of tasks in this occupation has meaningful AI exposure. The essential skill 'draw sketches to develop textile articles using softwares' is directly AI-augmentable — generative design tools can produce embroidery patterns, garment sketches, and decorative motifs at near-professional quality in 2026. Optional skills like '3D modelling' and '3D printing process' represent digitally mediated workflows where AI tools are increasingly capable. Quality inspection tasks ('ensure quality of final product,' 'inspect toys and games for damage') could in principle be partially supported by computer vision systems, though deployment in small Swiss workshops is limited. These digitally adjacent tasks represent perhaps 10–15% of the overall task portfolio.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of tasks in this occupation require direct physical manipulation of materials: sewing, embroidering, cutting, bundling, assembling, patching, darning, gluing, and softening leather. Robotics capable of replicating fine-motor needle-and-thread work, hand embroidery, or the tactile judgment needed to match thread colour and fabric texture remain far below human capability in 2026 — especially in the unstructured, varied environments typical of small garment workshops. The occupation explicitly notes work 'mainly by hand.' Sub-occupations like doll maker, glove maker, and embroiderer involve highly variable, often bespoke physical outputs that resist automation. Regulatory and safety requirements around toys and games (essential skills reference safety recommendations) add human accountability that further limits substitution.

**Net assessment:** This is a firmly physical, craft-based occupation. The primary work product is a physical textile or garment article, not a digital artifact. AI's 2026 capabilities in text, vision, and agentic workflows are largely irrelevant to the core tasks. The small design-sketching and pattern-generation component earns a marginal uplift above the absolute floor, placing this occupation solidly in the Low tier (score 2). Key uncertainty is the pace of industrial embroidery machine automation, but that affects the related 'sewing machine operator' (ISCO 8153) more than this hand-craft-oriented group.

title: Polsterer und verwandte Berufe
rationale: AI cannot cut fabric, install springs, sew upholstery, or shape padding onto furniture frames — the entire job is skilled physical manipulation of materials. Almost no core tasks are digitally automatable, keeping exposure very low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A small subset of peripheral tasks has some AI relevance. Pattern creation from sketches or blueprints could be assisted by CAD/AI design tools. Cost estimation and customer consultation (advising on fabric, colour, style) could be partially supported by AI-assisted quoting software. Reading engineering drawings and standard blueprints is a cognitive task where AI vision tools can assist. Optional skills like 'identify customer's needs' and 'estimate restoration costs' involve information processing that AI can partially support. However, these are auxiliary or preparatory tasks, not the core work product.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of essential skills — aligning components, cutting textiles, sewing fabric by hand, installing spring suspension, applying padding and covering material, performing upholstery repair, using manual sewing techniques, using power tools — require precise physical manipulation in unstructured environments. Upholstery involves handling irregular, deformable materials (fabric, leather, foam, springs) on varied furniture geometries; 2026 robotics cannot perform this work at commercial viability. Renovation of antique furniture, tufting by hand, and repairing orthopaedic appliances demand tactile judgment and dexterity that remains firmly human. Regulatory and quality standards in aircraft interior work add safety-critical oversight requirements that further resist automation.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits squarely in the Low (2) tier. The primary work product is a physically transformed object — upholstered furniture, vehicle interiors, mattresses — produced through skilled manual craft. AI tools may marginally assist with pattern design or quoting, but these represent a small fraction of working time. There is no plausible near-term pathway for AI or robotics to substitute meaningfully for the core physical skills. The score of 2 reflects the minor digital-assist potential in design and estimation tasks.

title: Pelzveredler, Gerber und Fellzurichter
rationale: AI cannot scrape hides, tan leather, apply dyes by hand, or stretch pelts — the entire job involves physically transforming animal skins through chemical and manual processes. A small sorting and grading task could use computer vision, but the core work is firmly hands-on.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The most AI-susceptible task in this occupation is sorting and grading pelts by colour, shading, size and density (task a), where computer vision systems are increasingly capable of quality classification in structured industrial settings. The essential skill 'differentiate nuance of colours' and optional skill 'manage quality of leather throughout the production process' have partial AI analogues in machine vision inspection tools already deployed in some tanneries. 'Think analytically' and 'identify defects on raw hides' could be partially assisted by AI-powered visual inspection, though defect identification on irregular biological materials remains challenging. These automatable sub-tasks represent perhaps 10–15% of total work content.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of tasks involve direct physical manipulation of biological materials: scraping flesh and fat from skins, removing hair, curing with salt, immersing in tanning solutions, applying dyes and oils by hand brush, stretching and smoothing pelts, and seasoning leather. These require dexterous manipulation in unstructured, wet, chemically complex environments — precisely the domain where robotics remains far below human capability in 2026. The physico-chemical properties of hides and skins vary enormously between individual animals, requiring tactile and visual judgment that current robotic systems cannot replicate reliably. Regulatory and quality standards in leather production (food-contact leather, luxury goods) further demand human accountability. The entire production chain is artisanal or semi-industrial, with no digital work product whatsoever.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 2). The primary work product is a physically transformed material — leather or dressed fur — produced through chemical treatment and manual craft. AI's contribution is limited to a minor quality-sorting assist via computer vision. The occupation's tiny Swiss employment base (35 workers) and apprenticeship-level entry reflect its craft nature. No meaningful displacement pathway exists from current or near-term AI capabilities.

title: Schuhmacher und verwandte Berufe
rationale: AI cannot cut leather, stitch soles, take foot casts, or hand-shape orthopaedic inserts — the core of this job is skilled physical manipulation of materials. The work is almost entirely hands-on craft, leaving very little for AI to automate.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A small subset of tasks involves information processing that AI could assist with: studying drawings and work orders (task g), pattern creation and grading, and checking specifications for conformity (task k). The optional skill 'use IT tools' and 'apply technical communication skills' suggest some digital touchpoints. AI-assisted pattern grading software already exists in the apparel/footwear industry and could modestly accelerate design layout. Quality control via computer vision is technically feasible for inspecting stitch conformity or leather texture, though adoption in small craft workshops is minimal. These automatable sub-tasks represent perhaps 10–15% of total work time.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of essential skills — apply assembling techniques, apply stitching techniques, manual cutting processes for leather, perform manual cutting for footwear uppers, apply footwear finishing techniques, apply pre-stitching techniques — are hands-on physical operations requiring fine motor control, tactile judgment, and tool manipulation. Orthopaedic shoemaking adds a clinical dimension: taking plaster casts of deformed feet (task d), preparing custom inserts from casts (task e), and modifying footwear per medical prescription. These require physical dexterity, patient interaction, and medical-adjacent judgment that 2026 robotics cannot replicate in unstructured, bespoke contexts. Leather assessment (task h — checking texture, colour, strength) relies on tactile and visual expertise that AI vision systems cannot yet reliably substitute in craft settings. The work is almost entirely physical transformation of real-world materials.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low (2) tier. The primary work product is a physical object — a repaired shoe, a custom orthopaedic insert, a stitched leather bag — produced through skilled manual craft. AI offers marginal assistance in pattern grading and specification review, but no commercially deployed AI or robotic system can perform the core physical tasks at acceptable quality in the bespoke, small-batch context typical of Swiss cobblers and orthopaedic shoemakers. Adoption barriers (small workshop scale, unstructured physical environment, bespoke customisation) further suppress near-term displacement. The score of 2 reflects the small but real digital periphery without overstating automation potential.

title: Taucher
rationale: AI cannot descend underwater to weld, repair hulls, lay foundations, or recover bodies — the entire job is physical work in an unstructured, hazardous environment. Almost nothing here is automatable by 2026 AI, placing this firmly in the minimal-exposure tier.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The only plausible AI touchpoints in this occupation are peripheral: 'keep records of work progress' and 'keep personal administration' could be assisted by AI tools, and surface-side planning (reviewing dive plans, checking environmental conditions) could benefit from AI-assisted data analysis. Underwater inspection tasks like hull or pipeline inspection could theoretically be augmented by remotely operated vehicles (ROVs) with computer vision, which is a real and growing technology. However, ROVs are a separate occupational category and a complementary tool rather than a substitute for the skilled diver performing hands-on interventions.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of essential skills — welding underwater, welding in hyperbaric conditions, using rigging equipment, using lift bags, performing search and rescue missions, coping with decompression, drilling holes for blasting, laying bridge and pier foundations, and collecting marine life — require physical presence in an unstructured, dynamic, and dangerous aquatic environment. 2026 robotics cannot reliably replicate the dexterous, adaptive physical manipulation required in these conditions. The safety-critical and legally regulated nature of diving operations (compliance with diving legislation, crisis intervention, first aid) further entrenches human judgment and accountability. Physical hazards, unpredictable underwater environments, and the need for real-time adaptive decision-making under pressure are precisely the conditions where current robotics and AI agents fail.

**Net assessment:** This occupation scores 1 — minimal exposure. The primary work product is physical transformation of underwater structures and environments, with no meaningful digital output. Even the inspection subtasks, while theoretically ROV-assisted, still require a certified diver for intervention and repair. The small administrative periphery does not materially shift the score. Key uncertainty: long-run ROV and underwater robotics development could eventually erode the inspection and survey subtasks, but this is well beyond the 2026 horizon and would not eliminate the hands-on construction, salvage, and rescue core.

title: Sprengmeister und Sprengbeauftragte
rationale: AI cannot load explosives, inspect blast holes, or physically detonate charges — the core of this job is hands-on work in unpredictable outdoor and underground environments. Record-keeping and blast planning have minor AI assistance potential, but the job remains overwhelmingly physical and safety-critical.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The most AI-amenable tasks are administrative and planning in nature: compiling and maintaining records about explosives usage (task k), planning blast hole layout and depth (task b), and determining quantity and type of explosives (task d). These involve structured data and rule-based decision-making that AI tools could assist with — for example, AI could help generate compliance documentation or suggest charge quantities based on geological input data. The optional skill 'report outcome of blast' and the essential skill 'troubleshoot' have partial digital analogs. However, even these planning tasks require site-specific physical assessment and licensed human sign-off under Swiss explosives law.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of core tasks are irreducibly physical and safety-critical: loading explosives into blast holes, assembling primer charges, connecting detonating circuits, tamping materials, checking borehole cleanliness and depth, and declaring blast areas safe. These require physical presence in unstructured, hazardous environments — underground mines, quarries, demolition sites — where robotics in 2026 cannot operate reliably. Regulatory and liability frameworks in Switzerland (Sprengstoffgesetz) mandate licensed human operators for every stage of the blasting process. The essential skill 'make independent operating decisions' reflects the high-stakes, real-time judgment required when conditions deviate from plan — a domain where AI agents are not trusted or legally permitted. Physical dexterity, situational awareness, and accountability under strict safety law create compounding barriers to AI substitution.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low (2) tier. The primary work product is a physical outcome — a controlled detonation in a real-world environment — not a digital artifact. AI can marginally assist with record-keeping and pre-blast planning documentation, but these are peripheral to the core work. No commercially deployed AI or robotic system in 2026 can perform the hands-on, safety-critical tasks that define this role. The tiny Swiss workforce (79 workers) and strict licensing requirements further insulate the occupation from near-term disruption.

title: Produkttester und -klassierer (ohne Nahrungsmittel und Getränke)
rationale: AI-powered computer vision and automated optical inspection can already handle many visual defect checks and measurement tasks, but physical handling, tactile assessment, and operating in unstructured production environments remain hard to automate. The mix of hands-on physical work and data-recording tasks places this occupation in the moderate tier.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A significant share of core tasks are already being automated or augmented by 2026 AI tools. Automated optical inspection (AOI) is explicitly listed as both an essential skill and a sub-occupation, and commercial AOI systems using computer vision already outperform humans on PCB defect detection and surface inspection at scale. Recording test data, analysing test data, preparing technical reports, writing inspection reports, and tracking KPIs are information-processing tasks that AI can handle well. Image analysis, interpreting electrical diagrams and blueprints, and checking conformity to specifications are tasks where AI vision and document-understanding models perform at near-professional quality. The 'automated optical inspection operator' sub-occupation is particularly exposed — the human role is increasingly supervisory over machines that do the actual inspection.

**Limiting factors:** A large portion of this occupation involves physical, hands-on work that resists automation in 2026. Measuring dimensions with calipers, gauges, and micrometers; preparing physical samples; grading natural materials like wool, veneer, and wood by tactile and visual judgment in unstructured settings; driving prototype vehicles; and operating battery test equipment all require dexterous physical presence. Non-destructive testing (ultrasound, X-ray, eddy current) in field environments — examining civil structures or vessels — involves unstructured physical contexts where robotics remain limited. Regulatory compliance roles (REACh, electrical equipment regulations, safety management) carry liability and accountability requirements that slow AI substitution. Many sub-occupations (automotive test driver, non-destructive testing specialist) have strong physical or safety-critical components.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the moderate tier (score 5). AI is already reshaping the most digitally-intensive sub-roles (AOI operator, PCB test technician) and the data-recording/reporting layer of nearly all roles, but the physical inspection, tactile grading, and field-testing tasks that define the majority of the occupation remain human-dependent. The heterogeneity across sub-occupations (from veneer grader to automotive test driver to PCB tester) creates uncertainty — some sub-roles score closer to 7, others closer to 3 — which is reflected in medium confidence.

title: Kammerjäger und andere Schädlingsbekämpfungsberufe
rationale: AI cannot mix chemicals, spray pesticides, set traps, or physically inspect and treat infested buildings — the core work is hands-on in unpredictable real-world environments. Some administrative tasks like inspection reports and scheduling are AI-assisted, but these are peripheral to the job.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A modest share of peripheral tasks have AI applicability. Report writing ('report on pest inspections') can be partially automated with AI-assisted templates. Customer communication and scheduling ('maintain customer service,' 'maintain professional administration') benefit from AI tools. Identifying infestation sources from photos using computer vision is an emerging capability, and AI could assist in recommending treatment types based on documented pest biology. These administrative and diagnostic-support functions represent perhaps 15–20% of total work time.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of core tasks are physically grounded and resist AI substitution entirely at the 2026 frontier. Mixing chemicals to specification, operating spray equipment, navigating unstructured environments (attics, crawl spaces, agricultural fields), setting traps, and applying pesticides to designated areas all require physical presence and dexterous manipulation in highly variable settings. Regulatory compliance ('European pesticide legislation,' 'follow control of substances hazardous to health procedures,' 'safe use of pesticides') demands certified human accountability and on-site judgment. Pest identification often requires direct physical inspection — assessing moisture levels, structural damage, entry points — that computer vision cannot yet reliably perform remotely. Robotics capable of operating spray equipment in unstructured indoor/outdoor environments are not commercially deployed at scale.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low (2) tier. The primary work product is a physically treated environment — a fumigated building, a cleared field — not a digital artifact. AI tools offer marginal productivity gains on administrative peripherals, but the core value delivered requires a trained human physically present with chemicals and equipment. No meaningful displacement pressure is expected by 2026; the occupation's trajectory is more analogous to an electrician than to an information worker.

title: Handwerks- und verwandte Berufe, anderweitig nicht genannt
rationale: Most work in this catch-all trades category involves hands-on physical tasks in varied, unstructured environments that robots and AI cannot yet reliably handle. The score stays low because the core output is physical craftsmanship, not digital information.
analysis:
ISCO 7549 is a residual 'not elsewhere classified' category for craft and related trades workers, meaning it aggregates highly heterogeneous sub-occupations — from instrument makers and taxidermists to sign writers and specialized repair technicians. Because ESCO task and skill data for this catch-all group is sparse and heterogeneous, the analysis must rely on the general profile of craft trades. The primary work product across these occupations is physical: fabricating, repairing, assembling, or finishing objects in real-world environments. Physical manipulation in unstructured or semi-structured settings remains well beyond 2026 robotic capabilities at commercial scale. Some sub-occupations may involve design sketching or order documentation on computers, but these are auxiliary tasks; the core value is manual skill and craft judgment.

Limiting factors are substantial. Physical dexterity, tool handling, on-site problem-solving, and working with varied materials are the defining competencies of trades workers. Current robotics achieve roughly 70% human efficiency only in highly structured warehouse environments; bespoke craft work — fitting, finishing, repairing unique objects — is far harder to automate. Regulatory and quality-assurance requirements in Swiss apprenticeship-based trades also slow adoption. Client-facing consultation and quality inspection require human judgment that AI cannot yet replicate reliably in physical domains.

The net assessment places this occupation at score 3 (Low). Some peripheral tasks — quoting, invoicing, parts lookup, scheduling — are increasingly AI-assisted, and AI image tools could marginally help with design visualization in certain sub-trades. However, these are minor relative to the physical core. Confidence is low because the NEC category spans sub-occupations with meaningfully different AI exposure profiles; a sign writer using digital design tools sits closer to 5, while a taxidermist or instrument repairer sits closer to 1-2.

title: Bediener von Anlagen und Maschinen und Montageberufe, onA
rationale: This catch-all category covers machine operators and assemblers who couldn't be classified elsewhere, meaning tasks range from semi-automated production line work to manual assembly. AI and robotics can handle repetitive structured tasks, but the unclassified nature suggests atypical or mixed physical work that resists easy automation.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** ISCO 8000 is a residual 'not elsewhere classified' category for plant and machine operators and assemblers. To the extent workers in this group perform repetitive, structured machine operation or assembly tasks, robotic process automation and computer vision systems are increasingly capable of handling such work. Warehouse robotics (e.g., pick-and-place systems) and CNC-style automated machinery already operate at ~70% human efficiency in structured environments. Monitoring equipment, adjusting settings, and logging production data — common operator tasks — are increasingly handled by sensor networks and AI-driven process control systems. Any digital reporting or quality-check documentation these workers produce is also susceptible to AI-assisted automation.

**Limiting factors:** The 'not elsewhere classified' designation is the critical complication here. Workers in this residual category likely perform tasks too varied, specialized, or atypical to fit standard machine-operator profiles — which often means unstructured physical environments, novel manipulation challenges, or hybrid roles combining machine operation with craft judgment. Physical dexterity in non-standardized settings, troubleshooting unfamiliar mechanical problems, and adapting to irregular materials or workflows remain firmly human-advantaged in 2026. Regulatory and safety requirements in Swiss manufacturing also slow autonomous system deployment. The very small Swiss employment count (139 workers) suggests a highly heterogeneous group.

**Net assessment:** The residual nature of this category makes scoring inherently uncertain. A mid-range score of 5 reflects that the *typical* machine operator/assembler role has meaningful but not dominant AI exposure — structured repetitive tasks are increasingly automatable, but the unclassified status implies atypical complexity. The score could reasonably range from 3 (highly manual, unstructured work) to 7 (repetitive line assembly) depending on the actual mix of workers captured here. Confidence is low due to this heterogeneity.

title: Bediener stationärer Anlagen und Maschinen, onA
rationale: AI and robotics can automate monitoring, quality checks, and some control tasks for stationary machines, but most work involves physical operation, adjustment, and troubleshooting in industrial environments. The broad catch-all nature of this category keeps exposure moderate.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** ISCO 8100 is a residual 'not elsewhere classified' category covering operators of stationary plant and machinery across diverse industries. Core tasks typically include monitoring equipment performance, adjusting controls, detecting malfunctions, and performing basic maintenance. AI-driven process control systems, predictive maintenance algorithms, and computer vision for quality inspection are already deployed in many industrial settings — these directly automate the monitoring and anomaly-detection subtasks. Agentic control systems can adjust machine parameters in response to sensor data without human intervention in well-structured environments. For operators whose primary role is watching dashboards and responding to alerts, AI substitution is already meaningful.

**Limiting factors:** The physical, hands-on dimension of this work — clearing jams, replacing components, making mechanical adjustments, responding to novel equipment failures — remains firmly human-advantaged. Robotic systems capable of unstructured physical intervention in industrial machinery are not yet commercially widespread. Many facilities also have safety regulations requiring a human operator on-site regardless of automation level. The extreme heterogeneity of this ISCO group (covering everything from paper mill operators to chemical plant controllers) means some sub-occupations face much higher exposure (e.g., process control in highly digitized plants) while others remain largely manual. This variance is the primary driver of low confidence.

**Net assessment:** A score of 4 (moderate) reflects the genuine but uneven AI penetration across this heterogeneous group. Monitoring and control tasks are increasingly automatable, but physical intervention, fault diagnosis in novel situations, and regulatory requirements for human presence anchor the score below the high tier. The wide variance across sub-occupations and the physical-environment constraint prevent a higher score; the real deployment of industrial AI and process automation prevent a lower one.

title: Bergleute und Grubenarbeiter
rationale: AI cannot operate drilling rigs, excavate rock, install roof bolts, or drive heavy underground equipment — the entire job is physical machinery operation in hazardous, unstructured environments. Automation exists in mining but requires massive capital investment and remains far from replacing skilled operators.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A small subset of tasks has meaningful AI involvement. Record-keeping ('maintain records of mining operations,' 'record drilling,' 'complete inter-shift communication') could be partially assisted by AI transcription or logging tools. Sensor-based monitoring of equipment performance and borehole depth checks could be augmented by computer vision or IoT analytics. Troubleshooting and fault diagnosis on machinery could benefit from AI-assisted diagnostics. These peripheral tasks represent perhaps 10–15% of working time.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of essential skills — operating drilling equipment, positioning drills, setting up drilling rigs, operating underground heavy equipment (LHD, continuous miner, longwall equipment, roof bolter), installing rock bolts, managing sumps and dewatering systems, collecting samples, and performing minor repairs — require physical presence, fine and gross motor control, and real-time judgment in highly unstructured, hazardous underground environments. Robotics in mining exists (autonomous haul trucks in open-pit surface mining at large-scale operations), but underground mining presents severe constraints: confined spaces, irregular geometry, dust, vibration, and rapidly changing geological conditions. The 2026 robotics frontier handles structured warehouse environments at ~70% human efficiency; underground mining is far more demanding. Regulatory safety requirements and the consequences of equipment failure underground create strong adoption barriers. Switzerland's small mining sector (431 workers) further reduces commercial incentive for bespoke automation.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 2). The primary work product is physical extraction and machinery operation in dangerous, unstructured environments — the exact domain where 2026 AI and robotics capabilities are weakest. The only upward pressure comes from minor administrative and monitoring subtasks. The score of 2 (rather than 0–1) reflects that digital record-keeping and equipment telemetry do provide some AI-assistable surface area, but these are clearly auxiliary to the core physical work.

title: Bediener von Maschinen zur Herstellung und Verarbeitung von Zement, Steinen und sonstigen Mineralien
rationale: AI can assist with monitoring gauges, logging production data, and optimizing machine settings, but the core work—physically operating heavy machinery, handling raw materials, and adjusting moulds—requires hands-on presence in an industrial environment. The job is anchored in physical machine operation with limited digital work product, keeping AI exposure low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A subset of tasks in this occupation are amenable to AI-assisted automation. Monitoring instruments such as temperature and pressure gauges (monitor gauge, monitor automated machines) can be augmented or partially replaced by sensor-based AI systems already deployed in industrial settings. Record-keeping tasks—record production data for quality control, write batch record documentation, keep records of work progress—are straightforward information-processing activities that AI tools handle well. Quality inspection tasks such as inspect batches, inspect quality of products, and inspect stone surface could benefit from computer vision systems, which are in early industrial deployment for defect detection. Machine setting optimization (set up the controller of a machine, quality and cycle time optimisation) is an area where AI-driven process control is increasingly used in cement and concrete manufacturing. These represent perhaps 20–30% of task time.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of tasks require direct physical manipulation in an industrial environment: feed raw material into plant machinery, assemble moulds, fill moulds, move filled moulds, remove finished casts, maneuver stone blocks, mix concrete, operate forklift, tend asphalt mixing machine, polish stone surfaces, and wash stone. Robotic systems capable of performing these varied, unstructured physical tasks at acceptable cost and reliability do not exist at scale in 2026. The work involves heavy, irregular materials in variable conditions—stone blocks, wet concrete, moulds of different sizes—which remain well beyond current robotic dexterity. Regulatory and safety requirements (apply health and safety standards, follow safety standards in industrial contexts, personal protective equipment) also demand human accountability on-site. The breadth of sub-occupations (asphalt plant operator, stone splitter, precast moulder, block machine operator) all share this physical-environment anchor.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). AI contributes meaningfully to monitoring, data logging, and quality inspection subtasks, but these are peripheral to the core physical work of operating and tending heavy industrial machinery. The primary work product is a physical object—concrete sections, stone products, asphalt mixes—produced through hands-on machine operation. Adoption of robotic automation in this specific domain is nascent and faces significant cost and technical barriers. The score of 3 reflects real but limited AI impact, primarily through process monitoring and record-keeping augmentation rather than substitution of the core role.

title: Bediener von Anlagen in der Metallerzeugung, -umformung und -veredlung, onA
rationale: AI can assist with monitoring sensor data and detecting anomalies in metal production equipment, but the core work involves physically operating and adjusting heavy industrial machinery in noisy, variable factory environments. Human presence, manual intervention, and real-time physical judgment keep AI substitution low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The monitoring and quality-control subtasks within metal production operations have meaningful AI exposure. Computer vision systems can inspect surface defects, and sensor-fusion AI can flag process deviations (temperature, pressure, feed rate) in real time. Predictive maintenance algorithms are already deployed in steel and aluminium plants, reducing the need for manual inspection rounds. These capabilities address perhaps 20–30% of the task portfolio — primarily the observational and data-logging duties that ISCO 8120 workers perform alongside machine operation.

**Limiting factors:** The dominant work product is physical: operating rolling mills, casting lines, extrusion presses, heat-treatment furnaces, and surface-finishing equipment. These machines require hands-on setup, tooling changes, material loading, and real-time mechanical adjustments in response to unexpected conditions (jams, material inconsistencies, equipment faults). The factory floor environment is unstructured, hot, loud, and physically hazardous — exactly the conditions where 2026 robotics remain far below human efficiency. Regulatory safety requirements in Swiss industrial settings further slow automation adoption. The 'onA' (ohne nähere Angabe — not further specified) classification also means the role spans a wide range of sub-processes, many of which involve bespoke legacy equipment that is not yet integrated with AI control systems.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 2). AI contributes modestly through process monitoring and anomaly detection, but cannot replace the physical operation, manual adjustment, and on-the-spot problem-solving that define the role. Adoption of robotic automation in Swiss metal production is progressing but remains partial and slow due to equipment heterogeneity, safety regulations, and capital investment cycles. The score could rise to 3 over a 5–10 year horizon as collaborative robotics and closed-loop process control mature, but for 2026 the exposure is genuinely low.

title: Bediener von Anlagen in der Metallerzeugung und -umformung
rationale: AI can assist with monitoring gauges, reading sensor data, and logging production records, but the core work—physically setting up machinery, adjusting valves, handling molten metal, and troubleshooting mechanical faults on the factory floor—requires hands-on presence. The job is anchored in physical process control in harsh industrial environments, keeping AI exposure low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A subset of tasks in this occupation involves structured data monitoring and record-keeping that AI can meaningfully support. Observing gauges and meters, recording production logs, monitoring automated machines, and quality/cycle time optimisation are tasks where sensor fusion, computer vision, and process-control AI are already deployed in advanced manufacturing. Predictive maintenance systems can flag equipment malfunctions before human operators notice them. The 'consult technical resources' and 'keep records of work progress' skills are partially automatable via AI-assisted documentation tools. These monitoring and logging functions represent perhaps 20–25% of the task portfolio.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of core tasks require direct physical intervention in a hazardous industrial environment: adjusting burner controls, admitting metals to furnaces, filling and cleaning moulds, replacing dies, handling molten metal under tension, and troubleshooting mechanical faults in real time. Robotics in unstructured heavy-industrial environments (variable temperatures, molten materials, irregular workpieces) remains far below human capability in 2026. The occupation also demands tactile judgment—assessing metal temperature by colour and behaviour, detecting mould uniformity by touch and inspection—that current AI-physical systems cannot replicate reliably. Regulatory and safety requirements in Swiss industrial settings further slow automation adoption. The 63 essential skills are dominated by physical manipulation tasks, not information processing.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). AI contributes modestly through process monitoring, anomaly detection, and documentation assistance, but cannot substitute for the physical setup, adjustment, and hands-on troubleshooting that define the role. The primary work product is a physically transformed metal object, not a digital artifact. Adoption barriers—harsh environments, safety regulations, and the cost of retrofitting legacy industrial equipment—further constrain near-term AI displacement. The score reflects genuine but limited complementarity rather than substitution risk.

title: Bediener von Anlagen zur Metallveredlung, Plattierung und Beschichtung von Metallen
rationale: AI can help optimize process parameters and flag quality defects via computer vision, but the core work—physically setting up, tending, and adjusting plating and coating machines in an industrial environment—requires hands-on operation. The job is anchored in physical machine operation and chemical process control, keeping AI exposure low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A subset of tasks in this occupation are amenable to AI-assisted automation. Quality inspection tasks such as 'detect product defects,' 'spot metal imperfections,' and 'recognise signs of corrosion' can increasingly be supported by computer vision systems deployed in industrial settings. Process monitoring tasks—'monitor electroplating baths,' 'monitor gauge,' 'monitor automated machines,' and 'monitor conveyor belt'—are candidates for sensor-driven AI oversight that can flag anomalies and adjust parameters. Data recording and production logging (checking plating thickness, writing production logs) could be partially automated through integrated measurement and MES systems. Predictive maintenance AI can assist with 'ensure equipment availability' and 'troubleshoot.' These represent perhaps 20–30% of task volume, primarily the monitoring and data-capture periphery of the role.

**Limiting factors:** The dominant share of core tasks are irreducibly physical: setting up machines ('set up the controller of a machine,' 'supply machine with appropriate tools'), physically tending equipment ('tend anodising machine,' 'tend electroplating machine,' 'tend dip tank,' etc.), preparing and mixing metallizing solutions, applying spraying techniques, performing enamelling, and operating rust-proofing spray guns. These require dexterous manipulation in semi-structured industrial environments where 2026 robotics remain well below human efficiency for the variety of workpiece geometries and process changeovers involved. Chemical process knowledge (anodising process, electrolytic passivation, dip-coating process) requires real-time physical judgment that AI cannot yet reliably substitute. Safety requirements ('wear appropriate protective gear,' 'health and safety in the workplace') and the physical consequences of errors in chemical handling further slow adoption. Regulatory and quality standards in metal finishing (e.g., for aerospace or automotive supply chains) impose validation burdens that discourage unsupervised automation.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). The primary work product is a physically transformed metal article, not a digital artifact. AI tools offer genuine but peripheral value in monitoring, defect detection, and data logging—enough to lift the score above minimal—but the hands-on machine operation, chemical preparation, and physical setup tasks that constitute the bulk of working time are resistant to current AI and robotics capabilities. The score reflects demonstrated 2026 capability, not speculative future automation.

title: Bediener von Anlagen und Maschinen für chemische Erzeugnisse
