rationale: AI can assist with monitoring sensor data and optimizing process parameters, but the core work—physically loading materials, adjusting machinery, collecting samples, and responding to equipment anomalies—requires hands-on presence in industrial environments. **Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). The score reflects genuine but limited complementarity rather than substitution risk.

title: Bediener von Anlagen für fotografische Erzeugnisse
rationale: AI can automate digital photo editing and colour correction, but the core work here is physical: handling chemicals, loading film into machines, and monitoring darkroom development baths. title: Bediener von Maschinen zur Herstellung von Gummiwaren

title: Bediener von Maschinen zur Herstellung von Textil-, Pelz- und Lederwaren, onA

analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A small subset of tasks involves monitoring and data interpretation that AI could assist with — reading gauges, monitoring automated machines, keeping records of work progress, and writing batch record documentation. Computer vision systems could theoretically assist with task (m), observing finished products for splits, cracks, and colour imperfections, as industrial vision inspection is a commercially deployed technology. Remote monitoring of kiln temperatures and valve states via sensors is also feasible with AI-assisted process control. These represent perhaps 10–15% of the listed essential skills.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of essential skills — adjust burner controls, blow moulding, clean moulds, cut clay, extract products from moulds, handle broken glass sheets, light auxiliary gas jets, maintain extrusion machines, operate grinding hand tools, position glass on trays, prepare kiln firebox, tend auger-press, tend drawing kiln, use power tools, wear appropriate protective gear — require direct physical manipulation in high-temperature, unstructured industrial environments. 2026 robotics cannot reliably perform delicate or variable physical tasks like smoothing glass edges, filling moulds with clay slip, or troubleshooting mechanical failures on bespoke legacy equipment. Regulatory and safety requirements in Swiss manufacturing further slow automation adoption. The work product is a physical object (glass, ceramics, bricks), not a digital artifact.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 2). AI contributes marginally through sensor-based process monitoring and automated visual inspection, but the core value delivered — physically operating, adjusting, and maintaining kilns, presses, and forming machines in a hot industrial setting — is robustly resistant to 2026 AI capabilities. The auxiliary digital tasks (record-keeping, consulting technical resources) are minor and do not shift the tier. Uncertainty is low given the clear physical-work dominance.

title: Bediener von Dampfmaschinen und -kesseln
rationale: AI can assist with monitoring sensor data and logging readings, but the core work—physically operating, adjusting, and repairing steam boilers and engines—requires hands-on intervention in industrial environments. The job is anchored in physical machinery operation and fault response, keeping AI exposure low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A subset of tasks involves structured data monitoring that AI can augment: reading gauges, recording instrument readings, monitoring automated machines, and tracking water/heat meters are all tasks where sensor-integrated AI systems and SCADA platforms already provide automated alerts and trend analysis. 'Perform risk analysis' and 'maintain records of maintenance interventions' involve information processing that AI tools can partially support. Predictive maintenance AI (vibration analysis, thermal imaging interpretation) is commercially deployed in industrial settings and can flag anomalies before human operators notice them. These monitoring and logging tasks represent perhaps 20–30% of the role's time.

**Limiting factors:** The dominant work product is physical: operating valves, firing furnaces, lubricating equipment, resolving equipment malfunctions, standing high temperatures, and performing hands-on repairs. These require physical presence in hot, high-pressure industrial environments where robotic systems are not yet deployed at scale for this type of unstructured maintenance work. Regulatory and safety frameworks (pressure vessel codes, boiler inspection certifications) mandate licensed human operators on-site. The 'stand high temperatures' and 'resolve equipment malfunctions' skills signal that much of the critical work happens in adverse physical conditions requiring dexterity and situational judgment that 2026 robotics cannot reliably replicate. Marine applications (ship boiler operation) add further complexity with dynamic, unstructured environments.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). AI contributes meaningfully to the monitoring and data-logging subtasks, and predictive maintenance tools are a genuine productivity complement. However, the core value delivered—keeping physical steam and power systems running safely through hands-on operation and repair—is resistant to AI substitution given current robotics limitations, safety regulations requiring certified human operators, and the physically demanding, variable environments involved. The score reflects real but bounded AI augmentation rather than substitution.

title: Bediener von Verpackungs-, Abfüll- und Etikettiermaschinen
rationale: AI and robotics can automate some monitoring and quality-inspection tasks on packaging lines, but the core work involves physically operating, supplying, and adjusting industrial machinery in variable production environments. The job remains anchored in hands-on physical operation, keeping AI exposure low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The most AI-susceptible tasks in this occupation are monitoring and quality control functions: 'check quality of products on the production line,' 'detect flaws in bottles,' 'monitor machine operations,' and 'ensure quality control in packaging.' Computer vision systems deployed in 2026 can perform inline defect detection and label verification at high speed and are already in commercial use in food and beverage manufacturing. 'Operate automated process control' and 'record cylinders information' involve data logging and parameter monitoring that can be partially handled by SCADA/PLC systems with AI overlays. Some scheduling and compliance documentation tasks (e.g., HACCP record-keeping, GMP adherence logs) could be assisted by AI tools.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of essential skills describe direct physical interaction with machinery and materials: 'supply machine,' 'set up machine controls,' 'fill cylinders,' 'maneuver cylinders,' 'tend canning machine,' 'operate heat sealing machinery,' 'perform packing of footwear and leather goods,' 'heat materials,' and 'handle chemicals for clean in place.' These require physical presence, dexterity, and real-time response to unstructured mechanical events. Robotic systems exist for some packaging sub-tasks but operate in highly structured, purpose-built environments; the heterogeneity of this ISCO group (gas cylinders, leather goods, footwear, food/beverage, tobacco) means no single automation solution covers the role. Regulatory requirements (GMP, HACCP, food safety legislation, gas cylinder safety) impose human accountability and inspection obligations that slow full automation. The physical environment — conveyors, heavy cylinders, chemical handling, unsafe environments — remains firmly human-operated at 2026 capability levels.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits solidly in the Low tier (score 3). AI contributes meaningfully only to the monitoring and inspection sub-tasks, which are real but peripheral to the core physical operation of machinery. The primary work product is a physically transformed or packaged good, not a digital artifact. Robotic automation is the more relevant long-run threat than AI per se, but 2026 deployment in the heterogeneous environments this group covers remains limited. The score reflects demonstrated, commercially deployed capability rather than speculative future robotics.

title: Bediener stationärer Anlagen und Maschinen, anderweitig nicht genannt
rationale: AI can help monitor automated machines and log quality data, but the core work—physically setting up, tending, and adjusting industrial cutting and banding machines—requires hands-on intervention in unstructured factory environments. The job is anchored in physical manipulation and real-time mechanical troubleshooting, keeping AI exposure low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A subset of tasks in this occupation are partially AI-addressable. Quality monitoring on production lines can be augmented by computer vision systems that detect defects in real time, and 'record production data for quality control' is straightforwardly automatable via sensor integration and MES software. 'Monitor automated machines' is increasingly handled by predictive maintenance AI that flags anomalies before human operators notice them. 'Consult technical resources' and 'read standard blueprints' are tasks where AI assistants can provide meaningful support. These represent perhaps 20–25% of the task portfolio.

**Limiting factors:** The dominant tasks—physically supplying machines with materials and tools, performing test runs, adjusting edge banding and slitting machinery, removing processed workpieces, disposing of cutting waste, and troubleshooting mechanical faults—require dexterous physical presence in a semi-structured industrial environment. 2026 robotics can handle structured pick-and-place at roughly 70% human efficiency, but the variability of materials (wood, metal, paper, plastic in different formats), the need to respond to jams or misfeeds, and the fine mechanical adjustments involved in setup all remain firmly human-advantaged. Regulatory and safety requirements around machinery operation also create adoption friction. The occupation's physical core is not meaningfully substitutable by current AI or robotics.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits solidly in the Low tier (score 3). AI contributes modestly through quality-monitoring vision systems and data logging, but the primary work product is a physically transformed material output achieved through hands-on machine operation. The 'information-work detection rule' does not apply here—if the computer broke, nearly all of this job would still need to happen in person. The small Swiss employment base (278 workers) and niche specialization (edge banding, slitting) also mean AI investment in full automation of these specific workflows is unlikely to be prioritized commercially in the near term.

title: Montageberufe, onA
rationale: Assembly workers fit parts together by hand in manufacturing environments — tasks that require dexterous manipulation, physical presence, and adapting to variation on the line. Robotic automation exists but struggles with unstructured assembly, keeping human workers relevant through 2026.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** ISCO 8210 assemblers perform repetitive, structured physical tasks — joining components, operating jigs and fixtures, quality checking finished parts — that are the primary target of industrial robotics and computer-vision inspection systems. In highly structured environments (automotive body assembly, electronics PCB placement), robotic automation is already mature and has displaced significant headcount over the past decade. AI-enhanced vision systems can now perform many in-line quality checks that were previously manual. For the most standardised sub-tasks (feeding components into automated lines, simple fastening operations), the automation share is already high in capital-intensive Swiss manufacturing.

**Limiting factors:** The ISCO 8210 category is broad and includes assembly work in small-batch, high-mix, or precision contexts (watchmaking components, medical devices, custom machinery) where robotic solutions remain economically or technically impractical. Unstructured physical manipulation — handling irregular parts, adapting grip to variation, troubleshooting jams — remains at roughly 70% human efficiency for robots even in 2026. Swiss manufacturing is disproportionately concentrated in high-precision, low-volume segments where flexible human dexterity retains a cost advantage. Regulatory requirements around traceability and quality sign-off in medical and aerospace supply chains also slow full automation. There are no meaningful AI tools for the cognitive or interpersonal dimensions of this role.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the Low tier (score 3). Physical dexterity in variable environments is the core work product, and while industrial robotics is the relevant automation vector, deployment in Swiss high-precision manufacturing contexts is constrained by technical and economic barriers. The score is slightly above the floor because structured sub-tasks are genuinely automatable and the long-run trajectory is toward further displacement — but the 2026 frontier does not yet threaten the majority of these workers. Confidence is medium because the category spans both highly automated (electronics assembly) and relatively protected (watchmaking, medtech) sub-occupations.

title: Berufe der Montage von mechanischen Bauteilen
rationale: AI-guided robotics can automate repetitive fastening and alignment steps on structured assembly lines, but fitting complex mechanical parts—especially in aircraft, turbines, or vessels—still requires hands-on dexterity and real-time physical judgment. The core work is physical transformation of materials, keeping overall AI exposure low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** Several tasks in this occupation are candidates for partial automation. Reviewing work orders, specifications, and drawings (listed as a core ISCO task) can be assisted by AI document-parsing tools. Recording production data on forms is already largely automated via MES/ERP systems. Quality inspection tasks—'inspect quality of products,' 'remove defective products,' 'monitor manufacturing quality standards'—are increasingly supported by computer vision systems deployed in structured factory environments. Robotic assembly cells (referenced directly in sub-occupation descriptions: 'CNC machines or robots') already handle repetitive fastening and alignment in high-volume automotive and electronics assembly. The optional skill 'automation technology' and 'ICT system programming' signal that some workers in this group already interact with automated systems.

**Limiting factors:** The dominant work product is physical: constructing, fitting, and installing prefabricated mechanical parts into engines, aircraft, turbines, and vessels. These are high-consequence, low-tolerance assemblies in unstructured or semi-structured environments. Aircraft assembly, turbine assembly, and vessel engine work involve complex spatial reasoning, torque verification, and tactile feedback that 2026 robotics cannot reliably replicate outside highly engineered, narrow task cells. Regulatory frameworks (aviation safety, railway vehicle compliance, vessel regulations—all listed as essential skills) impose human accountability and certification requirements that slow automation adoption. The breadth of sub-occupations (aircraft, rolling stock, motorcycles, vessels, mechatronics) means many workers operate in low-volume, high-variety contexts where bespoke robotic tooling is not economically justified. Troubleshooting, performing test runs, and maintaining installed equipment require adaptive physical problem-solving that remains firmly human-advantaged.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits in the Low tier (score 3). Robotic process automation and computer vision provide meaningful but partial assistance—primarily in high-volume automotive or standardized sub-assembly contexts. The majority of core tasks require physical manipulation, domain-specific mechanical judgment, and regulatory accountability in environments too varied for current robotics to fully address. AI amplifies productivity modestly (documentation, inspection support) but does not substitute for the hands-on assembly work that defines the occupation. Key uncertainty: the score could rise toward 4 if collaborative robotics ('cobots') mature rapidly in aerospace and rail assembly over the next few years.

title: Berufe der Montage von elektrischen und elektronischen Geräten
rationale: AI-guided robotics can automate some soldering and component placement, but the hands-on assembly, fine manipulation, and quality inspection in varied physical environments remain difficult to fully automate. The job is primarily physical manufacturing work, keeping AI exposure low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A meaningful subset of tasks in this occupation are already being automated or augmented by machines. Surface-mount technology (SMT) placement and wave soldering are increasingly handled by automated equipment — the SMT machine operator and wave soldering operator sub-roles are essentially machine-tending roles where the human oversees automated processes. Computer vision systems can perform quality inspection tasks such as 'inspect quality of products,' 'remove defective products,' and 'check for solder defects' at high throughput in structured factory environments. Reading blueprints, assembly drawings, and circuit diagrams could be partially assisted by AI tools that parse and interpret technical documents. Recording production data on forms is a low-complexity digital task easily handled by automated data capture. These automation-susceptible tasks represent perhaps 25–35% of the core workload.

**Limiting factors:** The dominant share of this occupation involves precise physical manipulation — aligning components, fastening units, soldering by hand, operating precision tools, wearing cleanroom suits, and manipulating delicate dental or electronic materials. Robotic systems in 2026 can handle structured pick-and-place at roughly 70% human efficiency in well-defined environments, but the variety of components, the fine dexterity required (micro-welding, dental instrument assembly, coil winding), and the need to adapt to non-standard assemblies or defects keep human hands essential. Regulatory requirements (medical device regulations, electrical equipment regulations, IPC standards, clinical guidelines for dental instruments) impose quality accountability that slows full automation adoption. The dental instrument assembler sub-occupation in particular involves highly varied, delicate manipulation that current robotics cannot reliably replicate.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). The primary work product is a physical assembled object, not a digital artifact. While automated machinery already handles some sub-tasks (SMT placement, wave soldering), the human role in setup, fine adjustment, inspection of edge cases, and manual assembly of complex or varied components remains central. AI and robotics are complementary tools here — increasing throughput and consistency — but do not yet substitute for the majority of skilled assembly work. The main uncertainty is the pace of robotic dexterity improvement; if general-purpose manipulation robots reach commercial viability by 2027–2028, this score could rise to 4–5.

title: Montageberufe, anderweitig nicht genannt
rationale: AI cannot physically assemble bicycle frames, fit jewellery parts, or fasten furniture components — the core work is hands-on manipulation of physical materials. Robotic assembly exists but requires structured, high-volume environments; this catch-all category covers too many varied, small-batch products to automate cheaply.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A handful of tasks in this occupation involve structured information processing that AI can assist with: reviewing work orders and assembly drawings, recording production data on forms, and performing pre-assembly quality checks against specifications. AI-assisted computer vision can support inspection tasks ('inspect quality of products,' 'remove defective products') in structured factory settings, and is already deployed in some high-volume assembly lines. CAD software appears as an optional skill, and AI tools can generate or interpret technical drawings. These peripheral tasks represent perhaps 10–15% of working time.

**Limiting factors:** The primary work product is entirely physical — assembling bicycle frames, fitting jewellery parts, fastening rubber goods, joining wood elements, building pyrotechnic devices, or manufacturing medical devices. These tasks require dexterous manipulation in varied, often small-batch or artisanal contexts (jewellery assembler, firework assembler, ammunition assembler). Robotic assembly at ~70% human efficiency exists only in highly structured, high-volume, single-product environments; the extreme heterogeneity of this ISCO catch-all category (from plastic toys to ammunition to eyeglass frames) makes broad robotic substitution economically unviable in the near term. Regulatory barriers are significant for sub-occupations like medical device assembly (cleanroom protocols, sterilisation, clinical guidelines) and firework/ammunition assembly (pyrotechnic permits, explosives legislation). Physical dexterity requirements — smoothing rough jewel parts, mounting stones, manipulating rubber — remain firmly beyond 2026 robotic capability in unstructured settings.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits solidly in the Low tier (score 2). The work is overwhelmingly physical transformation of materials, with digital tasks playing only an auxiliary role. While robotic assembly is a real long-run threat, the diversity of products covered by this residual ISCO category, combined with small-batch production norms in Switzerland's manufacturing sector, means AI/robotics adoption will be slow and partial. Confidence is medium because the sub-occupations are highly heterogeneous — a high-volume plastic products assembler faces more automation pressure than a jewellery or medical device assembler.

title: Fahrzeugführer und Bediener mobiler Anlagen, onA
rationale: AI can assist with route planning and logistics scheduling, but the core work—physically operating vehicles and mobile equipment in varied real-world environments—remains beyond reliable automation in 2026. The residual category nature of this group and ongoing but limited robotics deployment keep exposure low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** ISCO 8300 is a residual 'not elsewhere classified' category covering vehicle operators and mobile plant operators not captured by more specific codes (e.g., truck drivers, crane operators, forklift drivers). Across these roles, AI and automation do touch some tasks: route optimization software and GPS-assisted navigation reduce cognitive load in logistics; telematics systems monitor vehicle performance; and scheduling/dispatch workflows are increasingly automated through agentic tools. For operators of repetitive, structured-environment equipment (e.g., warehouse forklifts on fixed routes), early-stage autonomous systems are in limited deployment at roughly 70% human efficiency. Administrative tasks such as logging hours, completing digital checklists, or reporting incidents are partially automatable.

**Limiting factors:** The primary work product is physical movement and manipulation of vehicles or equipment in real-world environments—unstructured roads, construction sites, agricultural fields, or industrial yards. These environments are highly variable, requiring constant situational judgment, obstacle avoidance, and physical dexterity that 2026 robotics and autonomous systems handle poorly outside tightly controlled settings. Regulatory frameworks in Switzerland and the EU impose strict liability and certification requirements on autonomous vehicle operation, creating significant adoption barriers. Human presence is also often required for safety, customer interaction, or on-site problem-solving (e.g., securing loads, responding to mechanical issues). The 'onA' (not elsewhere classified) nature of this group means it likely captures the most heterogeneous and atypical operator roles, which are precisely those least amenable to standardized automation.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). While some peripheral tasks (scheduling, reporting, navigation assistance) are AI-augmented, the core physical operation work is not substitutable by 2026 AI or robotics at scale. Confidence is low because the residual ISCO category aggregates highly diverse sub-roles—some (structured warehouse equipment) face meaningfully higher automation pressure than others (specialized mobile plant in unstructured environments)—making a single score imprecise.

title: Lokomotivführer
rationale: AI and automation can assist with scheduling, monitoring gauges, and collision warnings, but actually driving a train through varied real-world conditions still requires a certified human operator by law. Regulatory mandates, safety liability, and the physical complexity of rail operations keep automation limited for now.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** Several peripheral tasks are meaningfully AI-assisted today. Schedule monitoring, freight shipment report preparation, and communications logging are information-processing tasks that AI tools handle well. Predictive maintenance systems already assist with 'check train engines' and 'ensure maintenance of trains' by analyzing sensor data. Automatic Train Protection (ATP) and Automatic Train Operation (ATO) systems — deployed on some Swiss metro lines (e.g., Lausanne m2) — automate speed control, braking, and station stopping, directly overlapping with 'control train movement' and 'locomotive brake applications.' Computer vision systems can assist with 'watching for track hazards.' These are real, deployed capabilities, not speculative.

**Limiting factors:** Full autonomous operation on mainline mixed-traffic rail (SBB intercity, freight corridors) faces severe barriers. Swiss and EU rail safety regulations (TSI, BAV certification) mandate a licensed human driver in the cab for virtually all revenue operations outside fully segregated automated metros. The physical environment is highly unstructured — level crossings, unexpected obstacles, weather, signal failures, and shunting in marshalling yards all require real-time human judgment. Tasks like 'shunt rolling stock in marshalling yards,' 'handle stressful situations,' 'focus on passengers,' and 'communicate with customers' involve physical presence, situational awareness, and accountability that current AI cannot replicate in open rail environments. The 'teach train driving principles' task is entirely human-facing. Regulatory and union barriers in Switzerland are substantial.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the low end of exposure (score 3). AI genuinely assists with monitoring, scheduling, and some braking automation on segregated lines, but the core work product — safely operating a train through a complex, regulated, physical environment — remains firmly human-dependent under current Swiss law and technology. The occupation is not immune to long-run disruption (driverless metro expansion is real), but the 2026 horizon shows incremental assistance rather than substitution for mainline locomotive drivers. Confidence is medium because the heterogeneity between automated metro driving and mainline freight/passenger driving creates meaningful within-occupation variance.

title: Bediener von Sicherungs-, Signal- und Leittechnik im Schienennetzbetrieb
rationale: AI can assist with scheduling, monitoring, and signal optimization, but the core work—physically coupling wagons, operating switches, inspecting brakes, and responding to live track incidents—requires hands-on presence in unpredictable environments. Safety-critical regulations and physical complexity keep automation limited.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A meaningful subset of tasks involves structured information processing that AI can support: monitoring train schedules, compiling signalling reports, reading railway circuit plans, and operating control panels are increasingly assisted by computerized traffic management systems. Modern rail control centers already use AI-assisted traffic optimization and automated signal logic. Tasks like 'control train arrivals/departures' and 'monitor conditions affecting train movement' are partially handled by centralized traffic management software in advanced rail networks (e.g., SBB's ETCS/ERTMS infrastructure). Writing rail defect records and maintaining inventory of track parts are also candidates for AI-assisted documentation.

**Limiting factors:** The dominant share of tasks in this occupation are physically grounded and safety-critical in ways that resist 2026 AI substitution. Shunting rolling stock, performing wagon coupling, operating hydraulic jack lifts, using rigging tools, testing brake force, and conducting physical track inspections all require dexterous manipulation in unstructured, outdoor, weather-affected rail yard environments—well beyond current robotic deployment capability. Regulatory frameworks (railway safety standards, level crossing regulations, railway framework legislation) impose strict human accountability requirements. The 'stay alert' and 'handle stressful situations' demands reflect real-time situational awareness in dynamic physical environments where AI agents cannot yet act autonomously. Even where automation exists (e.g., automated shunting in some yards), human oversight and exception-handling remain mandatory under Swiss and EU rail safety law.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). While digital monitoring and reporting subtasks are partially AI-assisted, the primary work product is physical—safe train movement achieved through hands-on equipment operation, coupling, inspection, and real-time yard management. The safety-critical regulatory environment and the unstructured physical nature of rail yards create strong adoption barriers. The score reflects meaningful but peripheral AI assistance rather than substitution of core tasks.

title: Motorradfahrer
rationale: AI cannot physically ride a motorcycle, navigate unpredictable urban traffic, or hand-deliver packages to recipients. The job is almost entirely physical and situational, leaving very little for AI to automate beyond route planning.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A small subset of tasks have meaningful AI exposure. Route optimization and reducing travel time ('analyse ways to reduce travel time', 'use geographic information systems') are already handled by mature AI-powered navigation tools like Google Maps or Waze. Scheduling and 'establish daily priorities' for delivery runs can be optimized by logistics software. Record-keeping ('keeping a record of journeys', 'maintain vehicle delivery documentation') and basic customer communication are partially automatable. These peripheral tasks represent perhaps 15–20% of the role's cognitive load.

**Limiting factors:** The core work product is physical delivery via motorcycle in real-world, unstructured environments — a domain where robotics and autonomous vehicles remain far from replacing human riders in Swiss urban and mixed-terrain conditions. 'Drive two-wheeled vehicles' and 'drive in urban areas' require continuous real-time physical judgment that 2026 AI cannot replicate on a motorcycle. Interpersonal tasks — 'communicate with customers', 'handle fragile items', 'process payments', 'handle delivered packages' — require physical presence and dexterous manipulation. Regulatory and insurance frameworks in Switzerland also create strong barriers to autonomous two-wheeled delivery vehicles. The physical, unstructured, and interpersonal nature of the work anchors this occupation firmly in the low-exposure tier.

**Net assessment:** This occupation scores 2. AI contributes modestly through navigation assistance and logistics scheduling, but the primary work — riding a motorcycle through real-world environments and completing physical handoffs — is essentially immune to 2026 AI substitution. The score reflects genuine but narrow AI complementarity (route planning tools) rather than any meaningful displacement risk.

title: Personenkraftwagen-, Taxi-, Kleintransporter- und Kleinbusfahrer
rationale: Self-driving vehicle technology is advancing but remains far from replacing human drivers in Swiss urban and mixed-traffic conditions by 2026. The core work is physical operation of a vehicle in unpredictable real-world environments, which AI cannot yet reliably perform autonomously.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A subset of tasks are AI-assisted today: route optimisation via GPS and navigation apps is largely automated, dispatch and scheduling via digital platforms (Uber, taxi apps) reduces human coordination overhead, and fare collection is increasingly cashless and automated. Documentation tasks (delivery records, patient journey records) can be partially automated. These peripheral tasks represent perhaps 15–20% of working time and are already being absorbed by platform software rather than displacing drivers wholesale.

**Limiting factors:** The core task — physically operating a vehicle safely in unstructured, dynamic real-world environments — remains firmly beyond 2026 AI deployment at scale in Switzerland. Autonomous vehicle technology exists in limited pilots but is not commercially deployed for taxis, patient transport, or delivery vans in Swiss urban and mixed-terrain conditions. Sub-occupations like patient transport, hearse driving, and armoured car driving add strong interpersonal, physical-assistance, and security dimensions (transferring patients, assisting disabled passengers, handling luggage, responding to emergencies) that are entirely human-dependent. Regulatory frameworks for autonomous vehicles in Switzerland are still nascent, adding a hard adoption barrier.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). AI tools assist with navigation, dispatch, and documentation but cannot substitute for the physical driving task or the interpersonal care elements. The main uncertainty is the pace of autonomous vehicle rollout — a breakthrough in AV regulation and deployment could shift this score upward within 5–10 years, but not by 2026. The heterogeneity of sub-occupations (armoured car vs. parking valet vs. patient transport) introduces some variance, but all share the same physical-operation core.

title: Busfahrer und Strassenbahnführer
rationale: AI can assist with route optimization and ticketing, but driving a bus or tram through urban traffic, managing passengers, and responding to emergencies requires physical presence and real-time judgment. Autonomous vehicle technology exists but is not yet deployed at scale for public transit in complex urban environments.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** Several peripheral tasks are already partially automated or AI-assisted. Fare collection and ticket verification are increasingly handled by automated systems (contactless payment, validators). Route navigation via GPS is standard. Schedule adherence can be monitored algorithmically. Passenger information provision is partially handled by automated announcements and digital displays. These tasks — operating GPS systems, reading maps, collecting fares — represent a meaningful but non-core share of the role. Autonomous vehicle technology (Waymo, autonomous bus pilots in controlled environments) demonstrates that the driving task itself is technically within reach for AI/robotics in constrained settings.

**Limiting factors:** The core work product is the safe physical transport of passengers through dynamic, unstructured urban environments — a task that remains firmly human-advantaged in 2026. Swiss urban transit involves complex tram infrastructure, mixed traffic, pedestrian interactions, and edge cases that current autonomous systems handle poorly at production scale. Essential skills like 'help to control passenger behaviour during emergency situations,' 'provide first aid,' 'assist disabled passengers,' 'lift heavy weights,' and 'tolerate stress' are inherently physical and interpersonal. Regulatory and liability frameworks in Switzerland and the EU have not approved fully autonomous public transit vehicles for revenue service. The physical environment — tram tracks, mixed urban traffic, accessibility requirements — creates substantial adoption barriers beyond pure technical capability.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). The driving and passenger-management core is physically grounded and not yet automatable at production scale in Swiss public transit. Some peripheral tasks (ticketing, navigation, scheduling) are already partially automated, pushing the score above the minimal tier. The key uncertainty is the medium-term trajectory of autonomous vehicle deployment in European urban transit — if regulatory approval accelerates post-2026, this score could rise significantly. For now, the physical, regulatory, and interpersonal barriers are decisive.

title: Fahrer schwerer Lastkraftwagen
rationale: AI can optimize routes and handle dispatch logistics, but driving a heavy truck through real-world traffic, loading cargo, and managing physical delivery still requires a human body in the cab. Autonomous heavy trucking exists in trials but is far from replacing Swiss drivers by 2026.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The most AI-susceptible tasks in this occupation are informational and logistical: route determination (GPS and AI-powered fleet routing tools already outperform manual planning), maintaining logbooks (increasingly automated via telematics), and adhering to transportation work schedules (managed by dispatch software). Operating GPS systems, reading maps, and interpreting traffic signals are partially automated through advanced driver-assistance systems (ADAS). Administrative tasks like revising certifications for dangerous goods transport and reporting on fuel distribution incidents involve structured document work that AI can assist with. These peripheral tasks represent perhaps 15–20% of working time.

**Limiting factors:** The core work product is the physical transport of goods through unstructured real-world environments — a fundamentally physical task. Maneuvering heavy trucks in urban areas, loading and unloading cargo, securing goods, operating concrete pumps, handling dangerous goods, and responding to road incidents all require embodied presence and fine motor judgment. Switzerland's complex terrain, dense urban delivery environments, and strict regulatory requirements (ADR dangerous goods, Swiss road transport legislation) add friction to automation adoption. The ESCO skill list is dominated by physical, safety-critical, and animal-welfare tasks that have no viable AI substitute in 2026. Autonomous heavy trucking (e.g., Waymo Via, Aurora) is in limited highway trials in the US but is not commercially deployed in Swiss mixed-traffic conditions. The sub-occupations — fire service vehicle operator, live animal transporter, concrete pump operator — involve emergency response and specialist physical operation that are deeply resistant to automation.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 2). AI contributes meaningfully only to route optimization and logistics scheduling — tasks that represent a small fraction of the job and that complement rather than replace the driver. The primary work product is physical transportation requiring a licensed human operator, and regulatory, safety, and infrastructure barriers make near-term substitution implausible. Confidence is medium rather than high because the long-run trajectory of autonomous trucking is real, but the 2026 horizon is too near for deployment at scale in Switzerland's operating environment.

title: Führer von mobilen land- und forstwirtschaftlichen Maschinen
rationale: AI cannot fell trees, operate harvesting machinery, or navigate unstructured forest terrain — the core work is physical machine operation in unpredictable outdoor environments. Autonomous agricultural and forestry machinery exists but remains limited, keeping overall AI displacement low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A subset of tasks involves structured decision-making that AI can assist with: route planning, yield forecasting ('forecast timber production', 'forecast workload'), pest and disease identification ('identify plants characteristics', 'plant disease control'), and precision farming applications ('apply precision farming'). GPS-guided auto-steering on tractors and precision agriculture software already automate some navigation and input-application decisions. Drone and satellite imagery can assist with field inspection ('inspect agricultural fields') and timber volume assessment ('assess felled timber volume'). These represent real but peripheral automation gains.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of essential skills are irreducibly physical: felling trees, de-limbing, loading timber onto skidders, operating forestry machinery in unstructured terrain, spraying pesticides, spreading fertiliser, and performing routine maintenance. Forestry environments are among the most unstructured physical settings imaginable — variable terrain, unpredictable tree fall dynamics, weather, and safety hazards ('assess hazards implied in tree operations', 'prepare emergency treework operations'). Fully autonomous forestry robots capable of replacing human operators do not exist at commercial scale in 2026; current autonomous agricultural machinery (e.g., autonomous tractors) handles only narrow, flat-field tasks and still requires human supervision. Regulatory and safety requirements around chainsaw and heavy machinery operation further slow any automation pathway.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 2). The primary work product is physical transformation of land and timber, not digital artifacts. AI tools provide marginal assistance in planning and monitoring subtasks, but the core machine-operation and physical-environment skills are resistant to 2026 AI capabilities. The score is 2 rather than 1 because precision farming tools and decision-support software do meaningfully touch a portion of the workflow, but substitution risk remains very low.

title: Führer von Erdbewegungs- und verwandten Maschinen, Strassenbau und Baumaschinen
rationale: AI cannot operate an excavator, bulldozer, or road roller — these machines require physical presence, real-time spatial judgment, and hands-on control in unpredictable outdoor environments. The job is almost entirely physical machine operation, leaving very little for AI to automate.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A small subset of tasks involves digital or information-processing elements that AI could assist with. Operating GPS systems, completing report sheets, interpreting 2D/3D plans, and conducting inter-shift communication are all partially automatable or AI-augmentable. Autonomous and semi-autonomous heavy equipment (e.g., GPS-guided graders, automated compaction rollers) is already commercially available and in early deployment on large infrastructure projects, meaning some precision grading and leveling tasks can be partially automated. Telematics and onboard diagnostics increasingly assist with equipment monitoring and maintenance scheduling. These represent perhaps 10–15% of core task time.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of tasks — operating excavators, bulldozers, pile drivers, dredges, and road rollers — require continuous physical presence, real-time tactile and spatial judgment, and adaptive decision-making in unstructured outdoor environments (mud, unstable ground, underground utilities, weather). Current robotics cannot reliably handle the full range of conditions these operators face: unexpected soil composition, proximity to live utilities, variable terrain, and coordination with other workers on dynamic construction sites. Regulatory and safety requirements (e.g., preventing damage to utility infrastructure, reacting to time-critical events) further demand human accountability. The physical transformation of earth and materials is the core work product, not a digital artifact, placing this firmly in the low-exposure tier.

**Net assessment:** This occupation scores 2. Semi-autonomous machine guidance and digital reporting tools provide modest AI assistance, but the core work — physically operating heavy machinery in unstructured environments — remains firmly beyond 2026 AI/robotics capabilities at production scale. Adoption of autonomous construction equipment is progressing but is years from displacing skilled operators across the full task range. Confidence is high given the clear physical-work primacy.

title: Kranführer, Aufzugmaschinisten und Bediener verwandter Hebeeinrichtungen
rationale: AI cannot physically operate a crane, rig loads, or react to dynamic site conditions in real time — these require hands-on control, spatial judgment, and split-second physical responses. The job is fundamentally about manipulating heavy physical loads in unstructured environments, keeping AI exposure very low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A narrow set of tasks have some AI relevance. Route scheduling and load chart interpretation could be partially assisted by AI planning tools. GPS operation and reading 2D/3D plans are digitally mediated tasks where AI can provide decision support. Automated cable vehicle controllers (one ESCO sub-occupation) already operate in semi-automated environments where supervisory control systems handle routine sequencing, and further automation of fixed-route cable cars and ski-lifts is plausible. Computer vision systems can assist with load positioning feedback in structured port environments (container crane operators). These represent perhaps 10–20% of core task content.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of tasks — rigging loads, determining centre of gravity in real time, reacting to time-critical events, operating mobile cranes in unstructured construction sites, following signalling instructions from ground crews, and performing high-risk work at heights — require physical presence, fine motor control, and dynamic situational awareness that 2026 robotics and AI cannot replicate at production scale. Unstructured outdoor environments (construction sites, mines, waterways) are precisely where current robotics remain far below human capability. Regulatory requirements for certified operators, safety liability, and the catastrophic consequences of errors create strong adoption barriers. The physical transformation of the real world — moving multi-tonne loads safely — is the core work product, not a digital artifact.

title: Gabelstaplerfahrer und verwandte Berufe
rationale: AI cannot drive a forklift, lift pallets, or navigate a physical warehouse floor — these are hands-on physical tasks requiring real-time spatial judgment. Some record-keeping and stock tracking are automatable, but the core job is physical and keeps this score low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A small subset of tasks involves digital or information work that AI can handle well. Maintaining a warehouse database, monitoring goods movement, and using barcode scanning equipment or warehouse management systems are increasingly automated through integrated WMS platforms and RFID/IoT systems. Record-keeping of work undertaken and breakdowns is largely automatable. These peripheral tasks represent perhaps 10–15% of the role's actual time and cognitive load. Agentic tools can also assist with stock rotation scheduling and order-picking optimization through algorithmic routing.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of this occupation's core tasks are irreducibly physical. Operating a forklift, positioning lifting devices under loaded pallets, stacking goods with precision, handling dangerous goods, and transferring stock all require real-time physical manipulation in unstructured or semi-structured warehouse environments. While warehouse robotics (e.g., autonomous mobile robots, automated guided vehicles) are advancing, full forklift automation in mixed human-robot environments with irregular loads, tight spaces, and variable pallet configurations remains far from displacing human operators at scale as of 2026. The skills 'estimate distances,' 'maintain concentration for long periods,' 'stay alert,' and 'lift heavy weights' reflect embodied, situational demands that current robotics handles poorly outside highly structured, purpose-built facilities. Regulatory requirements around dangerous goods handling and equipment inspection also demand accountable human presence.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low (2) tier. The primary work product is physical — moving goods through space safely and accurately — and AI/robotics cannot yet replicate this reliably in the varied real-world environments where forklift operators work. The digital components (database maintenance, WMS use) are genuine but auxiliary; if the computer broke, 90%+ of the job would still need to happen in person. The main uncertainty is the pace of warehouse automation investment, but even optimistic projections place full forklift automation in general warehousing beyond the 2026 horizon.

title: Deckspersonal auf Schiffen und verwandte Berufe
rationale: AI cannot steer ships through narrow waters, handle mooring ropes, paint decks, or fight fires — the core work is physical and happens in unpredictable maritime environments. A small number of navigation and monitoring tasks have AI assistance, but the job remains overwhelmingly hands-on.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A subset of tasks involve digital or information-processing elements where AI tools are relevant. Navigation aids (echo sounding, electronic navigational aids, waterway traffic control systems) increasingly incorporate AI-assisted decision support. Watch-keeping and lookout duties can be partially augmented by computer vision and radar-based collision-avoidance systems. Compliance tasks — applying cargo regulations, ensuring vessel compliance, reading stowage plans — involve structured rule-following that AI can assist with. Maritime communication (GMDSS, maritime English, riverspeak) benefits from AI transcription and translation tools. These represent perhaps 15–20% of the task portfolio.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of tasks are physical, real-time, and conducted in unstructured maritime environments. Steering vessels through locks, handling ropes and mooring equipment, painting and cleaning decks, launching lifeboats, fighting fires, handling cargo, and performing engine room maintenance all require physical dexterity and situational judgment in dynamic, often hazardous conditions. Robotics capable of performing these tasks in unstructured shipboard environments do not exist at commercial scale in 2026. Safety-critical regulatory frameworks (SOLAS, MARPOL, STCW) mandate certified human crew for watch-keeping and emergency response, creating hard adoption barriers. The interpersonal coordination role of a boatswain — directing crew under stress — is also highly resistant to substitution.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 2). The primary work product is physical transformation and real-time operational control of a vessel in an unstructured environment, not a digital artifact. AI provides marginal assistance on navigation monitoring and compliance documentation, but cannot replace the physical, safety-critical, and regulatory-mandated human presence that defines the role. The score of 2 reflects that some navigational and monitoring subtasks are touched by AI tools, but substitution or meaningful productivity amplification reducing headcount is negligible in the 2026 timeframe.

title: Hilfsarbeitskräfte, onA
rationale: Elementary workers in this catch-all category do physical tasks like cleaning, carrying, sorting, and basic assembly — most of which require hands-on presence that robots handle poorly outside structured warehouses. The score stays low because physical dexterity in unstructured environments remains a hard limit for AI in 2026.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** ISCO 9000 is a residual 'not elsewhere classified' category for elementary occupations, meaning workers perform simple, routine physical tasks such as manual sorting, carrying goods, basic cleaning, and unskilled assembly. Warehouse robotics (e.g., pick-and-pack systems) and automated sorting conveyors are in early deployment and can substitute for some of these tasks in highly structured logistics environments. Computer vision systems can perform basic quality inspection that might otherwise fall to unskilled visual checkers. For any sub-tasks that involve data entry, ticket scanning, or simple form-filling, AI/RPA tools are highly capable.

**Limiting factors:** The defining characteristic of this residual category is physical, unstructured, low-skill manual work. Current robotics achieve roughly 70% human efficiency in structured warehouse environments but struggle significantly with novel physical environments, irregular objects, outdoor settings, and tasks requiring adaptive dexterity — all common in elementary work. There are no regulatory barriers per se, but the economics of deploying robotics for low-wage, highly varied physical tasks are often unfavorable compared to human labor at this wage tier. The catch-all nature of ISCO 9000 means many incumbents work in construction support, agriculture, hospitality, or municipal services where physical presence and adaptability are essential.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the low end of the scale (score 3), slightly above pure manual trades, because a meaningful minority of sub-tasks (sorting, scanning, basic data entry) are automatable, and warehouse robotics represent a genuine medium-term displacement risk for the logistics subset. However, the majority of work in this heterogeneous category involves unstructured physical environments that remain firmly human-advantaged in 2026. Confidence is low because ISCO 9000 aggregates extremely diverse sub-occupations — exposure varies widely from near-zero (outdoor manual labor) to moderate (structured warehouse sorting).

title: Reinigungspersonal und Hilfskräfte in Privathaushalten
rationale: AI cannot vacuum floors, scrub toilets, iron clothes, or wash dishes — all core tasks here require physical presence and manual dexterity in unstructured home environments. This job is almost entirely physical labour with no meaningful digital work product, placing it at minimal AI exposure.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** Nearly every essential skill listed — clean rooms, vacuum surfaces, wash the dishes, iron textiles, make the beds, perform dusting activities, clean household linens — is a physical manipulation task in an unstructured, variable domestic environment. The only plausible AI-adjacent element is inventory management (maintain inventory of cleaning supplies, restock toilet facilities' supplies), which could theoretically be assisted by a smart-home app, but this represents a trivial fraction of working time. Robotic vacuum cleaners (Roomba-class) exist but handle only flat-surface vacuuming and cannot replace the full scope of cleaning tasks, let alone ironing, dishwashing, or bathroom scrubbing.

**Limiting factors:** The entire work product is physical transformation of a real-world space — clean surfaces, laundered linen, prepared food, tidy rooms. There is no digital artifact produced. Domestic environments are highly unstructured (different layouts, clutter, fragile objects, pets, children), which places them firmly beyond 2026 robotic capabilities. Humanoid robots capable of general household manipulation remain pre-commercial and far below human efficiency in these tasks. Regulatory and trust barriers are also high: clients admit workers into their private homes, creating a strong preference for known, trusted individuals over autonomous machines. Optional skills like supervising children, feeding pets, and dog walking add further interpersonal and physical dimensions that are entirely AI-resistant.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits squarely in the 0–1 tier. The primary work product is physical, the environment is unstructured, and no commercially deployed AI or robotic system in 2026 can perform the core tasks at acceptable quality or cost. The marginal digital component (shopping lists, supply tracking) is auxiliary and does not shift the score. A score of 1 rather than 0 reflects the existence of consumer robotic vacuums and smart-home scheduling tools that provide minor assistance, but these do not reduce headcount or reshape the occupation in any meaningful way.

title: Handwäscher und Handbügler
rationale: AI cannot wash, iron, or press garments by hand — these are physical tasks requiring dexterous manipulation of delicate fabrics in unstructured environments. Robotics capable of this work at commercial scale does not yet exist, keeping AI exposure very low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A small subset of peripheral tasks has some AI relevance. Inventory tracking and linen record-keeping (linen room attendant sub-occupation: 'keep inventory records') could be assisted by digital tools or automated systems. Handling customer complaints and maintaining customer service (optional skills) involve communication that AI chatbots can partially support. Coordinating manufacturing production activities could benefit from AI scheduling tools. These, however, are auxiliary or minor tasks, not the core work product.

**Limiting factors:** The primary work product is the physical transformation of textiles — washing, ironing, pressing, dry-cleaning, and minor repairs by hand. Tasks such as 'iron textiles,' 'distinguish fabrics,' 'alter wearing apparel,' and 'sew pieces of fabric' require fine motor dexterity, tactile judgment (detecting fabric damage, appropriate heat/pressure), and manipulation of soft, deformable materials in unstructured settings. As of 2026, robotic systems capable of reliably handling, sorting, and pressing varied garments at commercial quality and speed do not exist in deployed form. The 'distinguish fabrics' skill — knowing how different materials respond to heat, moisture, and chemicals — requires sensory feedback that current robotic systems cannot replicate reliably. Physical presence and manual skill are non-negotiable for the core output.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low (2) tier. The work is almost entirely physical manipulation of soft goods, with no meaningful digital work product. The only AI-adjacent tasks (inventory records, customer communication) are peripheral and already partially digitized without displacing workers. Robotic laundry automation remains a research challenge, not a commercial reality. The score of 2 reflects that some administrative and scheduling support tools exist but do not threaten the core manual work.

title: Fahrzeugreiniger
rationale: AI cannot wash, vacuum, polish, or detail a vehicle — these are hands-on physical tasks requiring dexterity in unstructured environments. Automated car washes exist but handle only basic exterior washing, leaving the skilled manual work of detailing firmly human.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The only meaningful AI-adjacent element in this occupation is peripheral: communicating with customers (optional skill) could be partially assisted by AI scheduling or chatbot tools, and reporting/documentation tasks are trivially automatable. Robotic car wash systems already automate basic exterior washing in structured tunnel environments, which represents the closest analogue to physical automation here. Computer vision could theoretically assist with stain detection or quality inspection, but no commercially deployed system performs the full range of detailing tasks at acceptable quality.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of core tasks — vacuuming interiors, dry-cleaning upholstery, applying stain removers, blackening tyres, cleaning engine bays, polishing windows, and treating fabrics — require fine motor manipulation in highly variable, unstructured physical environments (different vehicle models, stain types, material conditions). Current robotics operate at roughly 70% human efficiency only in structured warehouse pick-and-pack settings; the complex geometry of vehicle interiors, the variety of surfaces, and the judgment required to handle delicate materials (leather, fabric, painted surfaces) are well beyond 2026 robotic capability. There are no regulatory barriers needed — the physical task itself is the barrier.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low (2) tier. The primary work product is a physically cleaned vehicle, not a digital artifact. Automated tunnel washes handle the simplest exterior washing but cannot substitute for interior detailing or specialist cleaning. The optional customer communication and reporting skills add a marginal digital component but do not shift the core exposure. The score of 2 reflects that some peripheral scheduling or customer-facing tasks may be AI-assisted, but the hands-on physical core of the job is highly resistant to automation through 2026.

title: Sonstiges Reinigungspersonal
rationale: AI cannot physically scrub carpets, pressure-wash walls, unblock sewers, or maintain pool water chemistry — the entire job is hands-on work in varied physical environments. A small administrative fringe (scheduling, customer communication) has some AI assistance, but the core work is firmly manual.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The administrative and customer-facing fringe of this occupation has modest AI exposure. Tasks like 'communicate with customers,' 'guarantee customer satisfaction,' 'maintain customer service,' and 'follow up orders for customers' could be partially supported by AI chatbots or scheduling tools. Compliance documentation — 'ensure compliance with legal requirements,' 'waste transport legislation' — could be assisted by AI-generated checklists. These tasks, however, represent a small minority of actual working time.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of tasks are physical and require direct manipulation of real-world materials in unstructured environments: grooming carpets, operating centrifugal machines, pressure-washing stone walls and fascias, cleaning sewerage systems and pipelines, removing blockages, maintaining pool water chemistry, and applying chemical cleaning agents to stains. Robotics capable of performing these varied, physically demanding tasks in unpredictable environments (sewers, cooling towers, private homes, public pools) do not exist at commercial scale as of 2026. The work also involves safety-critical chemical handling ('swimming pool chemicals,' 'protective measures related to swimming pool chemicals,' 'use personal protection equipment') that requires on-site human judgment and accountability. Sub-occupations like sewerage cleaner involve confined-space entry and hazard assessment that are firmly beyond current robotic deployment.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits squarely in the Low tier (score 2). The primary work product is a physically clean surface, system, or facility — not a digital artifact. AI tools can marginally assist with scheduling or compliance paperwork, but cannot substitute for the manual, chemical, and mechanical work that constitutes 90%+ of the role. Adoption barriers are high: unstructured physical environments, safety regulations, and the sheer variety of cleaning contexts (carpets, sewers, pools, walls) make robotic substitution implausible within the 2026 horizon.

title: Hilfsarbeiter im Gemüse- und Obstbau
rationale: AI cannot pick fruit, prune vines, weed crops, or do the physical outdoor labour that makes up nearly all of this job. The work is hands-on in unstructured natural environments, which even advanced robotics handles poorly in 2026.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A small subset of tasks touches on areas where AI or automation has made inroads. Grading, sorting, and packing produce (listed under optional skills as 'measure fruits or vegetables' and 'pack vegetables or fruits') are targets for computer-vision-guided robotic systems in controlled packhouse environments. Disease and pest control decisions ('execute disease and pest control activities') can be partially supported by AI-powered crop monitoring tools and drone imagery. Driving agricultural machines ('drive agricultural machines') is an area where autonomous tractor technology is advancing. These represent perhaps 10–15% of the core task portfolio.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of tasks — hand-picking fruit and vegetables, hand pruning, trellis repairs, planting, weeding, canopy management, and tending vines — require dexterous manipulation in highly unstructured, variable outdoor environments. Robotic fruit-picking systems exist in prototype and early commercial form but remain far below human efficiency for most crops (especially soft fruits, grapes, and irregularly shaped vegetables) and are not cost-competitive at scale in 2026. The ISCO description explicitly notes 'simple and routine tasks,' but 'routine' here means physically repetitive in variable terrain, not digitally repetitive — a crucial distinction. There are no regulatory barriers per se, but the physical environment, crop fragility, and variability of growing conditions create hard engineering barriers. The entire work product is a physical transformation of the natural world, not a digital artifact.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low (2) tier. The primary work is physical, outdoor, and requires manipulation of living plants and produce in unstructured environments — exactly where 2026 AI and robotics are weakest. While selective harvesting robots and precision agriculture tools are advancing, they are not displacing field labourers at scale. The small share of sortable/packable tasks with some automation potential is insufficient to move the score above 2. The score could rise to 3 within a few years if robotic harvesting matures for specific crops, but that remains speculative for the 2026 horizon.

title: Hilfsarbeiter in der Tierhaltung
rationale: AI cannot feed animals, clean stalls, herd livestock, or perform the physical hands-on care that makes up nearly all of this job. Some monitoring tasks use sensors, but the core work is manual and unstructured, keeping exposure very low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A small subset of tasks touch on AI-adjacent technology. 'Computerised feeding systems' and 'keep task records' involve digital interfaces where AI-driven automation (automated feeders, sensor-based health monitoring, livestock tracking software) is already commercially deployed on modern farms. 'Monitor livestock and report on their condition' is increasingly assisted by computer vision systems that flag anomalies in animal behaviour or weight. 'Grading, sorting and packing produce' can be partially automated with machine vision in structured environments. These represent perhaps 10–15% of the listed tasks and skills.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of tasks are physical and unstructured: digging ditches, loading/unloading supplies, cleaning stalls, feeding and watering animals, herding and separating livestock, raking and stacking hay, minor repairs on fences and buildings, catching poultry, and hands-on horse care (cleaning legs, providing care, training young horses). Agricultural robotics in 2026 remains limited to highly structured environments; unstructured farm settings with mud, variable terrain, live animals, and unpredictable conditions are far beyond reliable robotic deployment. Tasks like 'assist animal birth,' 'control livestock disease,' 'train horses,' and 'manage animal hygiene' require tactile judgment and physical presence that no commercially available system replicates. Regulatory and biosecurity requirements (animal welfare legislation, biosecurity protocols) also demand accountable human presence.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 2). The primary work product is physical care and management of live animals in unstructured outdoor and barn environments — exactly the domain where AI and robotics have the least traction in 2026. The modest digital footprint (computerised feeders, record-keeping) provides marginal productivity uplift but does not reduce headcount meaningfully. The score is 2 rather than 1 because sensor-based monitoring and automated feeding systems are genuinely deployed on some farms, creating a small but real AI complement. Confidence is medium because the sub-occupations span from poultry catching to equine training, with slightly different automation profiles.

title: Hilfsarbeiter in Ackerbau und Tierhaltung (ohne ausgeprägten Schwerpunkt)
rationale: AI cannot dig ditches, pick fruit, feed animals, or stack hay — the core work is entirely physical and done outdoors in unstructured environments. A few monitoring and record-keeping tasks could be AI-assisted, but these are minor parts of the job.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A small subset of tasks has some AI relevance. 'Monitor fields' and 'monitor livestock and reporting on their condition' could be partially supported by computer vision sensors, drone imagery, or IoT devices that flag anomalies — though these tools augment farm management systems rather than replace the labourer's physical presence. 'Use agricultural information systems and databases' (optional skill) and 'agronomy' knowledge suggest occasional digital data entry or lookup tasks. Precision agriculture tools can automate irrigation scheduling ('irrigate soil') and fertilisation timing ('execute fertilisation'), reducing some decision-making burden. However, these are peripheral to the core physical labour.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of tasks listed — digging, loading/unloading, raking, weeding by hand, picking fruit and vegetables, planting by hand, feeding and watering animals, cleaning animal quarters, herding livestock, grading and packing produce, minor repairs — require dexterous physical manipulation in highly unstructured outdoor and barn environments. Current robotics (2026 frontier) can handle structured warehouse pick-and-pack at ~70% human efficiency, but selective fruit harvesting robots remain commercially limited, and tasks like herding, hand-weeding in irregular terrain, or cleaning animal stalls are far beyond reliable autonomous deployment. There are no regulatory barriers per se, but the physical and environmental complexity is the binding constraint. The typical education level (no formal/primary) and wage level reflect that this is manual labour with minimal information-processing content.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low (2) tier. The primary work product is physical transformation of agricultural environments — crops grown, animals tended, produce packed — not digital artifacts. AI and robotics touch only a thin slice of peripheral monitoring and scheduling tasks. Adoption of agricultural robotics is progressing but remains years away from displacing general farm labourers in the unstructured, multi-task environments described here. The score of 2 reflects that some sensor/monitoring tools provide marginal AI assistance, but substitution risk is minimal in the 2026 timeframe.

title: Hilfsarbeiter im Gartenbau
rationale: AI cannot water plants, pull weeds, prune hedges, or haul soil — this job is almost entirely physical outdoor labour with hand tools and machinery. A small number of tasks like pest identification could be assisted by AI apps, but the core work remains firmly manual.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A narrow slice of this occupation's tasks could benefit from AI assistance. Pest and disease identification (linked to 'pest control in plants,' 'execute disease and pest control activities,' 'plant disease control') can be supported by smartphone-based computer vision apps that identify plant diseases or pests from photos — a genuinely useful 2026 capability. Scheduling and planning tasks (when to water, fertilise, or prune based on weather data) could be optimised by AI-driven horticultural management software. These represent perhaps 5–10% of actual working time for a labourer at this skill level.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of tasks — preparing planting areas, watering, weeding, mowing, pruning hedges and trees, transplanting, harvesting, hauling supplies, minor repairs — require physical manipulation in unstructured outdoor environments. Current robotics cannot reliably perform delicate hand pruning, navigate varied garden terrain, or handle the full range of plant types and conditions at acceptable cost or quality. The ISCO description explicitly frames this as 'simple and routine tasks' performed with hand tools and simple machines, but 'simple' here means physically repetitive, not digitally automatable. There are no regulatory barriers per se, but the physical-world constraint is absolute: no digital AI system can pull a weed or move a bag of compost. The typical education level (no formal/primary) and wage level also indicate tasks that are valued for physical execution, not information processing.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low (2) tier. It is almost entirely physical outdoor labour with no meaningful digital work product. The auxiliary tool rule applies strongly: if every computer in Switzerland broke tomorrow, 99% of this job would continue unchanged. The marginal AI assistance available for pest identification or watering schedules does not materially reduce labour demand. Robotic lawnmowers exist as consumer products but do not displace the broader role. Score 2 reflects that some peripheral tasks touch structured information (pest ID apps, equipment manuals) without any core substitution risk.

title: Hilfsarbeiter im Bergbau und im Bau, onA
rationale: AI cannot dig trenches, carry materials, or do manual site cleanup — the core work is physical labour in unstructured outdoor environments. Some monitoring tasks may be assisted by sensors, but the hands-on work keeps exposure very low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A small share of tasks in this occupation touch areas where AI or automation has some foothold. Basic site monitoring, safety checks, and record-keeping (where required) could be partially assisted by digital tools or sensor systems. Warehouse and construction robotics are in early deployment for structured tasks like material transport in controlled environments, and autonomous vehicles are beginning to appear on large construction sites. These represent marginal exposure for the most routine logistics sub-tasks.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of work — digging, carrying, mixing, clearing rubble, supporting tradespeople on active construction or mining sites — involves unstructured physical environments, variable terrain, and ad-hoc problem-solving that 2026 robotics cannot reliably handle. ESCO skills such as 'remove construction waste,' 'carry out manual tasks,' and 'use hand tools' describe physical transformation of real-world materials. These tasks require dexterity, situational awareness, and adaptability to constantly changing site conditions. Regulatory requirements around site safety and the need for human presence in hazardous environments further slow any automation adoption. There is no meaningful digital work product; if every computer on site broke, 95%+ of this job would continue unchanged.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 2), slightly above the minimal tier because construction robotics and autonomous site vehicles are beginning to appear in structured sub-contexts, creating a small but non-zero exposure. The core work is manual, physical, and site-specific — exactly the domain where AI and robotics remain weakest in 2026. Uncertainty is moderate because the occupation label 'not elsewhere classified' covers heterogeneous sub-roles, some of which (e.g., basic data logging) may be slightly more exposed.

title: Hilfsarbeiter im Bergbau und in der Gewinnung von Steinen und Erden
rationale: AI cannot shovel rock, clean machinery, remove tunnel supports, or sort and load materials — this job is almost entirely physical labour in unstructured underground environments. The small administrative task of keeping operation records is the only meaningful AI touchpoint, keeping the score very low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The one task with genuine AI exposure is 'maintain records of mining operations' — logging shift data, equipment status, and material movements is information work that AI tools could assist with or partially automate. 'Conduct inter-shift communication' could also be supported by AI transcription or summarisation tools. Together these represent perhaps 5–10% of working time. No optional knowledge items point to software platforms or digital tools; the optional knowledge list is dominated by mechanical and geological topics.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of tasks are physical transformations in unstructured, hazardous underground environments: removing tunnel supports, clearing rock spills, cleaning machinery, assembling and dismantling equipment, sorting and loading materials. Current robotics (2026 frontier) achieves roughly 70% human efficiency in structured warehouse pick-and-pack — but underground mining environments are among the most unstructured, variable, and physically demanding settings imaginable, with irregular surfaces, confined spaces, dust, water, and safety-critical conditions. Autonomous mining equipment exists for large-scale haul trucks and drilling rigs (ISCO 8111), but the labourer role specifically covers the residual manual tasks that such machines cannot handle. Regulatory and safety requirements for underground work further slow any automation adoption. Physical dexterity, situational awareness, and the ability to respond to novel hazards are core competencies that 2026 AI and robotics cannot replicate reliably in this context.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low (2) tier. The primary work product is physical — cleared rock, cleaned equipment, assembled machinery — not a digital artefact. AI provides negligible substitution and only marginal productivity amplification through record-keeping assistance. The score of 2 (rather than 0–1) reflects that minor administrative tasks exist and that some monitoring/sensor technology is entering mining, but the core labouring work remains deeply resistant to automation at the 2026 frontier.

title: Hilfsarbeiter im Tiefbau
