rationale: AI cannot dig trenches, shovel gravel, lay pipes, or cut concrete — this job is almost entirely physical labour on unstructured outdoor construction sites. A small number of tasks involve guiding machinery, but the core work requires human hands and bodies in unpredictable environments.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A subset of optional skills — operating road rollers, rail-laying machines, tamping cars, and mobile cranes — involves controlling machinery that is increasingly monitored or assisted by digital systems. Computer vision and GPS-guided grading equipment can automate some precision earthmoving in structured conditions. Inspecting asphalt, drainage channels, and road signs could theoretically be augmented by drone or camera-based AI inspection tools. These represent perhaps 10–15% of the task portfolio and are at most productivity-amplifying rather than substitutive at the 2026 frontier.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of tasks — digging and filling holes with hand tools, shovelling and spreading gravel, trimming rock and concrete with jackhammers, loading/unloading materials, cleaning sites — require physical manipulation in highly unstructured outdoor environments. Robotics capable of performing these tasks reliably in variable terrain, weather, and site conditions do not exist at commercial scale in 2026. The work is inherently site-specific, reactive to unexpected ground conditions, and requires constant physical adaptation. Regulatory and safety requirements on construction sites further constrain autonomous operation. There are no digital work products; the output is a physical transformation of the built environment.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the 0–1 tier. The primary work product is physical labour in unstructured environments — exactly the domain where 2026 AI and robotics have the least traction. Machinery-guidance assistance is real but marginal and does not reduce headcount meaningfully. The score of 1 (rather than 0) reflects that some equipment-monitoring tasks are beginning to incorporate digital assistance, but this is a minor fraction of the role.

title: Hilfsarbeiter im Hochbau
rationale: AI cannot mix concrete, dig trenches, carry materials, or clean demolition sites — this job is entirely physical labour in unstructured outdoor environments. Almost no part of the work is digital or automatable with 2026 technology.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** Virtually none of the 19 essential skills involve digital information processing. The closest candidates are 'inspect construction supplies' and 'inspect masonry work,' where computer vision could theoretically assist quality checks, but these are minor, context-dependent tasks performed amid the physical chaos of a live construction site — far beyond reliable 2026 robotic deployment. Optional skills like 'read standard blueprints' involve some digital information, but this is a peripheral, occasional task, not the core work product.

**Limiting factors:** Every core task — mixing and pouring concrete, digging and filling trenches, loading and transporting materials by wheelbarrow, cleaning sites — requires dexterous physical manipulation in highly unstructured, dynamic outdoor environments. Current robotics achieve roughly 70% human efficiency only in structured warehouse settings; construction sites with irregular terrain, variable weather, and constantly changing layouts are among the hardest environments for robotic systems. There are no regulatory barriers needed here — the physical capability gap alone is the barrier. The work also requires real-time adaptation to unpredictable site conditions (unstable ground, unexpected obstructions, coordination with other trades) that agentic AI cannot handle.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the 0–1 tier. The primary work product is physical transformation of a construction site, not any digital artifact. No commercially available AI tool in 2026 meaningfully automates digging, carrying, mixing, or spreading on a live building site. The only uncertainty is whether robotic construction equipment (e.g., autonomous excavators) might eventually displace some mechanised digging tasks, but this remains a research-stage capability, not a deployed one. Score: 1.

title: Verpacker
rationale: Robots can handle structured pick-and-pack tasks in controlled warehouse environments, but hand packing often involves irregular shapes, delicate items, and varied materials that still challenge automation. Physical dexterity requirements and unstructured work environments keep AI displacement limited for now.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The core tasks — weighing, filling containers, labelling, and packing goods by hand — are precisely the category where warehouse robotics and computer vision are making inroads. Amazon-style fulfillment robots and pick-and-pack systems now handle a meaningful share of standardized, high-volume packing in structured environments. Skills like 'ensure correct goods labelling,' 'comply with checklists,' 'follow written instructions,' and 'maintain stock control systems' are highly automatable via barcode/RFID systems and agentic warehouse management software. 'Operate voice picking systems' is already a semi-automated workflow. For uniform, predictable products (bottles, boxes, cans), robotic automation is commercially deployed at roughly 60–70% human efficiency and improving.

**Limiting factors:** The ESCO skill list reveals significant heterogeneity: packing fragile items, stone products, timber, vegetables/fruits, leather goods, and clothing with haberdashery (buttons, zips, ribbons) all require fine motor dexterity and adaptive judgment that current robotic systems handle poorly. 'Decorate textile articles,' 'embroider fabrics,' 'alter wearing apparel,' and 'distinguish fabrics' point to tactile, craft-adjacent sub-tasks that are firmly in the human domain. 'Lift heavy weights' and 'perform warehousing operations' in unstructured physical environments remain robotically challenging. The clothing finisher sub-occupation in particular involves irregular, soft-goods manipulation that is well beyond current robotic capability at commercial scale.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the low end of the exposure range — a solid 3. Automation is real and advancing (structured warehouse robotics), but the breadth of irregular materials, delicate items, and tactile sub-tasks described in the ESCO data means full substitution is not imminent. The occupation is more likely to see gradual workforce reduction in high-volume standardized packing roles while retaining human workers for complex, varied, or small-batch packing. Confidence is medium because the sub-occupations span a wide range from near-fully-automatable (standard hand packer in a modern warehouse) to quite resistant (clothing finisher with haberdashery work).

title: Hilfsarbeiter bei der Herstellung von Waren, anderweitig nicht genannt
rationale: AI cannot convey goods, clear machine blockages, sort components, or caulk wooden hulls — these are hands-on physical tasks in unstructured factory environments. The job is almost entirely manual work with no meaningful digital output, keeping AI exposure very low.
analysis:

Quality control sorting could theoretically be assisted by computer vision systems in highly structured environments, and inventory/material tracking (implied by 'manage delivery of raw materials') can be partially automated via warehouse management software. The 'troubleshoot' optional skill could benefit from AI-assisted diagnostics in some settings. However, none of these represent the core work product of this occupation, and even these tasks require physical execution.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of tasks — conveying goods, loading/unloading vehicles, clearing machine blockages, cleaning machinery, supplying machines with tools, and traditional wood caulking (driving oakum into hull seams with hand tools) — are physical manipulations in unstructured or semi-structured environments. Current robotics (circa 2026) achieves roughly 70% human efficiency only in highly structured warehouse pick-and-pack scenarios; the varied, reactive, and often cramped physical contexts of general factory labour and ship maintenance remain firmly human-dominated. Regulatory compliance around vessel integrity and hazardous waste handling also requires accountable human presence. The typical education profile (no formal education) reflects that this work is valued precisely for physical dexterity and adaptability, not information processing.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits squarely in the Low (2) tier. The primary work product is physical transformation and maintenance in real-world environments, with no meaningful digital output. AI tools offer essentially no substitution for the core tasks and only marginal assistance on peripheral ones. The wood caulking sub-occupation is particularly resistant, involving craft-level manual skill with traditional materials. Uncertainty is minimal — this is a clear case of physical, non-digital labour with high adoption barriers for automation.

title: Führer von Handwagen und pedalbetriebenen Fahrzeugen
rationale: AI cannot pedal a bicycle, navigate physical obstacles, or hand-deliver packages in unstructured urban environments. The job is almost entirely physical, keeping AI exposure very low despite minor digital tasks like GPS use or payment processing.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A small subset of tasks have AI or automation relevance. Route optimization ('analyse ways to reduce travel time') is already handled by GPS and logistics software, and 'operate GPS systems' and 'organise mail deliveries' are partially automatable through routing algorithms. Payment collection ('process payments') can be digitized via contactless systems. These peripheral digital tasks represent perhaps 10–15% of the actual work content and are already largely automated in practice, meaning the marginal AI impact is limited.

**Limiting factors:** The core work product is the physical act of propelling a vehicle through unstructured urban environments and completing last-meter delivery or passenger transport. Tasks like loading/unloading goods, handling fragile items, navigating traffic in real time, and interacting with customers at the point of delivery require physical presence, dexterous manipulation, and situational judgment in unpredictable environments. Current robotics cannot operate a bicycle or cycle rickshaw in mixed urban traffic at acceptable cost or reliability. The 'ensure integrity of mail' and 'differentiate types of packages' tasks require hands-on physical assessment. Regulatory and infrastructure barriers to autonomous delivery vehicles (especially two-wheeled) remain substantial in Swiss urban contexts.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low (2) tier. The primary work product is physical transportation and delivery, not information processing. AI tools touch only peripheral scheduling and payment tasks, and even those are already largely handled by existing digital tools rather than representing new AI displacement. The main automation threat to this occupation is not AI per se but autonomous delivery robots or drones — technologies that remain pre-commercial for general urban deployment as of 2026. Score: 2.

title: Frachtarbeiter und verwandte Berufe
rationale: AI cannot physically lift, carry, pack, or load freight — the core work here is moving objects in unstructured real-world environments. Some warehouse scanning and routing tasks are being automated, but the physical handling work keeps this occupation firmly low-exposure.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A subset of tasks in this occupation involve digital or information-processing work that AI can assist with. 'Handle paperwork related to warehouse stock,' 'write inspection reports,' 'dispatch order processing,' 'maintain stock control systems,' and 'operate warehouse record systems' are all administrative tasks where AI tools (document processing, inventory management software, voice-picking systems) are already deployed. Route planning ('determine itineraries of bulk trucks,' 'oversee shipment routing') and compliance checking ('ensure that shipment contents correspond with shipping documentation') are also increasingly automated in logistics platforms. These represent perhaps 15–20% of the task portfolio.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of tasks — carrying objects, lifting heavy weights, loading pallets, stacking goods, packing furniture, handling intermodal equipment, working on uneven surfaces, connecting hoses between pipes and tankers — require physical manipulation in unstructured environments. As of 2026, robotics can handle structured warehouse pick-and-pack at roughly 70% human efficiency in controlled settings, but the heterogeneous, unpredictable physical environments described here (docks, aircraft aprons, moving trucks, residential relocations) remain firmly beyond reliable robotic deployment. Tasks like 'assess stability of vessels,' 'maintain counterweight inside modes of transport,' and 'work on uneven surfaces' require real-time physical judgment that current systems cannot replicate. Regulatory and safety requirements (dangerous goods laws, port regulations, ramp safety) also demand human accountability on-site.

**Net assessment:** This is a low-exposure occupation scoring 2. The primary work product is physical transformation — goods moved from one place to another — not a digital artifact. The small share of administrative and routing tasks that AI can assist with does not materially reduce headcount, as those tasks are ancillary to the physical labor. Warehouse automation (conveyor systems, RFID, voice-picking) has been incrementally reducing some roles for decades, but the unstructured, variable physical environments typical of freight handling create durable barriers to AI substitution through at least 2026.

title: Regalbetreuer und -auffüller
rationale: AI cannot physically stock shelves, rotate products, or unpack deliveries — the entire job involves moving goods in unstructured retail environments. analysis:

Price label checking and shelf label changes could theoretically be flagged by automated systems. These peripheral information tasks represent perhaps 10–15% of actual working time.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of this occupation's tasks are physical and unstructured: placing goods on racks, stacking bulky items, rotating stock by use-by date, unpacking deliveries, and inspecting merchandise for damage. As of 2026, robotic shelf-stocking remains in very early-stage pilots (e.g., Simbe Robotics' Tally for scanning, not stocking) and operates at well below human efficiency in the varied, cluttered aisles of real supermarkets. Handling diverse product shapes, weights, and packaging in dynamic retail environments is precisely the kind of unstructured physical manipulation where robotics still falls far short. Customer-facing tasks — directing shoppers, retrieving items — add an interpersonal layer that further resists automation. Compliance with food safety and hygiene requires real-time physical judgment that AI cannot yet exercise autonomously.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 2). The primary work product is physical shelf order and product availability, not a digital artifact. AI tools can assist with inventory tracking at the margins, but no commercially deployed system in 2026 can replace the physical labor of stocking shelves in a real retail environment. The score is 2 rather than 1 because inventory monitoring software does meaningfully touch part of the role, but the physical core is highly resistant to near-term automation.

title: Hilfskräfte in der Nahrungsmittelzubereitung, onA
rationale: Food preparation helpers do physical tasks like washing vegetables, peeling, portioning, and cleaning kitchens — work that requires hands-on manipulation in unstructured environments. Robotics can handle some structured tasks but cannot yet reliably replace the full range of physical kitchen work.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** Some repetitive, structured physical tasks performed by food preparation helpers — such as portioning identical items, basic sorting, or operating standardized equipment — are candidates for early-stage robotic automation. Warehouse-style food processing lines already use robotic pick-and-place systems for uniform products. Additionally, any scheduling, inventory tracking, or simple record-keeping that helpers may assist with is easily automated. These represent a minority of actual work time.

These tasks occur in unstructured, wet, cluttered, and rapidly changing settings where 2026 robotics perform poorly. Delicate manipulation (e.g., handling fragile produce, adapting to irregular shapes), spatial navigation around other workers, and responsiveness to real-time instructions from chefs all resist current AI and robotic capabilities. There are no digital work products, no information-processing core, and no regulatory pathway for autonomous kitchen robots in commercial food service. The auxiliary tool rule applies fully: if all computers broke, 100% of this job would still need to happen in person.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 2). The work is almost entirely physical manipulation in unstructured environments — exactly where 2026 AI and robotics are weakest. A marginal score above 1 reflects that structured food-processing lines (not typical restaurant kitchens) do deploy some automation, and that the occupation has no AI-resistant complexity barrier beyond physical dexterity. Uncertainty stems from the heterogeneity of settings (industrial canteen vs. restaurant kitchen), but the direction is clear.

title: Zubereiter von Fast Food und anderen Imbissen
rationale: AI cannot grill burgers, assemble pizzas, or hand food to customers — the core work is physical and happens in a kitchen. A few tasks like taking orders or processing payments have some automation potential, but the job is overwhelmingly hands-on.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A small subset of tasks have meaningful AI or automation exposure. Taking orders (including drive-through orders) is increasingly handled by kiosk screens and voice-AI systems already deployed in fast food chains. Processing payments is largely automated via POS terminals. Monitoring stock levels and checking deliveries could be assisted by inventory management software. These peripheral tasks represent perhaps 15–20% of actual working time, and the automation is already largely complete (kiosks, POS) rather than an emerging threat.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of tasks — operating grills, deep-fat fryers, and brick ovens; washing, cutting, and mixing ingredients; assembling sandwiches, pizzas, and burgers; cleaning surfaces and food preparation areas; maintaining hygiene standards; and physically handing food to customers — require dexterous manipulation in an unstructured, hot, wet, and fast-changing physical environment. 2026 robotics cannot reliably perform these tasks at commercial scale or cost. Burger-flipping robots exist in limited pilots but remain far below human efficiency and flexibility for the full range of tasks in this role. Food safety and hygiene compliance also requires physical presence and judgment. The interpersonal elements (greeting guests, upselling, maintaining customer service) further anchor the role in human interaction.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low (2–3) tier. The primary work product is physical food preparation and service, not a digital artifact. AI and automation touch only the order-taking and payment periphery, and even those automations are already largely deployed without dramatically reducing headcount. The core cooking, assembly, and cleaning tasks are robustly resistant to 2026 AI and robotics capabilities. The score of 2 reflects that some order-taking automation is real and ongoing, but the job as a whole is not meaningfully reshaped by AI at the current frontier.

title: Hilfsköche und Küchengehilfen

Robotics capable of performing these tasks at commercial kitchen speed and reliability do not exist at scale in 2026. Dish-washing robots exist in niche deployments but remain expensive, slow, and limited to structured setups. Food prep robots (peeling, chopping, mixing) are emerging in highly controlled factory settings but are not deployed in the varied, space-constrained environments of typical restaurant kitchens. The physical, interpersonal, and hygiene-compliance demands (COSHH procedures, food safety) further anchor this role in human execution.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low (2) tier. The primary work product is a physically clean and prepared kitchen environment — not a digital artifact. AI and robotics have essentially zero current deployment in this role's core tasks, and adoption barriers (cost, unstructured environments, food safety liability) are high. The score of 2 rather than 0-1 reflects that inventory and stock monitoring tools provide marginal AI assistance, and that longer-term robotics trajectories are worth acknowledging, but the 2026 frontier leaves this role almost entirely untouched.

title: Strassenverkäufer (ohne Lebensmittel)
rationale: Street vending is almost entirely physical: carrying goods, approaching people in public spaces, demonstrating products, and collecting cash on the spot. AI cannot replace the mobility, physical presence, and real-time human interaction that define this job.
analysis:

Payment processing (process payments, operate cash register) is increasingly automated via contactless terminals and point-of-sale apps. Product comprehension and sales argumentation could theoretically be supported by AI-generated scripts or product information tools. Electronic communication channels, listed as an optional skill, represent a minor digital touchpoint. These peripheral elements account for perhaps 10–15% of actual work time.

**Limiting factors:** The core work product is entirely physical and interpersonal: transporting goods to public locations, physically displaying merchandise, approaching strangers on streets or door-to-door, demonstrating product features in person, and collecting immediate payment. None of these tasks can be delegated to 2026 AI systems. Robotics capable of navigating unstructured urban environments, engaging passersby, and handling varied merchandise in outdoor conditions do not exist at commercial scale. The occupation also requires real-time adaptation to weather, crowd dynamics, and individual customer reactions — all deeply embodied skills. There are no regulatory barriers to AI adoption that matter here; the barrier is simply physical reality. The typical education profile (no formal education) and the Swiss employment count of 77 workers further suggest a niche, hands-on role with no meaningful digitization pathway.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low (2) tier. The primary work product is physical presence and direct human sales interaction in unstructured public environments — exactly the domain where AI and robotics remain weakest in 2026. The minor digital touchpoints (payment terminals, optional electronic communication) are auxiliary tools that do not shift the core exposure. Confidence is high given the unambiguous physical nature of all essential tasks.

title: Abfallentsorgungsarbeiter, onA
rationale: Collecting and sorting waste, operating trucks, and handling bins are physical tasks that AI-driven robots cannot yet reliably perform in unstructured outdoor environments. The job is almost entirely manual and location-dependent, keeping AI exposure very low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** ISCO 9610 waste disposal workers perform tasks such as collecting refuse from households and businesses, loading waste onto vehicles, and sorting materials at collection points. Route optimization software and sensor-equipped bins already assist logistics planning, and AI-powered sorting systems are deployed in some recycling facilities. These tools can reduce the number of workers needed for back-office scheduling and some facility-based sorting, representing a modest but real automation pressure on peripheral tasks.

**Limiting factors:** The core work product is the physical movement and handling of waste in unstructured, variable outdoor environments — streets, alleys, private properties — where robotic systems remain far below human efficiency. Delicate manipulation (e.g., navigating obstacles, handling irregular containers, working in adverse weather) and the need to respond to unexpected situations (blocked access, hazardous materials, public interaction) are firmly human-advantaged at the 2026 frontier. There are no digital work products; the 'auxiliary tool rule' applies fully here. Regulatory and safety requirements around waste handling also slow automation adoption.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits squarely in the Low (2) tier. A small share of peripheral tasks — route scheduling, facility sorting — face genuine AI/automation pressure, but the dominant physical, outdoor, and unstructured nature of the core work resists substitution. The score of 2 reflects that some structured digital tools are in use, but the primary work remains manual and AI-resistant for the foreseeable future.

title: Arbeiter in der Abfall- und Wertstoffsammlung
rationale: AI cannot lift bins, ride trucks, or physically collect and sort rubbish from streets and buildings. The job is almost entirely manual and outdoors, leaving very little for AI to automate.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A small number of tasks have some AI relevance. Maintaining waste collection records and maintaining recycling records are administrative tasks that could be partially automated through route-management software and digital logging tools. Assessing waste type could theoretically be assisted by computer vision systems already used in some automated sorting facilities. Reporting pollution incidents and identifying damage to public space could be supported by mobile apps with image recognition. These peripheral tasks represent perhaps 10–15% of actual working time.

**Limiting factors:** The core work product is entirely physical: lifting and emptying bins, riding on trucks, loading and unloading waste in unstructured outdoor environments. The ISCO task list is dominated by physical manipulation — collecting domestic and industrial waste, maintaining equipment, managing waste flows on-site. Robotic waste collection exists in very limited pilot form but operates at well below human efficiency in the unstructured, variable environments (narrow streets, mixed bin types, parked cars, weather) that define this role in Switzerland. Regulatory and safety requirements around hazardous waste handling, PPE use, and waste transport legislation require human accountability and physical presence. There are no meaningful adoption pathways for AI substitution in the 2026 timeframe.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 2). The primary work product is physical transformation of the environment — removing waste — not information processing. Digital tools play only an auxiliary role (record-keeping, route optimization) and do not reduce headcount. The main uncertainty is whether autonomous garbage trucks could eventually eliminate the riding/loading role, but that technology is not commercially deployed at scale and faces significant unstructured-environment challenges. A score of 2 reflects the small administrative task share that AI tools can assist with, without meaningfully reducing the need for workers.

title: Arbeiter in der Abfallsortierung
rationale: AI cannot physically sort waste, lift broken appliances, or operate recycling equipment on a sorting floor. The job is almost entirely hands-on physical work in unstructured environments, keeping AI exposure very low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A small subset of tasks touch on information processing: maintaining recycling records, ensuring compliance with waste legislative regulations, and communicating with waste collectors could be partially supported by AI tools (e.g., automated logging, regulatory lookup systems). Computer vision-based sorting robots exist in some industrial recycling facilities and can identify and separate certain material streams (paper, plastic, metal) with moderate accuracy in structured conveyor environments. This represents the most meaningful AI/automation pressure on the occupation, though it is robotic automation rather than generative AI per se.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of core tasks — physically searching through refuse, manually sorting cardboard/glass/plastic/aluminium, dismantling broken appliances, handling chemical agents, transporting items by hand or non-motorized vehicle, identifying reusable furniture and equipment — require physical manipulation in highly unstructured, variable environments. Waste streams are heterogeneous, contaminated, and unpredictable; robotic systems currently achieve only ~70% human efficiency even in structured warehouse settings, and recycling sorting floors are far less controlled. Regulatory compliance (waste legislative regulations, hazardous waste handling) requires on-site human judgment and accountability. Physical safety requirements (PPE use, hazardous liquid drainage) further anchor the role to human presence.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low (2) tier. The primary work product is physical sorting and handling of waste materials, not digital artifacts. Robotic sorting automation is an emerging pressure but remains limited to structured conveyor contexts and does not yet threaten the broad, unstructured physical work that defines this role. Administrative subtasks (record-keeping, compliance reporting) are minor and auxiliary. No meaningful adoption of AI tools is expected to reshape this occupation by 2026.

title: Strassenkehrer und verwandte Berufe
rationale: AI cannot sweep streets, shovel snow, or clean drains — this job is almost entirely physical outdoor work in unstructured environments. Some machine operation may be partially automated, but human presence and judgment remain essential.
analysis:

Operating mechanical and motorised street sweeping machines could eventually involve autonomous vehicle technology, and completing activity report sheets is a straightforward digital task AI could assist with. Waste management routing and scheduling — adjacent to this role — is already being optimised by AI logistics tools. These peripheral elements represent perhaps 10–15% of the actual work content.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of tasks — sweeping streets manually, shovelling snow, cleaning road drains, removing graffiti, handling chemical agents, and performing emergency clean-ups — require physical presence in unstructured outdoor environments. Current robotics operate at roughly 70% human efficiency only in highly structured settings (e.g., warehouses); public streets with variable terrain, weather, pedestrians, and unexpected debris are far beyond reliable autonomous deployment in 2026. The essential skills list is dominated by physical manipulation, safety compliance in traffic, and equipment maintenance — none of which AI can substitute for at scale. Regulatory compliance and waste legislation knowledge require contextual human judgment on-site.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low (2) tier. The primary work product is a physically clean public space, achieved through manual and machine-assisted labour in unpredictable outdoor conditions. AI contributes marginally through route optimisation software or report automation, but cannot replace the core physical work. Autonomous street-sweeping vehicles exist in limited pilots but are far from displacing human workers at scale by 2026. The score of 2 reflects minimal but non-zero digital/automation adjacency.

title: Boten, Paketauslieferer und Gepäckträger
rationale: AI cannot carry luggage, deliver packages on foot, or welcome guests in person — the entire job is physical presence and manual handling. A small route-planning task could be AI-assisted, but this barely affects the overall workload.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The one meaningful AI-touchable task is route planning and optimisation (task e: 'planning and following the most efficient route'), where tools like Google Maps or logistics algorithms already assist workers. Sorting items by delivery route (task f) is also partially automatable in warehouse settings. These tasks, however, represent a small fraction of total working time and are already largely handled by digital tools embedded in delivery apps — meaning the marginal AI uplift from 2026 capabilities is limited. The optional skill 'implement marketing strategies' and 'provide tourism related information' could theoretically be supported by AI-generated content, but these are peripheral to the core role.

**Limiting factors:** The primary work product is entirely physical: carrying and handing over luggage, delivering packages door-to-door, welcoming guests, parking vehicles, and providing in-person hospitality. Essential skills such as 'greet guests,' 'handle guest luggage,' 'assist clients with special needs,' and 'detect drug abuse' all require physical presence, human judgment in real-time interpersonal situations, and dexterous manipulation in unstructured environments (hotel lobbies, airports, residential doorsteps). Robotics capable of navigating these environments at acceptable cost and reliability remain far from commercial deployment at scale in Switzerland by 2026. The hotel porter and doorman sub-occupations are especially anchored in human presence and trust — guests expect a human face. Regulatory and liability considerations around guest safety and security further slow any automation.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low (2) tier. The work is almost entirely physical and interpersonal, with no digital work product. The only AI-relevant task — route optimisation — is already handled by existing tools and represents a minor share of the job. Autonomous delivery robots and humanoid hotel porters remain experimental and economically unviable for Swiss hospitality and last-mile delivery contexts in 2026. The score of 2 reflects marginal AI assistance in logistics planning while acknowledging that the core job is essentially robot- and AI-proof at the current frontier.

title: Gelegenheitsarbeiter
rationale: AI cannot repair windows, paint walls, replace tap washers, or carry out the hands-on physical maintenance that defines this job. The work is almost entirely physical and site-specific, leaving very little for AI to automate.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A small number of peripheral tasks have some AI relevance. Maintaining records of maintenance interventions could be assisted by AI-powered logging or voice-to-text tools. Calculating costs of repair operations could be partially supported by AI estimating tools. Reading technical datasheets is a task where AI can assist with interpretation. These represent perhaps 10–15% of the actual work content, and even here the AI role is assistive rather than substitutive — the worker still needs to physically execute the job.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of tasks — repairing broken windows, painting interior and exterior surfaces, replacing tap washers, adjusting doors and windows, installing floor coverings, repairing plumbing systems, performing ground maintenance — require physical presence, manual dexterity, and real-time problem-solving in unstructured environments. Current robotics (2026 frontier) cannot perform delicate multi-trade maintenance tasks in varied residential and commercial settings at acceptable cost or reliability. The role also requires direct customer interaction and on-site judgment about what repairs are needed. Regulatory and safety requirements (fire safety, working at heights, electrical wiring) further anchor the work to trained human execution. There are no digital work products; the output is a physically repaired or maintained building.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low (2) tier. The primary work product is physical transformation of real-world environments, and 2026 AI — including robotics — cannot replicate the multi-trade, unstructured, hands-on nature of odd-job maintenance work. The marginal digital tasks (record-keeping, cost calculation) are auxiliary and do not materially shift the exposure. The score of 2 reflects that basic digital tools may modestly assist scheduling and documentation, but the core job is essentially AI-resistant.

title: Zählerableser, Automatenbefüller und -kassierer
rationale: Smart meters and remote reading technology already automate the meter-reading part of this job, and cashless vending machines reduce cash collection rounds. However, physical route-driving, machine restocking, fault inspection, and handling unauthorized connections still require human presence.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** The meter-reading sub-occupation is under direct pressure from automatic meter reading (AMR/smart meter) technology, listed as an optional skill, which eliminates the need for physical visits to read electricity, gas, and water meters. This is not speculative — smart meter rollout in Switzerland and across Europe has already substantially reduced meter reader headcount. Recording and reporting consumption (tasks c, d, e, f) are fully automatable once remote sensors are in place. For vending machine operators, cashless payment systems and IoT-connected machines can report cash levels, stock levels, and faults remotely, reducing the frequency of physical visits and the cognitive load of route planning. GPS route optimization (already listed as a skill) is trivially automated. Record-keeping and stock rotation tracking are standard digital workflows.

**Limiting factors:** The core remaining work is irreducibly physical: driving routes, physically restocking vending machines, collecting cash from legacy coinboxes, inspecting meters for broken seals, unauthorized connections, and corrosion damage. These tasks require unstructured physical presence — entering buildings, handling goods, assessing physical condition — that 2026 robotics cannot perform cost-effectively at scale. Food hygiene compliance, cold chain management, and basic machine maintenance require hands-on judgment. Inspecting for unauthorized utility connections involves on-site assessment that cannot be remotely automated without dense sensor infrastructure. Many legacy meters and vending machines in Switzerland still lack smart connectivity, preserving demand for physical visits in the near term.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at moderate exposure (4). The meter-reading sub-occupation is being hollowed out by smart infrastructure — a structural, ongoing trend rather than a future risk — but the vending machine operator role retains a physical core that resists automation. The split nature of the ISCO group and the uneven pace of smart meter adoption create uncertainty. The score reflects that roughly half the task portfolio is already automated or automatable, but the remaining physical tasks are genuinely resistant, placing this at the lower end of the moderate tier rather than high.

title: Hilfsarbeitskräfte, anderweitig nicht genannt
rationale: Most tasks here are physical and interpersonal — directing patrons, operating rides, handling coats, cleaning facilities — things AI cannot do without robotics that aren't deployed at scale. The job is anchored in real-world presence and manual work, keeping AI exposure very low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A small subset of tasks has some AI relevance. Ticket checking could be partially automated via barcode scanners and computer vision (already common at many venues). Selling tickets and calculating parking charges are digitally automatable and largely already handled by self-service kiosks. Providing directions or amusement park information could theoretically be handled by chatbots or digital signage. These tasks represent perhaps 10–15% of the overall task portfolio across the sub-occupations listed.

**Limiting factors:** The overwhelming majority of essential skills — operating rides, checking safety restraints, monitoring ride communications, cleaning floors and glass surfaces, washing laundry, hanging advertising posters, tending to clients' personal items, managing lost-and-found articles, assisting visitors with special needs, controlling crowds, and providing emergency guidance — all require physical presence and manual dexterity in unstructured environments. Robotics capable of performing these tasks at acceptable cost and reliability are not commercially deployed at scale as of 2026. The interpersonal dimension (greeting guests, guaranteeing customer satisfaction, assisting distressed visitors) also resists AI substitution. Regulatory and safety requirements around ride operation and emergency procedures further anchor human presence.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 2). The primary work product is physical service delivery and real-world presence, not digital artifacts. The few digitally automatable tasks (ticketing, fee collection) are already being displaced by self-service technology rather than AI per se, and they represent a minor share of the role. The breadth of sub-occupations (locker room attendant, attraction operator, advertising installer, laundromat attendant) all share the same physical-world anchor. No meaningful AI substitution wave is imminent for the core work.

title: Medizintechniker im Bereich Labor und Pathologie
rationale: AI can automate data entry, flag abnormal results, and assist with image analysis of cell specimens, but the core work involves hands-on specimen handling, equipment calibration, and physical laboratory procedures. The job remains anchored in physical manipulation and regulated clinical validation, keeping AI exposure moderate.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** Several tasks in this occupation are meaningfully AI-assisted in 2026. Automated analysers already handle much of the quantitative chemical analysis of blood, urine, and other fluids — the 'automated analysers in the medical laboratory' skill reflects this existing automation layer. AI-powered image analysis tools can assist with 'examine cell specimens microscopically' and 'clinical cytology,' with FDA-cleared algorithms now flagging abnormalities in histopathology slides. Data entry and record-keeping tasks ('record data from biomedical tests,' 'manage healthcare users' data,' 'archive healthcare users' records') are highly automatable via LIS/LIMS integration and AI agents. Result validation workflows and quality control pattern detection ('implement quality control procedures,' 'validate biomedical analysis results') can be partially automated through statistical process control software enhanced by ML. These digital and analytical subtasks represent perhaps 25–35% of the role.

**Limiting factors:** The dominant share of this occupation involves irreducibly physical work: preparing samples, calibrating and maintaining equipment, performing biopsies, collecting biological samples, transporting specimens, and operating wet-lab procedures. Skills like 'carry out biopsy,' 'techniques of blood-sampling,' 'mix chemicals,' 'wear appropriate protective gear,' and 'apply safety procedures in laboratory' cannot be performed by software and are not yet within reach of deployed laboratory robotics at the precision and flexibility required for clinical settings. Regulatory frameworks (Swiss healthcare legislation, ISO 15189 accreditation) require human accountability for validated results, and the 'validate biomedical analysis results' task specifically demands a credentialed professional to sign off. Clinical judgment in ambiguous cases — distinguishing artifact from pathology, managing emergency care situations, and interacting with healthcare users — further resists substitution. The breadth of the essential skills list (111 items spanning microbiology, immunology, molecular biology, histopathology) reflects genuine multi-domain expertise that AI cannot yet integrate reliably in novel clinical contexts.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits at the lower end of moderate exposure (score 4). AI and automation are already embedded in the workflow via analysers and LIS systems, and will expand into image-assisted diagnostics and data management. However, the primary work product is not a digital artifact — it is a validated clinical result derived from physical specimen handling, wet-lab procedures, and regulated professional judgment. The 'auxiliary tool rule' applies: digital skills here support physical laboratory work rather than constituting the core output. Adoption barriers are high due to clinical safety regulation and the physical environment. The occupation is more likely to see productivity amplification (fewer technicians needed per test volume) than wholesale substitution, placing it solidly in the moderate tier rather than high.

title: Werkzeugmechaniker und verwandte Berufe
rationale: AI can assist with reading engineering drawings and generating CNC toolpaths, but the core work — physically shaping metal to fine tolerances, fitting parts, and repairing mechanisms — requires hands-on dexterity in unstructured environments. The job is anchored in physical transformation of materials, keeping AI exposure low.
analysis:
**Exposure drivers:** A meaningful subset of tasks involves structured information processing where AI tools provide genuine assistance. Reading and interpreting 2D/3D engineering drawings (interpret 2D plans, interpret 3D plans, read assembly drawings, read standard blueprints) can be partially supported by AI-assisted CAD analysis. CNC machine setup (set up the controller of a machine, monitor automated machines) increasingly involves AI-optimized toolpath generation. Calculating dimensions, tolerances, and shrinkage allowances (calculate allowances for shrinkage in casting processes, visualizing and computing dimensions) are amenable to AI-assisted computation. CAD software appears as an optional skill, and roughly 20–25% of listed optional skills relate to digital or analytical tasks. Administrative tasks like inventory planning, appointment scheduling, and quotation calculation are highly automatable.

**Limiting factors:** The dominant work product is physical — cutting, milling, grinding, boring, fitting, and assembling metal components to fine tolerances. Tasks such as positioning and securing metal stock, operating grinding and polishing hand tools, deburring, fitting jig and fixture parts, repairing firearms and lock mechanisms, and verifying dimensions with precision measuring instruments all require fine manual dexterity in variable, unstructured physical environments. Robotic systems in 2026 can handle structured pick-and-place at roughly 70% human efficiency but cannot replicate the adaptive tactile judgment needed for precision fitting, troubleshooting mechanical failures, or restoring old guns. Regulatory and safety considerations around firearms (repair firearms, assemble guns, instruct customers on ammunition usage) add further barriers. Customer-facing tasks (maintain customer service, provide customer information related to repairs, advise on strengthening security) require interpersonal trust that AI cannot substitute in this trade context.

**Net assessment:** This occupation sits firmly in the Low tier (score 3). The primary work product is physical transformation of metal to exacting tolerances — a domain where 2026 robotics and AI remain well below human capability in unstructured workshop environments. AI provides genuine but peripheral assistance in drawing interpretation, CNC programming, and administrative tasks, but these are auxiliary to the core craft. The occupation is analogous to an electrician or cook: digitally touched at the edges but physically anchored at the core. No significant uncertainty warrants a confidence reduction.
